\text{RMSE}_{\text{naive}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{t=2}^{n}\left(Y_t - Y_{t-1}\right)^2}
$$

This benchmark RMSE is the denominator in the **MASE** metric.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mean Forecast Benchmark

$$
\hat{Y}_{t+h} = \bar{Y} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t
$$

Its squared error is:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2 = (n-1)S^{\,2}
$$

where $S^{\,2}$ is the sample variance. Thus:

$$
\text{MSE}_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{(n-1)S^{\,2}}{n}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Drift Forecast Benchmark

The *drift* method allows the level to drift linearly across the historical mean:

$$
\hat{Y}_{t+h} = Y_t + h\left(\dfrac{Y_t - Y_1}{t - 1}\right)
$$

The drift term $d = (Y_t - Y_1)/(t-1)$ is the average per-period change over the historical span. This becomes the building block for random-walk-with-drift ARIMA models later in the course.

### 3.4 Derivation of MAPE and its Bias

By definition:

$$
\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\left\vert\dfrac{Y_t - \hat{Y}_t}{Y_t}\right\vert
$$

Note that MAPE is **asymmetric**: it penalizes positive errors more heavily than negative errors. If the true value $Y_t = 100$ and $\hat{Y}_t = 110$ (over-forecast by 10%), the absolute percentage error is:

$$
\text{APE}_1 = \left\vert\dfrac{100 - 110}{100}\right\vert = 10\%
$$

If instead $\hat{Y}_t = 90$ (under-forecast by 10), we get:

$$
\text{APE}_2 = \left\vert\dfrac{100 - 90}{100}\right\vert = 10\%
$$

These are equal in this symmetric example. However, if $\hat{Y}_t = 50$ (under-forecast by 50):

$$
\text{APE}_{\text{under}} = 50\%
$$

If $\hat{Y}_t = 150$ (over-forecast by 50):

$$
\text{APE}_{\text{over}} = \left\vert\dfrac{100 - 150}{100}\right\vert = 50\%
$$

They coincide here, but in general MAPE has *no upper bound on the under-forecast side* (e.g., if $\hat{Y}_t \to 0$, APE $\to 100\%$), and therefore **MASE** is preferred for cross-series comparison — a KTU favourite observation.

### 3.5 Derivation of the $(1-\alpha)$ Prediction Interval for a Random Walk

Assume $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ where $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_e^{\,2})$ i.i.d.

The $h$-step-ahead forecast is:

$$
\hat{Y}_{T+h} = Y_T
$$

The forecast error is:

$$
e_{T+h} = Y_{T+h} - \hat{Y}_{T+h} = \sum_{j=1}^{h}\varepsilon_{T+j}
$$

Because the $\varepsilon$'s are independent and identically distributed with zero mean:

$$
\mathbb{E}[e_{T+h}] = 0
$$

The variance is:

$$
\text{Var}(e_{T+h}) = h\,\sigma_e^{\,2}
$$

Hence the standard deviation of the forecast error grows with $\sqrt{h}$:

$$
\text{SD}(e_{T+h}) = \sigma_e\sqrt{h}
$$

The Gaussian $(1-\alpha)$ prediction interval is therefore:

$$
\hat{Y}_{T+h} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma_e\sqrt{h}
$$

This derivation is **board-critical** and appears verbatim in almost every KTU Time Series paper.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Forecast Step

| Step | Output Artifact | Common Student Error |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1. Problem Definition | Written objective statement | Skipping this in 14-mark answers loses 2 marks |
| 2. Data Collection | Raw series, frequency, sample size | Failing to record frequency |
| 3. Pre-processing | Cleaned series, transformation applied | Not reporting Box-Cox $\lambda$ chosen |
| 4. Exploratory TSA | ACF/PACF plots, decomposition plots | Not justifying additive vs multiplicative |
| 5. Model Selection | Final candidate model class | Jumping to ARIMA without EDA |
| 6. Diagnostics | Residual plots, Ljung-Box p-value | Forgetting to test residual stationarity |
| 7. Forecast + Evaluation | Point forecast + interval + accuracy metric | Quoting only point forecast, no interval |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Seven-Step Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[step1 Problem Definition] --> B[step2 Data Collection]
    B --> C[step3 Data Preparation]
    C --> D[step4 Exploratory TSA]
    D --> E[step5 Model Identification]
    E --> F[step6 Fit and Diagnostics]
    F --> G{step7 Residual Check Pass}
    G -->|No| E
    G -->|Yes| H[step7 Forecast Generation]
    H --> I[step7 Evaluation Metrics]
    I --> J[step7 Monitoring Loop]
    J -->|New Data| B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Forecast Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Observed Series Yt] --> B1[Hold out Split]
    B1 --> C1[Train Partition]
    B1 --> D1[Test Partition]
    C1 --> E1[Fitted Model]
    E1 --> F1[Fitted Values]
    F1 --> G1[In Sample Error]
    E1 --> H1[Out of Sample Forecast]
    D1 --> I1[Compute MAE]
    H1 --> I1
    D1 --> J1[Compute RMSE]
    H1 --> J1
    D1 --> K1[Compute MAPE]
    H1 --> K1
    I1 --> L1[Model Performance Report]
    J1 --> L1
    K1 --> L1
    L1 --> M1{Acceptable Accuracy}
    M1 -->|Yes| N1[Deploy Forecast]
    M1 -->|No| E1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decomposition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Obs[Observed Yt] --> Add[Additive Model]
    Obs --> Mul[Multiplicative Model]
    Add --> T1[Trend Tt]
    Add --> S1[Seasonal St]
    Add --> C1[Cycle Ct]
    Add --> I1[Irregular It]
    Mul --> T2[Trend Tt]
    Mul --> S2[Seasonal St]
    Mul --> C2[Cycle Ct]
    Mul --> I2[Irregular It]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in the Exam:**
> A common KTU 14-mark question asks: *"Draw the block diagram of the forecasting procedure and explain each block."* The seven-node flowchart above is the **canonical answer**. Memorize its block labels in the exact order — examiners award step-wise marks (2 marks per correctly labelled block).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three steps involved in the forecasting process. Briefly explain the role of the **data preparation step**.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three steps in the forecasting process are:
1. Problem definition and objective setting — establishes what to forecast and at what horizon.
2. Data collection — gathering the historical observations at the required frequency and granularity.
3. Model identification and selection — choosing an appropriate statistical model class based on observed patterns.

**Role of Data Preparation:** This step converts the raw, possibly noisy observations into a model-ready series. It involves handling missing values, detecting and correcting outliers, applying variance-stabilizing transformations (e.g., **Box-Cox**), and differencing to achieve stationarity. Without proper preparation, the fitted model may be biased, residual diagnostics may be invalid, and forecast accuracy may collapse.
*(1 mark per step + 2 marks for elaboration — examiners expect at least one concrete example of a transformation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)**. State one limitation of using MAPE as a forecast accuracy metric.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\left\vert\dfrac{Y_t - \hat{Y}_t}{Y_t}\right\vert
$$

where $Y_t$ is the observed value and $\hat{Y}_t$ is the forecast.

**Limitation:** MAPE is undefined (or infinite) when any observed value $Y_t = 0$. Furthermore, MAPE is asymmetric — it penalizes positive (over-forecast) errors more heavily than negative (under-forecast) errors of the same magnitude, making it unsuitable for series with values close to zero or for skewed distributions.
*(2 marks for formula + 1 mark for limitation; alternatively, 1 + 2 splits are also accepted.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]*

**(a)** *Explain, in detail, the **seven steps in the forecasting process**. State clearly the output artifact produced at each step. **[7 Marks]***

**(b)** *For a random-walk model $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ where $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_e^{\,2})$, derive the $(1-\alpha)$ prediction interval for an $h$-step-ahead forecast. **[7 Marks]***

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The seven steps, each worth 1 mark:

1. **Problem Definition:** Output — a written objective statement specifying the variable, horizon, and end-user.
2. **Data Collection:** Output — the raw time-indexed dataset at the chosen frequency (annual/quarterly/monthly/daily), with at least **$n \geq 50$** observations.
3. **Data Preparation:** Output — a cleaned, stationarity-adjusted series with missing values imputed, outliers flagged, and Box-Cox / differencing transformations applied as needed.
4. **Exploratory Time Series Analysis:** Output — time plot, ACF/PACF plots, decomposition into $T_t$, $S_t$, $C_t$, $I_t$.
5. **Model Identification:** Output — a shortlist of candidate model classes (Naïve, ETS, ARIMA, SARIMA).
6. **Model Fitting & Diagnostics:** Output — estimated parameters, residual plots, Ljung-Box p-values.
7. **Forecast Generation, Evaluation & Monitoring:** Output — point forecast $\hat{Y}_{T+h}$, $(1-\alpha)$ prediction interval, MAE / RMSE / MAPE values, and a monitoring schedule.

*(Allocation: 1 mark per step — full 7 marks only if all seven are correctly listed with valid outputs.)*

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**

$$
Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t,\qquad \varepsilon_t \overset{i.i.d.}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_e^{\,2})
$$

**Step 1 — Forecast for $h$-step ahead:** The optimal point forecast is the most recent observation:

$$
\hat{Y}_{T+h} = Y_T
$$

**Step 2 — Forecast error:**

$$
e_{T+h} = Y_{T+h} - \hat{Y}_{T+h} = Y_{T+h} - Y_T = \sum_{j=1}^{h}\varepsilon_{T+j}
$$

**Step 3 — Mean of error:**

$$
\mathbb{E}[e_{T+h}] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{j=1}^{h}\varepsilon_{T+j}\right] = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Variance of error (using independence of $\varepsilon$'s):**

$$
\text{Var}(e_{T+h}) = \sum_{j=1}^{h}\text{Var}(\varepsilon_{T+j}) = h\,\sigma_e^{\,2}
$$

**Step 5 — Standard deviation:**

$$
\text{SD}(e_{T+h}) = \sigma_e\sqrt{h}
$$

**Step 6 — Prediction Interval:** Because $\varepsilon_t$ is Gaussian, $e_{T+h}$ is Gaussian:

$$
e_{T+h} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\;h\,\sigma_e^{\,2}\right)
$$

The two-sided $(1-\alpha)$ prediction interval is therefore:

$$
\hat{Y}_{T+h} \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma_e\sqrt{h}
$$

For example, with $\alpha = 0.05$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, giving the **95% prediction interval**:

$$
\hat{Y}_{T+h} \;\pm\; 1.96\,\sigma_e\sqrt{h}
$$

*(Allocation: 1 mark forecast definition; 2 marks error construction; 1 mark expectation; 1 mark variance; 1 mark SD; 1 mark final interval expression.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]*

**(a)** *Define the following forecast accuracy metrics: **MAE**, **RMSE**, **MAPE**, and **MASE**. For each metric, write its formula and state **one practical limitation**. **[7 Marks]***

**(b)** *Consider the following six observations of a monthly demand series (in units): $Y_1=120,\;Y_2=135,\;Y_3=128,\;Y_4=142,\;Y_5=138,\;Y_6=150$. Using the **naïve forecast method**, compute the values of MAE, RMSE, and MAPE for the five forecast errors. **[7 Marks]***

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**(i) Mean Absolute Error (MAE):**

$$
\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\vert Y_t - \hat{Y}_t\vert
$$

Limitation: MAE treats all errors with equal linear weight; it does not penalize large errors more heavily, which can be undesirable in safety-critical engineering contexts (e.g., flood-level forecasting).

**(ii) Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):**

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \hat{Y}_t)^2}
$$

Limitation: RMSE is sensitive to outliers because the squaring operation amplifies large errors. It is also scale-dependent, so it cannot be used to compare accuracy across series with different units.

**(iii) Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE):**

$$
\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\left\vert\dfrac{Y_t - \hat{Y}_t}{Y_t}\right\vert
$$

Limitation: MAPE is undefined when $Y_t = 0$ and is asymmetric — it penalizes positive errors more than negative errors of equal magnitude.

**(iv) Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE):**

$$
\text{MASE} = \dfrac{\text{MAE}_{\text{model}}}{\text{MAE}_{\text{naive}}}
$$

Limitation: MASE depends on the choice of the naïve scaling benchmark; for series with strong trend, the naïve benchmark itself may be poor, distorting the ratio.

*(Allocation: 0.75 mark per formula + 0.75 mark per limitation + 0.5 mark final wrap-up; total 7 marks.)*

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Generate naïve forecasts:**

The naïve rule: $\hat{Y}_{t+1} = Y_t$.

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $\hat{Y}_t$ | $e_t = Y_t - \hat{Y}_t$ | $\vert e_t\vert$ | $e_t^{\,2}$ | $\vert e_t / Y_t\vert \times 100$ |
|-----|-------|-------------|--------------------------|------------------|--------------|-----------------------------------|
| 2 | 135 | 120 | 15 | 15 | 225 | 11.111 |
| 3 | 128 | 135 | -7 | 7 | 49 | 5.469 |
| 4 | 142 | 128 | 14 | 14 | 196 | 9.859 |
| 5 | 138 | 142 | -4 | 4 | 16 | 2.899 |
| 6 | 150 | 138 | 12 | 12 | 144 | 8.000 |
| **Sum** | — | — | — | **52** | **630** | **37.338** |

**Step 2 — MAE:**

$$
\text{MAE} = \dfrac{52}{5} = 10.4 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — RMSE:**

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{630}{5}} = \sqrt{126} \approx 11.225 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — MAPE:**

$$
\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{37.338}{5} = 7.4676 \approx 7.47\%
$$

*(Allocation: 2 marks table; 1.5 marks MAE; 1.5 marks RMSE; 2 marks MAPE.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on "Steps in Forecasting":**
> 1. **Listing only 4 or 5 steps** instead of the full 7 — the seventh (Monitoring) is often missed and costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Prediction with Forecasting** — a single point estimate is NOT a forecast; you must include uncertainty (prediction interval).
> 3. **Skipping the Problem Definition step** in 14-mark answers — examiners deduct 2 marks when this step is absent, regardless of how strong the rest is.
> 4. **Using the wrong MAPE base** — students occasionally divide by $\hat{Y}_t$ instead of $Y_t$; this is a formula error that costs full marks on that sub-part.
> 5. **Not justifying additive vs multiplicative decomposition** — KTU 2024 expects an explicit reason (constant vs growing seasonal amplitude), not a one-line choice.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Forecasting is a 7-step pipeline**: Problem Definition → Data Collection → Data Preparation → Exploratory TSA → Model Identification → Fit & Diagnostics → Forecast + Evaluation + Monitoring.
- The **prediction interval** for a random walk of horizon $h$ is $\hat{Y}_{T+h} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma_e\sqrt{h}$ — the $\sqrt{h}$ scaling is a board-favourite.
- **MAPE** is asymmetric and undefined at $Y_t = 0$; **MASE** is the recommended scale-free metric for cross-series comparison.
- **Minimum sample size** for ARIMA-type modelling: $\mathbf{n \geq 50}$, ideally $\mathbf{n \geq 100}$.
- **Additive decomposition** is used when seasonal amplitude is constant; **multiplicative** when it grows with the trend.
- Always apply **Ljung-Box test** to residuals before accepting a model.
- **Never report a forecast without a prediction interval** — KTU 2024 explicitly penalizes point-only answers.
- The classical **additive model** is $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$; the **multiplicative model** is $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$.
- For the **naïve method**, $\hat{Y}_{t+1} = Y_t$ — its RMSE serves as the denominator of the MASE metric.
- The **Box-Cox transformation** uses $\lambda$ to stabilize variance; $\lambda = 0$ corresponds to a log-transform.
- Remember the **forecasting hierarchy of benchmarks**: Mean → Naïve → Drift → Smoothing → ARIMA → ML — each layer must beat the previous to be acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Time Series Forecasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Time Series Forecasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **time series** is a sequence of observations recorded at successive, equally spaced time intervals. Formally, a univariate time series is denoted as:

$$ \{X_t : t \in T\} $$

where $T$ is the index set of time points (typically $T = \{1, 2, 3, \dots, n\}$) and $X_t$ is the observed value at time $t$. A time series is therefore a realization of a **stochastic process** — a family of random variables indexed by time.

**Time Series Forecasting** is the process of using a model fitted to historical observations to predict future values, i.e., estimating $X_{t+h}$ given $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_t$, where $h$ is the forecast horizon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The module introduces the nature of time series data, the difference between deterministic and stochastic processes, the goals of forecasting, the basic statistical and graphical descriptive techniques, and an overview of the principal model families (regression, exponential smoothing, ARIMA, and neural network based approaches).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a riverbank watching leaves float past you one by one, each carrying a small instrument that records the **temperature of the water at the exact moment it passed you**. You write down each reading in a notebook, in the order it arrived. That ordered notebook of numbers — indexed by *time* — is your time series.

Now, suppose you wish to know what the *next* leaf's thermometer will read. To do that intelligently you need to look at:
- The **recent average** of past readings (level),
- Whether readings have been **drifting upward or downward** (trend),
- Whether readings **rise every afternoon and fall every night** (seasonality),
- Any **sudden jumps** caused by storms (irregular/random shocks).

Forecasting is essentially the science of separating these four "voices" in the data and then projecting them forward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board Favourite):**
> - A **time series** is the *data* — the recorded history.
> - A **stochastic process** is the *underlying mechanism* that generated the data.
> - A **forecast** is a *statement about an unobserved future value*, typically accompanied by a **prediction interval** (uncertainty).

### 1.3 Why Forecasting Matters in Engineering

| Application Domain | Example Series | Typical Horizon |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical Load Demand | Hourly kWh consumption | Minutes – Days |
| Structural Health Monitoring | Vibration amplitude (mm) | Real-time |
| Telecom Traffic | Per-second packet count | Seconds – Hours |
| Manufacturing QC | Tool wear sensor reading | Per part batch |
| Financial Engineering | Stock closing price | Intraday / Daily |
| Environmental Engineering | River discharge (cumecs) | Hours – Weeks |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU examination answers, **always** state explicitly *which component* your model is trying to capture. Examiners reward precise terminology.

---

### 1.4 The Four Canonical Components of a Time Series

A time series $X_t$ is conventionally decomposed (either **additively** or **multiplicatively**) into four parts:

$$ X_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t \quad \text{(Additive Model)} $$

$$ X_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t \quad \text{(Multiplicative Model)} $$

where:
- $T_t$ — **Trend** component (long-term direction)
- $S_t$ — **Seasonal** component (fixed calendar periodicity)
- $C_t$ — **Cyclical** component (non-fixed period waves, e.g., business cycles)
- $I_t$ — **Irregular / Random** component (unpredictable noise)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Mistake:** Students often confuse *seasonal* with *cyclical*. **Seasonal** has a *known, fixed period* (e.g., 12 months, 7 days). **Cyclical** has an *unknown, variable period* (e.g., economic recessions that last 6–10 years). Examiners deduct marks for this error.

---

### 1.5 Goals of Time Series Analysis (KTU Framework)

The three primary goals — frequently asked as 3-mark questions — are:

1. **Description** — Graphical and numerical summarization to identify patterns.
2. **Explanation** — Modelling the data-generating process to understand causal mechanisms.
3. **Forecasting** — Predicting future values with associated uncertainty.
4. **Control / Optimization** — Adjusting a system variable to keep a monitored series within desired bounds (e.g., PID control tuning via ARMA models).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics That Distinguish Time Series Data

Unlike cross-sectional data, time series data have a natural **temporal ordering** which introduces:

- **Serial Dependence (Autocorrelation):** $X_t$ is correlated with $X_{t-1}, X_{t-2}, \dots$
- **Trend:** A long-term increase or decrease.
- **Seasonality:** Patterns that repeat at known calendar intervals.
- **Non-Stationarity:** Statistical properties (mean, variance) change over time.
- **Heteroscedasticity:** The variance of $X_t$ is not constant.

### 2.2 Stationarity — The Central Concept

A stochastic process is **(weakly) stationary** if:
1. The **mean** is constant: $\mathbb{E}[X_t] = \mu$ for all $t$
2. The **variance** is finite and constant: $\text{Var}(X_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ for all $t$
3. The **autocovariance** depends only on the lag $k$, not on $t$:
$$ \gamma(k) = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Most classical models (ARMA, ARIMA) **require stationarity**. If your series is non-stationary, you *difference* it (Module 2) to obtain stationarity. The Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test (Module 2) formally tests this.

### 2.3 Autocorrelation Function (ACF)

The **autocorrelation** at lag $k$ is the normalized autocovariance:

$$ \rho(k) = \frac{\gamma(k)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k})}{\text{Var}(X_t)} $$

The **sample autocorrelation function (SACF)** is the estimator:

$$ \hat{\rho}(k) = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n} (X_t - \bar{X})(X_{t-k} - \bar{X})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n} (X_t - \bar{X})^2} $$

where $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} X_t$.

Under the null hypothesis that the series is **white noise**, $\hat{\rho}(k) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1/n)$ for $k \geq 1$, giving the familiar $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ confidence bands.

### 2.4 White Noise

A **white noise** process $W_t$ satisfies:
- $\mathbb{E}[W_t] = 0$
- $\text{Var}(W_t) = \sigma_w^2$
- $\text{Cov}(W_t, W_s) = 0$ for $t \neq s$

In symbols:
$$ W_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_w^2) $$

> [!NOTE]
> White noise is the **building block** of all ARMA models. Every stationary series can be thought of as white noise *filtered* by a linear operator.

### 2.5 Random Walk

A **random walk** is defined as:
$$ X_t = X_{t-1} + W_t, \quad W_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_w^2) $$

Equivalently, $X_t = X_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t} W_i$. This is **non-stationary** (variance grows linearly with $t$): $\text{Var}(X_t) = t\sigma_w^2$. Differencing once gives white noise: $\nabla X_t = W_t$.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Purpose / Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Sample Mean | $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} X_t$ | Estimates level $\mu$ |
| Sample Variance | $s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(X_t-\bar{X})^2$ | Spread around the mean |
| Sample Autocovariance | $\hat{\gamma}(k) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t-k}-\bar{X})$ | Dependence structure |
| Sample ACF | $\hat{\rho}(k) = \hat{\gamma}(k)/\hat{\gamma}(0)$ | Correlation at lag $k$ |
| White Noise Variance | $\sigma_w^2$ | Innovation variance |
| Random Walk Variance | $\text{Var}(X_t) = t\sigma_w^2$ | Grows with time |
| Additive Decomposition | $X_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ | Constant-variance seasonality |
| Multiplicative Decomposition | $X_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$ | Proportional seasonality |
| 95% Confidence Band (ACF) | $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ | Significance test for sample ACF |
| Forecast Error | $e_t = X_t - \hat{X}_{t-1}$ | Residual series |
| Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2$ | In-sample accuracy |
| Mean Absolute Error | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| MAPE | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_t / X_t \vert \times 100$ | Scale-independent error |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Use `$\vert$` (vertical bar escape) instead of literal `|` inside markdown tables to prevent layout breakage. The examiner's PDF parser will respect the LaTeX command.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

- **Stationarity tests** (ADF, KPSS) decide whether differencing is needed in ARIMA pipelines.
- **ACF / PACF plots** are the *primary diagnostic tool* for choosing AR vs MA orders.
- **White noise residuals** are the *aspiration* of every well-fitted model (Ljung-Box test verifies this in Module 2).
- **Random walk models** form the theoretical basis of the *efficient market hypothesis* in financial engineering.

### 2.8 Naïve Forecasting Methods (Baseline Benchmarks)

These are the **four baseline models** that KTU frequently asks under "basic forecasting methods":

**(a) Mean Method** — forecast equals the historical average:
$$ \hat{X}_{t+h} = \bar{X} = \frac{1}{t}\sum_{i=1}^{t} X_i $$

**(b) Naïve Method** — forecast equals the last observation:
$$ \hat{X}_{t+h} = X_t $$

**(c) Seasonal Naïve Method** — forecast equals the value from the same season in the previous cycle:
$$ \hat{X}_{t+h} = X_{t+h-m(k+1)} \quad \text{(where $m$ = seasonal period)} $$

**(d) Drift Method** — extrapolates the average change between first and last observations:
$$ \hat{X}_{t+h} = X_t + h\left(\frac{X_t - X_1}{t-1}\right) $$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Properties of the Sample Autocorrelation Function

We start with the **population** autocovariance and **derive** the sample version KTU students must know.

**Step 1 — Definition of Population Autocovariance.**
For a stationary process, the autocovariance at lag $k$ is:
$$ \gamma(k) = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k}) = \mathbb{E}[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)] $$

**Step 2 — Replacing Expectations with Sample Averages.**
Since we only have $n$ observed data points, we cannot compute the expectation. By the **Law of Large Numbers**, we replace $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ with the corresponding sample mean:
$$ \hat{\gamma}(k) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(X_t - \bar{X})(X_{t+k} - \bar{X}) $$

**Step 3 — Normalizing to Obtain Autocorrelation.**
The lag-0 autocovariance equals the variance:
$$ \hat{\gamma}(0) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}(X_t - \bar{X})^2 \approx s^2 $$

**Step 4 — Final SACF Formula.**
Dividing the autocovariance by the variance gives a correlation coefficient bounded in $[-1, +1]$:
$$ \hat{\rho}(k) = \frac{\hat{\gamma}(k)}{\hat{\gamma}(0)} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(X_t - \bar{X})(X_{t+k} - \bar{X})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(X_t - \bar{X})^2} $$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic Distribution Under White Noise.**
By the central limit theorem, if $\{X_t\}$ is white noise, then for $k \geq 1$:
$$ \hat{\rho}(k) \;\overset{\text{approx}}{\sim}\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{1}{n}\right) $$

This produces the **95% confidence band** $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$. If a sample ACF spike exceeds this band, we declare that lag to be **statistically significant**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Variance of the Random Walk

Starting with the definition:
$$ X_t = X_{t-1} + W_t, \quad W_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma_w^2) $$

**Step 1 — Unroll the recursion.**
$$ X_t = X_{t-1} + W_t = X_{t-2} + W_{t-1} + W_t = \dots = X_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t} W_i $$

**Step 2 — Compute the variance.**
$$ \text{Var}(X_t) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t} W_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{t}\text{Var}(W_i) = t\,\sigma_w^2 $$

This uses the property $\text{Var}(W_i + W_j) = \text{Var}(W_i) + \text{Var}(W_j)$ when $\text{Cov}(W_i, W_j) = 0$.

**Step 3 — Conclude non-stationarity.**
Since the variance depends on $t$, the process is **non-stationary**. The standard deviation grows as $\sqrt{t}\sigma_w$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem.** Consider the series $X = \{3, 5, 4, 7, 6, 8\}$. Compute the sample mean, sample variance, and $\hat{\rho}(1)$.

**Step 1 — Sample Mean.**
$$ \bar{X} = \frac{3+5+4+7+6+8}{6} = \frac{33}{6} = 5.5 $$

**Step 2 — Deviations from the Mean.**

| $t$ | $X_t$ | $X_t - \bar{X}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | $-2.5$ |
| 2 | 5 | $-0.5$ |
| 3 | 4 | $-1.5$ |
| 4 | 7 | $+1.5$ |
| 5 | 6 | $+0.5$ |
| 6 | 8 | $+2.5$ |

**Step 3 — Sample Variance (using denominator $n$).**
$$ \hat{\gamma}(0) = \frac{1}{6}\!\left[(-2.5)^2+(-0.5)^2+(-1.5)^2+(1.5)^2+(0.5)^2+(2.5)^2\right] $$
$$ = \frac{6.25+0.25+2.25+2.25+0.25+6.25}{6} = \frac{17.5}{6} \approx 2.9167 $$

**Step 4 — Sample Autocovariance at Lag 1.**
$$ \hat{\gamma}(1) = \frac{1}{6}\sum_{t=1}^{5}(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t+1}-\bar{X}) $$
$$ = \frac{1}{6}\!\left[(-2.5)(-0.5)+(-0.5)(-1.5)+(-1.5)(1.5)+(1.5)(0.5)+(0.5)(2.5)\right] $$
$$ = \frac{1}{6}\!\left[1.25+0.75-2.25+0.75+1.25\right] = \frac{1.75}{6} \approx 0.2917 $$

**Step 5 — Sample ACF at Lag 1.**
$$ \hat{\rho}(1) = \frac{\hat{\gamma}(1)}{\hat{\gamma}(0)} = \frac{0.2917}{2.9167} \approx 0.10 $$

**Step 6 — Test Significance.**
For $n = 6$, the 95% band is $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{6} \approx \pm 0.80$. Since $|0.10| < 0.80$, the lag-1 autocorrelation is **not significant** at the 5% level — consistent with a near-white-noise realization.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners love asking: *"What is the approximate 95% confidence interval for the sample ACF of a series of length $n$?"* Memorize $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Identify Components Visually

| Pattern Visible in Time Plot | Likely Component Present | Recommended Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Smooth upward or downward curve | Trend $T_t$ | Moving average smoothing |
| Regular peaks every $m$ observations | Seasonality $S_t$ | ACF spikes at lags $m, 2m, 3m$ |
| Slow wave-like undulations, no fixed period | Cyclical $C_t$ | Spectral / wavelet analysis |
| Erratic, no pattern | Irregular $I_t$ only | White noise test (Ljung-Box) |
| Strongly trending, variance growing | Non-stationary | Differencing (Module 2) |
| Mean reverts around a constant | Stationary | ARMA models (Module 3) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Time Series Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Series X_t] --> B[Exploratory Analysis]
    B --> B1[Time Plot]
    B --> B2[Histogram and Q-Q Plot]
    B --> B3[ACF and PACF Plots]
    B1 --> C{Pattern Detected?}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C -- Trend --> D[Apply Differencing]
    C -- Seasonality --> E[Apply Seasonal Differencing]
    C -- Stationary --> F[Fit ARMA / ARIMA]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Diagnostics]
    G --> G1[Residual ACF]
    G --> G2[Ljung-Box Test]
    G --> G3[Normality of Residuals]
    G1 --> H{Residuals White Noise?}
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Generate Forecast and Intervals]
    I --> J[Evaluate with Test Set]
    J --> K[Deploy Model]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Four Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Obs[Observed X_t] --> Add{Decomposition}
    Add -->|Additive| T[T_t Trend]
    Add -->|Additive| S[S_t Seasonal]
    Add -->|Additive| C[C_t Cyclical]
    Add -->|Additive| I[I_t Irregular]
    Add -->|Multiplicative| T2[T_t]
    Add -->|Multiplicative| S2[S_t]
    Add -->|Multiplicative| C2[C_t]
    Add -->|Multiplicative| I2[I_t]
    T --> Sum[Sum of Components]
    S --> Sum
    C --> Sum
    I --> Sum
    T2 --> Prod[Product of Components]
    S2 --> Prod
    C2 --> Prod
    I2 --> Prod
    Sum --> Xhat[Reconstructed Series]
    Prod --> Xhat2[Reconstructed Series]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: The Forecasting Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Data Acquisition] --> S2[Cleaning and Imputation]
    S2 --> S3[Stationarity Test ADF KPSS]
    S3 -->|Non-Stationary| S4[Differencing Transformation]
    S3 -->|Stationary| S5[Model Identification]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Estimate Parameters]
    S6 --> S7[Diagnostic Checking]
    S7 -->|Adequate| S8[Forecast Generation]
    S7 -->|Inadequate| S6
    S8 --> S9[Prediction Intervals]
    S8 --> S10[Performance Metrics MAE RMSE MAPE]
    S9 --> S11[Decision Support Output]
    S10 --> S11
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing Between Naïve Forecasts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Data has trend?} -->|No| Q2{Data has seasonality?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{Seasonality present?}
    Q3 -->|No| Drift[Drift Method]
    Q3 -->|Yes| SeasonalDrift[Seasonal Drift]
    Q2 -->|No| Mean[Mean Method]
    Q2 -->|Yes| SNaive[Seasonal Naive]
    Q1 -->|Maybe| Naive[Naive Method X_t]
```

### 4.5 Conceptual Map: Where This Module Fits in the Full Course

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Module 1 Introduction] --> M2[Module 2 Stationarity and Smoothing]
    M2 --> M3[Module 3 ARIMA Models]
    M3 --> M4[Module 4 Seasonal and Multivariate]
    M4 --> M5[Module 5 Advanced and Neural Approaches]
    M1 -.Uses.-> Stats[Basic Statistics]
    M1 -.Uses.-> Prob[Probability Theory]
    M1 -.Foundation for.-> M2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define a time series. Differentiate between a stationary and a non-stationary time series with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A time series is a chronologically ordered sequence of observations $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ recorded at successive equally spaced time instants. It is a realization of a stochastic process.
- **Stationary (1 mark):** A series whose mean, variance, and autocovariance do not depend on time, i.e., $\mathbb{E}[X_t] = \mu$ and $\text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k}) = \gamma(k)$ for all $t$. *Example:* White noise $W_t \sim N(0,1)$.
- **Non-stationary (1 mark):** A series whose statistical properties change with time. *Example:* Random walk $X_t = X_{t-1} + W_t$ — its variance grows as $t\sigma_w^2$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the four components of a classical time series decomposition. How is the additive model different from the multiplicative model?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Trend (½ mark):** Long-term increase or decrease.
- **Seasonal (½ mark):** Fixed-period calendar effect.
- **Cyclical (½ mark):** Non-fixed period wave-like variations.
- **Irregular (½ mark):** Random unpredictable noise.
- **Additive vs Multiplicative (1 mark):** Additive: $X_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$, used when seasonal amplitude is **constant** over time. Multiplicative: $X_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$, used when seasonal amplitude **grows proportionally** with the trend (typical of economic and financial data).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Time Series Decomposition and ACF Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **classical decomposition** of a time series into its components. Compare the additive and multiplicative models, giving a criterion to choose between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the data: $X = \{12, 15, 14, 18, 16, 20, 19, 22, 21\}$, compute the sample mean, the sample variance, and the sample autocorrelation at lags 1 and 2. Test whether $\hat{\rho}(1)$ is statistically significant at the 5% level. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classical Decomposition (7 marks):**

A time series $X_t$ is the superposition of four components: Trend $T_t$, Seasonal $S_t$, Cyclical $C_t$, and Irregular $I_t$.

*Additive Model:*
$$ X_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t $$
Assumes the seasonal fluctuation is **independent** of the trend level. Recommended when the seasonal swing is roughly constant (e.g., monthly temperature data in degrees Celsius).

*Multiplicative Model:*
$$ X_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t $$
Assumes the seasonal swing **grows in proportion** to the trend. Recommended for sales, revenue, and economic indices.

*Selection Criterion:* Apply a logarithmic transformation $\log X_t = \log T_t + \log S_t + \log C_t + \log I_t$, converting multiplicative to additive form. If the logged series shows constant-variance seasonality, the multiplicative model is appropriate.

The trend is estimated by a centered moving average of order $m$ (the seasonal period). The detrended series $X_t - T_t$ (additive) or $X_t / T_t$ (multiplicative) is then averaged over each season to estimate $S_t$. The remainder is the irregular component.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sample Mean.**
$$ \bar{X} = \frac{12+15+14+18+16+20+19+22+21}{9} = \frac{157}{9} \approx 17.444 $$

**Step 2 — Deviations.**

| $t$ | $X_t$ | $X_t - \bar{X}$ | $(X_t - \bar{X})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12 | $-5.444$ | 29.638 |
| 2 | 15 | $-2.444$ | 5.974 |
| 3 | 14 | $-3.444$ | 11.862 |
| 4 | 18 | $+0.556$ | 0.309 |
| 5 | 16 | $-1.444$ | 2.085 |
| 6 | 20 | $+2.556$ | 6.533 |
| 7 | 19 | $+1.556$ | 2.420 |
| 8 | 22 | $+4.556$ | 20.753 |
| 9 | 21 | $+3.556$ | 12.642 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **92.222** |

**Step 3 — Sample Variance.**
$$ \hat{\gamma}(0) = \frac{92.222}{9} \approx 10.247 $$

**Step 4 — Sample ACF at Lag 1.**
$$ \hat{\gamma}(1) = \frac{1}{9}\sum_{t=1}^{8}(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t+1}-\bar{X}) $$

Computing the eight products:

| $t$ | $(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t+1}-\bar{X})$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | $(-5.444)(-2.444) = 13.306$ |
| 2 | $(-2.444)(-3.444) = 8.418$ |
| 3 | $(-3.444)(+0.556) = -1.915$ |
| 4 | $(+0.556)(-1.444) = -0.803$ |
| 5 | $(-1.444)(+2.556) = -3.691$ |
| 6 | $(+2.556)(+1.556) = 3.977$ |
| 7 | $(+1.556)(+4.556) = 7.088$ |
| 8 | $(+4.556)(+3.556) = 16.197$ |
| **Sum** | **42.577** |

$$ \hat{\gamma}(1) = \frac{42.577}{9} \approx 4.731, \quad \hat{\rho}(1) = \frac{4.731}{10.247} \approx 0.462 $$

**Step 5 — Sample ACF at Lag 2.**
$$ \hat{\gamma}(2) = \frac{1}{9}\sum_{t=1}^{7}(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t+2}-\bar{X}) $$

| $t$ | $(X_t-\bar{X})(X_{t+2}-\bar{X})$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | $(-5.444)(-3.444) = 18.749$ |
| 2 | $(-2.444)(+0.556) = -1.359$ |
| 3 | $(-3.444)(-1.444) = 4.973$ |
| 4 | $(+0.556)(+2.556) = 1.421$ |
| 5 | $(-1.444)(+1.556) = -2.247$ |
| 6 | $(+2.556)(+4.556) = 11.645$ |
| 7 | $(+1.556)(+3.556) = 5.532$ |
| **Sum** | **38.714** |

$$ \hat{\gamma}(2) = \frac{38.714}{9} \approx 4.302, \quad \hat{\rho}(2) = \frac{4.302}{10.247} \approx 0.420 $$

**Step 6 — Significance Test at 5%.**
$$ \text{Bounds} = \pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{9}} = \pm 0.653 $$

Since $|\hat{\rho}(1)| = 0.462 < 0.653$, the lag-1 autocorrelation is **not significant at the 5% level**. The series behaves close to white noise, although the small sample size ($n=9$) limits the test's power.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Naïve Forecasting Methods

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain any **four naïve forecasting methods** used as baselines in time series analysis. For each method, state one advantage and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The annual sales (in ₹ lakhs) of a product for the last 5 years are: $\{40, 44, 48, 52, 60\}$. Using the **naïve**, **mean**, and **drift** methods, produce **2-step-ahead forecasts** ($h = 1, 2$) and compute the in-sample MAPE for the mean method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Naïve Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Mean Method** (1.5 marks):
$$ \hat{X}_{t+h} = \bar{X} $$
*Advantage:* Very simple, requires no trend/seasonality assumption. *Limitation:* Ignores all recent information; poor for trending data.

2. **Naïve Method** (1.5 marks):
$$ \hat{X}_{t+h} = X_t $$
*Advantage:* Surprisingly competitive for many short, noisy series; often hard to beat for financial price data (random walk hypothesis). *Limitation:* Cannot capture trend or seasonality.

3. **Seasonal Naïve Method** (2 marks):
$$ \hat{X}_{t+h} = X_{t+h-m(k+1)}, \quad k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor $$
*Advantage:* Excellent baseline for strongly seasonal series (e.g., quarterly retail sales). *Limitation:* Assumes the seasonal pattern repeats exactly; sensitive to calendar shifts.

4. **Drift Method** (2 marks):
$$ \hat{X}_{t+h} = X_t + h\!\left(\frac{X_t - X_1}{t-1}\right) $$
*Advantage:* Allows a simple linear extrapolation, outperforming naïve on trending data. *Limitation:* Can produce absurd forecasts for very long horizons because the drift is treated as constant.

**(b) Numerical Forecasts (7 marks):**

Given series: $X = \{40, 44, 48, 52, 60\}$, with $t = 5$, $X_1 = 40$, $X_5 = 60$.

**Step 1 — Naïve Method.**
$$ \hat{X}_{6} = X_5 = 60, \quad \hat{X}_{7} = X_5 = 60 $$

**Step 2 — Mean Method.**
$$ \bar{X} = \frac{40+44+48+52+60}{5} = \frac{244}{5} = 48.8 $$
$$ \hat{X}_{6} = \hat{X}_{7} = 48.8 $$

**Step 3 — Drift Method.**
$$ \text{Drift} = \frac{X_5 - X_1}{5-1} = \frac{60 - 40}{4} = 5 $$
$$ \hat{X}_{6} = 60 + (1)(5) = 65 $$
$$ \hat{X}_{7} = 60 + (2)(5) = 70 $$

**Step 4 — In-sample MAPE for Mean Method.**

In-sample forecasts: $\hat{X}_1 = \hat{X}_2 = \hat{X}_3 = \hat{X}_4 = \hat{X}_5 = 48.8$.

| $t$ | $X_t$ | $\hat{X}_t$ (mean) | $e_t = X_t - \hat{X}_t$ | $\vert e_t / X_t \vert \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 40 | 48.8 | $-8.8$ | 22.00 |
| 2 | 44 | 48.8 | $-4.8$ | 10.91 |
| 3 | 48 | 48.8 | $-0.8$ | 1.67 |
| 4 | 52 | 48.8 | $+3.2$ | 6.15 |
| 5 | 60 | 48.8 | $+11.2$ | 18.67 |
| | | | **Mean** | **11.88%** |

$$ \text{MAPE} = \frac{22.00+10.91+1.67+6.15+18.67}{5} = \frac{59.40}{5} \approx 11.88\% $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "trend" with "cycle"** — Trend is *monotonic directional*; cycle is *undulating* with variable period.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalization by $\hat{\gamma}(0)$** when computing $\hat{\rho}(k)$ — many students compute a raw cross-product and report it as the ACF, losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Using $n$ vs $n-k$ in the autocovariance denominator** — KTU accepts both in textbook style, but the *international convention* (used in Box–Jenkins) uses $n$. State your choice explicitly to gain full marks.
> 4. **Reporting $\hat{\rho}(1) = 0.46$ without comparing to $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$** — the test of significance is mandatory for full credit in the "test" portion of part (b).
> 5. **In MAPE calculations, forgetting to multiply by 100** — MAPE is conventionally a percentage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Time series** = ordered sequence of observations at equally spaced time intervals, indexed by $t$.
- A time series is a **realization of a stochastic process** $\{X_t\}$.
- The **three goals** of time series analysis: **Description, Explanation, Forecasting** (and often Control).
- **Four components** of classical decomposition: **Trend $T_t$, Seasonal $S_t$, Cyclical $C_t$, Irregular $I_t$**.
- **Additive** model $\Rightarrow$ constant-amplitude seasonality; **Multiplicative** model $\Rightarrow$ amplitude grows with trend.
- **Stationarity** requires constant mean, finite constant variance, and autocovariance depending only on the lag.
- **White noise** $W_t$ has zero mean, constant variance $\sigma_w^2$, and zero autocorrelation for all $k \geq 1$.
- **Random walk** $X_t = X_{t-1} + W_t$ is the canonical **non-stationary** series; its variance grows as $t\sigma_w^2$.
- **Sample ACF** $\hat{\rho}(k) = \hat{\gamma}(k)/\hat{\gamma}(0)$ is the primary diagnostic; under white noise $\hat{\rho}(k) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1/n)$.
- **95% confidence band** for sample ACF: $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$.
- **Four baseline forecasters** (memorize the formulas):
  - Mean: $\hat{X}_{t+h} = \bar{X}$
  - Naïve: $\hat{X}_{t+h} = X_t$
  - Seasonal Naïve: $\hat{X}_{t+h} = X_{t+h-m(k+1)}$
  - Drift: $\hat{X}_{t+h} = X_t + h\!\left(\frac{X_t - X_1}{t-1}\right)$
- **Error metrics**: MSE (penalizes large errors), MAE (robust to outliers), MAPE (scale-independent, in %).
- **Always** state which component your model addresses; examiners reward precise terminology.
- **Log transform** converts multiplicative seasonality to additive — a key trick KTU loves to test.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Series Characteristics and Types of Data: Time Series Data, Cross-Section Data, Longitudinal Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Series Characteristics and Types of Data: Time Series Data, Cross-Section Data, Longitudinal Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Data in the Context of Time Series Modelling?

In **PEADT755 - Time Series Modelling**, before we build any forecasting model, we must first **classify the nature of the dataset** we are dealing with. The statistical tools, assumptions, and methodologies applied differ fundamentally based on whether observations are taken across **time**, across **entities at one instant**, or a **combination of both**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Understanding the *type of data* is the foundational step. The classical Box-Jenkins methodology, ARIMA models, Exponential Smoothing, and Spectral Analysis are all designed primarily for **Time Series Data**. Misclassifying the data type leads to incorrect model selection and poor forecasts.

---

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

#### (A) Time Series Data
A **Time Series** is a sequence of observations recorded at **successive (usually equally-spaced) time intervals**, where the ordering of observations carries information.

$$
\{Y_t : t \in T\}, \quad t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n
$$

where $Y_t$ is the value of the variable at time $t$, and $T$ is the index set representing time.

#### (B) Cross-Section Data
**Cross-Sectional Data** consists of observations on multiple entities (subjects, firms, households, countries) collected at a **single point in time** $\tau$.

$$
\{Y_{i,\tau} : i = 1, 2, \ldots, N\}, \quad \tau \text{ is fixed}
$$

where $i$ indexes the entity and $\tau$ is fixed.

#### (C) Longitudinal Data (Panel Data)
**Longitudinal Data** (also called **Panel Data**) tracks the **same set of entities** over **multiple time periods**. It is essentially a *hybrid* of time series and cross-sectional data.

$$
\{Y_{i,t} : i = 1, 2, \ldots, N;\ t = 1, 2, \ldots, T\}
$$

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Classroom Attendance Register"**
>
> - **Time Series Data** = Recording attendance of *one specific student* every day for 30 days. (One entity → Many time points)
> - **Cross-Section Data** = On *Monday*, recording attendance of *all 60 students* in the class at once. (Many entities → One time point)
> - **Longitudinal Data** = Recording attendance of *all 60 students* every day for 30 days. (Many entities → Many time points)

This simple analogy maps directly to:
| Data Type | Entities | Time Points |
|---|---|---|
| Time Series | Single | Many |
| Cross-Section | Many | Single |
| Longitudinal (Panel) | Many | Many |

---

### 1.4 Time Series — The Standard Metrics (KTU Board Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction:** In Time Series, the **index $t$ is meaningful** — the *order* of observations matters. Rearranging the data destroys its informational content.

Standard components of a univariate time series:
- **Mean Level** $\mu_t = E[Y_t]$: Central tendency at time $t$.
- **Variance** $\sigma_t^2 = Var(Y_t)$: Dispersion at time $t$.
- **Stationarity**: A series whose statistical properties do not change over time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Each Data Type

#### (A) Time Series Data — Operational Logic

A time series can be decomposed into four classical components (additive model):

$$
Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t
$$

where:
- $T_t$ = **Trend** component (long-term direction)
- $S_t$ = **Seasonal** component (regular periodic fluctuations)
- $C_t$ = **Cyclical** component (irregular long-wave oscillations)
- $\epsilon_t$ = **Irregular/Random** component (white noise)

**Why this matters in KTU exams:** Questions frequently ask to "identify the components" or "explain why time series is different from regression." The core reason is the **temporal dependence** between observations.

**Key Properties of Time Series Data:**
1. **Sequential dependence**: $Y_t$ is correlated with $Y_{t-1}, Y_{t-2}, \ldots$ (autocorrelation).
2. **Ordered observations**: Permuting the series invalidates it.
3. **One observation per time period** (typically).
4. **Often non-stationary**: Mean/variance may evolve with time.

---

#### (B) Cross-Section Data — Operational Logic

Cross-sectional data captures a *snapshot* in time. Statistical tools used:
- Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression
- ANOVA
- Chi-square tests
- Logistic regression

**Key Properties:**
1. **Independence assumption** (often): One entity's response does not influence another's.
2. **No temporal ordering**: $i=1$ and $i=2$ can be swapped.
3. **Single time point**: Static picture.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** Cross-sectional data is **NOT** what we analyse in this course (PEADT755). However, the KTU examiner often asks a comparison question to test whether students understand why time series methodology is needed.

---

#### (C) Longitudinal (Panel) Data — Operational Logic

Panel data combines both dimensions:

$$
Y_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta X_{i,t} + \epsilon_{i,t}
$$

where $\alpha_i$ captures unobserved **entity-specific heterogeneity**.

**Key Properties:**
1. Controls for individual heterogeneity.
2. Provides more variability, less collinearity.
3. Can study **dynamics** (changes over time).
4. Requires panel-data methods (Fixed Effects, Random Effects) — *not* classical time series methods.

**Balanced vs. Unbalanced Panel:**
- **Balanced**: All $N$ entities observed for all $T$ time periods.
- **Unbalanced**: Missing observations for some entity-time combinations.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Time Series Observation | $Y_t$, $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ | Value at time $t$ |
| Mean Function | $\mu_t = E[Y_t]$ | Expected value at time $t$ |
| Variance Function | $\sigma_t^2 = E[(Y_t - \mu_t)^2]$ | Variance at time $t$ |
| Autocovariance | $\gamma(t,s) = E[(Y_t - \mu_t)(Y_s - \mu_s)]$ | Cov between $Y_t$ and $Y_s$ |
| Autocorrelation (ACF) | $\rho(t,s) = \dfrac{\gamma(t,s)}{\sigma_t \sigma_s}$ | Standardised autocov |
| Lag-$k$ Autocovariance | $\gamma_k = E[(Y_t - \mu)(Y_{t-k} - \mu)]$ | Dependence on $k$-lag past |
| Lag-$k$ Autocorrelation | $\rho_k = \dfrac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}$ | Always $\vert\rho_k\vert \leq 1$ |
| Additive Decomposition | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t$ | Classical time series components |
| Multiplicative Decomposition | $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot \epsilon_t$ | When amplitude grows with trend |
| Cross-Section Snapshot | $Y_{i,\tau}$, $\tau$ fixed | $i$-th entity at fixed $\tau$ |
| Panel Model | $Y_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta X_{i,t} + \epsilon_{i,t}$ | Two-way indexed data |
| Sample ACF | $\hat{\rho}_k = \dfrac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t-k} - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2}$ | Estimated ACF at lag $k$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** In the ACF row, $\vert\rho_k\vert \leq 1$ — the absolute value is written using `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax.

---

### 2.3 Engineering / Real-World Utility

| Field | Example of Data Type |
|---|---|
| **Electrical Load Forecasting** (KTU EE) | Hourly power demand → Time Series |
| **Stock Market Analysis** | Daily closing prices → Time Series |
| **Economics — GDP across countries in 2023** | Cross-Section |
| **Medical — Tracking 100 patients over 5 years** | Longitudinal/Panel |
| **Weather — Temperature of 50 cities on 1st Jan** | Cross-Section |
| **Climate — Temperature of Kochi from 1950–2024** | Time Series |
| **Agricultural — Crop yield of 100 farms over 10 yrs** | Panel/Longitudinal |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sample Autocorrelation Function (ACF)

The **Sample ACF** is the most fundamental diagnostic in time series modelling. The KTU board expects students to be able to **derive it from first principles**.

---

**Step 1: Define the population autocovariance**

For a stationary time series $\{Y_t\}$, the autocovariance at lag $k$ is defined as:

$$
\gamma_k = \text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+k}) = E[(Y_t - \mu)(Y_{t+k} - \mu)]
$$

This measures how values $k$ periods apart co-vary.

---

**Step 2: Define the population autocorrelation**

Since $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(Y_t)$, dividing by $\gamma_0$ normalises the autocovariance:

$$
\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}, \quad -1 \leq \rho_k \leq 1
$$

This is the *population* (theoretical) autocorrelation function (ACF).

---

**Step 3: Replace population quantities with sample analogues**

From data $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_n$, compute the sample mean:

$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} Y_t
$$

The sample autocovariance at lag $k$ is:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n-k} (Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t+k} - \bar{Y})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Step:** The summation starts at $t=1$ and ends at $n-k$ because we need pairs $(Y_t, Y_{t+k})$ and the latest valid $t$ is $n-k$.

---

**Step 4: Derive the sample ACF**

Dividing the sample autocovariance by the sample variance $\hat{\gamma}_0$:

$$
\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t+k} - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2}
$$

This is the **Sample Autocorrelation Function** at lag $k$, and it is the cornerstone of model identification in the Box-Jenkins (ARIMA) methodology taught later in the syllabus.

---

**Step 5: Special case — lag 0**

At $k = 0$:

$$
\hat{\rho}_0 = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_t - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2} = \frac{\sum(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2}{\sum(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2} = 1
$$

Confirming that every time series is perfectly correlated with itself at lag 0.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cross-Section vs. Longitudinal Identifier

**Step 1:** Identify the dimensions of the dataset.
- 1 dimension varying (time) → **Time Series**
- 1 dimension varying (entity) → **Cross-Section**
- 2 dimensions varying (entity AND time) → **Longitudinal/Panel**

**Step 2:** Express formally.

For a dataset $\mathcal{D}$ with $N$ entities and $T$ time periods:

- If $N = 1$ and $T > 1$: **Time Series** (single trajectory).
- If $N > 1$ and $T = 1$: **Cross-Section** (single snapshot).
- If $N > 1$ and $T > 1$: **Longitudinal** (rich, two-dimensional).

**Step 3:** Compute the dimensionality.

The total number of observations in each case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{TS} &= T \\
N_{CS} &= N \\
N_{Panel} &= N \times T
\end{aligned}
$$

This explains why panel datasets are much larger and richer in information.

---

### 3.3 The Classical Decomposition — Step-by-Step Decomposition Logic

For the **additive model** $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t$, the KTU textbook method proceeds as follows:

**Step 1: Estimate Trend $T_t$.**
Apply a centred moving average of order $m$ (e.g., $m=4$ for quarterly data, $m=12$ for monthly):

$$
\hat{T}_t = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=-(m-1)/2}^{(m-1)/2} Y_{t+j}
$$

For even $m$ (e.g., 12), use a double moving average.

**Step 2: Detrend the series.**

$$
D_t = Y_t - \hat{T}_t = S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t
$$

**Step 3: Estimate Seasonal Indices $S_t$.**

Group $D_t$ by season (e.g., month) and average each group. Normalise so seasonal indices sum to zero (additive case).

**Step 4: Estimate Cyclical + Irregular $C_t + \epsilon_t$.**

$$
\hat{C}_t + \hat{\epsilon}_t = D_t - \hat{S}_t
$$

**Step 5: Residuals give the random component $\epsilon_t$.**

This decomposition directly addresses the **characteristics** of a time series — namely, trend, seasonality, cyclicity, and randomness — which are the *defining features* of time series data as opposed to cross-sectional data.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Taxonomy of Data Types

The following diagram maps the relationship between the three fundamental data types encountered in econometrics and time series analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["OBSERVATIONAL DATA"]:::root --> B["UNIDIMENSIONAL"]:::branch
    A --> C["BIDIMENSIONAL"]:::branch

    B --> D["TIME SERIES DATA"]:::ts
    B --> E["CROSS SECTION DATA"]:::cs

    C --> F["LONGITUDINAL OR PANEL DATA"]:::panel

    D --> D1["One entity"]
    D --> D2["Multiple time points"]
    D --> D3["Ordered and dependent"]

    E --> E1["Multiple entities"]
    E --> E2["Single time point"]
    E --> E3["Independent observations"]

    F --> F1["Multiple entities"]
    F --> F2["Multiple time points"]
    F --> F3["Tracks same units over time"]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1e3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#4a6fa5,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ts fill:#b8860b,stroke:#704c00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cs fill:#5d8a5d,stroke:#2f5d2f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef panel fill:#8b3a62,stroke:#5a1f3f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.2 Structural Comparison Block Diagram

The following block diagram depicts how the **three data structures** differ in terms of indexing, methodology, and statistical assumptions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TS["TIME SERIES"]
        TS1["Index: t only"] --> TS2["Ordering matters"]
        TS2 --> TS3["Serial dependence"]
        TS3 --> TS4["ARIMA, ES, Holt Winters"]
    end

    subgraph CS["CROSS SECTION"]
        CS1["Index: i only"] --> CS2["Ordering irrelevant"]
        CS2 --> CS3["Independence assumed"]
        CS3 --> CS4["OLS, ANOVA, Logit"]
    end

    subgraph PN["PANEL OR LONGITUDINAL"]
        PN1["Index: i and t"] --> PN2["Heterogeneity alpha_i"]
        PN2 --> PN3["Dynamics over t"]
        PN3 --> PN4["Fixed Effects, Random Effects, FGLS"]
    end

    CS --> PN
    TS --> PN
    PN --> MERGE["Combines temporal and cross sectional variation"]
    TS --> NOTE["Focus of PEADT755"]
    NOTE -.-> TS

    classDef tsBox fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#b8860b,color:#3a2900,stroke-width:1px
    classDef csBox fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#5d8a5d,color:#1f3d1f,stroke-width:1px
    classDef pnBox fill:#f4d6e6,stroke:#8b3a62,color:#3a0f25,stroke-width:1px
    classDef noteBox fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#cc6600,color:#3a1f00,stroke-width:1px
    class TS,TS1,TS2,TS3,TS4 tsBox
    class CS,CS1,CS2,CS3,CS4 csBox
    class PN,PN1,PN2,PN3,PN4,MERGE pnBox
    class NOTE,noteBox noteBox
```

---

### 4.3 Time Series Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Y["Observed Series Y_t"]:::input --> DEC{"Decomposition Model"}:::decision
    DEC -->|"Additive"| A1["Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + epsilon_t"]
    DEC -->|"Multiplicative"| M1["Y_t = T_t times S_t times C_t times epsilon_t"]

    A1 --> A2["MA Smoothing for Trend"]:::step
    A2 --> A3["Detrended D_t = Y_t minus T_t"]:::step
    A3 --> A4["Seasonal Index via Group Means"]:::step
    A4 --> A5["Residual = C_t + epsilon_t"]:::step

    M1 --> M2["Log Transform then Additive"]:::step
    M2 --> A3

    A5 --> OUT["Diagnostic Plots: ACF, PACF"]:::final
    A4 --> OUT

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b3d91,color:#0b1e3a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#3a2f00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef step fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#062c30,stroke-width:1px
    classDef final fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#0a2d10,stroke-width:2px
```

These diagrams map directly to **Module 1 learning outcomes** where students are expected to "classify data types and identify the structural components of a time series."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between Time Series Data and Cross-Sectional Data with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Time Series Data | Cross-Section Data |
|---|---|---|
| Dimension | Observations over time $t$ | Observations across entities $i$ |
| Ordering | Order is meaningful | Order is irrelevant |
| Dependence | Serial correlation present | Typically independent |
| Example | Daily NSE Sensex closing price for 2023 | Closing price of 50 stocks on 31 Dec 2023 |
| Methods | ARIMA, Exponential Smoothing | OLS regression, t-test, ANOVA |

(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for comparison/example → 3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**What is Longitudinal Data? How is it different from Time Series Data?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Longitudinal Data (1 mark):** Data that follows the **same set of cross-sectional units (entities)** over **multiple time periods**.

**Difference from Time Series (2 marks):**
- Time Series tracks **one entity** over many time points (e.g., GDP of India from 1960–2024).
- Longitudinal Data tracks **many entities** over many time points (e.g., GDP of 30 countries from 1960–2024).
- Time Series uses Box-Jenkins / ARIMA; Longitudinal uses Fixed Effects / Random Effects panel models.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice** is mandatory: students answer EITHER Option A OR Option B in full.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Time Series Characteristics

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **four main components** of a classical time series with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the **additive and multiplicative decomposition models**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Define **autocovariance** and **autocorrelation** for a stationary time series. Derive the **sample ACF estimator** and explain its role in time series modelling. (7 Marks)

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A univariate time series can be decomposed into four systematic components to analyse its behaviour:

$$
Y_t = f(T_t, S_t, C_t, \epsilon_t)
$$

**The Four Components (4 marks):**

1. **Trend ($T_t$):** Long-term gradual movement (upward, downward, or flat) of the series. E.g., rising global temperature over decades.

2. **Seasonal ($S_t$):** Regular, periodic fluctuations within a fixed period (year, week, day). E.g., ice-cream sales peaking every summer.

3. **Cyclical ($C_t$):** Long-wave oscillations of variable length (typically > 1 year), not of fixed period — e.g., business cycles of boom and recession.

4. **Irregular / Random ($\epsilon_t$):** Residual unpredictable variation; assumed to be white noise with $E[\epsilon_t] = 0$ and $Var(\epsilon_t) = \sigma^2$.

**Additive vs. Multiplicative (2 marks):**

$$
Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t \quad \text{(Additive — amplitude constant)}
$$

$$
Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot \epsilon_t \quad \text{(Multiplicative — amplitude grows with trend)}
$$

Use multiplicative when seasonal/cyclical variations appear proportional to the level (e.g., retail sales). Use additive when variations are absolute (e.g., temperature in °C). A log transform converts multiplicative to additive: $\log Y_t = \log T_t + \log S_t + \log C_t + \log \epsilon_t$.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Autocovariance Definition (2 marks):**

For a stationary time series, the autocovariance at lag $k$ is:

$$
\gamma_k = E[(Y_t - \mu)(Y_{t+k} - \mu)]
$$

This is symmetric: $\gamma_{-k} = \gamma_k$, and $\gamma_0 = \sigma^2$ is the variance.

**Autocorrelation Definition (1 mark):**

$$
\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}, \quad -1 \leq \rho_k \leq 1
$$

**Derivation of Sample ACF (3 marks):**

Step 1: Sample mean $\bar{Y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum Y_t$.

Step 2: Sample autocovariance at lag $k$:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t+k} - \bar{Y})
$$

Step 3: Sample ACF:

$$
\hat{\rho}_k = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t+k} - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2}
$$

**Role in Modelling (1 mark):** The sample ACF plot (correlogram) is the **primary tool for identifying ARIMA model orders** (Box-Jenkins methodology) and detecting non-stationarity (slow decay suggests non-stationarity).

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice on Data Types

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Time Series Data**, **Cross-Sectional Data**, and **Longitudinal (Panel) Data**. Give **two real-world engineering examples** of each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A researcher collects monthly electricity consumption (in kWh) of **50 households in Kerala** from **January 2018 to December 2023**. Classify this data. Justify your answer and explain which statistical methods would be appropriate. (7 Marks)

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Time Series | Cross-Section | Longitudinal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dimensions | Time only ($t$) | Entity only ($i$) | Both ($i$, $t$) |
| Sample size | $T$ | $N$ | $N \times T$ |
| Ordering | Critical | Irrelevant | Both axes relevant |
| Dependence | Serial | Often independent | Both serial & cross-sectional |
| Methods | ARIMA, ES | OLS, ANOVA | Fixed/Random Effects |

**Engineering Examples (4 marks — 2 per category):**

- **Time Series:** (1) Hourly voltage readings at a substation for one year; (2) Daily water level of a reservoir over 10 years.
- **Cross-Section:** (1) Power consumption of 100 households on 1st July 2023; (2) Tensile strength of 50 steel samples tested in one lab session.
- **Longitudinal:** (1) Monthly electricity bills of 50 households over 5 years; (2) Daily vibration data from 20 turbines over 2 years.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Classification (2 marks):** This is **Longitudinal (Panel) Data**.

**Justification (3 marks):**
- There are **two dimensions of variation**: 50 households (cross-sectional index $i$) and 72 months (time index $t$, i.e., 6 years × 12 months).
- Each household is observed repeatedly across time → panel structure.
- Total observations = $N \times T = 50 \times 72 = 3600$.

**Appropriate Statistical Methods (2 marks):**
- **Fixed Effects (FE) Model:** Controls for time-invariant household characteristics (e.g., house size, income bracket).
- **Random Effects (RE) Model:** Used when household-specific effects are uncorrelated with regressors.
- **Hausman test** to choose between FE and RE.
- If the focus is on **forecasting only**, one could fit a separate ARIMA model per household, but pooling with panel methods is more efficient.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating Time Series with Panel Data:** When data has multiple entities, students often incorrectly call it a "time series." Always check both dimensions.
> 2. **Ignoring the ordering aspect:** Cross-sectional data has NO meaningful order — students wrongly apply ARIMA to it.
> 3. **Forgetting the word "longitudinal"** in definitions; saying only "panel data" loses the 1 mark for keyword.
> 4. **Mixing additive/multiplicative** without specifying when to use which — KTU requires you to **state the assumption** (amplitude constant vs. proportional).
> 5. **Skipping the summation limits** in the sample ACF formula — examiners specifically check $t = 1$ to $n-k$.
> 6. **Confusing ACF with PACF** — Partial ACF removes intermediate effects; full ACF includes them.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1):**
>
> ☐ **Three data types** — Time Series (1×T), Cross-Section (N×1), Longitudinal (N×T).
>
> ☐ **Time series = ordered** — order is non-negotiable; cross-section = orderless.
>
> ☐ **Panel/Longitudinal = hybrid** — same units tracked across time.
>
> ☐ **Four components:** Trend ($T_t$), Seasonal ($S_t$), Cyclical ($C_t$), Random ($\epsilon_t$).
>
> ☐ **Additive model:** $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t$ — used when amplitude is constant.
>
> ☐ **Multiplicative model:** $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot \epsilon_t$ — used when amplitude scales with trend.
>
> ☐ **Log transform** converts multiplicative → additive.
>
> ☐ **Autocovariance:** $\gamma_k = E[(Y_t - \mu)(Y_{t+k} - \mu)]$.
>
> ☐ **Autocorrelation:** $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$, with $\vert\rho_k\vert \leq 1$.
>
> ☐ **Sample ACF estimator:**
>
> ☐ $\hat{\rho}_k = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(Y_t - \bar{Y})(Y_{t+k} - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(Y_t - \bar{Y})^2}$.
>
> ☐ **Sample size formulas:** $N_{TS} = T$, $N_{CS} = N$, $N_{Panel} = N \times T$.
>
> ☐ **ACF at lag 0** always equals 1 (a series is perfectly correlated with itself).
>
> ☐ **Stationarity is implicit** when defining ACF/$\gamma_k$ as constant in $t$.
>
> ☐ **KTU focus:** This course deals almost exclusively with **Time Series Data**, not cross-section.
>
> ☐ **Engineering examples** to remember: power load (TS), stock prices (TS), rainfall over years (TS), soil samples on one day (CS), patient cohort followed over years (Panel).
>
> ☐ **Pitfall to avoid:** Never apply classical time series (ARIMA) directly to panel data without first addressing entity-level heterogeneity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Time Series Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Understanding Time Series Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Time Series** is an ordered sequence of observations recorded at successive, equally spaced points in time. Mathematically, it is denoted as a set of random variables indexed by time:

$$\{Y_t : t \in T\}$$

where the index set $T = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, n\}$ represents discrete time points and $Y_t$ is the value of the variable observed at time $t$. A single realisation of this stochastic process is called a **time series sample path** of length $n$, written as $\{y_1, y_2, y_3, \ldots, y_n\}$.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education framework), the formal definition that students must memorise for board valuation is:

> **Time Series:** A collection of observations made sequentially over time, where the natural ordering of observations is itself a critical source of information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO1 – K2 Understand):**
> The ordering in time is the **fundamental** distinguishing feature of a time series. Unlike cross-sectional data (e.g., heights of 100 students in a class), in a time series, the **temporal sequence carries structural meaning** — past values influence future values through dependence structures called **autocorrelation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The ECG Heartbeat Strip

Imagine a doctor attaching an **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** machine to a patient. The machine does not record the patient's heart rate at random moments; instead, it plots the electrical activity of the heart as a continuous curve over time. 

- The **horizontal axis** is *time* (in milliseconds or seconds).
- The **vertical axis** is *voltage* (the heart's electrical signal).
- The **peaks and valleys** are not random noise — they follow a recognisable repeating **pattern** corresponding to the cardiac cycle.

This ECG strip is the perfect real-world analogy for a time series: **a single realisation of an underlying generating process (the heart's rhythm) sampled at uniform time intervals**. Just as a cardiologist learns to detect arrhythmia (anomalies) by understanding the *normal* heartbeat pattern, a data scientist uses time series analysis to detect anomalies, trends, and seasonalities in data ranging from stock prices to monsoon rainfall.

### 1.3 Real-World Examples of Time Series Data

| Domain | Time Series Variable | Sampling Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Finance** | Daily closing price of a stock (e.g., RELIANCE on NSE) | Daily |
| **Meteorology** | Maximum temperature recorded at Kochi airport | Daily |
| **Economics** | India's monthly Consumer Price Index (CPI) | Monthly |
| **Healthcare** | Patient's systolic blood pressure | Hourly |
| **Engineering** | Vibration amplitude of a turbine bearing | Per millisecond |
| **Retail** | Weekly sales of a product at a BigBazaar outlet | Weekly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** Whenever asked to "give examples of time series", the examiner expects a *time-indexed variable* — never give a *spatial* or *cross-sectional* example (e.g., "population of 50 cities" is **NOT** a time series).

### 1.4 Time Series vs. Cross-Sectional Data

| Feature | Time Series Data | Cross-Sectional Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Index** | Time ($t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$) | Individual / Entity ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$) |
| **Ordering** | Inherent and meaningful | Arbitrary (can be reshuffled) |
| **Dependence** | Observations are **autocorrelated** | Observations are **independent** |
| **Sample size** | Usually small ($n < 1000$) | Can be very large ($n$ in millions) |
| **Goal** | Forecasting future values | Comparing entities at one point |
| **Example** | Sales of iPhone from 2015 to 2024 | Sales of iPhone in 50 countries in 2024 |

### 1.5 Key Characteristics of a Time Series

1. **Ordered Nature** — The sequence $\{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n\}$ cannot be rearranged without destroying information.
2. **Temporal Dependence (Autocorrelation)** — $y_t$ is statistically dependent on past values $y_{t-1}, y_{t-2}, \ldots$
3. **Non-Independence of Errors** — Classical i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) assumptions of regression **do not hold** here.
4. **Trend and Seasonality** — Long-term movements and calendar-based periodic patterns are usually embedded in the data.
5. **Non-Stationarity** — Most real-world time series have means and variances that change over time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Notation of a Time Series

A discrete time series is formally a **realisation of a stochastic process** $\{Y_t\}$. For analysis, we observe a finite sample:

$$y_1, y_2, y_3, \ldots, y_t, \ldots, y_n$$

where:
- $y_t$ = observed value at time $t$
- $n$ = total number of observations (sample size)
- $T$ = length of the observation period (e.g., 10 years, 240 months)

### 2.2 The Four Classical Components of a Time Series

According to the **Classical Decomposition Model**, any time series $Y_t$ can be decomposed into four constituent components:

| Component | Symbol | Description | Time Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trend** | $T_t$ | Long-term gradual movement (upward or downward) | Years / Decades |
| **Seasonal** | $S_t$ | Regular periodic fluctuation within a year | Within a calendar year |
| **Cyclical** | $C_t$ | Irregular wave-like fluctuation, longer than a year | 2–10 years |
| **Irregular / Random** | $I_t$ | Unpredictable noise / residual | Random |

### 2.3 Decomposition Models: Additive vs. Multiplicative

The two principal ways to combine these components are:

**Additive Model** — Used when the seasonal/cyclical variation is **constant** over time:

$$Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$$

**Multiplicative Model** — Used when the seasonal/cyclical variation **grows proportionally** with the trend (very common in finance and economics):

$$Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Think of the additive model as a *sum of independent layers* (e.g., the noise floor of a microphone adds a fixed level). The multiplicative model represents *interactions* (e.g., a small error in a car's gear ratio compounds with each revolution).

### 2.4 Stationarity — The Most Critical Property

A time series is **stationary** if its statistical properties do not change with time. There are two levels of stationarity:

**Strict Stationarity:** The entire joint distribution of $(Y_{t_1}, Y_{t_2}, \ldots, Y_{t_k})$ is identical to that of $(Y_{t_1+h}, Y_{t_2+h}, \ldots, Y_{t_k+h})$ for any time lag $h$ and any $k$.

**Weak (Second-Order / Wide-Sense) Stationarity:** Only the first two moments are time-invariant:

1. **Constant Mean:** $\mathbb{E}[Y_t] = \mu$ for all $t$
2. **Constant Variance:** $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ for all $t$
3. **Autocovariance depends only on lag:** $\text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+h}) = \gamma(h)$ — independent of $t$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Stationarity Matters (KTU 2024 — CO1, CO2):** Almost all classical time series models (ARMA, ARIMA) **assume stationarity**. Non-stationary data must be transformed (e.g., by differencing) into a stationary series before modelling. This is the central message of Box & Jenkins methodology.

### 2.5 Autocovariance and Autocorrelation Functions

The **autocovariance function (ACVF)** at lag $h$ measures the linear dependence between $Y_t$ and $Y_{t+h}$:

$$\gamma(h) = \text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+h}) = \mathbb{E}[(Y_t - \mu)(Y_{t+h} - \mu)]$$

The **autocorrelation function (ACF)** is the normalised version:

$$\rho(h) = \text{Corr}(Y_t, Y_{t+h}) = \frac{\gamma(h)}{\gamma(0)}$$

where $\gamma(0) = \text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** $\rho(0) = 1$ always, and $\rho(h) = \rho(-h)$ (the ACF is symmetric about zero). For a white noise process, $\rho(h) = 0$ for all $h \neq 0$.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Range / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time Series Notation | $Y_t : t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ | Discrete-time |
| 2 | Additive Model | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ | Constant variation |
| 3 | Multiplicative Model | $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot I_t$ | Proportional variation |
| 4 | Mean Function | $\mu_t = \mathbb{E}[Y_t]$ | First moment |
| 5 | Variance Function | $\sigma_t^2 = \text{Var}(Y_t) = \mathbb{E}[(Y_t - \mu_t)^2]$ | Second central moment |
| 6 | Autocovariance at lag $h$ | $\gamma(t, t+h) = \mathbb{E}[(Y_t - \mu_t)(Y_{t+h} - \mu_{t+h})]$ | Joint moment |
| 7 | Autocorrelation at lag $h$ | $\rho(h) = \dfrac{\gamma(h)}{\gamma(0)} = \dfrac{\gamma(h)}{\sigma^2}$ | $\vert \rho(h) \vert \le 1$ |
| 8 | White Noise | $Y_t = \varepsilon_t,\; \varepsilon_t \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} (0, \sigma^2)$ | $\rho(h) = 0, h \ne 0$ |
| 9 | Random Walk | $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | Non-stationary |
| 10 | Weak Stationarity | $\mu_t = \mu$, $\sigma_t^2 = \sigma^2$, $\gamma(t, t+h) = \gamma(h)$ | Three conditions |
| 11 | Strict Stationarity | $F(y_1, \ldots, y_k) = F(y_{1+h}, \ldots, y_{k+h})$ | Distribution invariant |
| 12 | Sample ACF | $\hat{\rho}(h) = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-h}(y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t+h} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(y_t - \bar{y})^2}$ | Estimator |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners will deduct marks if you write $\gamma(0) = \mu$ instead of $\gamma(0) = \sigma^2$. The autocovariance at lag zero is the **variance**, not the mean.

### 2.7 Engineering Utility of Understanding Time Series

| Field | Application | Underlying Model Component |
|---|---|---|
