# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Simple Exponential Smoothing. State its forecasting equation and the admissible range of the smoothing constant $\alpha$.**

**Model Answer:**

Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) is a forecasting method for a time series with no trend and no seasonality. It updates the level recursively as a weighted average of the current observation and the previous level:

$$
\hat{y}_{t+1} = \ell_t = \alpha\,y_t + (1-\alpha)\,\ell_{t-1}, \quad 0 \le \alpha \le 1
$$

The smoothing constant $\alpha$ controls the responsiveness: $\alpha \to 1$ yields a naive last-value forecast; $\alpha \to 0$ yields a stable mean forecast. The forecast for any future horizon $h$ is simply $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between additive and multiplicative seasonality in the context of Holt–Winters method. When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Additive Seasonality | Multiplicative Seasonality |
|---|---|---|
| **Formulation** | $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h b_t + s_{t+h-m}$ | $\hat{y}_{t+h} = (\ell_t + h b_t)\cdot s_{t+h-m}$ |
| **Seasonal swing** | Constant in absolute units | Proportional to the level |
| **Preferred when** | Seasonal fluctuation is roughly the **same magnitude** year after year (e.g., monthly temperature anomalies of $\pm 2^\circ$C) | Seasonal swing **grows with the level** (e.g., quarterly sales of consumer durables scaling with the trend) |
| **Data requirement** | Series values may cross zero safely | Series must be strictly positive |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** mix up the *forecast* equation with the *update* equation. The forecast is always $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ldots$ (a function of *past* states), while the update gives the *new* state at time $t$. Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for this swap.
> - **Always** specify the parameter constraint range $0 \le \alpha, \beta, \gamma \le 1$. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
> - **Always** state the initialization assumptions (e.g., $\ell_0 = y_1$, $b_0 = y_2 - y_1$, or season initialization by averaging). Examiners in KTU Kerala specifically check this.
> - For 14-mark numerical questions, show **all** intermediate arithmetic in a table; presenting only the final SSE invites 4-mark deductions.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Holt's Linear Trend in Depth*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the recursive state-update equations for **Holt's Linear Trend method**. Clearly state the role of $\alpha$ and $\beta$, and explain why the forecast for $h$ steps ahead is $\hat{y}_{t+h} = \ell_t + h b_t$.  
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the annual sales data: $y_1 = 200, y_2 = 220, y_3 = 245, y_4 = 260, y_5 = 285$. Take $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.2$, with initial values $\ell_0 = 200$, $b_0 = 18$. Compute the level and trend for $t=1,\ldots,5$ and find the one-step-ahead forecasts. Also compute the SSE.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** [7 marks — break-up: 2 marks for level, 2 marks for trend, 1 mark for forecast, 2 marks for intuition]:

The level is updated by blending the *level + trend* prediction with the new observation, while the trend is updated by blending the *change in level* with the previous trend:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_t &= \alpha\,y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
b_t &= \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1} \\
\hat{y}_{t+h} &= \ell_t + h b_t
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $\alpha$ governs the level's response to the new observation (level smoothing), and $\beta$ governs how quickly the trend adapts. The $h$-step forecast extrapolates the local linear path $\ell_t + h b_t$, valid for short horizons where the trend is approximately constant.

**(b) Numerical Computation** [7 marks — break-up: 1 mark initialization, 3 marks table, 2 marks SSE, 1 mark final forecast]:

Initialize $\ell_0 = 200$, $b_0 = 18$, $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.2$.

**Step 1 — $t=1$:**
- $\hat{y}_1 = \ell_0 + b_0 = 218$
- $\ell_1 = 0.3(200) + 0.7(200+18) = 60 + 152.6 = 212.6$
- $b_1 = 0.2(212.6 - 200) + 0.8(18) = 2.52 + 14.4 = 16.92$

**Step 2 — $t=2$:**
- $\hat{y}_2 = 212.6 + 16.92 = 229.52$
- $\ell_2 = 0.3(220) + 0.7(212.6+16.92) = 66 + 160.66 = 226.66$
- $b_2 = 0.2(226.66 - 212.6) + 0.8(16.92) = 2.812 + 13.536 = 16.348$

**Step 3 — $t=3$:**
- $\hat{y}_3 = 226.66 + 16.348 = 243.01$
- $\ell_3 = 0.3(245) + 0.7(226.66+16.348) = 73.5 + 170.106 = 243.606$
- $b_3 = 0.2(243.606 - 226.66) + 0.8(16.348) = 3.389 + 13.078 = 16.467$

**Step 4 — $t=4$:**
- $\hat{y}_4 = 243.606 + 16.467 = 260.07$
- $\ell_4 = 0.3(260) + 0.7(243.606+16.467) = 78 + 182.051 = 260.051$
- $b_4 = 0.2(260.051 - 243.606) + 0.8(16.467) = 3.289 + 13.174 = 16.463$

**Step 5 — $t=5$:**
- $\hat{y}_5 = 260.051 + 16.463 = 276.51$
- $\ell_5 = 0.3(285) + 0.7(260.051+16.463) = 85.5 + 193.56 = 279.06$
- $b_5 = 0.2(279.06 - 260.051) + 0.8(16.463) = 3.802 + 13.170 = 16.972$

**Forecasts:** $\hat{y}_2 = 229.52$, $\hat{y}_3 = 243.01$, $\hat{y}_4 = 260.07$, $\hat{y}_5 = 276.51$.

**SSE calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SSE} &= (200-218)^2 + (220-229.52)^2 + (245-243.01)^2 + (260-260.07)^2 + (285-276.51)^2 \\
&= 324.00 + 90.63 + 3.96 + 0.0049 + 72.08 \\
&\approx 490.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**One-step-ahead forecast for $t=6$:** $\hat{y}_6 = \ell_5 + b_5 = 279.06 + 16.97 = 296.03$.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Holt–Winters with Estimation*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Write the full set of recursive equations for the **Holt–Winters Additive method**. Define each smoothing constant and explain the rationale behind the deseasonalization in the level equation: $\ell_t = \alpha(y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$.  
> **(b) [7 Marks]** For a quarterly series with $m=4$ and the following 8 observations: $(120, 135, 150, 165, 140, 158, 175, 192)$, take $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.2$, $\gamma = 0.3$, and initialize $\ell_4 = 145$, $b_4 = 12$, with seasonal indices $s_0 = s_1 = s_2 = s_3 = 0$ (centred). Compute the in-sample one-step-ahead forecasts for $t=5,6,7,8$ and the SSE. Also obtain the 2-step-ahead forecast from $t=8$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Recursive equations** [7 marks — 2 marks for the three equations, 2 marks for parameter roles, 3 marks for the deseasonalization rationale]:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level:}\quad \ell_t &= \alpha(y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
\text{Trend:}\quad b_t &= \beta(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1} \\
\text{Season:}\quad s_t &= \gamma(y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1}) + (1-\gamma)s_{t-m} \\
\text{Forecast:}\quad \hat{y}_{t+h} &= \ell_t + h b_t + s_{t+h-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $\alpha$ smooths the level against the innovation, $\beta$ smooths the trend, $\gamma$ smooths the seasonal index.
- The **deseasonalization** step $(y_t - s_{t-m})$ removes the seasonal effect of the *same* season last year before estimating the level, so the level reflects the underlying signal — not contaminated by the recurring seasonal bump.

**(b) Numerical Computation** [7 marks — 1 mark initialization, 3 marks update, 2 marks SSE, 1 mark forecast]:

Initialize: $\ell_4 = 145$, $b_4 = 12$. Seasonal indices at $t=0,1,2,3$: $s_0 = s_1 = s_2 = s_3 = 0$.

**Update $t=5$:**
- $\hat{y}_5 = \ell_4 + b_4 + s_1 = 145 + 12 + 0 = 157$
- $\ell_5 = 0.4(140 - s_1) + 0.6(145 + 12) = 0.4(140) + 0.6(157) = 56 + 94.2 = 150.2$
- $b_5 = 0.2(150.2 - 145) + 0.8(12) = 1.04 + 9.6 = 10.64$
- $s_5 = 0.3(140 - 145 - 12) + 0.7(s_1) = 0.3(-17) + 0 = -5.1$

**Update $t=6$:**
- $\hat{y}_6 = \ell_5 + b_5 + s_2 = 150.2 + 10.64 + 0 = 160.84$
- $\ell_6 = 0.4(158 - 0) + 0.6(150.2 + 10.64) = 63.2 + 96.504 = 159.704$
- $b_6 = 0.2(159.704 - 150.2) + 0.8(10.64) = 1.9008 + 8.512 = 10.4128$
- $s_6 = 0.3(158 - 150.2 - 10.64) + 0.7(0) = 0.3(-2.84) = -0.852$

**Update $t=7$:**
- $\hat{y}_7 = \ell_6 + b_6 + s_3 = 159.704 + 10.4128 + 0 = 170.117$
- $\ell_7 = 0.4(175 - 0) + 0.6(159.704 + 10.4128) = 70 + 102.070 = 172.070$
- $b_7 = 0.2(172.07 - 159.704) + 0.8(10.4128) = 2.473 + 8.330 = 10.803$
- $s_7 = 0.3(175 - 159.704 - 10.4128) + 0.7(0) = 0.3(4.883) = 1.465$

**Update $t=8$:**
- $\hat{y}_8 = \ell_7 + b_7 + s_4 = 172.07 + 10.803 + s_4$. We need $s_4$ from initialization: $s_4 = s_0 = 0$.
- $\hat{y}_8 = 172.07 + 10.803 + 0 = 182.873$
- $\ell_8 = 0.4(192 - s_4) + 0.6(172.07 + 10.803) = 0.4(192) + 0.6(182.873) = 76.8 + 109.724 = 186.524$
- $b_8 = 0.2(186.524 - 172.07) + 0.8(10.803) = 2.891 + 8.642 = 11.533$
- $s_8 = 0.3(192 - 172.07 - 10.803) + 0.7(s_4) = 0.3(9.127) + 0 = 2.738$

**Forecasts:** $\hat{y}_5 = 157$, $\hat{y}_6 = 160.84$, $\hat{y}_7 = 170.12$, $\hat{y}_8 = 182.87$.

**SSE calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SSE} &= (140-157)^2 + (158-160.84)^2 + (175-170.12)^2 + (192-182.87)^2 \\
&= 289 + 8.07 + 23.81 + 83.30 \\
&\approx 404.18
\end{aligned}
$$

**2-step-ahead forecast from $t=8$ (i.e., forecast for $t=10$):**

$$
\hat{y}_{10} = \ell_8 + 2\,b_8 + s_{10-4} = \ell_8 + 2 b_8 + s_6 = 186.524 + 2(11.533) + (-0.852) = 208.74
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Print This Out Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **SES** uses only the level $\ell_t$; forecast = $\ell_t$ for all $h$.
- ✅ **Holt's** adds trend $b_t$; forecast = $\ell_t + h b_t$.
- ✅ **Holt–Winters Additive** uses $s_{t+h-m}$ added; **Multiplicative** uses it multiplied.
- ✅ All smoothing constants are bounded: $0 \le \alpha,\beta,\gamma \le 1$. The damping $\phi$ in Holt-damped is also in $[0,1]$.
- ✅ Estimation is by **minimizing SSE = $\sum (y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2$** using numerical optimizers (BFGS / Nelder–Mead / grid search).
- ✅ **Model selection** uses **AIC**, **AICc**, or **BIC**: $AIC = n\ln(\hat\sigma^2) + 2k$, $BIC = n\ln(\hat\sigma^2) + k\ln(n)$.
- ✅ **Initialization** matters: $\ell_0 = y_1$, $b_0 = y_2 - y_1$ (Holt), or use the average of the first season (Holt–Winters).
- ✅ Exponential smoothing methods are **special cases of innovations state-space models** — recognizing this links to **ETS** and **Kalman filter** content in higher semesters.
- ✅ Damped trend is the **default robust choice** for short-horizon forecasts of series with moderating growth (winner of the M3 forecasting competition).
- ✅ **Multiplicative seasonality requires strictly positive data** — otherwise the seasonal index can become undefined.
- ✅ Always **diagnose residuals** for white-noise behaviour (Ljung–Box test) after fitting.
- ✅ The **forecast error** $e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$ is the *innovation*; in state-space form, all state updates are scaled multiples of $e_t$.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Update, Forecast, Repeat."* — Dr. Hyndman's Exponential Smoothing Mantra, KTU Board Recommended.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forecasting with ETS Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forecasting with ETS Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ETS Models (Error-Trend-Seasonality)** are a family of exponential smoothing forecasting methods in which the choice of the model components (Error, Trend, and Seasonality) explicitly characterizes the time series behavior. They are formally expressed as an **Innovation State Space Model** (Hyndman & Athanasopoulos framework), where each component can be specified as **Additive (A)** or **Multiplicative (M)**, and Trend can additionally be **Damped (D)** or **None (N)**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, ETS models are the formal state-space generalization of the classical Holt-Winters exponential smoothing methods. The state vector of an ETS model typically contains:

- **Level component ($\ell_t$)**: The baseline (mean) value of the series at time $t$.
- **Trend component ($b_t$)**: The slope of the series at time $t$ (may be additive, multiplicative, or damped).
- **Seasonal component ($s_t$)**: The seasonal effect at time $t$ (for seasonal data, period $m$).

The **observation equation** describes the relationship between the observed series $y_t$ and the state components, while the **transition equations** describe how the state components evolve over time. The error terms $\varepsilon_t$ are assumed to be Gaussian white noise: $\varepsilon_t \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Car Dashboard"**

Imagine you are driving a car and trying to predict your **fuel consumption rate** for the next few miles based on past data. The car's behavior depends on three things:

- **Error (E) — "Random Hiccups"**: Sudden variations like a gust of wind, road bump, or momentary driving habit. Just like a car's fuel reading jitters slightly each second due to noise.
- **Trend (T) — "Long-Road Slope"**: Whether you are going uphill (positive trend), downhill (negative trend), or on a flat highway (no trend). A **damped** trend is like seeing the hill flatten out gradually — momentum is slowing.
- **Seasonality (S) — "Traffic Lights"**: A repeating cycle — every morning rush hour (5 km extra), every weekend light (less consumption). The same cycle repeats predictably.

**ETS** simply asks: "Of these three ingredients, which is **Added** (Additive) like stacking blocks, and which is **Multiplied** (Multiplicative) like applying a percentage?"

| Real Driving Example | ETS Interpretation |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Fuel use grows by a fixed 2 L/hr each day | **Additive Trend** |
| Fuel use grows by 5% of yesterday's value | **Multiplicative Trend** |
| Hill flattens out after some miles | **Damped Trend** |
| Rush hour adds a fixed 5 L on top | **Additive Seasonality** |
| Rush hour multiplies by 1.3× | **Multiplicative Seasonality** |

## 1.3 Standard ETS Notation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ETS Notation Convention (Hyndman, Koehler, Ord, Snyder):**
>
> $$\text{ETS}(\text{Error}, \text{Trend}, \text{Seasonality})$$
>
> - **Error** ∈ {A, M} → **A**dditive, **M**ultiplicative
> - **Trend** ∈ {N, A, Ad, M, Md} → **N**one, **A**dditive, **A**dditive **d**amped, **M**ultiplicative, **M**ultiplicative **d**amped
> - **Seasonality** ∈ {N, A, M} → **N**one, **A**dditive, **M**ultiplicative

**Standard benchmark metrics** in ETS:
- **Smoothing parameters**: $\alpha$ (level), $\beta^*$ (trend), $\gamma^*$ (seasonal), $\phi$ (damping)
- **AIC / AICc** for model selection
- **MSE (Mean Squared Error)** for in-sample fit
- **MASE (Mean Absolute Scaled Error)** for forecast accuracy

## 1.4 Innovation State Space — The Engine of ETS

> [!NOTE]
> Every ETS model has the structure:
>
> - **Observation equation:** $y_t = w(\ell_{t-1}, b_{t-1}, s_{t-m}) + k(\ell_{t-1}, b_{t-1}, s_{t-m}) \cdot \varepsilon_t$
> - **Transition equations:** State components evolve with $\varepsilon_t$ driving the noise.
>
> The **innovation** $\varepsilon_t$ is the *one-step-ahead forecast error* that drives ALL state updates. This unified framework is the KTU board's preferred formalism.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Building Blocks: Three Components

### 2.1.1 Error Component (E)
The error term captures random, unpredictable variations.

- **Additive Error (A)**: $y_t = \text{(level, trend, seasonal effect)} + \varepsilon_t$
  - Errors are independent of the magnitude of the series. Used when the **variance of residuals is roughly constant** over time.
- **Multiplicative Error (M)**: $y_t = \text{(level, trend, seasonal effect)} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_t)$
  - Errors **scale with the level** of the series. Used when variance grows proportionally with the mean (heteroscedastic data).

### 2.1.2 Trend Component (T)
The trend captures the long-term direction of the series.

- **None (N)**: No trend — equivalent to **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)**.
- **Additive (A)**: Linear growth/decline — equivalent to **Holt's Linear Method**.
- **Additive Damped (Ad)**: Trend decays over time, approaching a horizontal asymptote.
- **Multiplicative (M)**: Geometric (exponential) growth — values grow by a percentage.
- **Multiplicative Damped (Md)**: Geometric growth with flattening.

### 2.1.3 Seasonality Component (S)
The seasonal component captures the periodic fluctuations within a fixed period $m$.

- **None (N)**: No seasonal pattern.
- **Additive (A)**: $s_t = s_{t-m} + \gamma^* \varepsilon_t$ — seasonal effect is constant in magnitude.
- **Multiplicative (M)**: $s_t = s_{t-m} (1 + \gamma^* \varepsilon_t)$ — seasonal effect scales with level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additive vs Multiplicative — KTU Decision Rule:**
> - If seasonal **amplitude** is roughly **constant** → Additive Seasonality
> - If seasonal **amplitude grows with the level** of the series → Multiplicative Seasonality
> - In **M3-competition data**, **multiplicative seasonality dominated**, but in practice both are tested via AIC.

## 2.2 The 30 Canonical ETS Models

Combining choices yields up to 30 models, but many are mathematically inadmissible (negative variances or undefined parameters) leaving approximately **15–18 commonly used models**:

| Model Name | E | T | S | KTU Use Case |
|------------|---|---|---|--------------|
| ETS(A,N,N) | A | N | N | Simple Exp. Smoothing — stationary data |
| ETS(M,N,N) | A | N | N | SES with multiplicative error |
| ETS(A,A,N) | A | A | N | Holt's linear method — trending data |
| ETS(M,A,N) | M | A | N | Holt with multiplicative error |
| ETS(A,Ad,N) | A | Ad | N | Damped trend |
| ETS(A,A,A) | A | A | A | Holt-Winters additive (additive trend, additive season) |
| ETS(A,A,M) | A | A | M | Holt-Winters multiplicative season |
| ETS(M,A,M) | M | A | M | Multiplicative everything — most common "real-world" |
| ETS(A,Ad,M) | A | Ad | M | Damped trend + multiplicative season |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are written in **standard KTU board notation**. Memorize the table; you will need it.

### 2.3.1 ETS(A,N,N) — Simple Exponential Smoothing

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} &= \ell_t \\
\ell_t &= \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha) \ell_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $0 < \alpha < 1$; forecasts are a flat line at level $\ell_t$.

### 2.3.2 ETS(A,A,N) — Holt's Linear Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} &= \ell_t + h b_t \\
\ell_t &= \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
b_t &= \beta^* (\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*) b_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $0 < \alpha, \beta^* < 1$.

### 2.3.3 ETS(A,Ad,N) — Damped Trend

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} &= \ell_t + (\phi + \phi^2 + \dots + \phi^h) b_t \\
\ell_t &= \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1}) \\
b_t &= \beta^* (\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*) \phi b_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $0 < \phi < 1$ is the **damping parameter**.
- Long-run forecast: $\hat{y}_{t+\infty} = \ell_t + \dfrac{\phi}{1-\phi} b_t$ (a horizontal asymptote).

### 2.3.4 ETS(A,A,A) — Holt-Winters Additive

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} &= \ell_t + h b_t + s_{t-m+\left[(h-1) \bmod m\right]+1} \\
\ell_t &= \alpha (y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
b_t &= \beta^* (\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*) b_{t-1} \\
s_t &= \gamma^* (y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1}) + (1-\gamma^*) s_{t-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.5 ETS(A,A,M) — Holt-Winters Multiplicative Seasonality

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} &= (\ell_t + h b_t)\, s_{t-m+\left[(h-1) \bmod m\right]+1} \\
\ell_t &= \alpha \frac{y_t}{s_{t-m}} + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
b_t &= \beta^* (\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*) b_{t-1} \\
s_t &= \gamma^* \frac{y_t}{\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}} + (1-\gamma^*) s_{t-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.6 ETS(M,A,M) — Multiplicative Error + Multiplicative Season

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_t &= (\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})\, s_{t-m}\,(1 + \varepsilon_t) \\
\ell_t &= (\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})(1 + \alpha \varepsilon_t) \\
b_t &= b_{t-1} + \beta^* (\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \varepsilon_t \\
s_t &= s_{t-m}\,(1 + \gamma^* \varepsilon_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.7 Forecast Variance (Generic ETS)

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t}) = \sigma^2 \left[1 + \sum_{i=1}^{h-1} \left(\frac{\partial \hat{y}_{t+h \vert t}}{\partial \varepsilon_{t+h-i}}\right)^2 \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-step forecast variance grows with $h$** (forecast intervals widen as horizon increases). The recursive sensitivity terms can be obtained from the state-space matrices.

### 2.3.8 Parameter Constraints

| Parameter | Range | Meaning |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| $\alpha$ | $(0, 1)$ | Level smoothing weight |
| $\beta^*$ | $(0, 1-\alpha)$ | Trend smoothing weight |
| $\gamma^*$ | $(0, 1-\alpha)$ | Seasonal smoothing weight |
| $\phi$ | $(0, 1)$ | Damping factor |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where ETS Models are used in Engineering Practice:**
> - **Energy load forecasting** (power grid demand with daily/weekly seasonality).
> - **Network traffic prediction** (multiplicative seasonality with damped trend).
> - **Sales/demand forecasting** in supply chain.
> - **Solar/wind power output** (multiplicative error, daily seasonality).
> - **Industrial process control** for KPI monitoring.
> - Used in **R's `ets()` function, Python's `statsmodels.tsa.holtwinters`, and Hyndman's `forecast` package**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Holt-Winters to ETS Innovation Form

We will derive how a classical Holt-Winters additive model is recast as an **innovation state space form** — this is a high-frequency KTU 14-mark derivation.

### Step 1: Classical Holt-Winters Equations (Given)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_t &= \alpha (y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
b_t &= \beta^* (\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*) b_{t-1} \\
s_t &= \gamma^* (y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1}) + (1-\gamma^*) s_{t-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Define the One-Step-Ahead Forecast Error

By definition, the one-step-ahead forecast from origin $t-1$ is:

$$
\hat{y}_{t \vert t-1} = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m}
$$

So the **innovation** (forecast error) at time $t$ is:

$$
\varepsilon_t = y_t - \hat{y}_{t \vert t-1} = y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1} - s_{t-m}
$$

### Step 3: Rewrite the Level Equation in Innovation Form

Starting from:
$$
\ell_t = \alpha (y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})
$$

Substitute $y_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_t &= \alpha \big[\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t - s_{t-m}\big] + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
\ell_t &= \alpha(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
\ell_t &= (\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) + \alpha \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The new level equals the prior forecast plus a fraction $\alpha$ of the new error.

### Step 4: Rewrite the Trend Equation

Start with:
$$
b_t = \beta^*(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*)b_{t-1}
$$

Use $\ell_t - \ell_{t-1} = b_{t-1} + \alpha \varepsilon_t$ from Step 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_t &= \beta^*(b_{t-1} + \alpha \varepsilon_t) + (1-\beta^*)b_{t-1} \\
b_t &= b_{t-1} + \alpha \beta^* \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Rewrite the Seasonal Equation

Start with:
$$
s_t = \gamma^*(y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1}) + (1-\gamma^*)s_{t-m}
$$

Use $y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1} = s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_t &= \gamma^*(s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t) + (1-\gamma^*)s_{t-m} \\
s_t &= s_{t-m} + \gamma^* \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Assemble the Innovation State Space

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final innovation state space form of ETS(A,A,A):**
>
> **Observation equation:**
> $$y_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t$$
>
> **State transition equations:**
> $$\ell_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \alpha \varepsilon_t$$
> $$b_t = b_{t-1} + \alpha \beta^* \varepsilon_t$$
> $$s_t = s_{t-m} + \gamma^* \varepsilon_t$$

This is the form KTU examiners expect in derivation problems.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — ETS(A,N,N) on Mini-Series

**Data:** $y = \{3, 5, 4, 6, 5, 7\}$, with $\alpha = 0.3$, $\ell_0 = y_1 = 3$.

### Step 1: Initialize
$\ell_0 = 3$ (the first observation is the initial level).

### Step 2: Compute $\ell_1$ and $\varepsilon_1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_1 &= \ell_0 = 3 \\
\varepsilon_1 &= y_1 - \hat{y}_1 = 3 - 3 = 0 \\
\ell_1 &= \alpha y_1 + (1-\alpha)\ell_0 = 0.3(3) + 0.7(3) = 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute $\ell_2$ and $\varepsilon_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_2 &= \ell_1 = 3.0 \\
\varepsilon_2 &= y_2 - \hat{y}_2 = 5 - 3.0 = 2.0 \\
\ell_2 &= 0.3(5) + 0.7(3.0) = 1.5 + 2.1 = 3.6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute $\ell_3, \ell_4, \ell_5, \ell_6$ (tabular form)

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\hat{y}_{t \vert t-1}=\ell_{t-1}$ | $\varepsilon_t = y_t - \hat{y}$ | $\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\ell_{t-1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 3.0 | 0.0 | 3.0 |
| 2 | 5 | 3.0 | 2.0 | 3.6 |
| 3 | 4 | 3.6 | 0.4 | 3.72 |
| 4 | 6 | 3.72 | 2.28 | 4.404 |
| 5 | 5 | 4.404 | 0.596 | 4.5828 |
| 6 | 7 | 4.5828 | 2.4172 | 5.30796 |

**Recursive verification for $t=3$:** $\ell_3 = 0.3(4) + 0.7(3.6) = 1.2 + 2.52 = 3.72$ ✓

### Step 5: Forecast $h=2$ Steps Ahead

$$
\hat{y}_{7 \vert 6} = \hat{y}_{8 \vert 6} = \ell_6 = 5.308
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For ETS(A,N,N), the forecast is constant at the last estimated level.

## 3.3 Derivation: Damped Trend Long-Run Forecast

From the damped model, the $h$-step forecast is:

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} = \ell_t + (\phi + \phi^2 + \dots + \phi^h) b_t
$$

### Geometric series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^i &= \phi \cdot \frac{1 - \phi^h}{1 - \phi} \quad \text{(finite sum)} \\
\lim_{h \to \infty} \sum_{i=1}^{h} \phi^i &= \frac{\phi}{1 - \phi} \quad \text{(infinite sum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\hat{y}_{t+\infty \vert t} = \ell_t + \frac{\phi}{1 - \phi} b_t
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **horizontal asymptote** the series converges to. It is one of the key KTU 14-mark derivation points: showing the damped trend model has a finite, bounded long-run forecast, which solves the "runaway linear trend" problem of plain Holt's method.

## 3.4 Loss Function & Parameter Optimization

ETS parameters are estimated by **minimizing the sum of squared one-step-ahead errors**:

$$
\text{SSR} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \varepsilon_t^2 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (y_t - \hat{y}_{t \vert t-1})^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SSE (not ML)?** In the **innovation form**, $\varepsilon_t$ are independent $N(0, \sigma^2)$, so the log-likelihood reduces to:
>
> $$\ell(\theta) = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \varepsilon_t^2$$
>
> Maximizing this w.r.t. $\sigma^2$ gives $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \text{SSR}/n$, so ML estimation **is equivalent to minimizing SSE**. This is why `ets()` in R uses numerical optimization of SSE by default.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right ETS Model

| Diagnostic on Data | Recommended ETS Model |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| No trend, no seasonality | ETS(A,N,N) |
| Linear trend, no seasonality | ETS(A,A,N) |
| Trend flattening | ETS(A,Ad,N) |
| Multiplicative variance | ETS(M,*,*) |
| Constant seasonal amplitude | ETS(*,*,A) |
| Seasonal amplitude grows | ETS(*,*,M) |
| Strongly trending + growth seasonality | ETS(M,A,M) or ETS(M,Ad,M) |

## 3.6 AIC and Model Selection

For a model with $k$ parameters and $n$ observations, Akaike's Information Criterion is:

$$
\text{AIC} = -2 \log L(\hat{\theta}) + 2k = n \log\left(\frac{\text{SSR}}{n}\right) + 2k + n \log(2\pi) + n
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since the last two terms are constant across models with the same data, KTU students can use the simplified form:
>
> $$\text{AIC} \approx n \log(\text{SSR}/n) + 2k$$
>
> **The model with the LOWEST AIC wins.** For small samples, use **AICc** (corrected):
> $$\text{AICc} = \text{AIC} + \frac{2k(k+1)}{n-k-1}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ETS Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Time Series Data] --> nodeB[Exploratory Analysis]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Identify Components}
    nodeC -->|Error| nodeD[Additive or Multiplicative Variance]
    nodeC -->|Trend| nodeE[None, Additive, Multiplicative, Damped]
    nodeC -->|Season| nodeF[None, Additive, Multiplicative]
    D --> nodeG[Grid Search over ETS Candidates]
    E --> nodeG
    F --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Optimize Smoothing Parameters]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute AIC / AICc]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Select Best Model]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Generate Point Forecasts]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Compute Prediction Intervals]
    K --> nodeM[Final Forecast Output]
    L --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 ETS Component Interaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Input y sub t] --> nodeB[Level Component L sub t]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Trend Component B sub t]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Seasonal Component S sub t]
    nodeE[Innovation Noise Epsilon sub t] --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeE --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeF[Observation y sub t]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Forecast y hat sub t plus h]
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeG
```

## 4.3 Damped Trend Convergence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Initial Level L sub t] --> nodeB[Initial Trend B sub t]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Damping Phi Applied}
    nodeC -->|h=1| nodeD[Forecast Y sub t+1 = L + Phi B]
    nodeC -->|h=2| nodeE[Forecast Y sub t+2 = L + Phi + Phi squared B]
    nodeC -->|h infinity| nodeF[Long Run Forecast L + Phi divided by 1 minus Phi times B]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Converging Path]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

## 4.4 ETS Model Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start] --> nodeB{Is there Seasonality?}
    nodeB -->|No| nodeC{Is there Trend?}
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeD{Constant Amplitude?}
    nodeC -->|No| nodeE[ETS A N N]
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeF{Linear or Damped?}
    nodeF -->|Linear| nodeG[ETS A A N]
    nodeF -->|Damped| nodeH[ETS A Ad N]
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeI[ETS A A A or ETS A Ad A]
    nodeD -->|No| nodeJ[ETS A A M or ETS A Ad M]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Done]
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a *computational step*, not a physical object. Arrows represent the *flow of data/information* through the ETS forecasting pipeline. The decision trees in 4.4 are particularly useful for KTU exam-time quick model selection.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define ETS models. List the three components of the ETS framework and explain what each represents in a time series context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**ETS** stands for **Error, Trend, Seasonality** — a family of exponential smoothing forecasting methods.

1. **Error (E):** Represents the random, unpredictable noise in the series. It can be Additive (A) or Multiplicative (M), indicating whether the residual variance is constant or proportional to the level.
2. **Trend (T):** Represents the long-term direction (upward, downward, or flat) of the series. It can be None (N), Additive (A), Multiplicative (M), or Damped (Ad/Md).
3. **Seasonality (S):** Represents the periodic repeating pattern with a fixed period $m$. It can be None (N), Additive (A), or Multiplicative (M).

The combination is denoted as **ETS(E, T, S)** per the Hyndman–Athanasopoulos innovation state space framework.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between additive and multiplicative seasonality in ETS models with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Additive Seasonality:** Seasonal fluctuations have **constant magnitude** regardless of the series level.
  $$y_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t$$
  *Example:* Monthly electricity consumption in a household where the summer peak adds a constant 200 kWh each July.

- **Multiplicative Seasonality:** Seasonal fluctuations **grow proportionally with the series level**.
  $$y_t = (\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1})\, s_{t-m}\,(1 + \varepsilon_t)$$
  *Example:* Quarterly revenue of a growing company where Q4 holiday sales are always 1.4× the trend.

**Decision rule:** Plot the data; if the seasonal wave amplitude grows with time, use multiplicative.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the **innovation state space form** of the **ETS(A,A,A)** (Holt-Winters Additive) model. Show all algebraic steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the time series data $y = \{10, 15, 13, 18, 16, 22\}$ and $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta^* = 0.2$, with initial values $\ell_0 = 10$, $b_0 = 1$:
   (i) Apply the **ETS(A,A,N)** model and compute $\ell_t$, $b_t$ for $t = 1, 2, 3$.
   (ii) Generate forecasts for $h = 1, 2$ steps ahead. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Derivation**

**Given classical Holt-Winters additive equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_t &= \alpha (y_t - s_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
b_t &= \beta^*(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*)b_{t-1} \\
s_t &= \gamma^*(y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1}) + (1-\gamma^*)s_{t-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Define the innovation.** The one-step-ahead forecast error is:
$$\varepsilon_t = y_t - \hat{y}_{t \vert t-1} = y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1} - s_{t-m}$$

**Step 2: Rewrite level equation.** Substituting $y_t = \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_t &= \alpha(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t) + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
\ell_t &= \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \alpha \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Rewrite trend equation.** From Step 2, $\ell_t - \ell_{t-1} = b_{t-1} + \alpha \varepsilon_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_t &= \beta^*(b_{t-1} + \alpha \varepsilon_t) + (1-\beta^*)b_{t-1} \\
b_t &= b_{t-1} + \alpha\beta^* \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Rewrite seasonal equation.** From Step 1, $y_t - \ell_{t-1} - b_{t-1} = s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_t &= \gamma^*(s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t) + (1-\gamma^*)s_{t-m} \\
s_t &= s_{t-m} + \gamma^* \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Innovation State Space Form:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
y_t &= \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + s_{t-m} + \varepsilon_t \\
\ell_t &= \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} + \alpha \varepsilon_t \\
b_t &= b_{t-1} + \alpha \beta^* \varepsilon_t \\
s_t &= s_{t-m} + \gamma^* \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
}
$$

This is the **ETS(A,A,A)** state space model in innovation form. ✓ **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical Computation**

**Initial values:** $\ell_0 = 10$, $b_0 = 1$, $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta^* = 0.2$.

**Recursion formulas (ETS(A,A,N)):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{t \vert t-1} &= \ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1} \\
\ell_t &= \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) \\
b_t &= \beta^*(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*)b_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**$t=1$:** $y_1 = 10$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{1 \vert 0} &= \ell_0 + b_0 = 10 + 1 = 11 \\
\varepsilon_1 &= 10 - 11 = -1 \\
\ell_1 &= 0.4(10) + 0.6(10 + 1) = 4.0 + 6.6 = 10.6 \\
b_1 &= 0.2(10.6 - 10) + 0.8(1) = 0.12 + 0.8 = 0.92
\end{aligned}
$$

**$t=2$:** $y_2 = 15$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{2 \vert 1} &= \ell_1 + b_1 = 10.6 + 0.92 = 11.52 \\
\varepsilon_2 &= 15 - 11.52 = 3.48 \\
\ell_2 &= 0.4(15) + 0.6(10.6 + 0.92) = 6.0 + 6.912 = 12.912 \\
b_2 &= 0.2(12.912 - 10.6) + 0.8(0.92) = 0.4624 + 0.736 = 1.1984
\end{aligned}
$$

**$t=3$:** $y_3 = 13$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{3 \vert 2} &= \ell_2 + b_2 = 12.912 + 1.1984 = 14.1104 \\
\varepsilon_3 &= 13 - 14.1104 = -1.1104 \\
\ell_3 &= 0.4(13) + 0.6(14.1104) = 5.2 + 8.46624 = 13.66624 \\
b_3 &= 0.2(13.66624 - 12.912) + 0.8(1.1984) \\
&= 0.2(0.75424) + 0.95872 = 0.150848 + 0.95872 = 1.109568
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $\hat{y}_{t \vert t-1}$ | $\varepsilon_t$ | $\ell_t$ | $b_t$ |
|-----|-------|------------------------|------------------|----------|--------|
| 1 | 10 | 11.000 | −1.000 | 10.600 | 0.920 |
| 2 | 15 | 11.520 | +3.480 | 12.912 | 1.198 |
| 3 | 13 | 14.110 | −1.110 | 13.666 | 1.110 |

**(ii) Forecasts from $t=3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{4 \vert 3} &= \ell_3 + (1)b_3 = 13.666 + 1.110 = 14.776 \\
\hat{y}_{5 \vert 3} &= \ell_3 + (2)b_3 = 13.666 + 2(1.110) = 15.886
\end{aligned}
$$

✓ **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **damped trend ETS(A,Ad,N)** model with equations. Derive its long-run forecast and explain how it differs from Holt's linear method in long-horizon forecasting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a series with $m = 4$ (quarterly), the additive seasonal indices are $s_1 = 5$, $s_2 = -3$, $s_3 = -4$, $s_4 = 2$. The current level is $\ell_t = 100$ and trend is $b_t = 2$. Using **ETS(A,A,A)** with $h=3$, generate the next three forecasts. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Damped Trend Model**

**Equations of ETS(A,Ad,N):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} &= \ell_t + (\phi + \phi^2 + \dots + \phi^h)\,b_t \\
\ell_t &= \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1}) \\
b_t &= \beta^*(\ell_t - \ell_{t-1}) + (1-\beta^*)\phi b_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of long-run forecast:** Let $S_h = \phi + \phi^2 + \dots + \phi^h$. This is a finite geometric series:

$$
S_h = \phi \cdot \frac{1 - \phi^h}{1 - \phi}
$$

As $h \to \infty$ and $0 < \phi < 1$, we have $\phi^h \to 0$:

$$
\lim_{h \to \infty} S_h = \frac{\phi}{1 - \phi}
$$

**Long-run forecast:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{y}_{t+\infty \vert t} = \ell_t + \frac{\phi}{1-\phi} b_t}
$$

**Comparison with Holt's linear method:**

| Feature | Holt's Linear ETS(A,A,N) | Damped ETS(A,Ad,N) |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| $h$-step forecast | $\ell_t + h b_t$ | $\ell_t + S_h b_t$ |
| Behavior as $h \to \infty$ | Linear (unbounded) | Horizontal asymptote $\ell_t + \frac{\phi}{1-\phi}b_t$ |
| Realism | Long-run forecasts explode | Long-run forecasts flatten |
| Recommended use | Stable linear growth | Growth that slows/levels off |

**Engineering intuition:** The damped model acknowledges that "trends cannot continue forever" — energy demand growth eventually plateaus, technology adoption curves saturate. ✓ **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Holt-Winters Additive Forecasts**

**Given:** $m = 4$, $\ell_t = 100$, $b_t = 2$, seasonal indices: $s_{t+1} = 5$, $s_{t+2} = -3$, $s_{t+3} = -4$, $s_{t+4} = 2$.

**Formula:**

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} = \ell_t + h b_t + s_{t+h-m+1}
$$

But more cleanly (using the seasonal index for the future season):

$$
\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t} = \ell_t + h b_t + s_{t+h \pmod m + m}
$$

**$h=1$ (next quarter):**

$$
\hat{y}_{t+1} = 100 + (1)(2) + 5 = 107
$$

**$h=2$ (two quarters ahead):**

$$
\hat{y}_{t+2} = 100 + (2)(2) + (-3) = 101
$$

**$h=3$ (three quarters ahead):**

$$
\hat{y}_{t+3} = 100 + (3)(2) + (-4) = 102
$$

**Summary of forecasts:**

| Horizon $h$ | Season | $\ell_t + h b_t$ | $s_{\text{season}}$ | $\hat{y}_{t+h}$ |
|-------------|--------|-------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Q+1 | 102 | +5 | **107** |
| 2 | Q+2 | 104 | −3 | **101** |
| 3 | Q+3 | 106 | −4 | **102** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of seasonal index selection:** With $m=4$ and the indices given for quarters 1, 2, 3, 4 (in order), the next quarter is the "next cycle." So $h=1 \Rightarrow$ season index 5, $h=2 \Rightarrow$ -3, $h=3 \Rightarrow$ -4. ✓ **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in ETS Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $b_t$ (trend) with the seasonal index.** Students often mis-assign $s_t$ in Holt-Winters equations. **Tip:** $s_t$ cycles through indices $1 \to 2 \to \dots \to m \to 1$, while $b_t$ is a single time series.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\phi$ in the damped trend level equation.** The level update is $\ell_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\ell_{t-1} + \phi b_{t-1})$, **not** $\ell_{t-1} + b_{t-1}$.
>
> 3. **Using $\beta$ instead of $\beta^*$.** In ETS framework, the trend smoothing is parameterized as $\beta^*$ which often equals $\alpha \beta$ (a reparameterization for numerical stability). Check which form the question asks.
>
> 4. **Misapplying the innovation form.** Students write $\varepsilon_t$ inside state equations but forget that the **observation equation is $y_t = \text{forecast} + \varepsilon_t$** — not $y_t = \ell_t + b_t + \dots$
>
> 5. **Forgetting to compute $\hat{y}_{t+h \vert t}$ for $h \geq 2$** in forecast questions. ETS gives a *full forecast trajectory*, not just one step.
>
> 6. **Not showing the algebraic step of substituting $y_t$** in innovation-form derivations — this is where the 7-mark derivation lives.
>
> 7. **Using absolute value bars `|x|` in markdown tables** — this breaks LaTeX rendering. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for ETS Models (KTU PEADT755):**

- ☐ **ETS = Error, Trend, Seasonality** — three-component decomposition framework.
- ☐ The general notation is **ETS(E, T, S)** with options A/M/N/Ad/Md.
- ☐ **Innovation state space** is the unified KTU-preferred formulation: $y_t = \text{state} + \varepsilon_t$ in observation equation.
- ☐ Smoothing parameters are $\alpha \in (0,1)$, $\beta^* \in (0, 1-\alpha)$, $\gamma^* \in (0, 1-\alpha)$, $\phi \in (0,1)$.
- ☐ **ETS(A,N,N)** = Simple Exponential Smoothing (no trend, no season).
- ☐ **ETS(A,A,N)** = Holt's Linear Method.
- ☐ **ETS(A,Ad,N)** = Damped trend — long-run forecast converges to $\ell_t + \frac{\phi}{1-\phi} b_t$.
- ☐ **ETS(A,A,A)** = Holt-Winters Additive season; **ETS(A,A,M)** = Multiplicative season.
- ☐ **ETS(M,A,M)** is the most commonly selected in M-competition data.
- ☐ **Multiplicative error** → residual variance scales with level; **additive error** → constant variance.
- ☐ **Multi-step forecasts** for ETS(A,A,N) are linear in $h$; for damped trend, geometric in $h$.
- ☐ **Model selection:** Minimum AIC (or AICc) wins; alternatives are cross-validation, MASE.
- ☐ Parameters estimated by **minimizing SSE** of one-step-ahead errors (equivalent to MLE in innovation form).
- ☐ **Prediction intervals** widen with horizon $h$; compute via state-space variance propagation.
- ☐ For 14-mark questions, **always** derive the innovation form by substituting $y_t = \hat{y} + \varepsilon_t$ into each state equation.
- ☐ For 7-mark numerical questions, build a **table** with columns $\{t, y_t, \hat{y}_{t \vert t-1}, \varepsilon_t, \ell_t, b_t, s_t\}$ — examiners award full marks for organized tabular work.
- ☐ **The damping parameter $\phi$ is the key innovation** of ETS over classical Holt-Winters — it provides a bounded long-run forecast and is preferred in nearly all empirical M-competition studies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Extension Techniques for Time Series Data: Types of Stationary Behaviour in Time Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regression Extension Techniques for Time Series Data: Types of Stationary Behaviour in Time Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Regression Extension Techniques for Time Series Data

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *Regression Extension Technique* in time series analysis refers to a class of statistical models that generalize the classical linear regression framework to handle temporally ordered, serially correlated observations. These extensions explicitly model the dependence of the current observation $Y_t$ on its own past values (auto-regressive component), past and present error terms (moving average component), and/or exogenous regressors (distributed lag component). The principal family members include the **Autoregressive (AR)** model, the **Moving Average (MA)** model, the **Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA)** model, the **Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA)** model, the **Distributed Lag (DL)** model, and the **Error Correction Model (ECM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, regression extensions are positioned as the bridge between econometric regression (causal/explanatory) and stochastic time series modelling (Box-Jenkins paradigm). Students must recognize *when* to use which extension.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English):**
Imagine you are forecasting **tomorrow's classroom attendance** of students. A simple regression would say: "Attendance depends on the day of the week and whether there is a holiday." That is fine, but the prediction is naive. A *regression extension* would add memory: "Tomorrow's attendance also depends on **yesterday's attendance** (because habits persist), on **how badly students were punished last week for absence** (a distributed lag effect), and on the **surprise cancellation shock** that happened three days ago (an error/moving average term)." 

In essence, regression extensions give the regression equation a *memory* and a *shock-handling mechanism*, converting it from a static snapshot predictor into a dynamic, time-aware forecaster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction to Memorize:**
> - *Classical Regression:* $Y_t = f(X_t) + \varepsilon_t$ — errors assumed independent.
> - *Regression Extension:* $Y_t = f(Y_{t-1}, Y_{t-2}, \ldots, \varepsilon_{t-1}, \varepsilon_{t-2}, \ldots, X_t, X_{t-1}, \ldots) + \varepsilon_t$ — errors carry temporal structure.

---

### 1.2 Types of Stationary Behaviour in Time Series

**Formal Academic Definition:**
A time series $\{Y_t\}$ is said to exhibit *stationary behaviour* when its statistical properties do not change with time. The types of stationarity relevant to the KTU 2024 scheme are:

1. **Strict Stationarity (Strong Stationarity):** The joint distribution of any finite collection of observations $\{Y_{t_1}, Y_{t_2}, \ldots, Y_{t_k}\}$ is identical to that of $\{Y_{t_1+h}, Y_{t_2+h}, \ldots, Y_{t_k+h}\}$ for any time shift $h$.

2. **Weak Stationarity (Second-Order / Covariance Stationarity / Wide-Sense Stationarity):** The mean $E(Y_t)$ is constant, the variance $\text{Var}(Y_t)$ is finite and constant, and the autocovariance $\text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+h})$ depends only on the lag $h$ and not on $t$.

3. **Trend Stationarity:** The series is stationary around a deterministic trend. The series can be expressed as $Y_t = f(t) + u_t$ where $f(t)$ is a deterministic function of time and $u_t$ is a stationary stochastic process.

4. **Difference Stationarity:** The series becomes stationary only after applying first-differencing one or more times, i.e., $(1 - B)^d Y_t$ is stationary where $B$ is the backshift operator and $d \geq 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** Most theoretical results (Wold decomposition, Box-Jenkins methodology) require only **weak stationarity**, not strict stationarity. Weak stationarity is the working assumption in 95% of KTU-level problems.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture the **height of water in a calm swimming pool**:
- *Strictly stationary:* the pool is perfectly still and the rules governing its ripples never change — not a breeze ever alters the wave physics.
- *Weakly stationary:* the pool has ripples whose average height, average spread, and lag-2 correlation stay the same all day, but the exact wave pattern looks different every second.
- *Trend stationary:* the pool has a slowly rising drain plug — the water level itself follows a predictable deterministic curve, but the ripples around that curve are stationary.
- *Difference stationary:* the water level is wandering randomly like a drunkard's walk; only by measuring **how much it changed from one minute to the next** do the ripples become predictable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition for All Stationarity Types:** Stationarity implies the series has a *finite, time-invariant second moment structure*. Series with **unit roots**, **structural breaks**, or **deterministic trends** violate stationarity and must be transformed before ARMA modelling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Family of Regression Extension Models

#### 2.1.1 Autoregressive Model of Order p — AR(p)
The current value is regressed on its own $p$ past values:

$$Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \phi_2 Y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_p Y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Why this works:** Captures *persistence* and *momentum* — the idea that the system "remembers" its recent states.
**How:** Solved by OLS once stationarity is established, or by Yule-Walker equations for the AR coefficients.

#### 2.1.2 Moving Average Model of Order q — MA(q)
The current value is regressed on $q$ past white-noise shocks:

$$Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \cdots + \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}$$

**Why:** Captures *transient shocks* — events that briefly jolt the system and then fade away.
**How:** Not directly solvable by OLS (errors are unobserved); estimated via Maximum Likelihood or Hannan-Rissanen algorithm.

#### 2.1.3 ARMA(p, q) Model
The combined short-memory model:

$$Y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$$

#### 2.1.4 ARIMA(p, d, q) Model
For difference-stationary series, integrate $d$ times before applying ARMA:

$$\Delta^d Y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \Delta^d Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$$

#### 2.1.5 Distributed Lag Model — DL(k)
Regresses $Y_t$ on $k$ lagged values of an exogenous predictor $X$:

$$Y_t = \alpha + \beta_0 X_t + \beta_1 X_{t-1} + \cdots + \beta_k X_{t-k} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Why:** Models the *gradual transmission* of a policy/cause effect through time.
**How:** Suffers from multicollinearity among lagged X's — solved by *Almon lag* (polynomial restriction) or *Koyck geometric lag*.

#### 2.1.6 Autoregressive Distributed Lag — ARDL(p, k)
The most general single-equation regression extension for time series:

$$Y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i Y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=0}^{k} \beta_j X_{t-j} + \varepsilon_t$$

#### 2.1.7 Error Correction Model — ECM
Bridges short-run dynamics and long-run equilibrium:

$$\Delta Y_t = \alpha_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{p-1} \gamma_i \Delta Y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \delta_j \Delta X_{t-j} + \lambda \left[ Y_{t-1} - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{t-1}) \right] + \varepsilon_t$$

Here, $\lambda$ is the **speed of adjustment** to long-run equilibrium (must satisfy $-1 < \lambda < 0$ for stability).

---

### 2.2 Formal Conditions for the Four Stationarity Types

Let $\{Y_t\}$ be a stochastic process with mean function $\mu(t) = E(Y_t)$, variance function $\sigma^2(t) = E[(Y_t - \mu(t))^2]$, and autocovariance function $\gamma(t, t+h) = E[(Y_t - \mu(t))(Y_{t+h} - \mu(t+h))]$.

| Stationarity Type | Condition on Mean | Condition on Variance | Condition on Autocovariance | Equivalent Statement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strict (Strong)** | Identical for all finite joint distributions | Implied | Implied | $F_{Y_{t_1}, \ldots, Y_{t_k}}(\cdot) = F_{Y_{t_1+h}, \ldots, Y_{t_k+h}}(\cdot) \;\; \forall h, k$ |
| **Weak (Covariance / WSS)** | $\mu(t) = \mu$ (constant) | $\sigma^2(t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ (constant) | $\gamma(t, t+h) = \gamma(h)$ (depends only on lag) | Second moments time-invariant |
| **Trend Stationary** | $\mu(t) = f(t)$ deterministic trend | $\sigma^2(t) = \sigma^2$ (constant around trend) | $\gamma(t, t+h) = \gamma(h)$ | $Y_t - f(t)$ is covariance-stationary |
| **Difference Stationary** | $\mu(t)$ may drift | $\sigma^2(t)$ grows with $t$ | $\gamma(t, t+h)$ depends on $t$ | $(1-B)^d Y_t$ is stationary, $d \geq 1$ |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Condition | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | Autoregressive AR(p) model | $Y$ in original units |
| 2 | $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$ | Moving Average MA(q) model | $Y$ in original units |
| 3 | $\phi(L) Y_t = \theta(L) \varepsilon_t$ | ARMA in lag operator form | $L$ is the lag operator |
| 4 | $\phi(L)(1-B)^d Y_t = c + \theta(L) \varepsilon_t$ | ARIMA(p, d, q) form | $d \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ |
| 5 | $E(Y_t) = \mu \;\; \forall t$ | Weak stationarity — constant mean | scalar |
| 6 | $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty \;\; \forall t$ | Weak stationarity — finite constant variance | $(\text{units of }Y)^2$ |
| 7 | $\text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+h}) = \gamma(h)$ | Weak stationarity — autocovariance lag-dependent | $(\text{units of }Y)^2$ |
| 8 | $\rho(h) = \gamma(h)/\gamma(0)$ | Autocorrelation function (ACF) | $\vert \rho(h) \vert \leq 1$ |
| 9 | $\phi_{hh} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t-h} \vert Y_{t-1}, \ldots, Y_{t-h+1})}{\text{Var}(Y_t \vert Y_{t-1}, \ldots, Y_{t-h+1})}$ | Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF) | $\vert \phi_{hh} \vert \leq 1$ |
| 10 | $\Delta Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1} = (1-B)Y_t$ | First-difference operator | same units as $Y$ |
| 11 | $(1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i z^i) \neq 0 \;\;\; \forall \vert z \vert \leq 1$ | Stationarity condition for AR(p) roots | roots lie **outside** unit circle |
| 12 | $(1 + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j z^j) \neq 0 \;\;\; \forall \vert z \vert \leq 1$ | Invertibility condition for MA(q) | roots lie **outside** unit circle |
| 13 | $Y_t = f(t) + u_t$ with $u_t$ stationary | Trend stationarity definition | $f(t)$ deterministic |
| 14 | $(1-B)^d Y_t$ stationary, $d \geq 1$ | Difference stationarity definition | $d$ is order of integration |
| 15 | $\lambda \in (-1, 0)$ | Speed of adjustment in ECM for stability | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These models underpin demand forecasting in power grids (ARIMA load prediction), structural health monitoring (vibration AR models), signal processing (MA smoothing), and macroeconomic policy simulation (ARDL/ECM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on the $\vert z \vert$ notation:** In exam scripts, write $\vert z \vert$ for absolute value but be aware that the KTU Formula Sheet above uses \vert for vertical-bar delimiter to remain safe in markdown table syntax.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Yule-Walker Equations for AR(1)

We wish to derive the relationship between the AR(1) parameter $\phi_1$ and the autocorrelation function $\rho(h)$.

**Starting Model:**
$$Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$$

**Step 1 — Take expectations on both sides** (assuming $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$ and stationarity so $E(Y_t) = \mu$):

$$\mu = c + \phi_1 \mu \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = (1 - \phi_1)\mu$$

**Step 2 — Mean-adjusted form.** Subtract $\mu$ from both sides (define $y_t = Y_t - \mu$):

$$y_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $y_{t-h}$ and take expectations:**

$$E(y_t y_{t-h}) = \phi_1 E(y_{t-1} y_{t-h}) + E(\varepsilon_t y_{t-h})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the orthogonality property** $E(\varepsilon_t y_{t-h}) = 0$ for $h \geq 1$ (since $\varepsilon_t$ is white noise and $y_{t-h}$ depends on $\varepsilon_{t-h}, \varepsilon_{t-h-1}, \ldots$):

$$E(y_t y_{t-h}) = \phi_1 E(y_{t-1} y_{t-h})$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the left side as $\gamma(h)$ and the right side as $\gamma(h-1)$:**

$$\gamma(h) = \phi_1 \gamma(h-1)$$

**Step 6 — Divide both sides by $\gamma(0)$** to convert to the autocorrelation function $\rho(h) = \gamma(h)/\gamma(0)$:

$$\rho(h) = \phi_1 \rho(h-1)$$

**Step 7 — Solve the recurrence relation** for $h = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$:

$$\rho(h) = \phi_1^h$$

**Step 8 — Enforce the stationarity bound $\vert \rho(h) \vert \leq 1$ for all $h$:**

$$\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** An AR(1) process is (weakly) stationary if and only if the absolute value of its autoregressive coefficient is **strictly less than 1**. This is equivalent to the characteristic root $z = 1/\phi_1$ lying outside the unit circle in the complex plane.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean and Variance of a Stationary AR(1)

**Step 1 — Mean:** Already derived in 3.1 Step 1: $\mu = c / (1 - \phi_1)$ provided $\phi_1 \neq 1$.

**Step 2 — Variance:** Multiply the mean-adjusted form $y_t = \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ by itself and take expectations:

$$E(y_t^2) = \phi_1^2 E(y_{t-1}^2) + 2 \phi_1 E(y_{t-1} \varepsilon_t) + E(\varepsilon_t^2)$$

**Step 3 — Apply stationarity** $E(y_t^2) = E(y_{t-1}^2) = \gamma(0)$ and orthogonality $E(y_{t-1} \varepsilon_t) = 0$:

$$\gamma(0) = \phi_1^2 \gamma(0) + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\gamma(0)$:**

$$\gamma(0) = \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude** that stationarity ($\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$) ensures the variance is *finite* and *time-invariant*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: The AR(1) as a Distributed Lag (Infinite Lag Representation)

**Step 1 — Begin with the AR(1) form and recursively substitute $y_{t-1}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_t &= \phi_1 y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t \\
y_t &= \phi_1 (\phi_1 y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_{t-1}) + \varepsilon_t \\
y_t &= \phi_1^2 y_{t-2} + \phi_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t \\
y_t &= \phi_1^3 y_{t-3} + \phi_1^2 \varepsilon_{t-2} + \phi_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Continue recursively** until the initial condition is reached:

$$y_t = \phi_1^k y_{t-k} + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \phi_1^j \varepsilon_{t-j}$$

**Step 3 — Let $k \to \infty$. Since $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$, the term $\phi_1^k y_{t-k} \to 0$:**

$$y_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi_1^j \varepsilon_{t-j}$$

**Step 4 — This is the *infinite distributed lag* or *Wold moving-average representation* of the AR(1) process.**

**Step 5 — Take the variance of both sides** (using independence of $\varepsilon$'s):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma(0) &= \text{Var}\left( \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi_1^j \varepsilon_{t-j} \right) \\
&= \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi_1^{2j} \text{Var}(\varepsilon_{t-j}) \\
&= \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi_1^{2j} \\
&= \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \phi_1^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — This matches Step 4 of 3.2**, confirming internal consistency. The geometric series converges **iff** $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$, reaffirming the stationarity condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** It shows the deep equivalence between an *autoregressive* structure and a *distributed lag* structure — justifying why AR models can capture the "gradual transmission" intuition of lag models.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Which Stationarity Does My Series Have?

| Diagnostic Output | Strict Stationary | Weak Stationary | Trend Stationary | Difference Stationary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ACF decays to zero quickly | Yes | Yes | Yes (after detrending) | Only after differencing |
| Mean $E(Y_t)$ is constant | Yes | Yes | No (deterministic trend present) | No (stochastic trend) |
| $\gamma(h)$ depends only on $h$ | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (without differencing) |
| Variance grows with $t$ | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| ADF / KPSS test: $H_0$ rejected | n/a | n/a | n/a | ADF: unit root present |
| Behaviour of shocks | Transitory | Transitory | Transitory (around trend) | **Permanent** |
| Spurious regression risk with $X_t$ | Low | Low | Low | **High** if not differenced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The *single most important practical distinction* is between **trend stationarity** and **difference stationarity** — they look similar in plots but have *opposite* shock dynamics. In trend stationary, shocks dissipate; in difference stationary, shocks are permanent (random walk behaviour). Misclassification leads to wrong forecasts.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Classifying Stationarity

**Problem.** A KTU examiner gives you the following fitted AR(1) model on quarterly rainfall data: $Y_t = 2.0 + 0.95 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$. Classify the series and comment on forecast reliability.

**Solution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AR(1) coefficient } \phi_1 &= 0.95 \\
\text{Check: } \vert \phi_1 \vert &= 0.95 < 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{weakly stationary} \\
\text{Mean: } \mu &= c/(1 - \phi_1) = 2.0 / (1 - 0.95) = 2.0 / 0.05 = 40 \\
\text{Variance: } \gamma(0) &= \sigma_\varepsilon^2 / (1 - 0.95^2) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 / 0.0975 \approx 10.26 \, \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \\
\text{ACF at lag } h: \rho(h) &= 0.95^h \;\;\text{(decays slowly)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Although technically stationary (since $\vert 0.95 \vert < 1$), the series is *near-unit-root*. Shocks decay very slowly ($\rho(20) = 0.95^{20} \approx 0.36$), making the series **practically indistinguishable from a random walk** over typical sample sizes. Forecast confidence intervals will be very wide. A logit-class alternative is to test whether $\phi_1 = 1$ via an ADF test.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define weak stationarity. State the three conditions a stochastic process must satisfy to be classified as weakly stationary.*

**Model Answer:**
Weak stationarity (also called second-order or covariance stationarity) requires that:

1. The **mean** of the series is constant across time: $E(Y_t) = \mu \;\; \forall t$.
2. The **variance** is finite and constant across time: $\text{Var}(Y_t) = E[(Y_t - \mu)^2] = \sigma^2 < \infty \;\; \forall t$.
3. The **autocovariance** depends only on the time lag $h$, not on the absolute time $t$: $\text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+h}) = \gamma(h) \;\; \forall t$.

A process satisfying all three is said to be weakly stationary, and its entire second-moment structure can be summarized by the autocorrelation function $\rho(h) = \gamma(h)/\gamma(0)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between trend stationarity and difference stationarity. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Trend Stationarity | Difference Stationarity |
|---|---|---|
| Decomposition | $Y_t = f(t) + u_t$ where $u_t$ stationary | $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ (random walk) |
| Effect of shocks | **Transitory** (fade out) | **Permanent** (accumulate) |
| Recovery mechanism | Mean-reverts to the deterministic trend | Mean-reverts only after differencing |
| Example | Global mean temperature with linear trend | Stock market index levels |

**Example 1 (Trend Stationary):** Quarterly GNP of an economy with a deterministic linear growth trend — shocks to GNP eventually dissipate.

**Example 2 (Difference Stationary):** Daily closing price of a stock index — shocks to the price are essentially permanent and accumulate into the new price level.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often *omit the stationarity check* before fitting ARMA. This loses 2-3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** When asked to "state the conditions for stationarity of an AR(p) model", students write the *invertibility* conditions instead. The two are different — invertibility applies to the MA part, stationarity to the AR part.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the backshift operator $B$ with the forward shift. $B Y_t = Y_{t-1}$, not $Y_{t+1}$.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In ECM derivations, students forget to state the stability constraint $\lambda \in (-1, 0)$ and lose the "interpretation" mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Module 2]**

**(a)** *Explain the family of regression extension models for time series data. Discuss the AR(p), MA(q), ARMA(p, q), and ARIMA(p, d, q) models in detail, with their structural equations and the rationale behind each extension.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *For a stationary AR(1) process $Y_t = 2 + 0.7 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, 4)$:*
  - (i) Compute the long-run mean $\mu$ and the variance $\gamma(0)$. **(2 Marks)**
  - (ii) Derive the autocorrelation function $\rho(h)$ for $h = 1, 2, 3$. **(3 Marks)**
  - (iii) Comment on the stationarity of the process and the persistence of shocks. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Model Family Explanation:**

- **AR(p):** Models the *memory* of the series. The current observation is a linear combination of its $p$ past values plus white noise. Useful for series with strong autocorrelation and gradual decay in PACF. Equation: $Y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$.

- **MA(q):** Models the *shocks* hitting the series. Each current observation is influenced by the present and $q$ past white-noise errors. The MA coefficients capture the transient response to shocks. Equation: $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$.

- **ARMA(p, q):** Combines AR and MA to model both *memory* and *shocks* simultaneously. Parsimonious — captures rich autocorrelation structure with fewer parameters than a pure AR or pure MA. Equation: $Y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$.

- **ARIMA(p, d, q):** Extends ARMA to *non-stationary* series by differencing $d$ times. After differencing, the integrated series $W_t = \Delta^d Y_t$ is fit by an ARMA(p, q). This handles random-walk and unit-root processes elegantly. Equation: $\phi(L)(1-B)^d Y_t = c + \theta(L) \varepsilon_t$.

**Rationale (Why and How):**
The classical regression $Y_t = \alpha + \beta X_t + \varepsilon_t$ assumes *independent* errors, which is violated in time series where observations are serially correlated. Regression extensions relax this assumption by:
- *Augmenting* the right-hand side with lagged $Y$'s (AR component — captures persistence).
- *Modelling* the error structure as autocorrelated (MA component — captures shock propagation).
- *Differencing* to induce stationarity (Integration — handles trending data).

This converts regression from a static causal model into a dynamic forecasting tool.

---

**(b) Numerical Solutions:**

**(b)(i) Mean and Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1} = \frac{2}{1 - 0.7} = \frac{2}{0.3} = \frac{20}{3} \approx 6.667 \\
\gamma(0) &= \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2} = \frac{4}{1 - 0.49} = \frac{4}{0.51} \approx 7.843
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)(ii) Autocorrelation Function:**

The Yule-Walker result for AR(1) is $\rho(h) = \phi_1^h = 0.7^h$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho(1) &= 0.7^1 = 0.700 \\
\rho(2) &= 0.7^2 = 0.490 \\
\rho(3) &= 0.7^3 = 0.343
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)(iii) Stationarity and Shock Persistence:**

- *Stationarity:* Since $\vert \phi_1 \vert = 0.7 < 1$, the process **is (weakly) stationary**. The unit-root condition is not satisfied, so the characteristic root $z = 1/0.7 \approx 1.43$ lies **outside the unit circle**.

- *Shock Persistence:* The autocorrelation at lag $h$ decays **geometrically** as $0.7^h$. Specifically, $\rho(10) = 0.7^{10} \approx 0.028$, meaning that a shock today retains about 2.8% of its impact ten periods later. Shocks are **transitory** but with a half-life of approximately $\ln(0.5)/\ln(0.7) \approx 1.94$ periods. This moderate-to-slow decay means the series exhibits **substantial short-run memory** — appropriate for, e.g., a quarterly macroeconomic indicator.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Module 2]**

**(a)** *Discuss the four types of stationary behaviour in time series: strict stationarity, weak stationarity, trend stationarity, and difference stationarity. For each type, provide a real-world example and the appropriate modelling strategy.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A KTU econometrics dataset gives the following fitted model for a company's quarterly sales $S_t$ (in lakh ₹):*
$$S_t = 1.5 + 0.6 S_{t-1} + 0.2 S_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, 9)$$
- (i) Write the characteristic equation and find its roots. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Determine whether the process is stationary. Justify using the roots. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) Compute $E(S_t)$ assuming the process is stationary. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Types of Stationary Behaviour — Discussion:**

1. **Strict Stationarity (Strong Stationarity):**
   - The *entire* finite-dimensional joint distribution is time-invariant.
   - Mathematical condition: $F_{Y_{t_1}, \ldots, Y_{t_k}}(x_1, \ldots, x_k) = F_{Y_{t_1+h}, \ldots, Y_{t_k+h}}(x_1, \ldots, x_k) \;\; \forall h, k$.
   - *Example:* Pure white noise (iid sequence).
   - *Modelling strategy:* Generally strict stationarity is too strong and hard to test; in practice, weak stationarity is assumed.

2. **Weak Stationarity (Second-Order / Covariance Stationarity):**
   - Only the first two moments (mean, variance, autocovariance) are time-invariant.
   - Mathematical conditions: $E(Y_t) = \mu$, $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$, $\text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+h}) = \gamma(h)$.
   - *Example:* Stationary AR(1) with $\vert \phi \vert < 1$, such as the residual process of a properly specified ARMA model.
   - *Modelling strategy:* Apply Box-Jenkins ARMA modelling after confirming stationarity via ADF/KPSS tests.

3. **Trend Stationarity:**
   - The series is stationary *around* a deterministic trend function $f(t)$.
   - Mathematical form: $Y_t = f(t) + u_t$ where $u_t$ is covariance-stationary.
   - *Example:* Annual mean global surface temperature, which has a deterministic upward trend plus stationary climate variability.
   - *Modelling strategy:* Detrend the series (subtract $f(t)$), then fit ARMA to the residuals. *Never* difference a trend-stationary series — that introduces a unit root.

4. **Difference Stationarity:**
   - The series becomes stationary only after differencing $d$ times.
   - Mathematical form: $Y_t \sim I(d)$, meaning $(1-B)^d Y_t$ is stationary for some $d \geq 1$.
   - *Example:* Daily stock price index, GDP level in nominal terms.
   - *Modelling strategy:* Test for unit root using ADF or PP test; if present, fit an ARIMA(p, d, q) model. Cointegrated systems require ECM or VECM.

**Distinguishing the Two Most Confused Types:** The practical diagnostic is the **variance growth**: in difference stationary, $\text{Var}(Y_t) \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$ (variance grows linearly for $I(1)$); in trend stationary, $\text{Var}(Y_t)$ is bounded by the variance of $u_t$ plus the bounded trend.

---

**(b) Numerical Solutions for the AR(2) Model:**

**(b)(i) Characteristic Equation and Roots:**

For AR(2): $\phi(L) = 1 - 0.6 L - 0.2 L^2 = 0$.

Solve $1 - 0.6 z - 0.2 z^2 = 0$ (using $z$ for the reciprocal lag variable) which equals $-0.2 z^2 - 0.6 z + 1 = 0$ or equivalently $0.2 z^2 + 0.6 z - 1 = 0$.

Using the quadratic formula $z = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a = 0.2$, $b = 0.6$, $c = -1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= \frac{-0.6 \pm \sqrt{0.36 - 4(0.2)(-1)}}{2(0.2)} \\
z &= \frac{-0.6 \pm \sqrt{0.36 + 0.8}}{0.4} \\
z &= \frac{-0.6 \pm \sqrt{1.16}}{0.4} \\
z &= \frac{-0.6 \pm 1.0770}{0.4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_1 &= \frac{-0.6 + 1.0770}{0.4} = \frac{0.4770}{0.4} = 1.1925 \\
z_2 &= \frac{-0.6 - 1.0770}{0.4} = \frac{-1.6770}{0.4} = -4.1925
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)(ii) Stationarity Verdict:**

The stationarity condition for AR(p) is that all characteristic roots must lie **outside the unit circle** in the complex plane, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert > 1$ for all $i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert z_1 \vert &= 1.1925 > 1 \;\;\checkmark \\
\vert z_2 \vert &= 4.1925 > 1 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Both roots lie outside the unit circle. Therefore, the AR(2) process is (weakly) stationary.** No differencing is required; the series can be directly modelled as AR(2) without integration.

**(b)(iii) Long-Run Mean:**

For a stationary AR(p) process $Y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$, the long-run mean is:

$$\mu = \frac{c}{1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i}$$

For our AR(2) model with $c = 1.5$, $\phi_1 = 0.6$, $\phi_2 = 0.2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{1.5}{1 - (0.6 + 0.2)} \\
\mu &= \frac{1.5}{1 - 0.8} \\
\mu &= \frac{1.5}{0.2} \\
\mu &= 7.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore, the long-run expected quarterly sales is ₹7.5 lakhs.** The company can plan capacity around this mean value, recognizing that quarter-to-quarter fluctuations around it will be governed by the AR(2) structure with shocks of standard deviation $\sigma_\varepsilon = 3$ lakhs.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High Density Bullets**

- **Regression Extensions** are the family of models that *generalize linear regression* to handle *serially correlated* time series data: **AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA, DL, ARDL, ECM**.
- The **AR(p) model** regresses $Y_t$ on its $p$ past values; the **MA(q) model** regresses $Y_t$ on $q$ past shocks; **ARMA(p, q)** combines both; **ARIMA(p, d, q)** adds $d$-fold differencing for unit-root series.
- The **Distributed Lag (DL) model** captures gradual transmission of an exogenous shock; the **ARDL** combines it with autoregressive dynamics; the **ECM** adds the long-run equilibrium term with speed-of-adjustment parameter $\lambda \in (-1, 0)$.
- **Stationarity** is the *bedrock assumption* of all time series modelling — without it, OLS inference is invalid, forecasts explode, and Granger causality tests become unreliable.
- The **four types of stationarity** are: **Strict (Strong), Weak (Covariance/WSS), Trend, and Difference**. KTU exams most frequently test the distinction between **Weak, Trend, and Difference** stationarity.
- **Strict stationarity** = entire joint distribution is time-invariant (very strong assumption).
- **Weak stationarity** = only the *first two moments* (mean, variance, autocovariance) are time-invariant — this is the working assumption in 95% of KTU problems.
- **Trend stationarity** = series is stationary *around* a deterministic trend $f(t)$; shocks are **transitory**; detrend before modelling.
- **Difference stationarity** = series becomes stationary only *after* differencing; shocks are **permanent**; fit ARIMA(p, d, q).
- **Yule-Walker equations** for AR(1): $\rho(h) = \phi_1^h$, **stationarity condition:** $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$, **variance:** $\gamma(0) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 / (1 - \phi_1^2)$, **mean:** $\mu = c / (1 - \phi_1)$.
- **General stationarity condition for AR(p):** All roots of the characteristic polynomial $\phi(z) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i z^i$ must lie **outside** the unit circle, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert > 1$.
- **Invertibility condition for MA(q):** All roots of $\theta(z) = 1 + \sum_{j=1}^{q} \theta_j z^j$ must lie **outside** the unit circle.
- The **backshift operator** $B$ is defined by $B Y_t = Y_{t-1}$. The differencing operator is $\Delta = 1 - B$.
- The **autocorrelation function (ACF)** $\rho(h)$ decays geometrically for AR(1) with $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$. The **partial autocorrelation function (PACF)** cuts off after lag $p$ for a pure AR(p) — a critical identification tool.
- **Practical implication of near-unit-root:** When $\phi_1 \approx 1$ (e.g., 0.95), the series is technically stationary but *behaves* like a random walk — forecasts are highly uncertain.
- **The "memory" in AR models is geometric:** an AR(1) is equivalent to an **infinite distributed lag** $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \phi_1^j \varepsilon_{t-j}$ — this is the **Wold representation theorem** in action.
- **ECM stability:** The speed-of-adjustment coefficient $\lambda$ must satisfy $-1 < \lambda < 0$ for the system to be mean-reverting to its long-run equilibrium.
- **KTU Pitfall Avoidance Checklist:** (1) Always state the stationarity assumption. (2) Never confuse invertibility with stationarity. (3) Always compute both roots when checking AR(2) stationarity. (4) Always include units and the time domain (e.g., quarterly, daily) in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Making Data Stationary
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Making Data Stationary

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Stationarity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stationarity (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):** A time series $\{Y_t\}$ is said to be **(weakly) stationary** if **all three** of the following moment conditions hold for every time index $t$ and every time lag $k \in \mathbb{Z}$:
> 1. The **mean** is constant in time: $\mathbb{E}[Y_t] = \mu$ for all $t$.
> 2. The **variance** is finite and constant in time: $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ for all $t$.
> 3. The **autocovariance** depends only on the lag $k$, not on $t$: $\gamma(k) = \text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+k})$ for all $t, k$.

**Strict Stationarity** requires the entire joint distribution $F_{Y_{t_1}, Y_{t_2}, \ldots, Y_{t_n}}$ to be invariant under a time shift, i.e., identical for all $t$. In practice, KTU board problems and standard econometric practice rely on **weak (second-order) stationarity**, since it is the condition required for valid inference in models like ARMA, ARIMA, and the Box-Jenkins methodology.

### 1.2 Why Must We Make Data Stationary?

Most powerful time-series tools — **ARMA(p,q)**, **Box-Jenkins**, **spectral analysis**, **Wold decomposition** — are built on the assumption of stationarity. If the series is non-stationary:

- The **autocorrelation function (ACF)** decays very slowly, making lag order selection impossible.
- Standard errors of OLS estimators become **spuriously low**, leading to the **spurious regression problem** (Granger & Newbold).
- Forecasts from a non-stationary model have **unbounded variance**, hence are unreliable.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are trying to predict the **height of water in a swimming pool**.
> - If the pool has a **steady water level** (always near 1.5 m, with small ripples), the pool is **stationary**. A model only needs to learn the small ripples.
> - If the pool is being **continuously filled from a tap** (water level rises steadily), it is **non-stationary** (it has a **trend**). You cannot build a stable model on a rising series — the average height keeps changing.
> - If the pool is **emptied and refilled every 24 hours** (a daily cycle), it is **seasonally non-stationary**. The pattern repeats but is not constant in level around a fixed mean.
>
> **"Making data stationary"** is the act of mathematically removing the tap, the emptying cycle, and any growing variance — leaving only the **stationary ripples** that we can statistically model.

### 1.4 Common Sources of Non-Stationarity

A time series can fail to be stationary due to one or more of the following:

| Source | Mathematical Signature | Visual Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic Trend** | $\mathbb{E}[Y_t] = \alpha + \beta t$ | Smooth upward/downward slope |
| **Stochastic Trend (Unit Root)** | $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | Random-walk-like wandering |
