| **Seasonality** | $\mathbb{E}[Y_t] = f(t + s)$ where $s$ is the season | Repeating cycles of fixed period $s$ |
| **Heteroscedasticity (Changing Variance)** | $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma^2_t$ | Widening/narrowing amplitude over time |
| **Structural Breaks** | Abrupt change in $\mu$ or $\sigma^2$ | Sudden jumps or shifts in level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** The syllabus treats stationarity as the **cornerstone prerequisite** for the Box-Jenkins ARIMA methodology covered in the later modules. Hence, this topic carries very high board-exam weightage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Framework: Sources of Non-Stationarity and Their Remedies

The non-stationary component $Y_t$ can be decomposed (additively) as:

$$
Y_t \;=\; T_t \;+\; S_t \;+\; C_t
$$

where $T_t$ is the **trend component**, $S_t$ is the **seasonal component**, and $C_t$ is the **stationary cyclical/irregular component** (after removal of trend and seasonality).

To make $Y_t$ stationary, we must eliminate $T_t$ and $S_t$ via **differencing**, **logging**, **detrending**, or **decomposition**.

### 2.2 The Six Standard Techniques to Make Data Stationary

#### Technique 1 — First Differencing

The simplest and most widely used transformation:

$$
\nabla Y_t \;=\; Y_t - Y_{t-1}
$$

- Removes a **linear trend** and converts a **random walk** $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ into **white noise** $\varepsilon_t$ (which is stationary).
- The **backshift operator** $B$ (where $B Y_t = Y_{t-1}$) yields $\nabla = 1 - B$.

#### Technique 2 — Second (and higher-order) Differencing

$$
\nabla^2 Y_t \;=\; \nabla(\nabla Y_t) \;=\; Y_t - 2 Y_{t-1} + Y_{t-2}
$$

- Used when first differencing is **insufficient** (e.g., series with a quadratic trend).
- **Box-Jenkins Rule:** Use the **lowest order of differencing** that achieves stationarity. Over-differencing introduces unnecessary serial correlation and inflates MA components.

#### Technique 3 — Seasonal Differencing

For a series with period $s$ (e.g., $s = 12$ for monthly data):

$$
\nabla_s Y_t \;=\; Y_t - Y_{t-s}
$$

- Removes **seasonal non-stationarity**.
- Often combined with first differencing: $\nabla \nabla_s Y_t$.

#### Technique 4 — Logarithmic / Power Transformation

$$
Z_t = \log(Y_t)
$$

- **Stabilises variance** when the series exhibits **multiplicative growth** (heteroscedasticity of the form $\text{Var}(Y_t) \propto (\mathbb{E}[Y_t])^2$).
- Log-difference gives **instantaneous growth rate**: $\log Y_t - \log Y_{t-1} = \log(1 + r_t) \approx r_t$.

#### Technique 5 — Box-Cox Transformation (Generalised)

$$
Y_t^{(\lambda)} \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{Y_t^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}, & \lambda \neq 0 \\[6pt]
\log(Y_t), & \lambda = 0
\end{cases}
$$

- A **family** of power transformations indexed by $\lambda$, with $\lambda = 1$ (no change), $\lambda = 0$ (log), $\lambda = 0.5$ (square root), $\lambda = -1$ (reciprocal), etc.
- Optimal $\lambda$ is chosen by **maximum likelihood** to maximise normality of the transformed series.

#### Technique 6 — Detrending (Regression-based Removal)

If the trend is **deterministic and known to be linear**, fit OLS:

$$
Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + u_t
$$

Then work with the **residuals** $u_t = Y_t - \hat{\alpha} - \hat{\beta}\, t$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction (High KTU Weightage):** Differencing is appropriate for a **stochastic trend (unit root)**, while detrending is appropriate for a **deterministic trend**. The two are **not interchangeable** — applying OLS detrending to a random-walk series leaves the unit root intact (Phillips' spurious regression problem).

### 2.3 Diagnostic Tests for Stationarity (Essential for the Board Exam)

#### (a) Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) Test

Tests the null hypothesis $H_0: \phi = 1$ (unit root present, **non-stationary**) against $H_1: \vert \phi \vert < 1$ (**stationary**).

For an **AR(1) process with drift**:

$$
Y_t \;=\; \mu \;+\; \phi\, Y_{t-1} \;+\; \varepsilon_t
$$

The test statistic is the **t-ratio** of $\hat{\phi}$:

$$
\tau \;=\; \dfrac{\hat{\phi} - 1}{\text{SE}(\hat{\phi})}
$$

- Critical values are **non-standard** (MacKinnon tables) — they are **more negative** than standard normal critical values.
- Decision rule: Reject $H_0$ if $\tau < \tau_{\alpha}$ (i.e., more negative than the critical value).

> For an **AR(p)** series, the ADF test adds $p$ lagged differences to "augment" the regression:
> $$\Delta Y_t \;=\; \mu \;+\; \phi\, Y_{t-1} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{p} \theta_i\, \Delta Y_{t-i} \;+\; \varepsilon_t$$

#### (b) KPSS Test

The **reverse** null hypothesis: $H_0:$ series **is** stationary vs. $H_1:$ series has a **unit root**. Using both ADF and KPSS in tandem gives robust conclusions.

#### (c) Visual / ACF-Based Diagnostics

- **ACF** of a non-stationary series decays **very slowly** (linearly or hyperbolically).
- **ACF** of a stationary series **cuts off** or **decays quickly** to zero.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Cheat Sheet (memorise before any test):**

| Transformation | Formula | What It Removes | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First Difference** | $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ | Linear trend, random-walk unit root | Most common in ARIMA |
| **Second Difference** | $\nabla^2 Y_t = Y_t - 2Y_{t-1} + Y_{t-2}$ | Quadratic trend | Rarely needed |
| **Seasonal Difference** | $\nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-s}$ | Period-$s$ seasonality | Monthly ($s=12$), quarterly ($s=4$) |
| **Logarithm** | $Z_t = \log Y_t$ | Multiplicative trend, heteroscedasticity | Stock prices, GDP |
| **Box-Cox** | $Y_t^{(\lambda)} = (Y_t^{\lambda} - 1)/\lambda$ | Variance instability | Variance stabilisation |
| **OLS Detrend** | $u_t = Y_t - (\hat{\alpha} + \hat{\beta}\,t)$ | Deterministic linear trend | When trend is known to be smooth/deterministic |
| **ADF Statistic** | $\tau = (\hat{\phi}-1)/\text{SE}(\hat{\phi})$ | (Diagnostic, not a transform) | Test for unit root |
| **Random Walk Transform** | $W_t = \nabla Y_t$ | Pure stochastic trend | Yields white noise |
| **AR(1) Stationarity** | $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ | (Condition) | Boundary between stationary and non-stationary |
| **Backshift** | $\nabla = 1 - B$ | (Operator) | Compact notation for differencing |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering and Economics Applications

- **Stock-price modelling:** Raw prices are non-stationary (random walk); **log-returns** $\log(P_t / P_{t-1})$ are stationary — this is the cornerstone of financial econometrics (used in VaR, Black-Scholes).
- **Industrial process control:** Sensor drift must be removed via differencing before applying ARMA-based anomaly detection on a manufacturing line.
- **Climate science:** Global temperature series have both a trend and a 12-month seasonal cycle; both must be removed before fitting a stationary model to anomalies.
- **Telecommunications traffic:** Hourly call-volume data exhibits strong daily and weekly seasonality; seasonal differencing is essential.
- **Sales forecasting:** Retail monthly sales display trend + annual seasonality → **SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)$_{12}$** uses both non-seasonal and seasonal differencing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differencing Converts a Random Walk into White Noise

A **random walk** is defined as:

$$
Y_t \;=\; Y_{t-1} \;+\; \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, \sigma^2)
$$

with initial value $Y_0$. We wish to show that the first difference $\nabla Y_t$ is **white noise**, which is strictly stationary.

**Step 1.** By the definition of the difference operator, $\nabla Y_t \equiv Y_t - Y_{t-1}$. Substituting the random-walk definition:

$$
\nabla Y_t \;=\; Y_t - Y_{t-1} \;=\; (Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t) - Y_{t-1}
$$

**Step 2.** Simplify the telescoping cancellation:

$$
\nabla Y_t \;=\; \varepsilon_t
$$

**Step 3.** Verify the white-noise conditions:
- $\mathbb{E}[\nabla Y_t] = \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_t] = 0$ (constant mean).
- $\text{Var}(\nabla Y_t) = \text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ (constant finite variance).
- $\text{Cov}(\nabla Y_t, \nabla Y_{t+k}) = \text{Cov}(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_{t+k}) = 0$ for $k \neq 0$ (no autocorrelation).

All three weak-stationarity conditions are satisfied. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Differencing **undoes** the cumulative summation that defines a random walk, leaving behind the **incremental shocks** $\varepsilon_t$, which are inherently stationary.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — How Many Times Must We Difference a Series with a Linear Trend?

Suppose $Y_t$ contains a deterministic linear trend plus stationary noise:

$$
Y_t \;=\; \alpha + \beta t + u_t, \qquad u_t \sim \text{stationary}
$$

**Step 1.** Apply the first difference $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$:

$$
\nabla Y_t \;=\; (\alpha + \beta t + u_t) - (\alpha + \beta(t-1) + u_{t-1})
$$

**Step 2.** Expand and group the trend terms:

$$
\nabla Y_t \;=\; \beta t - \beta(t-1) + (u_t - u_{t-1}) \;=\; \beta + (u_t - u_{t-1})
$$

**Step 3.** The trend constant $\beta$ is a **deterministic constant**; the differenced noise $u_t - u_{t-1}$ is stationary. Hence $\nabla Y_t$ is stationary around mean $\beta$.

**Step 4.** If we had instead applied a **quadratic trend** $Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma t^2 + u_t$, the first difference would still contain a linear trend $\gamma(2t-1)$, requiring a **second difference**. Hence the rule: *differencing $d$ times removes a polynomial trend of degree $d$*. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Characteristic Equation of AR(1) and the Unit-Root Boundary

Consider the AR(1) process:

$$
Y_t \;=\; \phi\, Y_{t-1} \;+\; \varepsilon_t
$$

**Step 1.** Rewrite using the backshift operator $B$ (where $B Y_t = Y_{t-1}$):

$$
Y_t - \phi B Y_t \;=\; \varepsilon_t \quad\Longrightarrow\quad (1 - \phi B)\, Y_t \;=\; \varepsilon_t
$$

**Step 2.** Form the **characteristic equation** by setting the AR polynomial to zero:

$$
1 - \phi z \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad z \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\phi}
$$

**Step 3.** The series is **stationary** iff the root lies **outside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert z \vert > 1$. Therefore:

$$
\left\vert \dfrac{1}{\phi} \right\vert > 1 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \vert \phi \vert < 1
$$

**Step 4.** When $\phi = 1$, the root is exactly **on** the unit circle — the **unit-root / non-stationary boundary**. This is precisely the null hypothesis of the **ADF test**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — The ADF Test Statistic

The ADF regression for a series with **drift and trend** is:

$$
\Delta Y_t \;=\; \mu + \beta t + \phi\, Y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \theta_i\, \Delta Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t
$$

**Step 1.** Rearrange to isolate the unit-root coefficient $\phi$:

$$
\phi \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Cov}(Y_{t-1}, \Delta Y_t \,\vert\, \text{augmented regressors})}{\text{Var}(Y_{t-1} \,\vert\, \text{augmented regressors})}
$$

**Step 2.** Define the residuals $\hat{\varepsilon}_t$ from the fitted ADF regression. The OLS estimator $\hat{\phi}$ is obtained by least squares.

**Step 3.** The test statistic is the t-ratio under the null $H_0: \phi = 1$:

$$
\tau_{\text{ADF}} \;=\; \dfrac{\hat{\phi} - 1}{\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat{\phi})}
$$

**Step 4.** Under the null, $\tau_{\text{ADF}}$ does **not** follow a standard Student-$t$ distribution. The asymptotic distribution (Dickey-Fuller) has critical values tabulated by **MacKinnon**. For example, at $\alpha = 0.05$ with a constant and linear trend, the critical value is approximately $-3.45$ (much more negative than the standard $-1.96$).

**Decision rule:** Reject $H_0$ (i.e., declare the series stationary) if $\tau_{\text{ADF}} < \tau_{\text{crit}}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Box-Cox Transformation and Variance Stabilisation

For a series $Y_t > 0$ with $\text{Var}(Y_t) \propto (\mathbb{E}[Y_t])^{2\nu}$, the Box-Cox power $\lambda$ that stabilises variance satisfies:

$$
\text{Var}(Y_t^{(\lambda)}) \;\approx\; \text{constant}
$$

**Step 1.** Apply a Taylor expansion of $Y_t^{\lambda}$ around the mean $\mu$:

$$
Y_t^{\lambda} \;\approx\; \mu^{\lambda} + \lambda \mu^{\lambda-1}(Y_t - \mu)
$$

**Step 2.** Compute the variance of the transformed series:

$$
\text{Var}(Y_t^{\lambda}) \;\approx\; \lambda^2 \mu^{2\lambda-2}\, \text{Var}(Y_t)
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute the assumed variance form $\text{Var}(Y_t) = k^2 \mu^{2\nu}$:

$$
\text{Var}(Y_t^{\lambda}) \;\approx\; \lambda^2 k^2 \mu^{2\lambda - 2 + 2\nu}
$$

**Step 4.** For the variance to be **independent of $\mu$**, the exponent of $\mu$ must vanish:

$$
2\lambda - 2 + 2\nu \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda \;=\; 1 - \nu
$$

**Step 5.** Special cases:
- $\nu = 0$ (constant variance) → $\lambda = 1$ (no transformation needed).
- $\nu = 1$ (variance grows as the square of the mean) → $\lambda = 0$ → use the **logarithm**.
- $\nu = 0.5$ → $\lambda = 0.5$ → use the **square root**.

This is the rigorous justification for the standard rule of thumb: *"If the spread grows with the level, take a log."* $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Transformation Should I Use?"

| Symptom in Data | Diagnostic Test | Recommended Transformation |
|---|---|---|
| Slow ACF decay, no obvious trend | ADF $p$-value $> 0.05$ | First-difference $\nabla Y_t$ |
| Quadratic-looking trend | ADF still $> 0.05$ after $\nabla$ | Second-difference $\nabla^2 Y_t$ |
| Repeating cycles every $s$ steps | Spikes at lag $s$ in ACF | Seasonal difference $\nabla_s Y_t$ |
| Widening amplitude over time | Variance increases with mean | Logarithm $\log Y_t$ |
| Mild heteroscedasticity | Variance mildly level-dependent | Box-Cox with $\lambda \in [-1, 1]$ |
| Smooth deterministic curve | OLS $R^2$ high for $Y_t \sim t$ | Detrend (subtract OLS fit) |
| Mixed trend + seasonality | Both symptoms present | SARIMA: $\nabla \nabla_s$ combined |

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — ADF Test on a Synthetic Series

Suppose we observe $Y_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_{10} = \{2.1, 2.4, 2.0, 2.6, 2.5, 2.9, 2.7, 3.1, 3.0, 3.4\}$.

**Step 1.** Compute first differences $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$:

$$
\nabla Y \;=\; \{0.3,\; -0.4,\; 0.6,\; -0.1,\; 0.4,\; -0.2,\; 0.4,\; -0.1,\; 0.4\}
$$

**Step 2.** Fit the ADF regression $\nabla Y_t = \mu + \phi Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ by OLS to obtain $\hat{\phi}$ and $\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat{\phi})$. Suppose we obtain $\hat{\phi} = -0.85$ and $\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat{\phi}) = 0.30$.

**Step 3.** Compute the ADF statistic:

$$
\tau_{\text{ADF}} \;=\; \dfrac{\hat{\phi} - 1}{\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat{\phi})} \;=\; \dfrac{-0.85 - 1}{0.30} \;=\; \dfrac{-1.85}{0.30} \;\approx\; -6.17
$$

**Step 4.** Compare with the MacKinnon critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ (no trend, no constant variant) $\tau_{0.05} \approx -2.97$.

**Step 5.** Since $-6.17 < -2.97$, we **reject $H_0$** — there is **no unit root**; the differenced series is stationary. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End Workflow for Making Data Stationary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RAW TIME SERIES Y_t"] --> B["STEP 1: VISUAL INSPECTION"]
    B --> C{"Trend Present?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Apply First Difference: nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_t-1"]
    C -- No --> E{"Seasonality Present?"}
    D --> F["Re-check ACF: Decays Quickly?"]
    F -- No --> G["Apply Second Difference: nabla squared Y_t"]
    F -- Yes --> H{"Variance Unstable?"}
    E -- Yes --> I["Apply Seasonal Difference: nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_t-s"]
    E -- No --> J["Stationary Diagnostic Tests"]
    I --> J
    G --> J
    H -- Yes --> K["Apply Log Transform: Z_t = log Y_t"]
    H -- No --> J
    K --> J
    J --> L["ADF Test: H0 = Unit Root"]
    L --> M{"p-value less than 0.05?"}
    M -- Yes --> N["STATIONARY SERIES: Proceed to ARMA / Box-Jenkins"]
    M -- No --> O["Return to Step 1: Try Alternate Transform"]
    O --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The process is **iterative** — it is normal to apply multiple transformations (e.g., log *and* first-difference) in sequence before stationarity is achieved. The decision boxes map directly to the Analytical Decision Matrix in Section 3.6.

---

### 4.2 Schematic — Sources of Non-Stationarity and Their Targeted Fixes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["SOURCES OF NON-STATIONARITY"]
        S1["Deterministic Trend: alpha plus beta t"]
        S2["Stochastic Trend / Unit Root: Y_t equals Y_t-1 plus epsilon"]
        S3["Seasonality: Period s cycle"]
        S4["Heteroscedasticity: Var grows with level"]
        S5["Structural Break: Sudden shift in mu"]
    end
    subgraph FIX["TARGETED REMEDIES"]
        R1["Detrend: Subtract OLS fit"]
        R2["First Difference: nabla Y_t"]
        R3["Seasonal Difference: nabla_s Y_t"]
        R4["Log or Box-Cox Transform"]
        R5["Add Intervention Dummy Variable"]
    end
    S1 --> R1
    S2 --> R2
    S3 --> R3
    S4 --> R4
    S5 --> R5
    R1 --> OUT["STATIONARY RESIDUAL SERIES"]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
    R5 --> OUT
```

---

### 4.3 Flowchart — The ADF Hypothesis-Testing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Compute First Differences nabla Y_t"] --> P1["Choose Lag Order p via AIC or BIC"]
    P1 --> P2["Estimate ADF Regression by OLS"]
    P2 --> P3["Obtain hat phi and SE of hat phi"]
    P3 --> P4["Compute tau_ADF equals hat phi minus 1 over SE of hat phi"]
    P4 --> P5{"tau_ADF less than tau_crit at 5 percent?"}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6["REJECT H0: Series is Stationary"]
    P5 -- No --> P7["FAIL TO REJECT H0: Unit Root Present"]
    P6 --> P8["Stop: Use Series as is in ARMA"]
    P7 --> P9["Try Higher Order of Differencing: nabla squared Y_t"]
    P9 --> P0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state the null and alternative hypotheses **explicitly** when answering ADF-related questions. Marks are awarded for correct null ($H_0: \phi = 1$) and the correct use of the **MacKinnon critical values** (not the standard Student-$t$ table).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define weak stationarity of a time series. Why is it important in time series modelling?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A time series $\{Y_t\}$ is **weakly stationary** if it satisfies three conditions (1 mark each):
1. Constant mean: $\mathbb{E}[Y_t] = \mu$ for all $t$.
2. Finite, constant variance: $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ for all $t$.
3. Autocovariance depends only on lag: $\gamma(k) = \text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+k})$ for all $t$ and $k$.

**Importance:** Weak stationarity is a prerequisite for fitting ARMA models, computing valid ACF/PACF, and ensuring OLS estimators have valid standard errors. Non-stationary data leads to spurious regression and unreliable forecasts.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Equivalent]**
*What is the Box-Cox transformation? State its formula and explain how the parameter $\lambda$ is selected.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Box-Cox transformation is a family of power transformations used to stabilise variance and improve normality (1 mark):

$$
Y_t^{(\lambda)} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Y_t^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}, & \lambda \neq 0 \\[6pt] \log(Y_t), & \lambda = 0 \end{cases}
$$

(1 mark for the formula).

The parameter $\lambda$ is selected by **maximum likelihood estimation** to maximise a profile log-likelihood under the assumption that the transformed data are approximately normally distributed (1 mark for the selection method).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test in detail. State the null and alternative hypotheses, the test statistic, and the decision rule. Discuss how the test result guides the choice of differencing order. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the AR(1) process $Y_t = 0.7 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, where $\varepsilon_t \sim \text{WN}(0, 1)$. Examine whether the process is stationary. If $Y_t = 1.2 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ instead, comment on stationarity and explain how differencing restores it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Purpose:** The ADF test is a unit-root test used to determine whether a time series is stationary or possesses a stochastic trend (unit root).

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Hypotheses:**
- Null hypothesis: $H_0: \phi = 1$ (unit root present, **non-stationary**).
- Alternative: $H_1: \vert \phi \vert < 1$ (**stationary**).

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Test Regression (with constant and $p$ augmentation lags):**

$$
\Delta Y_t \;=\; \mu \;+\; \phi\, Y_{t-1} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{p} \theta_i\, \Delta Y_{t-i} \;+\; \varepsilon_t
$$

The lagged differences "augment" the regression to remove residual serial correlation, ensuring that $\varepsilon_t$ is white noise.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Test Statistic:**

$$
\tau_{\text{ADF}} \;=\; \dfrac{\hat{\phi} - 1}{\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat{\phi})}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Decision Rule:** Under $H_0$, the statistic follows the **non-standard Dickey-Fuller distribution** (MacKinnon critical values). Reject $H_0$ if $\tau_{\text{ADF}} < \tau_{\text{crit}}$ (e.g., $-2.89$ at 5% level for the constant-only case). 

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Implication for Differencing:**
- If we fail to reject $H_0$ → series is non-stationary → apply first difference $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ and **re-test**.
- If we still fail to reject → apply second difference $\nabla^2 Y_t$.
- Box-Jenkins rule: use the **minimum order** of differencing that achieves stationarity.

---

#### **Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Case 1 (3 marks):** $Y_t = 0.7 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$.

This is an AR(1) process with coefficient $\phi = 0.7$. Apply the stationarity condition derived in Section 3.3:

$$
\vert \phi \vert = 0.7 < 1
$$

Hence, the process is **stationary** (weakly). Its mean is $\mu = 0/(1-0.7) = 0$ and its variance is $\sigma^2/(1-\phi^2) = 1/(1-0.49) = 1.961$.

**Case 2 (4 marks):** $Y_t = 1.2 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$.

Here $\vert \phi \vert = 1.2 > 1$, so the AR polynomial $1 - 1.2 B$ has a root $z = 1/1.2 = 0.833$ **inside** the unit circle. The process is **explosive** — even worse than non-stationary; shocks grow geometrically. 

**Restoration via differencing:** Even in this explosive case, differencing changes the data-generating process fundamentally. Defining $W_t = \nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$, the original DGP becomes $W_t = 0.2 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, which can be re-parameterised, and one can iterate. More importantly, in the typical **unit-root boundary** case $\phi = 1$ (random walk), the difference yields:

$$
\nabla Y_t = \varepsilon_t \sim \text{white noise (stationary)}
$$

This is the standard pre-processing step before fitting an ARMA model to a unit-root series. (Practically, an explosive series is rare in real data; a random-walk is the common case.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of differencing in making a time series stationary. Explain first differencing, second differencing, and seasonal differencing with their mathematical forms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monthly sales series $\{Y_t\}$ exhibits an upward trend and a strong seasonal cycle with period $s = 12$. The Box-Jenkins team decides to apply the transformation $W_t = (1 - B)(1 - B^{12}) \log Y_t$. Expand this operator and explain, term by term, the role of each component in achieving stationarity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Differencing is the standard Box-Jenkins method of removing trend and seasonality by computing successive differences. It transforms a non-stationary series into a stationary one suitable for ARMA modelling.

**First Differencing (2 marks):**

$$
\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1} = (1 - B) Y_t
$$

- Removes a linear deterministic trend and converts a random walk into white noise.
- Most common in practice.

**Second Differencing (2 marks):**

$$
\nabla^2 Y_t = Y_t - 2 Y_{t-1} + Y_{t-2} = (1 - B)^2 Y_t
$$

- Removes a quadratic trend.
- Used only when first differencing is insufficient.

**Seasonal Differencing (2 marks):**

$$
\nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-s} = (1 - B^s) Y_t
$$

- Removes a seasonal cycle of period $s$ (e.g., $s=4$ for quarterly, $s=12$ for monthly).
- Often combined with non-seasonal differencing: $\nabla \nabla_s Y_t$.

---

#### **Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Expand the operator:**

$$
W_t \;=\; (1 - B)(1 - B^{12}) \log Y_t
$$

First, expand $(1 - B^{12})$:

$$
W_t \;=\; (1 - B)\bigl[\log Y_t - \log Y_{t-12}\bigr]
$$

Now expand $(1 - B)$:

$$
W_t \;=\; \bigl[\log Y_t - \log Y_{t-12}\bigr] - \bigl[\log Y_{t-1} - \log Y_{t-12-1}\bigr]
$$

**Step 2 (5 marks — Term-by-term role):**

- **$\log Y_t$:** The **logarithm** stabilises the variance — appropriate when sales grow multiplicatively over time.
- **$-\log Y_{t-12}$:** The **seasonal lag** at $s = 12$ removes the annual seasonal cycle by subtracting the value from 12 months ago.
- **$-\log Y_{t-1}$:** The **first lag** removes the non-seasonal (trend-related) unit root.
- **$+\log Y_{t-13}$:** The **cross-term** adjusts the seasonal differencing for the previous period, ensuring that the trend-removal is applied consistently to the seasonally-adjusted series.

**Result:** $W_t$ is the **log-return net of both trend and seasonality** — the standard pre-processed input to a SARIMA$(p,1,q)(P,1,Q)_{12}$ model. This is the canonical "airline-passenger" type transformation used in Box-Jenkins seasonal modelling.

---

### 5.X KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (Verified by KTU Valuation Panels):**
> 1. **Confusing deterministic and stochastic trend removal** — Detrending by OLS does **not** fix a unit root. Always check by re-running ADF after detrending.
> 2. **Using the standard Student-$t$ critical value in the ADF test** — the MacKinnon tables give **more negative** values (e.g., $-2.89$ vs. $-1.96$). Wrong table → wrong decision → 0 marks for the test step.
> 3. **Over-differencing** — A common error is to apply $\nabla^2$ when $\nabla$ would suffice. Over-differencing introduces a spurious MA(1) structure and worsens forecasts. Box-Jenkins specifies the *minimum* $d$.
> 4. **Confusing differencing with detrending** in notation — the **backshift operator** $B$ and the **difference operator** $\nabla = 1 - B$ are tools; the operations are distinct from OLS detrending.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the null hypothesis** explicitly in ADF-based questions — examiners consistently deduct 1 mark for an unstated $H_0$.
> 6. **Misapplying Box-Cox to data with zero or negative values** — the transformation is undefined for $Y_t \leq 0$. Add a positive constant or rescale first.

---

### 5.Y Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final-Day Memorisation Sheet):**
> - **Stationarity** = constant mean + constant variance + lag-dependent autocovariance.
> - **Strict stationarity** = invariance of the *entire* joint distribution under time-shift.
> - **Weak stationarity** = invariance of the *first two moments* — what is used in practice.
> - **First difference** $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ removes linear trend and unit roots.
> - **Second difference** removes quadratic trend (use only when $\nabla$ fails).
> - **Seasonal difference** $\nabla_s Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-s}$ removes period-$s$ cycles.
> - **Log transform** stabilises variance when the spread grows with the level.
> - **Box-Cox** generalises the log via a power $\lambda$ chosen by maximum likelihood.
> - **OLS detrending** is for deterministic trends; **differencing** is for stochastic/unit-root trends.
> - **ADF null hypothesis** is $H_0: \phi = 1$ (unit root present).
> - **ADF test statistic** $\tau = (\hat{\phi} - 1)/\widehat{\text{SE}}(\hat{\phi})$ — compared against **MacKinnon** (not Student-$t$) critical values.
> - **AR(1) stationarity condition** is $\vert \phi \vert < 1$; the boundary $\phi = 1$ is the unit-root case.
> - **Backshift operator** $B Y_t = Y_{t-1}$, so $\nabla = 1 - B$.
> - **Box-Jenkins rule:** Use the **minimum** order of differencing $d$ that achieves stationarity.
> - **Visual diagnostic:** ACF of a stationary series **decays quickly**; of a non-stationary series **decays very slowly**.
> - **Random walk** $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ → $\nabla Y_t = \varepsilon_t$ (white noise, hence stationary).
> - **KPSS test** reverses the null of ADF — using both gives robust conclusions.
> - **Common pairing:** ADF says "non-stationary" + KPSS says "non-stationary" → differencing required.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) Test

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) Test** is a formal statistical hypothesis test used in time series analysis to determine the presence of a **unit root** in a univariate time series, thereby testing the **stationarity** of the series. It is an *augmented* extension of the original Dickey-Fuller (DF) test, where the augmentation lies in the inclusion of **lagged difference terms** of the dependent variable to account for higher-order auto-correlation in the error structure.

Formally, the ADF test estimates the following regression:

$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

and tests the null hypothesis $H_0: \gamma = 0$ (presence of a unit root) against the alternative $H_1: \gamma < 0$ (stationarity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus Point:** The ADF statistic does **not** follow the standard t-distribution. It follows the non-standard **Dickey-Fuller distribution**, requiring special tabulated critical values (MacKinnon, 1996). This is a frequently tested conceptual distinction.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Sailing Boat and the Anchor"**
> Imagine a boat (your time series) floating on a sea. You throw an anchor (a shock) into the water. A **stationary series** is like a boat tied firmly to a *shallow anchor* — the shock from the anchor causes a small splash, but the boat quickly returns to its rest position. A **non-stationary series (with unit root)** is like a boat in deep ocean with *no anchor at all* — once a shock hits, the boat drifts off permanently and never returns to its original position. The **ADF test is essentially asking: "Does my boat have an anchor?"** by measuring whether shocks die out (return to mean) or persist forever.

### Standard Metrics & Key Symbols

- **γ (Gamma)** = Coefficient on lagged level $y_{t-1}$ — this is the *focal* coefficient under test.
- **α (Alpha)** = Drift / intercept term (constant).
- **β (Beta)** = Coefficient on deterministic time trend $t$.
- **δ_i (Delta)** = Coefficients on the *augmenting* lagged differenced terms $\Delta y_{t-i}$.
- **p** = Lag order of the augmentation (selected via **AIC** or **BIC**).
- **ε_t** = White noise error term, assumed i.i.d. $N(0, \sigma^2)$.
- **τ (tau)** = The ADF test statistic (analogous to t-statistic).

### The Three Test Regressions (Equation Forms)

| Form | Equation | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| **None** (No constant, no trend) | $\Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | Series fluctuates around zero mean |
| **Drift** (Constant, no trend) | $\Delta y_t = \alpha + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | Series has non-zero mean |
| **Trend** (Constant and trend) | $\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | Series has a deterministic trend |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: From Random Walk to ADF

### Why the Test Exists — The "Why"

A standard AR(1) process: $y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ is **stationary** if and only if $\vert \phi \vert < 1$. The problematic case is $\phi = 1$, which yields the **random walk**: $y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, where shocks are *permanent* and the series has **infinite variance**. Detecting whether $\phi = 1$ is the central problem.

### Construction of the Test Equation (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1: Begin with the AR(1) model**
$$y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2: Subtract $y_{t-1}$ from both sides to introduce differencing**
$$y_t - y_{t-1} = (\phi - 1) y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 3: Define key quantities**
$$\Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$
where $\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$ and $\gamma = \phi - 1$.

**Step 4: Hypothesis Translation**
- $\phi = 1 \iff \gamma = 0$ → **Unit root present** (non-stationary)
- $\phi < 1 \iff \gamma < 0$ → **Stationary**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Asymmetry:** The test is **one-sided (left-tailed)**. We reject $H_0$ only for *very negative* τ-statistics. A *positive* τ-statistic is *never* evidence against the null.

## 2.2 The "Augmentation" — Handling Auto-Correlation

If the error term $\varepsilon_t$ is auto-correlated, the simple DF test is invalid. The **augmentation** adds $p$ lagged difference terms to *soak up* the auto-correlation structure, leaving $\varepsilon_t$ as white noise:

$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \delta_1 \Delta y_{t-1} + \delta_2 \Delta y_{t-2} + \cdots + \delta_p \Delta y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

### Lag Selection (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> - **AIC (Akaike Information Criterion):** Tends to select *larger* lag orders (consistent under finite samples).
> - **BIC / SIC (Schwarz Bayesian Criterion):** More *parsimonious*, penalizes complexity more heavily.
> - **General-to-Specific (GS):** Start with maximum lag, drop insignificant terms sequentially.
> - **Common practice in KTU-style problems:** Use AIC unless sample size is very large.

## 2.3 Decision Rule (Board Critical)

1. Estimate the chosen ADF regression via **OLS**.
2. Compute the τ-statistic = $\hat{\gamma} / SE(\hat{\gamma})$.
3. Compare with **MacKinnon critical values** (not the standard t-table).
4. **Decision:**
   - If $\tau <$ critical value → **Reject $H_0$** → Series is **stationary**.
   - If $\tau \geq$ critical value → **Fail to reject $H_0$** → Series has a **unit root** (non-stationary).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — directly testable in KTU ESE 2024.**

| Component | Mathematical Expression | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **ADF Test Equation (General)** | $\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | The full augmented regression |
| **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)** | $\gamma = 0$ | Unit root exists, non-stationary |
| **Alternative ($H_1$)** | $\gamma < 0$ | Stationary |
| **Test Statistic (τ)** | $\tau = \hat{\gamma} / SE(\hat{\gamma})$ | DF-distributed, not t-distributed |
| **Stationarity Condition** | $\vert \phi \vert < 1$ in $y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | Equivalent to $\gamma < 0$ |
| **Random Walk** | $y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ ($\phi = 1$) | Non-stationary benchmark |
| **Lag Selection** | $AIC = -2 \ln L + 2k$ | Minimize to choose $p$ |
| **Lag Selection** | $BIC = -2 \ln L + k \ln(n)$ | Minimize to choose $p$ |
| **ADF Critical Values (1%, 5%, 10%)** | MacKinnon Tables (e.g., -3.43, -2.86, -2.57 for constant) | Compare with computed τ |
| **Variance of Random Walk** | $Var(y_t) = t \sigma^2$ | Grows linearly with time → non-stationary |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where ADF is used in practice:**
> - **Financial econometrics:** Testing whether stock prices, exchange rates, or commodity prices are stationary (often they are not — *efficient market hypothesis*).
> - **Macroeconomics:** GDP, inflation, unemployment rate modelling.
> - **Signal processing:** Detecting drift in sensor data streams.
> - **Climate science:** Testing trends in temperature/humidity time series.
> - **Forecasting prerequisite:** Box-Jenkins (ARIMA) methodology requires stationarity before modelling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From AR(1) to ADF Test Statistic

### Stage 1: Origin in the AR(1) Process

Consider a simple first-order autoregressive process:

$$y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$$

The characteristic equation is $1 - \phi z = 0$, giving root $z = 1/\phi$. Stationarity requires $\vert z \vert > 1 \iff \vert \phi \vert < 1$.

### Stage 2: Algebraic Reformulation

Subtract $y_{t-1}$ from both sides:

$$y_t - y_{t-1} = \phi y_{t-1} - y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

Group the right-hand side:

$$\Delta y_t = (\phi - 1) y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

Define the **focus parameter**:

$$\gamma \equiv \phi - 1$$

Therefore the equation becomes:

$$\Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

### Stage 3: Translation of Hypotheses

The unit root condition is $\phi = 1$, which translates to $\gamma = 0$.

- **$H_0$ (Unit root):** $\gamma = 0 \iff \phi = 1$ → Random walk → non-stationary.
- **$H_1$ (Stationary):** $\gamma < 0 \iff \phi < 1$ → Stationary AR(1).

> **Conversion logic:** The test moves the critical boundary $\phi = 1$ (a value that is hard to test directly) to $\gamma = 0$ (a value amenable to standard regression output). This is the *defining trick* of the Dickey-Fuller framework.

### Stage 4: Adding the Augmentation

In real data, $\varepsilon_t$ rarely satisfies the white noise assumption. To address auto-correlation, the AR(1) error is replaced with an AR(p) structure:

$$\varepsilon_t = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + u_t, \quad u_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$$

Substituting back yields the **Augmented** Dickey-Fuller equation:

$$\Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + u_t$$

### Stage 5: Adding Deterministic Components

For flexibility, intercept and trend may be added:

$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + u_t$$

### Stage 6: Computing the Test Statistic

Run **OLS** estimation. The standard regression output yields:

$$\hat{\gamma}, \quad SE(\hat{\gamma})$$

Construct the τ-statistic:

$$\tau_{ADF} = \frac{\hat{\gamma}}{SE(\hat{\gamma})}$$

### Stage 7: Decision via Critical Value Comparison

Compare $\tau_{ADF}$ with critical values from **MacKinnon (1996) tables** for the chosen regression form (None / Drift / Trend). For example, for a *Drift* model at 5% significance, the critical value is approximately **-2.86**.

$$\text{If } \tau_{ADF} < \tau_{crit} \Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0 \Rightarrow \text{Stationary}$$

$$\text{If } \tau_{ADF} \geq \tau_{crit} \Rightarrow \text{Fail to reject } H_0 \Rightarrow \text{Unit root}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical Illustration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-Style):** Suppose for a series $y_t$, the ADF regression with constant and 2 lags yields $\hat{\gamma} = -0.42$ and $SE(\hat{\gamma}) = 0.15$. The MacKinnon 5% critical value for the drift model is $-2.86$. Test for stationarity at 5% level.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: State the hypotheses**

$$H_0: \gamma = 0 \quad \text{(unit root, non-stationary)}$$

$$H_1: \gamma < 0 \quad \text{(stationary)}$$

**Step 2: Compute the test statistic**

$$\tau_{ADF} = \frac{\hat{\gamma}}{SE(\hat{\gamma})} = \frac{-0.42}{0.15} = -2.80$$

**Step 3: Identify the critical value**

At the 5% significance level, for the drift (constant only) model, MacKinnon's critical value is $\tau_{0.05} = -2.86$.

**Step 4: Apply the decision rule**

$$\tau_{ADF} = -2.80 \quad \text{vs} \quad \tau_{crit} = -2.86$$

Since $-2.80 > -2.86$, the test statistic is *not* sufficiently negative. Therefore:

$$\text{Fail to reject } H_0$$

**Step 5: Conclusion**

The series $y_t$ is **non-stationary** at the 5% level — it contains a unit root.

**Step 6: Engineering Interpretation**

Differencing is required (e.g., model $\Delta y_t$ instead of $y_t$) before applying ARMA/ARIMA modelling.

> **Conversion logic recap:** The *sign* of the test statistic matters. Since the alternative is one-sided ($\gamma < 0$), only *very negative* τ values constitute evidence against $H_0$. The student must visualize the number line: values further left (more negative) push us towards rejection.

## 3.3 Summary Matrix: When to Use What Form

| Visual Pattern of Series | Recommended ADF Form | Rationale |
|--------------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Series crosses zero mean, no drift | **None** | No deterministic terms needed |
| Series has non-zero constant mean | **Drift** ($\alpha$ only) | Captures level shift |
| Series trends upward/downward | **Trend** ($\alpha + \beta t$) | Captures deterministic trend |
| Series is highly auto-correlated | Increase lag $p$ | Augment with more $\Delta y_{t-i}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Complete ADF Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Time Series y_t] --> B[Plot Series Visually]
    B --> C{Identify Pattern}
    C -->|Mean Zero| D[Choose None Model]
    C -->|Non-zero Mean| E[Choose Drift Model]
    C -->|Upward Downward Trend| F[Choose Trend Model]
    D --> G[Select Lag p via AIC or BIC]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Estimate ADF Equation by OLS]
    H --> I[Extract gamma_hat and SE gamma_hat]
    I --> J[Compute tau = gamma_hat / SE]
    J --> K[Lookup MacKinnon Critical Value tau_crit]
    K --> L{tau less than tau_crit}
    L -->|Yes| M[Reject H0: Series is Stationary]
    L -->|No| N[Fail to Reject H0: Unit Root Present]
    M --> O[Proceed to ARMA Modelling]
    N --> P[Difference the Series and Re-test]
    P --> Q{Stationary Now}
    Q -->|Yes| O
    Q -->|No| R[Apply Second Differencing]
    R --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: ADF Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lagged Level y of t-1] --> B[ADF Regression Engine]
    C[Constant alpha] --> B
    D[Trend beta times t] --> B
    E[Lagged Differences delta_i times delta y of t-i] --> B
    B --> F[OLS Estimator]
    F --> G[gamma_hat and SE gamma_hat]
    G --> H[Tau Statistic Calculator]
    H --> I[MacKinnon Critical Value Comparator]
    I --> J{Decision Output}
    J -->|Reject H0| K[Stationary Series]
    J -->|Fail to Reject| L[Non Stationary Series]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Hypothesis Testing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Null Hypothesis: gamma equals 0] --> B[Compute ADF tau Statistic]
    B --> C{Compare with Critical Value}
    C -->|tau very negative| D[Reject H0]
    C -->|tau not negative enough| E[Fail to Reject H0]
    D --> F[Conclude: Series is Stationary]
    E --> G[Conclude: Unit Root Exists]
    G --> H[Action: Apply Differencing]
    H --> I[Re-test Differenced Series]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024]`

**Q:** Define the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test. State the null and alternative hypotheses clearly.

**Model Answer:**

The Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test is a statistical test used to check the stationarity of a time series by detecting the presence of a unit root. It augments the basic Dickey-Fuller equation with lagged difference terms $\Delta y_{t-i}$ to account for auto-correlation.

**Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \gamma = 0 \quad \text{(unit root present, non-stationary)}$$

$$H_1: \gamma < 0 \quad \text{(series is stationary)}$$

The test statistic $\tau = \hat{\gamma} / SE(\hat{\gamma})$ is compared with MacKinnon's tabulated critical values (not the standard t-distribution).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024]`

**Q:** Why is the augmentation needed in the ADF test? How is the lag order $p$ selected?

**Model Answer:**

The **augmentation** is needed because the original Dickey-Fuller test assumes the error term $\varepsilon_t$ is white noise. In real data, errors are often auto-correlated, which invalidates the test. By including $p$ lagged difference terms $\sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i}$, the augmentation absorbs the auto-correlation, leaving a white-noise residual.

**Lag selection methods:**
- **AIC (Akaike Information Criterion):** $AIC = -2 \ln L + 2k$ — minimize.
- **BIC (Schwarz Information Criterion):** $BIC = -2 \ln L + k \ln n$ — minimize.
- **General-to-Specific:** Start high, drop insignificant lags.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **one full question** (either Option A or Option B).

### Option A — Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test equation starting from the AR(1) process. Clearly state the null and alternative hypotheses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A time series $y_t$ is tested using the ADF regression with intercept and 2 lags, yielding $\hat{\gamma} = -0.55$ and $SE(\hat{\gamma}) = 0.18$. The MacKinnon 5% critical value for the drift model is $-2.86$. Test for stationarity at the 5% level and provide an engineering interpretation.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Starting point:** AR(1) process
$$y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 1:** Subtract $y_{t-1}$ from both sides
$$y_t - y_{t-1} = \phi y_{t-1} - y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2:** Define differencing
$$\Delta y_t = (\phi - 1) y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 3:** Let $\gamma = \phi - 1$
$$\Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 4:** Augment with $p$ lagged differences (to handle auto-correlation)
$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 5:** Hypotheses

$$H_0: \gamma = 0 \quad \text{(unit root, non-stationary)}$$

$$H_1: \gamma < 0 \quad \text{(stationary)}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Compute the test statistic

$$\tau = \frac{\hat{\gamma}}{SE(\hat{\gamma})} = \frac{-0.55}{0.18} \approx -3.06$$

**Step 2:** Critical value at 5% (drift model)

$$\tau_{0.05} = -2.86$$

**Step 3:** Decision

$$\tau = -3.06 < -2.86 = \tau_{crit}$$

Since the test statistic is *more negative* than the critical value, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Step 4:** Conclusion

The series $y_t$ is **stationary** at the 5% significance level.

**Step 5:** Engineering interpretation

The series can be modelled directly using ARMA(p, q) without differencing. There is no need for an ARIMA formulation.

---

### Option B — Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three regression forms of the ADF test. How does the choice of form depend on the visual pattern of the data?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of the MacKinnon critical values in the ADF test. Why can't the standard t-distribution be used?

#### Model Solution to (a):

**The three ADF regression forms:**

**Form 1: No constant, no trend (None)**
$$\Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

Used when the series oscillates around a zero mean — pure random fluctuations without a drift or trend.

**Form 2: Constant (Drift)**
$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

Used when the series has a non-zero mean but no deterministic trend.

**Form 3: Constant and trend (Trend)**
$$\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$$

Used when the series exhibits a clear upward or downward trend over time.

**Selection logic (visual pattern → form):**

- Series crossing zero mean with no drift → **None**
- Series with constant non-zero mean → **Drift**
- Series with monotonic trend → **Trend**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**MacKinnon critical values:**

The ADF test statistic $\tau = \hat{\gamma}/SE(\hat{\gamma})$ does **not** follow a standard Student-t distribution. The presence of the *stochastic regressor* $y_{t-1}$ (which is non-stationary under the null) causes the limiting distribution to be a non-standard one, known as the **Dickey-Fuller distribution**.

MacKinnon (1996) computed critical values for this distribution via simulation, tabulating them for the three regression forms at standard significance levels (1%, 5%, 10%).

**Why not t-distribution?**

Under $H_0$, $y_{t-1}$ is non-stationary ($I(1)$), violating the classical OLS assumption of stationary regressors. The standard t-test theory breaks down, and the limiting distribution of $\tau$ is *non-standard*, requiring MacKinnon's special tables. Using the standard t-table would lead to incorrect inference — over-rejecting $H_0$ and falsely concluding stationarity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Using standard t-table critical values** instead of MacKinnon values. This is a *guaranteed* 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Two-sided testing:** The ADF test is *one-sided* ($\gamma < 0$). Do not reject $H_0$ for large *positive* τ values.
> 3. **Forgetting the augmentation** — describing only the basic Dickey-Fuller without lagged difference terms.
> 4. **Confusing DF and ADF:** The basic DF test is invalid under auto-correlated errors; only ADF handles this.
> 5. **Skipping the differencing step** after failing to reject $H_0$ — the practical follow-up action must be mentioned.
> 6. **Mistaking AIC/BIC as hypothesis tests** — they are model selection criteria, not inference tools.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ADF Test**

- **ADF = Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test** — a unit root test for stationarity.
- **Equation:** $\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$.
- **Null hypothesis $H_0: \gamma = 0$** → unit root present → non-stationary.
- **Alternative $H_1: \gamma < 0$** → series is stationary.
- **Test statistic:** $\tau = \hat{\gamma}/SE(\hat{\gamma})$ — *one-sided* (left-tailed).
- **Critical values come from MacKinnon tables**, NOT the standard t-table.
- **Three regression forms:** None, Drift, Trend — choose based on the visual pattern.
- **Augmentation terms:** $\sum \delta_i \Delta y_{t-i}$ handle auto-correlation in errors.
- **Lag selection:** Use **AIC** (general preference) or **BIC** (more parsimonious).
- **Decision rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\tau < \tau_{crit}$ (i.e., τ is sufficiently *negative*).
- **Practical action if non-stationary:** Apply differencing and re-test.
- **Limitation:** Low power against near-unit-root alternatives and trend-stationary alternatives.
- **Origin:** Built from AR(1) process; unit root corresponds to $\phi = 1$ in $y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$.
- **Engineering use:** Pre-requisite step before fitting ARIMA models in Box-Jenkins methodology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Stationary Data Techniques: Differencing, Random Walk, Trend Differencing, Seasonal Differencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Using Stationary Data Techniques: Differencing, Random Walk, Trend Differencing, Seasonal Differencing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Stationarity – The Prerequisite for ARMA Modelling In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, almost all classical time series models (AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA) are built upon the foundational assumption that the underlying series is **weakly stationary** (or covariance stationary). A series $\{Y_t\}$ is weakly stationary if: - Its mean is constant over time: $E[Y_t] = \mu$ for all $t$ - Its variance is finite and constant: $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ - Its autocovariance depends only on the lag $k$, not on time $t$: $\text{Cov}(Y_t, Y_{t+k}) = \gamma_k$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** If raw economic, climatic, or signal data is **non-stationary** (e.g., trending GDP, rising temperatures, growing sales), we **cannot** directly fit ARMA models. We must first *transform* the series into a stationary one. The four canonical techniques are **Differencing, Random Walk identification, Trend Differencing, and Seasonal Differencing**. ## 1.2 The Family of Differencing Techniques – An Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Stair-Step" Camera Trick** > Imagine you are filming a person walking on a moving walkway in an airport. The person appears to move forward (the camera captures a *trending* path), but their *step-to-step displacement* is the only thing that matters for analyzing their gait. > In time series, the **level** of the series (the absolute value) is the moving walkway. The **difference** (step-to-step change) is the actual signal we want to model. Differencing strips away the "moving walkway" and leaves us the pure random shocks. The four techniques discussed in this module are essentially *lenses* of the same camera trick: | Technique | What it Removes | Best Used For | |---|---|---| | **First Differencing** | Linear trend / Random walk drift | Series with constant upward/downward slope | | **Second/Order-$d$ Differencing** | Quadratic (or higher) trends | Series that still trends after first differencing | | **Random Walk Modelling** | Stochastic trend (cumulative shocks) | Financial prices, FX rates, Brownian motion | | **Seasonal Differencing** | Periodic (seasonal) pattern | Monthly/quarterly data with yearly cycles | ## 1.3 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 1 – First Difference Operator (∇)** > The first difference of a series is defined as: > $$Y'_t = \nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$$ > where $t = 2, 3, \dots, n$ and $\nabla$ is the **backward difference operator**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 2 – Random Walk Process** > A series $\{Y_t\}$ is a **random walk** if it satisfies: > $$Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$ > where $\{\varepsilon_t\}$ is a **white noise** process with mean $0$ and variance $\sigma^2$. The random walk is said to be *with drift* if: > $$Y_t = c + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 3 – Seasonal Difference Operator ($\nabla_m$)** > For a series with period $m$, the seasonal difference is: > $$\nabla_m Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-m}$$ > For example, $m=12$ for monthly data showing yearly seasonality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 4 – $d$-th Order Difference** > Repeated application of $\nabla$: > $$\nabla^d Y_t = \nabla(\nabla^{d-1} Y_t)$$ > In practice, **98% of real-world series** require at most $d = 2$ differencing. --- > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility** > - **Civil Engineering:** Removing long-term drift from structural vibration sensors before modal analysis. > - **Electrical Engineering:** Removing DC offset from noisy AC signals to obtain stationary residuals. > - **Financial Engineering:** Converting non-stationary stock prices into stationary returns for volatility (ARCH/GARCH) modelling. > - **Climatology:** Eliminating the global warming trend from temperature anomalies to study El Niño cycles. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Differencing Works – The Theoretical Mechanism A non-stationary series with a deterministic trend can be expressed as: $$Y_t = f(t) + X_t$$ where $f(t)$ is a deterministic function (e.g., $a + bt$) and $X_t$ is a stationary process. When we apply the difference operator: $$\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1} = [f(t) - f(t-1)] + [X_t - X_{t-1}]$$ - If $f(t) = a + bt$ (linear), then $f(t) - f(t-1) = b$ (a constant, easily absorbed). - If $f(t) = a + bt + ct^2$ (quadratic), then $f(t) - f(t-1) = b + 2ct - c$, which is **still trending**! Hence we need a **second difference** $\nabla^2$. ## 2.2 The Random Walk – A Deeper Look The random walk $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ has a remarkable property: its variance **grows linearly with time**. $$\text{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma^2$$ This unbounded variance is the smoking-gun signature of non-stationarity. By taking the first difference: $$\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1} = \varepsilon_t$$ we recover **pure white noise** – the most stationary process in existence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Random Walk vs. Stationary Series – The KTU Diagnostic Test** > - Plot the series: a random walk wanders indefinitely; a stationary series oscillates around a fixed mean. > - Examine the **ACF plot**: a random walk has an ACF that *decays extremely slowly* (almost linearly from 1 toward 0); a stationary series has ACF that drops to zero quickly. > - Apply the **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test**: Null hypothesis $H_0$ is *unit root present* (i.e., non-stationary). If ADF $p$-value $> 0.05$, the series is a random walk. ## 2.3 Trend Differencing vs. Seasonal Differencing – The Critical Distinction | Feature | Trend Differencing ($\nabla^d Y_t$) | Seasonal Differencing ($\nabla_m Y_t$) | |---|---|---| | **Lag used** | Consecutive observations ($Y_{t-1}, Y_{t-2}$) | Same season in previous cycle ($Y_{t-m}$) | | **Removes** | Polynomial trend (linear, quadratic) | Periodic seasonal pattern | | **When to apply** | Series trends monotonically | Series has repeating peaks/troughs every $m$ periods | | **Risk of over-differencing** | Introduces artificial negative MA(1) pattern | Introduces seasonal MA pattern at lag $m$ | > [!WARNING] > **The KTU Examiner's Trap:** Never apply both trend and seasonal differencing blindly. Apply **seasonal differencing first** if strong seasonality is present, then apply additional regular differencing only if a trend remains. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **📋 KTU 2024 Cheat Sheet – Differencing & Stationarity** | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | First Difference | $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ | Removes linear trend / random walk drift | | 2 | Second Difference | $\nabla^2 Y_t = \nabla Y_t - \nabla Y_{t-1}$ | Removes quadratic trend | | 3 | $d$-th Order Difference | $\nabla^d Y_t = \nabla(\nabla^{d-1}Y_t)$ | Removes polynomial trend of degree $d$ | | 4 | Random Walk (no drift) | $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | Cumulative sum of white noise shocks | | 5 | Random Walk with Drift $c$ | $Y_t = c + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ | Cumulative sum of shocks + linear growth | | 6 | Seasonal Difference | $\nabla_m Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-m}$ | Removes periodicity of order $m$ | | 7 | Lag Operator Equivalent | $\nabla Y_t = (1 - L)Y_t$ where $L^k Y_t = Y_{t-k}$ | Polynomial form used in Box–Jenkins | | 8 | Random Walk Variance | $\text{Var}(Y_t) = t\sigma^2$ | Proves non-stationarity (variance $\to \infty$) | | 9 | ADF Test Statistic | $\tau = \dfrac{\hat{\rho}}{\text{SE}(\hat{\rho})}$ from regression $\nabla Y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \rho Y_{t-1} + \sum \delta_i \nabla Y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | $\tau < $ critical value $\Rightarrow$ reject unit root | | 10 | Over-differencing Signature | Negative spike in ACF at lag 1 (regular) or at lag $m$ (seasonal) | Indicates need to *reduce* order of differencing | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Decision Tree: Which Differencing to Apply? **Step 1** → Plot the series and the ACF. - ACF decays slowly ⇒ likely unit root ⇒ apply **first difference**. - ACF has a large spike at lag $m$ ⇒ seasonality ⇒ apply **seasonal difference**. **Step 2** → Re-plot and re-examine ACF. - ACF now decays quickly (cuts off) ⇒ **stop**. Series is stationary. - ACF still has positive autocorrelations for many lags ⇒ apply **second difference** or seasonal difference. **Step 3** → Check for over-differencing. - First ACF value is *very negative* (close to $-0.5$) ⇒ you have over-differenced ⇒ go back one order. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility Recap** > - In **signal processing**, $\nabla$ is analogous to a *high-pass filter* (blocks low-frequency trends, passes high-frequency shocks). > - In **control systems**, $\nabla$ is the discrete-time equivalent of the derivative operator $d/dt$, which naturally removes DC offsets. > - In **econometrics**, this framework underpins the **ARIMA($p,d,q$)** model where $d$ is the order of differencing. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: First Difference Removes a Random Walk Drift **Given:** $Y_t = c + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$ and $c$ is the drift constant. **Step 1** – Apply the definition of first difference: $$\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$$ **Step 2** – Substitute the random walk equation: $$\nabla Y_t = (c + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t) - Y_{t-1}$$ **Step 3** – Cancel $Y_{t-1}$ on both sides: $$\nabla Y_t = c + \varepsilon_t$$ **Step 4** – Verify stationarity: - Mean: $E[\nabla Y_t] = c$ (constant ✓) - Variance: $\text{Var}(\nabla Y_t) = \sigma^2$ (constant ✓) - Autocovariance: $\text{Cov}(\nabla Y_t, \nabla Y_{t+k}) = 0$ for $k \geq 1$ ✓ **Conclusion:** After one difference, the random walk with drift becomes a **white noise with mean $c$**, which is strictly stationary. ## 3.2 Derivation: Why Variance of a Random Walk Grows with Time **Given:** $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $Y_0 = 0$ and $\varepsilon_t$ i.i.d. with mean 0 and variance $\sigma^2$. **Step 1** – Expand recursively: $$Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t = Y_{t-2} + \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t = \dots = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i$$ **Step 2** – Compute the variance (using independence of $\varepsilon_i$): $$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \text{Var}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i\right)$$ **Step 3** – Apply the additive variance rule for independent variables: $$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \sigma^2 = t\sigma^2$$ **Conclusion:** $\text{Var}(Y_t) \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$, confirming the series is **non-stationary**. The longer you observe, the larger the dispersion. ## 3.3 Derivation: Second Difference Removes a Quadratic Trend **Given:** $Y_t = a + bt + ct^2 + X_t$ where $X_t$ is stationary. **Step 1** – Apply first difference: $$\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1} = [a + bt + ct^2 + X_t] - [a + b(t-1) + c(t-1)^2 + X_{t-1}]$$ **Step 2** – Simplify the deterministic part: $$bt - b(t-1) = b$$ $$ct^2 - c(t-1)^2 = c(t^2 - (t^2 - 2t + 1)) = 2ct - c$$ **Step 3** – Combine: $$\nabla Y_t = (b - c) + 2ct + (X_t - X_{t-1})$$ **Step 4** – Apply second difference to remove the remaining $2ct$ term: $$\nabla^2 Y_t = \nabla Y_t - \nabla Y_{t-1} = 2c + (X_t - 2X_{t-1} + X_{t-2})$$ **Step 5** – Final result: $$\nabla^2 Y_t = 2c + \nabla^2 X_t$$ The deterministic part is now the constant $2c$, and $\nabla^2 X_t$ is stationary (difference of a stationary process). ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: First-Differencing a Quarterly Sales Series **Given Data** (Quarterly Sales in ₹ Lakhs): $$\{Y_t\} = \{100, 112, 125, 118, 130, 145, 140, 155, 168\}$$ **Step 1** – Compute the first difference series: $$\begin{aligned} \nabla Y_2 &= 112 - 100 = 12 \\ \nabla Y_3 &= 125 - 112 = 13 \\ \nabla Y_4 &= 118 - 125 = -7 \\ \nabla Y_5 &= 130 - 118 = 12 \\ \nabla Y_6 &= 145 - 130 = 15 \\ \nabla Y_7 &= 140 - 145 = -5 \\ \nabla Y_8 &= 155 - 140 = 15 \\ \nabla Y_9 &= 168 - 155 = 13 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2** – Calculate the mean of the differenced series: $$\overline{\nabla Y} = \frac{12 + 13 - 7 + 12 + 15 - 5 + 15 + 13}{8} = \frac{68}{8} = 8.5$$ **Step 3** – Calculate the variance of the differenced series: $$\sigma^2_{\nabla Y} = \frac{1}{7}\sum_{i=2}^{9}(\nabla Y_i - 8.5)^2$$ $$= \frac{(3.5)^2 + (4.5)^2 + (-15.5)^2 + (3.5)^2 + (6.5)^2 + (-13.5)^2 + (6.5)^2 + (4.5)^2}{7}$$ $$= \frac{12.25 + 20.25 + 240.25 + 12.25 + 42.25 + 182.25 + 42.25 + 20.25}{7} = \frac{572}{7} \approx 81.71$$ **Step 4** – Compute the lag-1 autocorrelation of the differenced series: $$r_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=3}^{9}(\nabla Y_i - 8.5)(\nabla Y_{i-1} - 8.5)}{\sum_{i=2}^{9}(\nabla Y_i - 8.5)^2}$$ | $i$ | $\nabla Y_i$ | $\nabla Y_i - 8.5$ | $\nabla Y_{i-1} - 8.5$ | Product | |---|---|---|---|---| | 3 | 13 | 4.5 | 3.5 | 15.75 | | 4 | -7 | -15.5 | 4.5 | -69.75 | | 5 | 12 | 3.5 | -15.5 | -54.25 | | 6 | 15 | 6.5 | 3.5 | 22.75 | | 7 | -5 | -13.5 | 6.5 | -87.75 | | 8 | 15 | 6.5 | -13.5 | -87.75 | | 9 | 13 | 4.5 | 6.5 | 29.25 | Sum of products $= 15.75 - 69.75 - 54.25 + 22.75 - 87.75 - 87.75 + 29.25 = -231.75$ $$r_1 = \frac{-231.75}{572} \approx -0.405$$ **Step 5** – Interpretation: - $r_1 \approx -0.405$ is significantly negative, indicating possible **over-differencing** or a strong mean-reverting pattern. - The mean of $\nabla Y$ is **8.5** (constant), confirming the differenced series is stationary. ## 3.5 Worked Example: Seasonal Differencing of Monthly Data with $m = 12$ **Given:** Monthly airline passenger data $\{Y_t\}$ from Jan 1949 to Dec 1960 (144 observations). A clear yearly cycle is observed. **Step 1** – Apply seasonal difference: $$\nabla_{12} Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-12}, \quad t = 13, 14, \dots, 144$$ **Step 2** – Effect: The "January 1950 − January 1949" comparison removes the seasonal surge, leaving the **year-over-year growth** – which is approximately stationary. **Step 3** – Diagnostic: Plot the ACF of $\nabla_{12} Y_t$. - If the ACF at lags 12, 24, 36… disappears, seasonal differencing was sufficient. - If a long-memory trend remains, an additional first difference $\nabla \nabla_{12} Y_t$ is applied (this is the famous *airline model* $\text{ARIMA}(0,1,1)(0,1,1)_{12}$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In 1976, Box and Jenkins famously used $\nabla \nabla_{12}$ on this airline data and obtained a near-perfect model. This is a *must-know* classic example for KTU exams. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Differencing Workflow Flowchart The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision-making process a KTU student must follow when applying differencing techniques to a non-stationary series. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Raw Time Series Y_t"] --> B{"Plot Series and ACF"} B -- "Slow ACF decay
No clear cycle" --> C["Apply First Difference
nabla Y_t = Y_t minus Y_t-1"] B -- "Large ACF spike
at lag m" --> D["Apply Seasonal Difference
nabla m Y_t = Y_t minus Y_t-m"] B -- "Both slow decay
and lag-m spike" --> E["Apply Seasonal First
Then Trend Difference"] C --> F{"Re-examine ACF"} D --> F E --> F F -- "ACF cuts off quickly
No lag-m spike" --> G["Stationary Series Achieved
Proceed to ARMA Fitting"] F -- "ACF still decays slowly" --> H["Apply Second Difference
nabla squared Y_t"] F -- "First ACF value is very
negative near minus 0.5" --> I["Over-differenced
Reduce Order by 1"] H --> J{"Re-examine ACF Again"} J -- "ACF cuts off" --> G J -- "Still trending" --> K["Re-evaluate Model
Consider Log Transform"] G --> L["Fit ARIMA p,d,q or SARIMA
Model and Validate"] I --> L K --> L L --> M["End: Validated
Stationary Model"] ``` ## 4.2 Random Walk vs. Stationary Series – Visual Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "RANDOM WALK" RW1["Non-stationary"] RW2["Variance grows with t"] RW3["ACF decays very slowly"] RW4["Y_t = Y_t-1 + epsilon_t"] RW5["First difference yields white noise"] end subgraph "STATIONARY SERIES" SS1["Weakly stationary"] SS2["Variance is finite and constant"] SS3["ACF cuts off quickly"] SS4["Y_t = mu + X_t"] SS5["Differencing causes over-differencing"] end RW1 --- RW2 RW2 --- RW3 RW3 --- RW4 RW4 --- RW5 SS1 --- SS2 SS2 --- SS3 SS3 --- SS4 SS4 --- SS5 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The ARIMA Pre-Processing Pipeline This block diagram depicts how the four stationarity techniques integrate into the broader Box–Jenkins modelling pipeline used in KTU Module 2. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph "Input Stage" IN["Raw Non-Stationary Series Y_t"] end subgraph "Transformation Stage" T1["Trend Differencing Module
nabla to the power d"] T2["Seasonal Differencing Module
nabla to the power D with lag m"] T3["Log or Power Transform Module
Stabilizes Variance"] T4["Random Walk Identification
ADF Test Module"] end subgraph "Diagnostic Stage" D1["ACF Plot Analyzer"] D2["PACF Plot Analyzer"] D3["Unit Root Test ADF
p value less than 0.05"] end subgraph "Output Stage" OUT1["Stationary Series W_t"] OUT2["Determined Orders d, D, m"] end IN --> T4 T4 -- "Unit root confirmed" --> T1 T4 -- "Seasonal unit root" --> T2 IN -- "Variance instability" --> T3 T1 --> D1 T2 --> D1 T3 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 -- "Stationary" --> OUT1 D3 --> OUT2 D3 -- "Non-stationary" --> T1 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Effect of Differencing Operators on a Trending Sinusoid ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Y_t equals Linear Trend plus
Annual Seasonality plus Noise"] --> B["Apply First Difference nabla"] B --> C["Removes Linear Trend
Seasonality Remains Intact"] C --> D["Apply Seasonal Difference nabla 12"] D --> E["Removes Seasonality
Residual is Pure Noise"] E --> F["Stationary Output
Ready for ARMA"] A2["Y_t equals Quadratic plus
Quarterly Seasonality plus Noise"] --> B2["Apply First Difference nabla"] B2 --> C2["Linear Trend Remains
Seasonality Remains"] C2 --> D2["Apply Second Difference nabla squared"] D2 --> E2["Removes Quadratic Trend"] E2 --> F2["Apply Seasonal Difference nabla 4"] F2 --> G2["Final Stationary Series"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** > **Define a random walk process. Show that a random walk is non-stationary by computing its variance.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** A random walk is a non-stationary time series process defined as: $$Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$ where $\{\varepsilon_t\}$ is a white noise process with mean 0 and variance $\sigma^2$, and $Y_0$ is the initial value. **Variance Proof (2 Marks):** By recursive expansion: $$Y_t = Y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i$$ $$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \text{Var}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{t} \varepsilon_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} \text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = t\sigma^2$$ Since $\text{Var}(Y_t)$ depends on $t$ and grows without bound as $t \to \infty$, the series violates the weak stationarity condition (constant variance). **Hence, a random walk is non-stationary.** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > **What is seasonal differencing? With a suitable example, explain how it removes the seasonal component from a time series.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Seasonal differencing is the operation of computing the difference between an observation and the corresponding observation from the previous seasonal cycle. For a series with period $m$: $$\nabla_m Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-m}$$ **Example and Explanation (2 Marks):** Consider monthly retail sales data ($m = 12$) showing a strong December peak every year. Computing $\nabla_{12} Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-12}$ compares each month's value with the same month from the previous year. This subtraction cancels the recurring December peak (and every other month-specific seasonal effect), leaving the year-over-year *growth* signal, which is typically stationary. After seasonal differencing, the ACF spikes at lags 12, 24, etc., disappear, confirming removal of the seasonal pattern. --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice Format > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Regulation Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer ONE full question (both sub-parts). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** > **(a)** Explain the concept of *first-order and second-order differencing* in time series. Derive the algebraic form of a first-differenced series when applied to (i) a series with a linear trend, and (ii) a series with a quadratic trend. **[(7 Marks)]** > **(b)** Consider the following time series representing the monthly sales (in units) of a product: > $$\{Y_t\} = \{50, 53, 51, 55, 60, 58, 63, 65, 68, 70, 72, 75\}$$ > Compute the first-difference series, its mean, variance, and lag-1 autocorrelation coefficient. State whether the differenced series appears stationary. **[(7 Marks)]** #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) – 7 Marks:** The backward difference operator $\nabla$ is defined as $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$, and the $d$-th order difference is $\nabla^d Y_t = \nabla(\nabla^{d-1}Y_t)$. **Case (i): Linear Trend (3 Marks)** Let $Y_t = a + bt + X_t$, where $X_t$ is stationary. $$\nabla Y_t = (a + bt + X_t) - (a + b(t-1) + X_{t-1}) = b + (X_t - X_{t-1})$$ The deterministic part collapses to the constant $b$, and the stochastic part is the difference of a stationary process, which is also stationary. Hence, **first differencing suffices** for a linear trend. **Case (ii): Quadratic Trend (4 Marks)** Let $Y_t = a + bt + ct^2 + X_t$. $$\nabla Y_t = (a + bt + ct^2 + X_t) - (a + b(t-1) + c(t-1)^2 + X_{t-1})$$ $$= b + c(2t - 1) + (X_t - X_{t-1})$$ The term $2ct$ is still time-dependent. Applying a second difference: $$\nabla^2 Y_t = \nabla Y_t - \nabla Y_{t-1} = 2c + (X_t - 2X_{t-1} + X_{t-2})$$ Now the deterministic component is the constant $2c$, confirming that **second differencing** is required to remove a quadratic trend. --- **Part (b) – 7 Marks:** **Step 1: First-Difference Computation (2 Marks)** $$\begin{aligned} \nabla Y_2 &= 53 - 50 = 3 \\ \nabla Y_3 &= 51 - 53 = -2 \\ \nabla Y_4 &= 55 - 51 = 4 \\ \nabla Y_5 &= 60 - 55 = 5 \\ \nabla Y_6 &= 58 - 60 = -2 \\ \nabla Y_7 &= 63 - 58 = 5 \\ \nabla Y_8 &= 65 - 63 = 2 \\ \nabla Y_9 &= 68 - 65 = 3 \\ \nabla Y_{10} &= 70 - 68 = 2 \\ \nabla Y_{11} &= 72 - 70 = 2 \\ \nabla Y_{12} &= 75 - 72 = 3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Mean and Variance (2 Marks)** $$\overline{\nabla Y} = \frac{3 - 2 + 4 + 5 - 2 + 5 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 2 + 3}{11} = \frac{25}{11} \approx 2.273$$ $$\sigma^2_{\nabla Y} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=2}^{12}(\nabla Y_i - 2.273)^2$$ $$= \frac{(0.727)^2 + (-4.273)^2 + (1.727)^2 + (2.727)^2 + (-4.273)^2 + (2.727)^2 + (-0.273)^2 + (0.727)^2 + (-0.273)^2 + (-0.273)^2 + (0.727)^2}{10}$$ $$\approx \frac{0.529 + 18.259 + 2.983 + 7.437 + 18.259 + 7.437 + 0.075 + 0.529 + 0.075 + 0.075 + 0.529}{10} \approx \frac{56.19}{10} \approx 5.619$$ **Step 3: Lag-1 Autocorrelation (2 Marks)** | $i$ | $\nabla Y_i$ | $e_i = \nabla Y_i - 2.273$ | $e_{i-1}$ | $e_i \cdot e_{i-1}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 3 | -2 | -4.273 | 0.727 | -3.107 | | 4 | 4 | 1.727 | -4.273 | -7.380 | | 5 | 5 | 2.727 | 1.727 | 4.710 | | 6 | -2 | -4.273 | 2.727 | -11.652 | | 7 | 5 | 2.727 | -4.273 | -11.652 | | 8 | 2 | -0.273 | 2.727 | -0.745 | | 9 | 3 | 0.727 | -0.273 | -0.198 | | 10 | 2 | -0.273 | 0.727 | -0.198 | | 11 | 2 | -0.273 | -0.273 | 0.075 | | 12 | 3 | 0.727 | -0.273 | -0.198 | Sum of products $\approx -30.345$ $$r_1 = \frac{-30.345}{56.19} \approx -0.540$$ **Step 4: Stationarity Conclusion (1 Mark)** The mean and variance of the differenced series are approximately constant. However, $r_1 \approx -0.540$ is strongly negative, indicating either an MA(1) signature in the differenced series or mild over-differencing. The series is **weakly stationary** with a likely MA(1) structure suitable for ARIMA$(0,1,1)$ modelling. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Differentiate between *trend differencing* and *seasonal differencing*. Under what conditions would you apply each? Describe the phenomenon of *over-differencing* and its signature in the ACF plot. **[(7 Marks)]** > **(b)** A researcher is modelling the quarterly revenue (in ₹ Crores) of a company. The data shows both an upward trend and a strong quarterly seasonal pattern with period $m = 4$. The observed series for 8 quarters is: > $$\{Y_t\} = \{10, 15, 12, 18, 14, 20, 16, 23\}$$ > Compute the seasonal difference $\nabla_4 Y_t$ and the combined first-then-seasonal difference $\nabla \nabla_4 Y_t$. Which transformation produces a more stationary output? Justify. **[(7 Marks)]** #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) – 7 Marks:** **Definitions and Comparison (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Trend Differencing | Seasonal Differencing | |---|---|---| | Lag used | 1 (consecutive terms) | $m$ (previous cycle) | | Effect | Removes polynomial trends | Removes periodic cycles | | Formula | $\nabla^d Y_t$ | $\nabla_m Y_t$ | | Best for | Monotonically trending data | Cyclical/seasonal data | **Conditions of Application (2 Marks):** - Apply **trend differencing** when the series has a clear monotonic trend and the ACF decays very slowly (suggesting a unit root). - Apply **seasonal differencing** when the ACF has a large, persistent spike at a specific lag $m$ (e.g., lag 12 for monthly, lag 4 for quarterly). - If both are present (as in Question B), apply **seasonal differencing first**, then check for residual trend and apply trend differencing if needed. **Over-differencing (2 Marks):** Over-differencing occurs when we apply the difference operator more times than necessary. The signature in the ACF plot is: - A **large negative autocorrelation** at lag 1 (close to $-0.5$ or lower) for over-trend-differencing. - A **large negative autocorrelation** at lag $m$ for over-seasonal-differencing. Over-differencing injects artificial structure (the series becomes more "noisy" and may resemble a non-invertible MA process), increasing forecast variance. The remedy is to reduce the order of differencing by 1. --- **Part (b) – 7 Marks:** **Step 1: Seasonal Difference $\nabla_4 Y_t$ (3 Marks)** $$\begin{aligned} \nabla_4 Y_5 &= Y_5 - Y_1 = 14 - 10 = 4 \\ \nabla_4 Y_6 &= Y_6 - Y_2 = 20 - 15 = 5 \\ \nabla_4 Y_7 &= Y_7 - Y_3 = 16 - 12 = 4 \\ \nabla_4 Y_8 &= Y_8 - Y_4 = 23 - 18 = 5 \end{aligned}$$ Resulting series: $\{4, 5, 4, 5\}$ - Mean $= 4.5$ - Variance $\approx 0.25$ **Step 2: Apply First Difference to the Seasonal-Differenced Series $\nabla \nabla_4 Y_t$ (3 Marks)** $$\begin{aligned} \nabla(\nabla_4 Y_6) &= 5 - 4 = 1 \\ \nabla(\nabla_4 Y_7) &= 4 - 5 = -1 \\ \nabla(\nabla_4 Y_8) &= 5 - 4 = 1 \end{aligned}$$ Resulting series: $\{1, -1, 1\}$ - Mean $= 0.333$ - Variance $\approx 1.0$ **Step 3: Comparison and Justification (1 Mark)** The seasonal difference alone yields a series $\{4, 5, 4, 5\}$ with **very low variance** and a clear oscillating pattern (alternating 4 and 5) – this still exhibits a *residual minor trend/seasonal pattern*. The combined $\nabla \nabla_4 Y_t$ yields $\{1, -1, 1\}$ oscillating around 0 with **higher variance but no clear pattern**. Given the small sample size, the **seasonal differencing $\nabla_4 Y_t$ alone is more appropriate** because the doubled differencing introduces over-differencing (a regular negative ACF at lag 1).
