The series is stationary after a single seasonal difference. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to specify the lag in seasonal differencing.** Students often write $\nabla Y_t$ instead of $\nabla_4 Y_t$, losing 1–2 marks. > 2. **Not showing the recursive expansion for variance proof.** Direct jumps to $t\sigma^2$ without intermediate steps will cost full marks in the derivation question. > 3. **Confusing the order of differencing application.** A common mistake is applying $\nabla$ before $\nabla_m$. The KTU-recommended order (per Box–Jenkins) is **seasonal first, then trend**. > 4. **Ignoring over-differencing.** Marks are awarded for diagnosing and avoiding the over-differencing trap, especially in long-answer questions. > 5. **Writing "the series is stationary" without justification.** Always compute the mean, variance, and at least one autocorrelation to support the claim. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Stationarity Definition:** Constant mean, constant variance, autocovariance depends only on lag $k$. - **First Difference:** $\nabla Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}$ — removes linear trend and random walk drift. - **Second Difference:** $\nabla^2 Y_t = \nabla Y_t - \nabla Y_{t-1}$ — removes quadratic trend. - **Lag Operator Form:** $\nabla Y_t = (1 - L)Y_t$ where $L Y_t = Y_{t-1}$. - **Random Walk:** $Y_t = Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ — has variance $t\sigma^2$ (non-stationary). - **Random Walk with Drift:** $Y_t = c + Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ — first differencing yields $\nabla Y_t = c + \varepsilon_t$ (stationary). - **Seasonal Difference:** $\nabla_m Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-m}$ — removes periodic pattern of period $m$. - **Order-of-Application Rule:** Apply **seasonal differencing FIRST**, then trend differencing if needed. - **ADF Test:** Null hypothesis is "unit root present". Reject if $p < 0.05$. - **ACF Signature – Random Walk:** Slow linear decay from 1. - **ACF Signature – Stationary:** Cuts off quickly (geometric decay). - **ACF Signature – Seasonal:** Large spike at lag $m$. - **ACF Signature – Over-differenced:** Large *negative* spike at lag 1 (regular) or lag $m$ (seasonal). - **Practical Limit:** $d \leq 2$ for 98% of real-world series. - **Box–Jenkins Airline Model:** $\text{ARIMA}(0,1,1)(0,1,1)_{12}$ — apply $\nabla \nabla_{12}$ to airline passenger data. - **Engineering Analogy:** Differencing = high-pass filter / discrete-time derivative. - **Common Mistake:** Over-differencing → reduce $d$ by 1, do not blindly increase. - **Why it matters:** ARMA/ARIMA models **require** stationarity — differencing is the gateway step. - **Log/Power Transform:** Use *before* differencing if variance is non-constant (heteroscedastic).

topics.title: Time Series as a Discrete Parameter Stochastic Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Time Series as a Discrete Parameter Stochastic Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **stochastic process** $\{Z(t), t \in T\}$ is a family of random variables defined on a common probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$, where the index set $T$ denotes time and the state space $S$ denotes the values taken by the process.

A **time series** is mathematically modelled as a realization (sample path) of a stochastic process, observed at a finite or infinite sequence of time points. When the index set $T$ is a countable (discrete) set such as:

$$T = \{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\} \quad \text{or} \quad T = \{0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$$

then the process is called a **Discrete Parameter Stochastic Process** (also called a *discrete-time stochastic process*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology Distinction:**
> - **Discrete Parameter** = Discrete time index $t \in T$ (countable set)
> - **Discrete State** = Discrete values of $Z(t)$ (e.g., only 0 or 1)
> - **Continuous State** = Continuous values of $Z(t)$ (e.g., real numbers)
>
> A time series is typically a **discrete-parameter, continuous-state** process.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Infinite Coin-Flipping Lottery" 🪙

Imagine a giant lottery machine containing **infinitely many balls**, each ball representing one possible *future* of the world. Every ball is a complete recorded history of, say, daily stock prices from 2000 to 2030.

When you reach into the machine, you pull out **one single ball** — that ball is your **observed time series** (a single realization). The *set of all balls* in the machine is the **stochastic process** itself.

- **The parameter (time)** $t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ corresponds to *day 1, day 2, day 3, ...*
- **The random variable** $Z(t)$ corresponds to the *price on day $t$*
- **The sample path** (realization) is the *single ball you pulled out*

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Insight for KTU Board Exam:**
> We almost always observe **only one realization** (one ball) of the process, but the underlying process contains the entire *ensemble* (population) of all possible realizations. This is why time series analysis is fundamentally harder than classical statistics — we must infer properties of the whole population from a *single* sample path.

---

### 1.3 Why Model a Time Series as a Stochastic Process?

In the Box-Jenkins (1976) paradigm, a time series $\{Z_t\}$ is decomposed as:

$$Z_t = m_t + X_t + \epsilon_t$$

where:
- $m_t$ = deterministic trend component
- $X_t$ = stochastic (random) component
- $\epsilon_t$ = white-noise residual

The randomness in $Z_t$ arises because:
1. The data-generating mechanism involves **chance** (economic shocks, weather variability, measurement noise).
2. Even if the mechanism were deterministic, we may **not know** the initial conditions perfectly (chaotic behaviour).
3. There are always **unobservable factors** influencing the series.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **standard deviation** $\sigma$ and **mean** $\mu$ are the fundamental parameters used to characterise the marginal distribution of any discrete-parameter stochastic process.

---

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Stochastic Processes (KTU Classification)

| Classification | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Discrete-Parameter, Continuous-State** | $T$ countable, $Z_t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Daily stock closing price |
| **Discrete-Parameter, Discrete-State** | $T$ countable, $Z_t \in \{0,1\}$ | Daily rainfall occurrence |
| **Continuous-Parameter, Continuous-State** | $T \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $Z(t) \in \mathbb{R}$ | Seismic wave amplitude |
| **Continuous-Parameter, Discrete-State** | $T \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $Z(t) \in \{0,1\}$ | Telegraph signal |

Our focus in **Time Series Modelling (PEADT755)** is exclusively on **Discrete-Parameter, Continuous-State** processes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of a Discrete-Parameter Stochastic Process

A discrete-parameter stochastic process $\{Z_t : t \in T\}$ can be analysed at multiple statistical levels, each requiring progressively more data/information:

**Level 1 — Marginal Distribution (One time point):**
For each fixed $t$, $Z_t$ is just a random variable with its own CDF:
$$F_t(z) = P(Z_t \le z), \quad z \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Level 2 — Joint Distribution of Two Time Points:**
$$F(s, t; z_1, z_2) = P(Z_s \le z_1, \, Z_t \le z_2)$$

**Level 3 — Finite-Dimensional Distributions (Kolmogorov's Extension):**
For any finite set $\{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\} \subset T$:
$$F(z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_n; t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n) = P(Z_{t_1} \le z_1, \ldots, Z_{t_n} \le z_n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kolmogorov's Existence Theorem:** A stochastic process is **completely characterised** by the family of all its finite-dimensional distributions. In practice, we work with the first two moments because the full joint distribution is infeasible to estimate from a single realization.

---

### 2.2 The Four Fundamental Statistical Functions (The "Big Four")

For a discrete-parameter stochastic process $\{Z_t\}$, define:

**① Mean Function:**
$$\mu(t) = E[Z_t] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \, dF_t(z)$$

**② Variance Function:**
$$\sigma^2(t) = \text{Var}(Z_t) = E[(Z_t - \mu(t))^2]$$

**③ Autocovariance Function (ACVF):**
$$\gamma(s, t) = \text{Cov}(Z_s, Z_t) = E[(Z_s - \mu(s))(Z_t - \mu(t))]$$

**④ Autocorrelation Function (ACF):**
$$\rho(s, t) = \frac{\gamma(s, t)}{\sqrt{\sigma^2(s) \cdot \sigma^2(t)}} = \frac{\gamma(s, t)}{\sqrt{\gamma(s,s)\gamma(t,t)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Auto"?** Because we are correlating the process *with itself* at two different time points. If $s = t$, then $\gamma(t,t) = \sigma^2(t)$ and $\rho(t,t) = 1$.

---

### 2.3 Stationarity: The Centrepiece of Time Series Analysis

Stationarity is the property that makes a stochastic process *statistically tractable* from a single realization.

#### 2.3.1 Strict Stationarity (Strong Stationarity)

A process $\{Z_t\}$ is **strictly stationary** if for every finite set $\{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$ and every integer shift $k$, the joint distribution is invariant:

$$P(Z_{t_1} \le z_1, \ldots, Z_{t_n} \le z_n) = P(Z_{t_1+k} \le z_1, \ldots, Z_{t_n+k} \le z_n)$$

In words: **the probabilistic mechanism generating the series does not change with time.**

#### 2.3.2 Weak (Second-Order / Wide-Sense) Stationarity

A process $\{Z_t\}$ is **weakly stationary** (or *covariance-stationary*) if:

| # | Condition | Mathematical Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Constant Mean | $\mu(t) = \mu \quad \forall t$ |
| 2 | Constant Variance | $\sigma^2(t) = \sigma^2 \quad \forall t$ |
| 3 | Autocovariance depends only on lag | $\gamma(s,t) = \gamma(\lvert s - t \rvert)$ |

For a weakly stationary process, the autocovariance function simplifies to:

$$\gamma_k = \gamma(t, t+k) = \text{Cov}(Z_t, Z_{t+k})$$

and the autocorrelation function becomes:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0}, \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma_0 = \sigma^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Stationarity $\Rightarrow$ Weak Stationarity only if** the mean and variance exist (finite second moments). The converse is **not true** in general. However, for Gaussian processes, the two are **equivalent**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol | Name | Definition | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_t$ | Stochastic process | Random variable indexed by $t$ | $t \in T$ |
| 2 | $\mu(t)$ | Mean function | $E[Z_t]$ | All $t$ |
| 3 | $\sigma^2(t)$ | Variance function | $E[(Z_t - \mu(t))^2]$ | All $t$ |
| 4 | $\gamma(s,t)$ | Autocovariance | $E[(Z_s - \mu(s))(Z_t - \mu(t))]$ | $s,t \in T$ |
| 5 | $\rho(s,t)$ | Autocorrelation | $\gamma(s,t)/\sqrt{\gamma(s,s)\gamma(t,t)}$ | $s,t \in T$ |
| 6 | $\gamma_k$ | Lag-$k$ autocovariance | $\text{Cov}(Z_t, Z_{t+k})$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 7 | $\rho_k$ | Lag-$k$ autocorrelation | $\gamma_k / \gamma_0$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 8 | $\bar{Z}$ | Sample mean | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N} Z_t$ | $N$ = sample size |
| 9 | $c_k$ | Sample autocovariance | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N-k}(Z_t - \bar{Z})(Z_{t+k}-\bar{Z})$ | $0 \le k \le N-1$ |
| 10 | $r_k$ | Sample autocorrelation | $c_k / c_0$ | $0 \le k \le N-1$ |
| 11 | — | Strict stationarity | Joint CDF invariant under time shift | All $n, k$ |
| 12 | — | Weak stationarity | $\mu$ const, $\sigma^2$ const, $\gamma_k$ depends only on $k$ | $k = 0, \pm 1, \ldots$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Properties of the Autocovariance Function:**
> - $\gamma_0 \ge 0$ (variance is non-negative)
> - $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$ (symmetric in lag)
> - $\vert \gamma_k \vert \le \gamma_0$ (bounded by variance)
> - $\gamma_k$ is a *positive semi-definite* sequence: $\sum_{i,j} a_i a_j \gamma_{t_i - t_j} \ge 0$ for all $\{a_i\}$

---

| **Electrical Engineering** | Modelling noise in communication channels, signal processing |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Vibration analysis of rotating machinery, fatigue-life prediction |
| **Civil Engineering** | Structural health monitoring (seismic loads, wind gusts) |
| **Economics / Finance** | Stock returns, GDP, inflation modelling |

### 2.6 Canonical Examples of Discrete-Parameter Stochastic Processes

#### Example 1 — Bernoulli White Noise
$$Z_t \stackrel{iid}{\sim} \text{Bernoulli}(p), \quad t = 1, 2, \ldots$$

- $\mu(t) = p$, $\sigma^2(t) = p(1-p)$
- $\gamma_k = 0$ for $k \ne 0$ (uncorrelated)
- $\gamma_k = p(1-p)\delta_k$ (Kronecker delta)

#### Example 2 — Gaussian White Noise
$$Z_t \stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)$$

- $\mu(t) = 0$, $\sigma^2(t) = \sigma^2$
- $\gamma_k = \sigma^2 \delta_k$

#### Example 3 — Random Walk
$$Z_t = Z_{t-1} + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \stackrel{iid}{\sim} WN(0, \sigma^2)$$

- $E[Z_t] = 0$ (assuming $Z_0 = 0$)
- $\text{Var}(Z_t) = t\sigma^2$ — **non-stationary!**
- $\gamma(s,t) = \min(s,t)\sigma^2$

#### Example 4 — Moving Average MA(1)
$$Z_t = \epsilon_t + \theta \epsilon_{t-1}$$

- $\mu(t) = 0$
- $\gamma_0 = (1+\theta^2)\sigma^2$
- $\gamma_1 = \theta\sigma^2$, $\gamma_k = 0$ for $\lvert k \rvert \ge 2$

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Autocovariance of a Random Walk

> **Problem Statement:** Let $\{Z_t\}$ be a random walk defined as $Z_t = Z_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$ with $Z_0 = 0$ and $\epsilon_t \stackrel{iid}{\sim} WN(0, \sigma_\epsilon^2)$. Show that $\text{Var}(Z_t) = t\sigma_\epsilon^2$ and $\text{Cov}(Z_s, Z_t) = \min(s,t)\sigma_\epsilon^2$.

**Step 1 — Expand $Z_t$ as a telescoping sum:**

$$
Z_t = Z_{t-1} + \epsilon_t = Z_{t-2} + \epsilon_{t-1} + \epsilon_t = \cdots = \sum_{j=1}^{t} \epsilon_j
$$

The initial condition $Z_0 = 0$ is used here. This shows that $Z_t$ is just a cumulative sum of independent white-noise shocks.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean function:**

$$
\mu(t) = E[Z_t] = E\left[\sum_{j=1}^{t} \epsilon_j\right] = \sum_{j=1}^{t} E[\epsilon_j] = \sum_{j=1}^{t} 0 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance function (using independence of $\epsilon_j$):**

$$
\sigma^2(t) = \text{Var}(Z_t) = \text{Var}\left(\sum_{j=1}^{t} \epsilon_j\right)
$$

$$
= \sum_{j=1}^{t} \text{Var}(\epsilon_j) \quad \text{(since } \epsilon_j \text{ are independent, all cross-covariances vanish)}
$$

$$
= \sum_{j=1}^{t} \sigma_\epsilon^2 = t \, \sigma_\epsilon^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\sigma^2(t) = t\sigma_\epsilon^2$ depends on $t$, the random walk is **NOT weakly stationary** — its variance grows without bound. This is why differencing is needed to make a random walk stationary.

**Step 4 — Compute the autocovariance for $s \le t$:**

$$
Z_s = \sum_{j=1}^{s} \epsilon_j, \quad Z_t = \sum_{j=1}^{t} \epsilon_j
$$

$$
\gamma(s, t) = \text{Cov}(Z_s, Z_t) = \text{Cov}\left(\sum_{j=1}^{s}\epsilon_j, \, \sum_{k=1}^{t}\epsilon_k\right)
$$

$$
= \sum_{j=1}^{s} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \text{Cov}(\epsilon_j, \epsilon_k)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the double sum:**

Because $\epsilon_j$ are independent, $\text{Cov}(\epsilon_j, \epsilon_k) = 0$ for $j \ne k$ and equals $\sigma_\epsilon^2$ for $j = k$. The only terms that survive are those with $j = k$ and $j = k \le s$ (since $s \le t$):

$$
\gamma(s, t) = \sum_{j=1}^{s} \sigma_\epsilon^2 = s \, \sigma_\epsilon^2
$$

By symmetry in $s$ and $t$, for the general case $s, t \ge 1$:

$$
\boxed{\gamma(s,t) = \min(s,t) \, \sigma_\epsilon^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the autocorrelation:**

$$
\rho(s, t) = \frac{\gamma(s,t)}{\sqrt{\gamma(s,s)\gamma(t,t)}} = \frac{\min(s,t)\sigma_\epsilon^2}{\sqrt{s\sigma_\epsilon^2 \cdot t\sigma_\epsilon^2}} = \frac{\min(s,t)}{\sqrt{s \cdot t}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The autocorrelation $\rho(s,t) = \min(s,t)/\sqrt{st}$ depends on both $s$ and $t$ (not just on the lag $k = t-s$), confirming that the random walk is **non-stationary**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Autocovariance of MA(1) Process

> **Problem Statement:** Let $Z_t = \epsilon_t + \theta \epsilon_{t-1}$ where $\epsilon_t \stackrel{iid}{\sim} WN(0, \sigma^2)$. Compute $\gamma_0$, $\gamma_1$, and $\gamma_k$ for $k \ge 2$.

**Step 1 — Mean function:**

$$
\mu(t) = E[Z_t] = E[\epsilon_t] + \theta E[\epsilon_{t-1}] = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Lag-0 autocovariance (variance):**

$$
\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(Z_t) = E[Z_t^2] = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta \epsilon_{t-1})^2]
$$

$$
= E[\epsilon_t^2] + 2\theta E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1}] + \theta^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2]
$$

$$
= \sigma^2 + 2\theta \cdot 0 + \theta^2 \sigma^2 = (1 + \theta^2)\sigma^2
$$

The cross-term vanishes because $\epsilon_t$ and $\epsilon_{t-1}$ are independent with zero mean.

**Step 3 — Lag-1 autocovariance:**

$$
\gamma_1 = \text{Cov}(Z_t, Z_{t-1}) = E[Z_t Z_{t-1}] \quad (\text{since mean} = 0)
$$

$$
= E[(\epsilon_t + \theta \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-1} + \theta \epsilon_{t-2})]
$$

$$
= E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-1}] + \theta E[\epsilon_t \epsilon_{t-2}] + \theta E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] + \theta^2 E[\epsilon_{t-1}\epsilon_{t-2}]
$$

$$
= 0 + 0 + \theta\sigma^2 + 0 = \theta\sigma^2
$$

**Step 4 — Lag-2 autocovariance:**

$$
\gamma_2 = E[Z_t Z_{t-2}] = E[(\epsilon_t + \theta \epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-2} + \theta \epsilon_{t-3})]
$$

All four products involve *disjoint* indices ($\epsilon_t$ vs $\epsilon_{t-2}$, $\epsilon_t$ vs $\epsilon_{t-3}$, $\epsilon_{t-1}$ vs $\epsilon_{t-2}$, $\epsilon_{t-1}$ vs $\epsilon_{t-3}$), so by independence:

$$
\gamma_2 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — General lag $k \ge 2$:**

$$
\boxed{\gamma_k = \begin{cases} (1+\theta^2)\sigma^2, & k = 0 \\ \theta\sigma^2, & k = \pm 1 \\ 0, & \lvert k \rvert \ge 2 \end{cases}}
$$

**Step 6 — Autocorrelation function (ACF):**

$$
\boxed{\rho_k = \begin{cases} 1, & k = 0 \\ \dfrac{\theta}{1+\theta^2}, & k = \pm 1 \\ 0, & \lvert k \rvert \ge 2 \end{cases}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Sample Autocovariance from a Single Realization

> **Problem Statement:** Given a single realization $\{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_N\}$, derive the sample autocovariance $c_k$.

**Step 1 — The sample mean as an estimator:**

$$
\bar{z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N} z_t
$$

**Step 2 — The sample autocovariance at lag $k$:**

For a weakly stationary process, we use the standard unbiased estimator:

$$
c_k = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N-k}(z_t - \bar{z})(z_{t+k} - \bar{z}), \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the divisor is $N$, not $N-k$. The unbiased estimator (using $N-k$) has larger mean-squared error for moderate $k$. Box-Jenkins (1976) recommend the biased estimator above for practical use.

**Step 3 — Sample autocorrelation:**

$$
r_k = \frac{c_k}{c_0}, \quad \text{where } c_0 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N}(z_t - \bar{z})^2
$$

**Step 4 — Large-sample distribution under white noise null:**

For testing $H_0: \rho_k = 0$, the statistic:

$$
t = \frac{r_k}{\sqrt{1/N}} = r_k \sqrt{N}
$$

is asymptotically $N(0,1)$. A lag-$k$ autocorrelation is statistically significant if $\lvert r_k \rvert > 1.96/\sqrt{N}$ (95% confidence).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Stationary vs Non-Stationary

| Property | Strictly Stationary | Weakly Stationary | Random Walk (Non-Stationary) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean | $\mu$ (constant) | $\mu$ (constant) | $0$ (constant but variance grows) |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ (constant) | $\sigma^2$ (constant) | $t\sigma^2$ (grows with $t$) |
| ACVF | $\gamma_k$ | $\gamma_k$ | $\min(s,t)\sigma^2$ (depends on $s, t$) |
| ACF | $\rho_k$ | $\rho_k$ | $\min(s,t)/\sqrt{st}$ |
| Differencing required? | No | No | Yes (first-difference is WN) |
| Can forecast reliably? | Yes | Yes | Unreliable long-term |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

Define the following with respect to a discrete-parameter stochastic process:
(a) Mean function
(b) Autocovariance function
(c) Autocorrelation function

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Mean function:** The mean function of a discrete-parameter stochastic process $\{Z_t, t \in T\}$ is defined as $\mu(t) = E[Z_t]$. It gives the expected value of the random variable $Z_t$ at each time $t$ and is the first moment of the marginal distribution $F_t(z)$.

(b) **Autocovariance function (ACVF):** The autocovariance function is a two-argument function defined as:
$$\gamma(s, t) = \text{Cov}(Z_s, Z_t) = E[(Z_s - \mu(s))(Z_t - \mu(t))]$$
It quantifies the linear dependence between the process values at two (possibly different) time points.

(c) **Autocorrelation function (ACF):** The autocorrelation function normalises the autocovariance to lie in $[-1, 1]$:
$$\rho(s, t) = \frac{\gamma(s,t)}{\sqrt{\gamma(s,s)\gamma(t,t)}}$$
It is a dimensionless measure of linear association between $Z_s$ and $Z_t$.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

Differentiate between strict stationarity and weak stationarity of a stochastic process. State one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Strict Stationarity | Weak (Wide-Sense) Stationarity |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | All finite-dimensional distributions are invariant under time shift | Only mean, variance, and autocovariance are time-invariant |
| Mathematical condition | $F(z_1, \ldots, z_n; t_1, \ldots, t_n) = F(z_1, \ldots, z_n; t_1+k, \ldots, t_n+k)$ | $\mu(t) = \mu$, $\sigma^2(t) = \sigma^2$, $\gamma(s,t) = \gamma(\vert s-t \vert)$ |
| Moments required | None (distribution-level) | First two moments must exist |
| Example | IID Bernoulli sequence | MA(1) process $Z_t = \epsilon_t + 0.5\epsilon_{t-1}$ |
| Implication | Strict $\Rightarrow$ Weak (if moments exist) | Weak $\nRightarrow$ Strict in general |

For a **Gaussian process**, strict and weak stationarity are equivalent because the multivariate Gaussian is fully determined by its mean vector and covariance matrix.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Define a discrete-parameter stochastic process. State and explain the conditions for a process to be strictly stationary. Show that strict stationarity with finite second moments implies weak stationarity.

(b) **[7 Marks]** Consider the moving average process $Z_t = \epsilon_t + 0.8\epsilon_{t-1}$, where $\epsilon_t \stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(0, 4)$. Compute the mean $\mu$, variance $\gamma_0$, and autocovariances $\gamma_1, \gamma_2, \gamma_3$. Comment on the stationarity of the process.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Definition:** A *discrete-parameter stochastic process* is a family $\{Z_t : t \in T\}$ of random variables defined on a common probability space, where $T = \{\ldots, -1, 0, 1, \ldots\}$ or $T = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ is a countable index set (discrete time).

**Conditions for Strict Stationarity:**
A process $\{Z_t\}$ is strictly stationary if for every $n \ge 1$, every choice of time points $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n \in T$, every integer shift $k$, and every $z_1, \ldots, z_n \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$P(Z_{t_1} \le z_1, \ldots, Z_{t_n} \le z_n) = P(Z_{t_1+k} \le z_1, \ldots, Z_{t_n+k} \le z_n)$$

**Proof that Strict Stationarity + Finite Second Moments $\Rightarrow$ Weak Stationarity:**

*Step 1:* Setting $n=1$ and using strict stationarity:
$$P(Z_{t+k} \le z) = P(Z_t \le z) \quad \forall t, k, z$$
Hence $F_t(z) = F_{t+k}(z)$ for all $t, k$, so the marginal CDF is independent of $t$. Since the second moment exists, $E[Z_t]$ is well-defined and constant: $\mu(t) = \mu$.

*Step 2:* Setting $n=2$ and using strict stationarity:
$$P(Z_s \le z_1, Z_t \le z_2) = P(Z_{s+k} \le z_1, Z_{t+k} \le z_2)$$

This implies $\text{Cov}(Z_{s+k}, Z_{t+k}) = \text{Cov}(Z_s, Z_t)$, so the autocovariance depends only on the lag $\tau = t - s$:
$$\gamma(s, t) = \gamma(\tau)$$

*Step 3:* With constant mean and lag-dependent autocovariance, all three conditions for weak stationarity are satisfied. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** **Given:** $Z_t = \epsilon_t + 0.8\epsilon_{t-1}$, $\epsilon_t \sim N(0, 4)$, i.e., $\sigma_\epsilon^2 = 4$.

**Mean:**
$$\mu = E[Z_t] = E[\epsilon_t] + 0.8 E[\epsilon_{t-1}] = 0 + 0 = 0$$

**Variance ($\gamma_0$):**
$$\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(Z_t) = \text{Var}(\epsilon_t + 0.8\epsilon_{t-1}) = \text{Var}(\epsilon_t) + 0.64 \text{Var}(\epsilon_{t-1})$$
$$\gamma_0 = 4 + 0.64 \cdot 4 = 4 + 2.56 = 6.56$$

**Autocovariance at lag 1:**
$$\gamma_1 = E[Z_t Z_{t-1}] = E[(\epsilon_t + 0.8\epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-1} + 0.8\epsilon_{t-2})]$$
$$= 0 + 0 + 0.8 \cdot E[\epsilon_{t-1}^2] + 0 = 0.8 \cdot 4 = 3.2$$

**Autocovariance at lag 2:**
$$\gamma_2 = E[Z_t Z_{t-2}] = E[(\epsilon_t + 0.8\epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-2} + 0.8\epsilon_{t-3})] = 0$$
(all indices are distinct, so all products have zero expectation)

**Autocovariance at lag 3:**
$$\gamma_3 = E[Z_t Z_{t-3}] = E[(\epsilon_t + 0.8\epsilon_{t-1})(\epsilon_{t-3} + 0.8\epsilon_{t-4})] = 0$$

**Stationarity:** Since $\mu(t) = 0$ (constant), $\sigma^2(t) = 6.56$ (constant), and $\gamma_k$ depends only on the lag (with $\gamma_k = 0$ for $\lvert k \rvert \ge 2$), the process is **weakly stationary**. As $\epsilon_t$ is Gaussian, the process is also **strictly stationary**.

**Summary Table:**
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\mu$ | $0$ |
| $\gamma_0$ | $6.56$ |
| $\gamma_1$ | $3.2$ |
| $\gamma_2$ | $0$ |
| $\gamma_3$ | $0$ |

---

**Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Define the autocovariance function and autocorrelation function of a discrete-parameter stochastic process. Prove that $\vert \rho_k \vert \le 1$ for all $k$ and that $\rho_k = \rho_{-k}$.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A random walk is defined by $Z_t = Z_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$ with $Z_0 = 0$ and $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, 9)$. Compute the mean function $\mu(t)$, variance function $\sigma^2(t)$, and autocovariance $\gamma(s,t)$ for $s, t \ge 1$. Is the random walk stationary? Justify.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Definitions:**

The *autocovariance function* of a discrete-parameter stochastic process $\{Z_t\}$ is:
$$\gamma(s, t) = E[(Z_s - \mu(s))(Z_t - \mu(t))]$$

The *autocorrelation function* is the autocovariance normalised by the marginal standard deviations:
$$\rho(s, t) = \frac{\gamma(s, t)}{\sqrt{\gamma(s, s) \gamma(t, t)}}$$

**Proof that $\vert \rho_k \vert \le 1$:**

For a stationary process, $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$. We use the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which states that for any two random variables $X, Y$ with finite second moments:
$$\lvert E[XY] \rvert \le \sqrt{E[X^2] \cdot E[Y^2]}$$

Apply this with $X = Z_t - \mu$ and $Y = Z_{t+k} - \mu$:
$$\lvert E[(Z_t - \mu)(Z_{t+k} - \mu)] \rvert \le \sqrt{E[(Z_t-\mu)^2] \cdot E[(Z_{t+k}-\mu)^2]}$$
$$\lvert \gamma_k \rvert \le \sqrt{\gamma_0 \cdot \gamma_0} = \gamma_0$$

Dividing both sides by $\gamma_0$ (which is non-negative):
$$\lvert \rho_k \rvert = \frac{\lvert \gamma_k \rvert}{\gamma_0} \le 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Proof that $\rho_k = \rho_{-k}$:**

By definition, $\rho_{-k}$ corresponds to the correlation between $Z_t$ and $Z_{t-k}$. Setting $s = t$ and $t' = t - k$:

$$\gamma(t-k, t) = \text{Cov}(Z_{t-k}, Z_t) = \text{Cov}(Z_t, Z_{t-k}) = \gamma(t, t-k) = \gamma_k$$

Hence $\gamma_{-k} = \gamma_k$ for all $k$, and dividing by $\gamma_0$:
$$\rho_{-k} = \frac{\gamma_{-k}}{\gamma_0} = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \rho_k \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b)** **Given:** $Z_t = Z_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$, $Z_0 = 0$, $\epsilon_t \sim WN(0, 9)$ (so $\sigma_\epsilon^2 = 9$).

**Mean function:**
By induction, $Z_t = \sum_{j=1}^{t} \epsilon_j$. Then:
$$\mu(t) = E[Z_t] = \sum_{j=1}^{t} E[\epsilon_j] = 0$$

**Variance function:**
Using independence of $\{\epsilon_j\}$:
$$\sigma^2(t) = \text{Var}(Z_t) = \text{Var}\left(\sum_{j=1}^{t} \epsilon_j\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{t} \text{Var}(\epsilon_j) = 9t$$

**Autocovariance function:**
For $s, t \ge 1$, assume WLOG $s \le t$:
$$\gamma(s, t) = \text{Cov}(Z_s, Z_t) = \text{Cov}\left(\sum_{j=1}^{s}\epsilon_j, \sum_{k=1}^{t}\epsilon_k\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{s}\sum_{k=1}^{t}\text{Cov}(\epsilon_j, \epsilon_k)$$

Since $\epsilon_j$ are independent, $\text{Cov}(\epsilon_j, \epsilon_k) = 9\delta_{jk}$:
$$\gamma(s, t) = \sum_{j=1}^{s} 9 = 9s = 9\min(s, t)$$

**Stationarity check:**
- Mean is constant ($\mu = 0$). ✓
- Variance is **not** constant: $\sigma^2(t) = 9t$ depends on $t$. ✗
- Autocovariance depends on $s, t$ jointly, not just on $\lvert s - t \rvert$. ✗

Since the variance grows without bound and the autocovariance is not lag-only, the random walk is **NOT weakly stationary** (and hence also not strictly stationary).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mark Loss Trap #1:** Students often forget to check the *variance constancy* condition and only check the mean. You lose marks if you do not verify all three weak-stationarity conditions explicitly.
> 2. **Mark Loss Trap #2:** For autocovariance derivation of the random walk, students frequently forget that $Z_t$ is a *sum* of independent shocks and incorrectly treat it as a single random variable. Always expand $Z_t = \sum \epsilon_j$ first.
> 3. **Mark Loss Trap #3:** Cauchy-Schwarz is the expected tool for proving $\lvert \rho_k \rvert \le 1$. Using only "since it's a correlation, it's bounded by 1" without proof will not earn full marks.
> 4. **Mark Loss Trap #4:** In MA(1) problems, students often write $\gamma_1 = \theta\sigma^2$ but forget to show the *full expansion* with all four cross-product terms. Examiners require every term to appear.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision — Must Memorise Before Exam**

- ✅ A **time series** is a realisation of a **discrete-parameter stochastic process** $\{Z_t, t \in T\}$.
- ✅ **Mean function** $\mu(t) = E[Z_t]$; **Autocovariance** $\gamma(s,t) = E[(Z_s - \mu(s))(Z_t - \mu(t))]$.
- ✅ **Autocorrelation** $\rho(s,t) = \gamma(s,t) / \sqrt{\gamma(s,s)\gamma(t,t)} \in [-1, 1]$.
- ✅ **Strict stationarity**: joint distributions invariant under time shift (distribution-level).
- ✅ **Weak stationarity**: $\mu$ const, $\sigma^2$ const, $\gamma_k$ depends only on lag $|t-s|$.
- ✅ Strict $\Rightarrow$ Weak **only if** second moments exist.
- ✅ For **Gaussian** processes: Strict $\Leftrightarrow$ Weak.
- ✅ **White noise**: $\mu = 0$, $\gamma_k = \sigma^2 \delta_k$ (Kronecker delta).
- ✅ **MA(1)** $Z_t = \epsilon_t + \theta \epsilon_{t-1}$: $\gamma_0 = (1+\theta^2)\sigma^2$, $\gamma_1 = \theta\sigma^2$, $\gamma_k = 0$ for $|k| \ge 2$.
- ✅ **Random walk** $Z_t = Z_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$: $\mu = 0$, $\sigma^2(t) = t\sigma^2$, **non-stationary**.
- ✅ $\gamma_k = \gamma_{-k}$ (symmetry), $|\gamma_k| \le \gamma_0$ (bounded by variance).
- ✅ **Sample ACF**: $r_k = c_k/c_0$ where $c_k = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N-k}(z_t - \bar{z})(z_{t+k} - \bar{z})$.
- ✅ Under $H_0: \rho_k = 0$ for white noise, $r_k \sim N(0, 1/N)$; significant if $|r_k| > 1.96/\sqrt{N}$.
- ✅ First differencing $W_t = Z_t - Z_{t-1}$ converts a random walk into white noise.
- ✅ Always expand $Z_t$ as a *sum* of independent shocks before computing its variance/covariance.
- ✅ Cauchy-Schwarz inequality is the standard tool for proving $|\rho_k| \le 1$.
- ✅ **Box-Jenkins decomposition**: $Z_t = m_t + X_t + \epsilon_t$ (trend + stochastic + noise).
- ✅ KTU exam tip: write out **every** term in cross-product expansions for autocovariance — partial work loses marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autocorrelation Function (ACF), Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF), and Cross Correlations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Autocorrelation Function (ACF), Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF), and Cross Correlations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Autocorrelation Function (ACF) — Formal Definition

The **Autocorrelation Function (ACF)** of a stationary stochastic process $\{X_t\}$ quantifies the linear statistical dependence between observations separated by a time lag $k$. Formally, it is the normalized second-moment product between $X_t$ and $X_{t+k}$.

$$\rho_k \;=\; \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} \;=\; \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(X_t,\, X_{t+k})}{\operatorname{Var}(X_t)}$$

where $\gamma_k = \operatorname{E}\!\left[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)\right]$ is the **autocovariance function** at lag $k$, and $\gamma_0$ is the process variance.

## 1.2 Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF) — Formal Definition

The **Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)**, denoted $\phi_{kk}$, measures the correlation between $X_t$ and $X_{t+k}$ *after* removing the linear effects of the intervening observations $X_{t+1}, X_{t+2}, \dots, X_{t+k-1}$.

Equivalently, $\phi_{kk}$ is the **last coefficient** in the best linear regression of $X_t$ on $X_{t-1}, X_{t-2}, \dots, X_{t-k}$.

## 1.3 Cross-Correlation Function (CCF) — Formal Definition

The **Cross-Correlation Function (CCF)** $\rho_{XY}(k)$ quantifies the linear dependence between two *different* stationary time series $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$ separated by lag $k$:

$$\rho_{XY}(k) \;=\; \frac{\gamma_{XY}(k)}{\sqrt{\gamma_{XX}(0)\,\gamma_{YY}(0)}}$$

where $\gamma_{XY}(k) = \operatorname{E}[(X_t - \mu_X)(Y_{t+k} - \mu_Y)]$.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogies

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — Memory of a Signal:** Imagine recording the daily temperature at Kottayam for 10 years. The *autocorrelation* at lag 1 answers: *"How strongly does today's temperature resemble yesterday's?"* The *partial autocorrelation* answers: *"After I already know yesterday's temperature, does the day-before-yesterday still add new information?"*

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Two Chatty Friends (CCF):** Consider rainfall in Kerala ($X$) and reservoir inflow ($Y$). The cross-correlation at lag 2 tells us: *"Is rainfall today a useful predictor of inflow two days from now?"* A strong negative peak at negative lag implies $X$ *follows* $Y$, while a positive peak at positive lag implies $Y$ *follows* $X$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these three functions matter in Time Series Modelling:**
> Per the **Box–Jenkins methodology**, the ACF and PACF together act as the diagnostic "fingerprint" used to identify the order of AR, MA, and ARMA models. The CCF is essential in **transfer-function modelling**, **Granger causality**, and **distributed-lag regression**. In every KTU 2024 Scheme question on model identification, you must compute and interpret these functions.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used Throughout

| Metric | Symbol | Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Lag | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | $0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots$ |
| Autocorrelation coefficient | $\rho_k$ | $-1 \le \rho_k \le 1$ |
| PACF coefficient | $\phi_{kk}$ | $-1 \le \phi_{kk} \le 1$ |
| Cross-correlation coefficient | $\rho_{XY}(k)$ | $-1 \le \rho_{XY}(k) \le 1$ |
| Bartlett's standard error | $SE(r_k)$ | $\approx 1/\sqrt{n}$ for white noise |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of the ACF (Stationary Series)

A covariance-stationary process $\{X_t\}$ satisfies:

1. **Normalization:** $\rho_0 = 1$ (every series is perfectly correlated with itself at lag 0).
2. **Boundedness:** $\vert \rho_k \vert \le 1$ for all $k$, with equality iff $X_t$ is a deterministic sinusoid.
3. **Symmetry:** $\rho_k = \rho_{-k}$ (the autocorrelation is an *even* function).
4. **Non-negative definiteness:** For any constants $c_1, \dots, c_n$,

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_i c_j \,\gamma_{\vert i-j \vert} \;\ge\; 0$$

5. **Decay behavior:** For an AR($p$) process, $\rho_k$ decays as a mixture of damped exponentials/sinusoids; for an MA($q$) process, $\rho_k = 0$ for all $k > q$ (*cuts off*).

## 2.2 Properties of the PACF

1. $\phi_{00} = 1$ (by convention).
2. $\phi_{11} = \rho_1$.
3. $\phi_{kk}$ is **not** symmetric: only $\phi_{kk}$ for $k \ge 1$ is typically reported.
4. For an AR($p$) process, $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for all $k > p$ (*cuts off after lag $p$*).
5. $\vert \phi_{kk} \vert \le 1$ always.

## 2.3 Properties of the CCF

1. **Asymmetry:** $\rho_{XY}(k) \ne \rho_{YX}(k)$ in general; $\rho_{XY}(k) = \rho_{YX}(-k)$.
2. **Boundedness:** $\vert \rho_{XY}(k) \vert \le 1$.
3. **Not symmetric** about lag 0 — peak position reveals *lead–lag* structure.
4. For independent series, $\rho_{XY}(k) = 0$ for all $k \ne 0$.

## 2.4 Yule–Walker Equations (Bridge Between ACF and PACF)

For a stationary AR($p$) process
$$X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$
with $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$, dividing by $\gamma_0$ and using $\rho_{-j} = \rho_j$ yields:

$$\rho_k \;=\; \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2} + \dots + \phi_p \rho_{k-p}, \qquad k \ge 1$$

In matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \rho_1 & \rho_2 & \cdots & \rho_{p-1} \\ \rho_1 & 1 & \rho_1 & \cdots & \rho_{p-2} \\ \rho_2 & \rho_1 & 1 & \cdots & \rho_{p-3} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \rho_{p-1} & \rho_{p-2} & \rho_{p-3} & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \\ \phi_3 \\ \vdots \\ \phi_p \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho_1 \\ \rho_2 \\ \rho_3 \\ \vdots \\ \rho_p \end{bmatrix}$$

This system is the gateway from sample ACFs to AR coefficients — and equivalently, the foundation for the PACF recursion.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Population Formula | Sample Estimator |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Autocovariance | $\gamma_k = \operatorname{E}[(X_t - \mu)(X_{t+k} - \mu)]$ | $c_k = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-k} - \bar{x})$ |
| 2 | Autocorrelation | $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ | $r_k = \dfrac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-k} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^{2}}$ |
| 3 | PACF Recursion | $\phi_{kk} = \dfrac{\rho_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\phi_{k-1,j}\,\rho_{k-j}}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\phi_{k-1,j}\,\rho_{j}}$ | $r_{kk}$ via Durbin–Levinson |
| 4 | PACF Base | $\phi_{11} = \rho_1$ | $r_{11} = r_1$ |
| 5 | PACF Step (k=2) | $\phi_{22} = \dfrac{\rho_2 - \rho_1^{2}}{1 - \rho_1^{2}}$ | $r_{22}$ analogous |
| 6 | Bartlett SE | — | $SE(r_k) \approx 1/\sqrt{n}$ (white noise) |
| 7 | Cross-covariance | $\gamma_{XY}(k) = \operatorname{E}[(X_t-\mu_X)(Y_{t+k}-\mu_Y)]$ | $c_{XY}(k) = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(y_{t+k} - \bar{y})$ |
| 8 | Cross-correlation | $\rho_{XY}(k) = \dfrac{\gamma_{XY}(k)}{\sqrt{\gamma_{XX}(0)\,\gamma_{YY}(0)}}$ | $r_{XY}(k) = \dfrac{c_{XY}(k)}{\sqrt{c_{XX}(0)\,c_{YY}(0)}}$ |
| 9 | Yule–Walker | $\rho_k = \sum_{j=1}^{p}\phi_j \rho_{\vert k-j \vert}$ | Solved via Toeplitz inversion |
| 10 | 95% CI for $r_k$ | $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ | Same (large-sample normal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **signal processing**, ACF is used for pitch detection in speech; in **econometrics**, PACF underpins the order selection of AR models for GDP forecasting; in **biomedical engineering**, CCF aligns ECG and blood-pressure signals; in **IoT/IIoT** systems, ACF/PACF identify sensor drift and seasonal patterns before feeding ARIMA predictors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the PACF Recursion (Durbin–Levinson Algorithm)

We derive the recursive relation that expresses $\phi_{kk}$ in terms of $\phi_{k-1,j}$ and the autocorrelations $\rho_1, \dots, \rho_k$.

**Step 1 — Define the best linear predictor at lag $k$.**
The best linear predictor of $X_t$ given $X_{t-1}, \dots, X_{t-k}$ is

$$\hat{X}_t^{(k)} = \phi_{k1} X_{t-1} + \phi_{k2} X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_{kk} X_{t-k}$$

The prediction error is $e_t^{(k)} = X_t - \hat{X}_t^{(k)}$, with variance $v_k = \operatorname{E}[(e_t^{(k)})^{2}]$.

**Step 2 — Orthogonality conditions.**
Multiplying $e_t^{(k)}$ by $X_{t-j}$ and taking expectations for $j = 1, \dots, k$:

$$\operatorname{E}[(X_t - \hat{X}_t^{(k)}) X_{t-j}] = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma_j = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \phi_{ki} \,\gamma_{j-i}$$

Dividing through by $\gamma_0$:

$$\rho_j = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \phi_{ki} \,\rho_{j-i}, \qquad j = 1, 2, \dots, k \tag{★}$$

**Step 3 — Relate consecutive predictors.**
Suppose we have already computed $\hat{X}_t^{(k-1)}$ using lags $1, \dots, k-1$. We extend to $\hat{X}_t^{(k)}$ by adding the term $\phi_{kk} X_{t-k}$. Equivalently,

$$\hat{X}_t^{(k)} = \phi_{k-1,1}X_{t-1} + \dots + \phi_{k-1,k-1}X_{t-k+1} + \phi_{kk} X_{t-k}$$

The Durbin–Levinson recursion introduces an innovation weighting. Comparing the regression at order $k$ against order $k-1$ and using (★) for $j = k$ at both orders, one obtains after algebraic elimination:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{kk} &= \frac{\rho_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_{k-1,j}\,\rho_{k-j}}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \phi_{k-1,j}\,\rho_j} \\
\phi_{k,j} &= \phi_{k-1,j} - \phi_{kk}\,\phi_{k-1,k-j}, \qquad j = 1, \dots, k-1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Initialize.**
For $k = 1$:

$$\phi_{11} = \frac{\rho_1 - 0}{1 - 0} = \rho_1$$

For $k = 2$:

$$\phi_{22} = \frac{\rho_2 - \phi_{11}\,\rho_1}{1 - \phi_{11}\,\rho_1} = \frac{\rho_2 - \rho_1^{2}}{1 - \rho_1^{2}}$$

For $k = 3$:

$$\phi_{33} = \frac{\rho_3 - \phi_{21}\rho_2 - \phi_{22}\rho_1}{1 - \phi_{21}\rho_1 - \phi_{22}\rho_2}$$

with $\phi_{21} = \phi_{11} - \phi_{22}\phi_{11} = \phi_{11}(1-\phi_{22}) = \rho_1(1-\phi_{22})$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Yule–Walker from AR($p$) Specification

Starting with the AR($p$) equation:

$$X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides by $X_{t-k}$ and take expectations** for $k \ge 1$:

$$\operatorname{E}[X_t X_{t-k}] = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j \operatorname{E}[X_{t-j} X_{t-k}] + \operatorname{E}[\varepsilon_t X_{t-k}]$$

Since $\varepsilon_t$ is white noise and independent of $X_{t-k}$ for $k \ge 1$, the last term vanishes:

$$\gamma_k = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j \,\gamma_{k-j}, \qquad k \ge 1$$

**Step 2 — Divide by $\gamma_0$ to obtain the ACF version:**

$$\rho_k = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j \,\rho_{k-j}, \qquad k \ge 1$$

**Step 3 — For $k = 1, 2, \dots, p$, write the system:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_1 &= \phi_1 + \phi_2 \rho_1 + \phi_3 \rho_2 + \dots + \phi_p \rho_{p-1} \\
\rho_2 &= \phi_1 \rho_1 + \phi_2 + \phi_3 \rho_1 + \dots + \phi_p \rho_{p-2} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\rho_p &= \phi_1 \rho_{p-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{p-2} + \dots + \phi_p
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Yule–Walker system**, a symmetric positive-definite Toeplitz system uniquely solvable for $\phi_1, \dots, \phi_p$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Sample ACF Estimator $r_k$

Given observations $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ with sample mean $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} x_t$:

**Step 1 — Method-of-moments analogue of $\gamma_k$.** Define

$$c_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n-k} (x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})$$

**Step 2 — Normalize by the sample variance** $c_0$:

$$r_k = \frac{c_k}{c_0} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t+k} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Large-sample distribution (Bartlett's result):** Under the null hypothesis that $\{X_t\}$ is white noise,

$$\sqrt{n}\,r_k \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathcal{N}(0,1), \qquad k \ne 0$$

Hence the $\approx \pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ confidence bounds are routinely plotted.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

Let a sample yield the following autocorrelations from a series of $n = 100$:

$$r_1 = 0.50, \quad r_2 = 0.30, \quad r_3 = 0.10$$

**Compute PACF up to lag 3.**

**Lag 1:** $\phi_{11} = r_1 = 0.50$.

**Lag 2:** $\phi_{22} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1^{2}}{1 - r_1^{2}} = \dfrac{0.30 - 0.25}{1 - 0.25} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.75} = 0.0667$.

**Lag 3:** First compute $\phi_{21} = \phi_{11} - \phi_{22}\phi_{11} = 0.50(1 - 0.0667) = 0.4667$.

Then $\phi_{33} = \dfrac{r_3 - \phi_{21}r_2 - \phi_{22}r_1}{1 - \phi_{21}r_1 - \phi_{22}r_2} = \dfrac{0.10 - (0.4667)(0.30) - (0.0667)(0.50)}{1 - (0.4667)(0.50) - (0.0667)(0.30)}$

Numerator: $0.10 - 0.1400 - 0.0333 = -0.0733$.
Denominator: $1 - 0.2333 - 0.0200 = 0.7467$.

$$\phi_{33} = \frac{-0.0733}{0.7467} \approx -0.0982$$

Bartlett's bound at 95% is $1.96/\sqrt{100} = 0.196$. Hence only $\phi_{11}$ is significant — consistent with an **AR(1)** model.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Model Identification Patterns

| Model | ACF behaviour | PACF behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| AR($p$) | Tails off (damped exponentials/sinusoids) | **Cuts off** after lag $p$ |
| MA($q$) | **Cuts off** after lag $q$ | Tails off (damped exponentials/sinusoids) |
| ARMA($p,q$) | Tails off after lag $q$ | Tails off after lag $p$ |
| White noise | All $\rho_k = 0$ for $k \ne 0$ | All $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for $k \ge 1$ |
| Random walk | $\rho_k \to 1$ slowly | $\phi_{11} \approx 1$, others small |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Correlation Pattern Tip:** If $\rho_{XY}(k)$ peaks at a positive lag $k^{*}$, then $Y$ leads $X$ by $k^{*}$ (i.e., past $Y$ helps predict present $X$). If it peaks at a *negative* lag, then $X$ leads $Y$. This directional asymmetry is the key reason CCF is *not* symmetric.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ACF–PACF Decision Flow (Box–Jenkins Identification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stationary Series X_t"] --> B["Compute Sample ACF: r_k"]
    A --> C["Compute Sample PACF: phi_kk"]
    B --> D{"r_k cuts off\nafter lag q?"}
    C --> E{"phi_kk cuts off\nafter lag p?"}
    D -- Yes --> F["MA(q) model"]
    D -- No --> G["r_k tails off"]
    E -- Yes --> H["AR(p) model"]
    E -- No --> I["phi_kk tails off"]
    G --> I
    I --> J["ARMA(p,q) candidate"]
    F --> K["Fit and Diagnostic Check"]
    H --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{"Residuals white\nnoise?"}
    L -- Yes --> M["Model Accepted"]
    L -- No --> N["Re-identify or\nAdd Differencing"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ACF/PACF/CCF Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"]
        X1["Raw Time Series X_t (n samples)"]
        Y1["Optional Second Series Y_t"]
    end
    subgraph PREP["Pre-Processing"]
        P1["Mean Centering: x_t - x_bar"]
        P2["Variance Scaling: divide by c_0"]
        P3["Detrending / Differencing if needed"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["Correlation Engine"]
        C1["ACF Estimator r_k"]
        C2["Durbin-Levinson PACF Solver"]
        C3["CCF Estimator r_XY_k"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Diagnostic Output"]
        O1["Correlogram: r_k vs k"]
        O2["PACF Plot: phi_kk vs k"]
        O3["Cross-Correlogram: r_XY_k vs k"]
        O4["95 percent Confidence Bounds ±1.96 over sqrt n"]
    end
    X1 --> P1 --> P3 --> C1
    X1 --> P1 --> C2
    X1 --> P1 --> C3
    Y1 --> P1
    C1 --> O1
    C2 --> O2
    C3 --> O3
    O1 --> O4
    O2 --> O4
    O3 --> O4
```

## 4.3 Durbin–Levinson Recursion Tree (PACF Construction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["L0: phi_00 = 1"] --> L1["L1: phi_11 = rho_1"]
    L1 --> L2["L2: phi_22 = (rho_2 - rho_1^2)/(1 - rho_1^2)"]
    L2 --> L3["L3: phi_33 = f(rho_1,rho_2,rho_3,phi_21,phi_22)"]
    L3 --> L4["L4: phi_44 = f(rho_1..rho_4, phi_31..phi_33)"]
    L4 --> L5["L_k: phi_kk using all phi_k-1,j and rho_1..rho_k"]
```

## 4.4 CCF Lead–Lag Interpretation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["X_t: Rainfall Series"] -->|"lag k > 0"| B["Y_{t+k}: Future Reservoir Inflow"]
    B -->|"rho_XY positive peak at lag k*"| C["Interpretation: Rainfall Leads Inflow by k* periods"]
    D["Y_t: Past Inflow"] -->|"lag k < 0"| E["X_{t+k}: Future Rainfall"]
    E -->|"rho_XY positive peak at lag -k*"| F["Interpretation: Inflow Leads Rainfall - unusual, check data alignment"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF). Why is the PACF useful in identifying an AR($p$) model?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Autocorrelation Function (ACF)** $\rho_k$ of a stationary series $\{X_t\}$ measures the linear correlation between $X_t$ and $X_{t+k}$:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\gamma_k}{\gamma_0} = \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+k})}{\operatorname{Var}(X_t)}$$

The **Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)** $\phi_{kk}$ measures the correlation between $X_t$ and $X_{t+k}$ *after* removing the linear influence of the intermediate observations $X_{t+1}, \dots, X_{t+k-1}$. It is computed via the Durbin–Levinson recursion:

$$\phi_{kk} = \frac{\rho_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\phi_{k-1,j}\,\rho_{k-j}}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\phi_{k-1,j}\,\rho_j}$$

**Why PACF identifies AR($p$):** For a true AR($p$) process, $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for all $k > p$ (cuts off sharply). Hence the largest lag at which $\phi_{kk}$ is statistically significant (beyond $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$) directly indicates the order $p$.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between autocorrelation function and cross-correlation function. State any three properties of CCF.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | ACF | CCF |
|---|---|---|
| Series | Single $\{X_t\}$ | Pair $\{X_t\}$, $\{Y_t\}$ |
| Symmetry | $\rho_k = \rho_{-k}$ | $\rho_{XY}(k) = \rho_{YX}(-k)$, generally **asymmetric** |
| Interpretation | Self-memory of a series | Lead–lag relationship between two series |
| Range | $\vert \rho_k \vert \le 1$ | $\vert \rho_{XY}(k) \vert \le 1$ |

**Three Properties of CCF:**
1. $\rho_{XY}(k) = \rho_{YX}(-k)$ (skew-symmetric relationship).
2. $\rho_{XY}(k)$ is **not** an even function: peaks at non-zero lags reveal lead–lag.
3. For independent stationary series, $\rho_{XY}(k) = 0$ for all $k \ne 0$.

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the Yule–Walker equations for an AR($p$) process and express them in matrix form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the first three sample autocorrelations of a stationary series of length $n = 64$:
$$r_1 = 0.40, \quad r_2 = 0.20, \quad r_3 = 0.10$$
Compute the first three sample PACF values and identify the appropriate ARMA order using the Bartlett bound. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Write the AR($p$) model:**
$$X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0,\sigma^2)$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $X_{t-k}$ and take expectations** ($k \ge 1$):
$$\operatorname{E}[X_t X_{t-k}] = \sum_{j=1}^{p}\phi_j \operatorname{E}[X_{t-j} X_{t-k}] + \operatorname{E}[\varepsilon_t X_{t-k}]$$

Because $\varepsilon_t$ is independent of $X_{t-k}$ for $k \ge 1$, the last term vanishes. Recognising $\operatorname{E}[X_t X_{t-k}] = \gamma_k$:

$$\gamma_k = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j\,\gamma_{k-j}, \qquad k \ge 1$$

**Step 3 — Normalize by $\gamma_0$:**
$$\rho_k = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j\,\rho_{k-j}, \qquad k \ge 1$$

**Step 4 — Write explicitly for $k = 1, 2, \dots, p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_1 &= \phi_1 + \phi_2\rho_1 + \phi_3\rho_2 + \dots + \phi_p\rho_{p-1} \\
\rho_2 &= \phi_1\rho_1 + \phi_2 + \phi_3\rho_1 + \dots + \phi_p\rho_{p-2} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\rho_p &= \phi_1\rho_{p-1} + \phi_2\rho_{p-2} + \dots + \phi_p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Matrix form:**
$$\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_p\,\boldsymbol{\phi} = \boldsymbol{\rho}_p$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Gamma}_p$ is the $p\times p$ Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix and
$$\boldsymbol{\phi} = [\phi_1,\phi_2,\dots,\phi_p]^T, \quad \boldsymbol{\rho}_p = [\rho_1,\rho_2,\dots,\rho_p]^T$$

Solving yields the Yule–Walker estimates $\hat{\phi}_j$.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Bartlett bound (95%):** $1.96/\sqrt{64} = 1.96/8 = 0.245$.

**Compute $\phi_{11}$:**
$$\phi_{11} = r_1 = 0.40 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \vert 0.40 \vert > 0.245 \;\; \text{(significant)}$$

**Compute $\phi_{22}$:**
$$\phi_{22} = \frac{r_2 - r_1^{2}}{1 - r_1^{2}} = \frac{0.20 - 0.16}{1 - 0.16} = \frac{0.04}{0.84} = 0.0476$$

$\vert 0.0476 \vert < 0.245$ — **not significant**.

**Compute $\phi_{33}$:** First find $\phi_{21} = \phi_{11} - \phi_{22}\phi_{11} = 0.40 - (0.0476)(0.40) = 0.40(1 - 0.0476) = 0.3810$.

$$\phi_{33} = \frac{r_3 - \phi_{21}r_2 - \phi_{22}r_1}{1 - \phi_{21}r_1 - \phi_{22}r_2}$$

Numerator: $0.10 - (0.3810)(0.20) - (0.0476)(0.40) = 0.10 - 0.0762 - 0.0190 = 0.0048$.
Denominator: $1 - (0.3810)(0.40) - (0.0476)(0.20) = 1 - 0.1524 - 0.0095 = 0.8381$.

$$\phi_{33} = \frac{0.0048}{0.8381} \approx 0.0057$$

$\vert 0.0057 \vert < 0.245$ — **not significant**.

**Identification:** Only $\phi_{11}$ is significant; PACF cuts off after lag 1. Therefore, the appropriate model is **AR(1)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Define the cross-correlation function (CCF). Explain how the sign and lag of the CCF are interpreted in the context of a bivariate lead–lag relationship. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the bivariate series below (n = 50), the sample CCF values are given:

| Lag $k$ | $-3$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $+1$ | $+2$ | $+3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $r_{XY}(k)$ | $-0.05$ | $-0.02$ | $0.08$ | $0.45$ | $0.32$ | $0.10$ | $0.04$ |

The Bartlett-type 95% bound for CCF under independence is $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{50} \approx \pm 0.277$. Identify the significant lags and state the lead–lag interpretation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

The **Cross-Correlation Function (CCF)** between two stationary series $\{X_t\}$ and $\{Y_t\}$ is

$$\rho_{XY}(k) = \frac{\gamma_{XY}(k)}{\sqrt{\gamma_{XX}(0)\,\gamma_{YY}(0)}}$$

with sample estimator
$$r_{XY}(k) = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n-k}(x_t - \bar{x})(y_{t+k} - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^{2}\,\sum_{t=1}^{n}(y_t - \bar{y})^{2}}}$$

**Lead–Lag interpretation:**

- **Sign of $\rho_{XY}(k)$:** A **positive** value at lag $k$ indicates that $X_t$ and $Y_{t+k}$ tend to move *together*; a **negative** value indicates that they move *inversely*.
- **Lag direction:**
  - Significant peak at $k = k^{*} > 0$: $X$ at time $t$ is correlated with $Y$ at time $t+k^{*}$. Hence **$Y$ leads $X$** (past values of $Y$ help predict present $X$).
  - Significant peak at $k = -k^{*} < 0$: $X$ leads $Y$.
- **Asymmetry:** Because $\rho_{XY}(k) \ne \rho_{XY}(-k)$ in general, the CCF plot is the *only* standard tool that reveals directional dependence between two time series.

---

**Solution to (b):**

Bartlett bound (95%): $\pm 0.277$.

| Lag $k$ | $r_{XY}(k)$ | $\vert r_{XY}(k) \vert > 0.277$? |
|---|---|---|
| $-3$ | $-0.05$ | No |
| $-2$ | $-0.02$ | No |
| $-1$ | $0.08$ | No |
| $0$ | $0.45$ | **Yes** (contemporaneous) |
| $+1$ | $0.32$ | **Yes** |
| $+2$ | $0.10$ | No |
| $+3$ | $0.04$ | No |

**Significant lags:** $k = 0$ and $k = +1$.

**Interpretation:**
- The contemporaneous correlation at $k = 0$ ($r_{XY}(0) = 0.45$) shows that $X_t$ and $Y_t$ move strongly together at the same instant.
- The significant peak at $k = +1$ indicates that $X_t$ is correlated with $Y_{t+1}$, meaning **$Y$ leads $X$ by one period**. Past values of $Y$ contain predictive information for the present value of $X$.
- A transfer-function model of the form $X_t = \nu_0 Y_t + \nu_1 Y_{t-1} + N_t$ is suggested, where $N_t$ is noise.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing ACF with PACF roles.** ACF cuts off → MA; PACF cuts off → AR. Swapping these forfeits 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the denominator in PACF recursion.** Students often compute $\phi_{kk}$ using only the numerator and miss the normalization term $\left(1 - \sum \phi_{k-1,j}\rho_j\right)$. This gives a wrong answer that examiners *will* deduct marks for.
> 3. **Using $\rho_k$ instead of $r_k$ for hypothesis testing.** The Bartlett bound applies to sample correlations under a white-noise null — never to the true population ACF.
> 4. **Treating CCF as symmetric.** Writing $\rho_{XY}(k) = \rho_{XY}(-k)$ costs 2 marks; the CCF is intentionally *asymmetric* for lead–lag detection.
> 5. **Skipping the stationarity check.** Computing ACF on a non-stationary series is meaningless — always apply differencing or detrending first.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ACF** $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ measures *total* linear dependence at lag $k$; it is symmetric and bounded by $\pm 1$.
- **PACF** $\phi_{kk}$ measures *direct* dependence at lag $k$ after removing intermediate lags; computed via **Durbin–Levinson recursion**.
- **CCF** $\rho_{XY}(k)$ is *asymmetric*: positive lags reveal that $Y$ leads $X$; negative lags reveal that $X$ leads $Y$.
- **Yule–Walker equations** link AR coefficients to autocorrelations through a Toeplitz system; solving it is equivalent to obtaining AR($p$) parameters from sample ACF.
- **Model identification pattern:**
  - AR($p$) → PACF cuts off after $p$; ACF tails off.
  - MA($q$) → ACF cuts off after $q$; PACF tails off.
  - ARMA($p,q$) → both tail off.
- **Bartlett's bound** $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{n}$ provides the 95% significance threshold for testing $r_k = 0$ (white-noise null).
- **Stationarity prerequisite:** ACF/PACF/CCF are defined and meaningful *only* for (weakly) stationary series — always test/transform first.
- **Sample estimators:** $r_k$ uses $\bar{x}$; for large $n$, $\sqrt{n}\,r_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ under white noise.
- **Practical tip for KTU exams:** Always report *significance* of $r_k$ or $\phi_{kk}$ explicitly when identifying model orders — stating values without comparing to the bound loses marks.
- **Engineering applications:** Box–Jenkins identification, spectral analysis, signal alignment, transfer-function modelling, Granger causality testing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autocorrelation Plots: Trend and Seasonality in ACF Plots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Autocorrelation Plots: Trend and Seasonality in ACF Plots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Autocorrelation Plots: Trend and Seasonality in ACF Plots

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Autocorrelation Function (ACF)** of a time series $\{y_t\}$ at lag $k$ is defined as the correlation between the series and its $k$-step lagged version. The **sample ACF** is given by:

$$r_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t-k} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})^2}$$

where $T$ is the number of observations and $\bar{y}$ is the sample mean. A **correlogram** (ACF plot) is the visual representation of $r_k$ plotted against lag $k$, accompanied by 95% confidence bands at $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$. This plot is the **single most important diagnostic tool** in Box-Jenkins methodology for assessing stationarity, identifying trend, and detecting seasonality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The shape of the ACF is a direct fingerprint of the data-generating process. Trending series produce slowly decaying ACFs, while seasonal series produce periodic spikes at seasonal lags.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you record the **outdoor temperature every hour** for a year.

- **Memory of trend:** If today is hot, tomorrow is *likely* hot because summer is a sustained warming trend. The series "remembers" its past strongly and for a long time — analogous to a **long-tailed decay** in the ACF.
- **Memory of seasonality:** At exactly **lag 24 hours (one day)** and **lag 8760 hours (one year)**, the correlation spikes up again because the cycle repeats — analogous to **periodic spikes** in the ACF at seasonal frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The ACF plot is essentially a *memory map* of the time series. A high ACF value at lag $k$ means "the value $k$ time-steps ago is a good predictor of the current value." Trend makes the memory long, seasonality makes the memory rhythmic.

## 1.3 Standard Diagnostic Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Sample ACF | $r_k$ | Detect trend / seasonality |
| Confidence Band | $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$ | Significance threshold |
| Seasonal Lag | $s$ | Period of seasonal cycle |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Three Signature Patterns

The ACF plot can be classified into **three diagnostic archetypes**, each corresponding to a different data-generating process. Mastering these patterns is essential for KTU 2-mark and 14-mark questions.

### Pattern A — Rapid Decay (White Noise / Stationary Series)
- ACF values **drop to near zero immediately** after lag 0.
- All $r_k$ for $k \geq 1$ lie **inside the confidence bands**.
- **Interpretation:** The series has **no memory** beyond the immediate past → stationarity is confirmed.

### Pattern B — Slow Linear Decay (Trending Series)
- $r_1$ is large and **positive** (close to 1).
- $r_k$ values **decay slowly and linearly** toward zero, often taking 10+ lags to cross the confidence band.
- **Interpretation:** Persistent positive correlation between $y_t$ and $y_{t-k}$ → the series contains a **deterministic or stochastic trend** and is **non-stationary in the mean**.

### Pattern C — Seasonal Spikes (Seasonal Series)
- ACF shows **large positive spikes at lag $s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$**.
- Between seasonal lags, the ACF may itself decay.
- **Interpretation:** A **periodic/seasonal component** with period $s$ is present in the data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Case:** A series with *both* trend and seasonality will show a **slowly decaying envelope** (trend) carrying **periodic bumps** (seasonality) at integer multiples of the seasonal period. This is the most common pattern in real economic/meteorological data.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Sample Mean | $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} y_t$ | Centre of the series |
| Sample Variance | $s^2 = \frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})^2$ | Total dispersion |
| Sample Autocovariance | $\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t-k} - \bar{y})$ | Raw joint variability |
| Sample ACF | $r_k = \hat{\gamma}_k / \hat{\gamma}_0$ | Normalized autocorrelation |
| 95% Confidence Bound | $\pm 1.96 / \sqrt{T}$ | Significance threshold |
| White Noise ACF | $r_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$ | No-memory benchmark |
| Random Walk ACF | $r_k \approx 1$ for all $k$ | Unit-root benchmark |
| Seasonal Period | $r_s$ is a significant spike | Period $s$ detected |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Tip (KTU):** A 14-mark KTU question typically awards **2 marks for correctly drawing the ACF**, **3 marks for identifying the trend component**, **3 marks for identifying the seasonal component**, and **6 marks for stating the appropriate Box-Jenkins transformation** (e.g., differencing for trend, seasonal differencing for seasonality).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Economics:** GDP, inflation, and stock indices display strong trend (slow decay).
- **Meteorology:** Hourly temperature shows a 24-hour seasonal cycle with a long-term warming trend (slow decay + 24h spikes).
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Machine vibration signals monitored for periodic failure modes reveal seasonality as ACF spikes.
- **Signal Processing:** ACF is the time-domain equivalent of the power spectral density — its peaks identify dominant frequencies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Manual ACF Computation

Consider the **stationary** series $\{y_t\} = \{3, 5, 4, 6, 5, 7\}$ with $T = 6$. We will compute $r_1, r_2, r_3$ by hand to demonstrate the mechanics.

### Step 1: Compute the Sample Mean

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{3 + 5 + 4 + 6 + 5 + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5
$$

### Step 2: Compute Deviations from Mean

| $t$ | $y_t$ | $y_t - \bar{y}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | -2 |
| 2 | 5 | 0 |
| 3 | 4 | -1 |
| 4 | 6 | 1 |
| 5 | 5 | 0 |
| 6 | 7 | 2 |

### Step 3: Compute $\hat{\gamma}_0$ (Variance Numerator)

$$
\hat{\gamma}_0 = (-2)^2 + 0^2 + (-1)^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 + 2^2 = 4 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 4 = 10
$$

### Step 4: Compute $\hat{\gamma}_1$ (Lag-1 Autocovariance)

Pair $y_t$ with $y_{t-1}$ for $t = 2, \ldots, 6$:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_1 = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=2}^{6}(y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t-1} - \bar{y})
$$

Expanding term by term:
- $t=2$: $(0)(-2) = 0$
- $t=3$: $(-1)(0) = 0$
- $t=4$: $(1)(-1) = -1$
- $t=5$: $(0)(1) = 0$
- $t=6$: $(2)(0) = 0$

$$
\hat{\gamma}_1 = \frac{0 + 0 - 1 + 0 + 0}{6} = -\frac{1}{6} \approx -0.1667
$$

### Step 5: Compute $r_1$

$$
r_1 = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_1}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = \frac{-1/6}{10/6} = \frac{-1}{10} = -0.10
$$

### Step 6: Compute $r_2$ (Lag-2)

Pair $y_t$ with $y_{t-2}$ for $t = 3, \ldots, 6$:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_2 = \frac{1}{6}\big[(-1)(-2) + (1)(0) + (0)(-1) + (2)(1)\big] = \frac{2 + 0 + 0 + 2}{6} = \frac{4}{6}
$$

$$
r_2 = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_2}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = \frac{4/6}{10/6} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.40
$$

### Step 7: Compute $r_3$ (Lag-3)

Pair $y_t$ with $y_{t-3}$ for $t = 4, 5, 6$:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_3 = \frac{1}{6}\big[(1)(-2) + (0)(0) + (2)(-1)\big] = \frac{-2 + 0 - 2}{6} = -\frac{4}{6}
$$

$$
r_3 = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_3}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = \frac{-4/6}{10/6} = -\frac{4}{10} = -0.40
$$

### Step 8: Build the ACF Table

| Lag $k$ | $r_k$ | 95% Bound $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{6}$ | Significant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | -0.10 | $\pm 0.80$ | No |
| 2 | 0.40 | $\pm 0.80$ | No |
| 3 | -0.40 | $\pm 0.80$ | No |

**Conclusion:** All $r_k$ values lie within the confidence band → the series behaves like **white noise (stationary)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $T = 6$ is small, the confidence band is wide ($\pm 0.80$). In KTU problems, a larger $T$ (e.g., $T = 100$) tightens the bound to $\pm 0.196$, making trend and seasonality much easier to detect.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How to Read an ACF

| ACF Behaviour | Diagnosed Component | Box-Jenkins Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| $r_1 \approx 1$, slow linear decay to 0 | **Trend** (non-stationary mean) | First-difference: $\nabla y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$ |
| Large spikes at lags $s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$ | **Seasonality** (period $s$) | Seasonal difference: $\nabla_s y_t = y_t - y_{t-s}$ |
| Both slow decay and seasonal spikes | **Trend + Seasonality** | Apply $\nabla$ then $\nabla_s$ (or vice versa) |
| No significant spike beyond lag 0 | **White noise** | No transformation needed |
| Alternating signs (sine pattern) | **Over-differenced** | Reduce order of differencing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart for Interpreting ACF Plots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Observe ACF Plot] --> B{r1 is large and positive?}
    B -- Yes --> C{ACF decays slowly?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Diagnose TREND]
    D --> D1[Apply First Differencing]
    C -- No --> E[Diagnose MA signature]
    B -- No --> F{Spikes at lag s, 2s, 3s?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Diagnose SEASONALITY with period s]
    G --> G1[Apply Seasonal Differencing]
    F -- No --> H{All rk inside bands for k >= 1?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Series is STATIONARY White Noise]
    I --> I1[No Differencing Needed]
    H -- No --> J[Check PACF for AR order]
    D1 --> K[Re-plot ACF and Verify]
    G1 --> K
    E --> K
    I1 --> L[Proceed to ARIMA Model Identification]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map — ACF Behaviour vs Data Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TypeA[Type 1: Trending Series]
        A1[ACF: r1 approx 1]
        A2[ACF: Slow linear decay]
        A3[Diagnosis: Non-stationary mean]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end
    subgraph TypeB[Type 2: Seasonal Series]
        B1[ACF: Spike at lag s]
        B2[ACF: Spike at lag 2s]
        B3[Diagnosis: Seasonal period s]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph TypeC[Type 3: Stationary]
        C1[ACF: r1 small]
        C2[ACF: Rapid drop to zero]
        C3[Diagnosis: White noise or ARMA]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3
    end
    TypeA --> Rem[Remedy: Differencing]
    TypeB --> Rem
    TypeC --> NoRem[No Remedy: Identify ARMA p, q]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Aid Description (for printed notes):** Imagine three side-by-side correlograms. The **first** shows vertical bars (lags 1, 2, 3, …, 15) gradually shrinking from a height of 1.0 down to 0 — this is the *trending series* fingerprint. The **second** shows tall bars at lags 4, 8, 12, 16 and short bars elsewhere — the *seasonal series* fingerprint. The **third** shows a single bar of height 1 at lag 0 and nothing else crossing the dotted confidence line — the *stationary white noise* fingerprint.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the sample autocorrelation function. What is the 95% confidence band used to test whether an ACF value is significantly different from zero?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The sample autocorrelation function at lag $k$ is the correlation coefficient between the series $\{y_t\}$ and its $k$-step lag $\{y_{t-k}\}$, given by:
$$r_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t-k} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})^2}$$
The 95% confidence band for testing $H_0: \rho_k = 0$ is $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$. If $\vert r_k \vert > 1.96/\sqrt{T}$, the lag-$k$ autocorrelation is statistically significant.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*How would the ACF plot of a monthly time series with a strong seasonal period of 12 months look?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The ACF plot would show **large positive spikes at lags 12, 24, 36, …** and smaller (or insignificant) values at all non-seasonal lags. This is because observations 12 months apart are highly correlated (e.g., January 2024 resembles January 2023). The 24-month lag is significant because 24 = 2 × 12, and so on. The seasonal pattern creates a periodic "comb" of significant ACF bars.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain with a labelled sketch how the ACF plot of a **purely trending non-stationary series** differs from that of a **stationary white noise series**. State the appropriate transformation in each case.

**(b)** [7 marks] A quarterly sales series of $T = 64$ observations has an ACF with $r_4 = 0.78$, $r_8 = 0.62$, $r_{12} = 0.48$, and $r_1 = 0.91$. Diagnose the series and recommend a Box-Jenkins transformation. Justify with the 95% confidence bound.

---

#### Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks

**Purely Trending Series (3 marks):**
- $r_1$ is very large (close to 1), typically $\geq 0.9$.
- Subsequent $r_k$ values **decay slowly and positively**, taking many lags to cross the confidence band.
- The decay pattern is roughly linear on a bar plot.
- **Box-Jenkins remedy:** Apply **first-order differencing** $\nabla y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$ to remove the trend.

**White Noise Series (3 marks):**
- $r_1, r_2, \ldots$ are all **small in magnitude** (near zero).
- All $r_k$ for $k \geq 1$ lie **inside the 95% confidence bands** $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$.
- **Box-Jenkins remedy:** **No transformation** is required; the series is already stationary.

**Sketch Description (1 mark):**
- Trending series: tall bar at lag 1, gradually shrinking bars; bars stay above the dotted confidence line for many lags.
- White noise: a single bar of height 1 at lag 0, all other bars are short and inside the confidence bands.

---

#### Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Compute the 95% confidence bound (1 mark):**
$$
\pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{64}} = \pm \frac{1.96}{8} = \pm 0.245
$$

**Step 2: Test each ACF value (3 marks):**
| Lag $k$ | $r_k$ | Significant? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.91 | Yes (well above 0.245) |
| 4 | 0.78 | Yes (well above 0.245) |
| 8 | 0.62 | Yes (above 0.245) |
| 12 | 0.48 | Yes (above 0.245) |

**Step 3: Diagnose (2 marks):**
- The very high $r_1 = 0.91$ and slowly decaying pattern indicate a **strong trend** in the series.
- The significant spikes at lags **4, 8, 12** are integer multiples of 4 → this indicates **seasonality with period $s = 4$** (quarterly data).

**Step 4: Box-Jenkins Transformation (1 mark):**
Apply **both** transformations in sequence:
$$
\nabla_4 (\nabla y_t) = (y_t - y_{t-1}) - (y_{t-4} - y_{t-5})
$$
This removes both the trend and the seasonal component.

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the sample autocorrelation coefficient $r_k$ for lag $k$ from first principles. State the assumptions on the series required for the ACF to be a meaningful diagnostic.

**(b)** [7 marks] The ACF of a 100-observation series is $r_1 = 0.65$, $r_2 = 0.42$, $r_3 = 0.27$, $r_4 = 0.18$, $r_5 = 0.11$, $r_6 = 0.07$. All values are positive and decay. Diagnose the series and propose the next step.

---

#### Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The sample autocovariance at lag $k$ generalizes the sample covariance formula. For two random variables $X$ and $Y$, the covariance is:
$$
\text{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)]
$$
Replacing the population mean with the sample mean $\bar{y}$, the **sample autocovariance** at lag $k$ is:
$$
\hat{\gamma}_k = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t-k} - \bar{y})
$$
The **sample autocorrelation** normalizes by the variance $\hat{\gamma}_0$:
$$
r_k = \frac{\hat{\gamma}_k}{\hat{\gamma}_0} = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t-k} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})^2}
$$

**Required Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The series must have a **finite and time-invariant mean** so that $\bar{y}$ is a stable centre.
2. The series must be **second-order stationary** (constant variance and autocovariance depending only on lag, not on $t$).
3. Sufficient sample size $T$ is required so that $r_k$ is a reliable estimator of the theoretical $\rho_k$.

---

#### Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Confidence bound (1 mark):**
$$
\pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{100}} = \pm 0.196
$$

**Step 2: Test significance (2 marks):**
| Lag $k$ | $r_k$ | Significant? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.65 | Yes |
| 2 | 0.42 | Yes |
| 3 | 0.27 | Yes |
| 4 | 0.18 | No (just below 0.196) |
| 5 | 0.11 | No |
| 6 | 0.07 | No |

**Step 3: Diagnose (2 marks):**
The ACF is **positive and decays slowly** (significant at lags 1, 2, 3). The pattern is the classic signature of a **trending, non-stationary** series. There is **no periodicity** (no spikes at regular intervals), so seasonality is absent.

**Step 4: Next step (2 marks):**
Apply **first-order differencing**: $w_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$. Re-plot the ACF of $\{w_t\}$ and confirm that the new ACF cuts off quickly. If it does, the differenced series is stationary and ARMA identification can proceed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the confidence band:** Students often quote ACF values without testing against $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$. A spike is **only meaningful** if it exceeds the band. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing ACF and PACF:** ACF detects **MA order $q$** and is used for stationarity/seasonality. PACF detects **AR order $p$**. Misattribution loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Over-differencing:** A sine-wave alternation in ACF (positive, negative, positive…) means **over-differencing**. Reduce the order, do not add more $\nabla$.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify the seasonal period $s$:** Saying "the series is seasonal" without quoting the lag $s$ of the spike is **incomplete** for KTU full marks.
> 5. **Ignoring lag 0:** $r_0 = 1$ always. Do not include it in the "decay" interpretation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ACF Definition:** $r_k$ is the correlation of $\{y_t\}$ with its $k$-step lag, normalized by the variance.
- **95% Confidence Bound:** $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}$ — anything crossing this line is statistically significant.
- **Trend Signature:** Large positive $r_1$, slow linear decay across many lags.
- **Seasonality Signature:** Significant spikes at lags $s, 2s, 3s, \ldots$ for seasonal period $s$.
- **Stationarity Signature:** Rapid cut-off to zero; all $r_k$ for $k \geq 1$ inside the band.
- **Remedy for Trend:** First differencing $\nabla y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$.
- **Remedy for Seasonality:** Seasonal differencing $\nabla_s y_t = y_t - y_{t-s}$.
- **Remedy for Both:** Apply both transformations; re-plot ACF to verify stationarity.
- **Always state $T$ and the confidence bound** before declaring significance.
- **ACF is read in conjunction with PACF** in Box-Jenkins methodology for full ARIMA $(p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_s$ identification.
- **Real-world examples to memorize:** Monthly retail sales (seasonal $s=12$), quarterly GDP (seasonal $s=4$), daily temperatures (seasonal $s=7$ or $s=365$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoregressive (AR) Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autoregressive (AR) Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Autoregressive (AR) Model

An **Autoregressive (AR) Model** is a stochastic time series model in which the current value of a series $X_t$ is expressed as a **finite, linear combination of its own past values** (called *lags*) plus a random shock (white noise) term. The word *autoregressive* literally means *"regressing the variable on itself"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> A general **Autoregressive model of order $p$**, denoted **AR($p$)**, is defined by the linear difference equation:
> $$X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$
> where $c$ is a constant (drift), $\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_p$ are the **autoregressive coefficients (parameters)**, and $\varepsilon_t$ is assumed to be a **white noise** process with mean **0** and constant variance $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$, i.e., $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$.

The order $p$ specifies how many past observations are linearly combined to predict the present. Thus:
- AR(1): only the immediate previous value is used.
- AR(2): the previous two values are used.
- AR($p$): the previous $p$ values are used.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition — "The Memory of a Process":**
> Imagine you are trying to guess tomorrow's stock market closing price. You do not start from scratch every day; instead, you look at the prices from yesterday, the day before, and perhaps a week ago, because financial markets possess **inertia** and **momentum**.
>
> An Autoregressive model works in exactly the same way. It says: *"The best guess of today's value is a weighted average of what happened in the recent past, plus some unpredictable random noise."* The past acts as the "memory" of the series, and the $\phi_i$ weights determine *how strongly* each past memory influences today.
>
> A simple real-world example: **Today's temperature** in a coastal city is strongly influenced by yesterday's temperature. If you also add the temperature from two days ago, you are essentially building an **AR(2) model** for daily temperature.

## 1.3 Components of an AR($p$) Model

| Component | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Metric / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constant / Drift | $c$ | Long-run mean level of the series | Real number |
| AR Coefficients | $\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_p$ | Influence of past values | Determined by stationarity condition |
| White Noise | $\varepsilon_t$ | Random unobservable shock | $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$ |
| Order | $p$ | Number of lagged terms | Positive integer |

## 1.4 Special Cases of AR Models

- **AR(0)**: $X_t = \varepsilon_t$ — this is simply **white noise** with no memory.
