- **AR(1)**: $X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ — the simplest "memory" model.
- **AR(2)**: $X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$ — captures cyclical/oscillatory behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024):** AR models are a special case of the more general **ARMA (Autoregressive Moving Average)** family, where only the autoregressive (past-value) part is retained, with no moving-average component.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need AR Models?

Most real-world time series (stock prices, weather, EEG signals, sales data) exhibit **serial correlation** — meaning consecutive observations are not independent. A naïve assumption of independence leads to:
- Biased standard errors
- Inefficient forecasts
- Poor residual diagnostics

AR models provide a **parsimonious, interpretable, and statistically efficient** framework to capture this temporal dependence using only a few parameters.

## 2.2 Stationarity Condition for AR($p$) — The "Why" and "How"

An AR($p$) model is **second-order stationary** *only if* its parameters satisfy the **stationarity condition**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why stationarity matters:** A stationary process has a constant mean, constant variance, and a covariance that depends *only* on the lag between observations, not on actual time. Without stationarity, classical statistical inference (confidence intervals, hypothesis tests) breaks down.

### 2.2.1 Characteristic Equation Approach (The "How")

Rewrite the AR($p$) model using the **backshift operator** $B$, where $B X_t = X_{t-1}$:

$$X_t = c + \phi_1 B X_t + \phi_2 B^2 X_t + \dots + \phi_p B^p X_t + \varepsilon_t$$

Bring all terms to one side and factor:

$$(1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p) X_t = c + \varepsilon_t$$

Define the **AR characteristic polynomial**:

$$\Phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Stationarity Rule:**
> The AR($p$) process is **stationary if and only if all roots of the characteristic equation $\Phi(B) = 0$ lie strictly outside the unit circle** (i.e., $|z_i| > 1$ for all $i = 1, 2, \dots, p$). Equivalently, all roots of the **reciprocal polynomial** $1 - \phi_1 z - \phi_2 z^2 - \dots - \phi_p z^p = 0$ must lie **inside the unit circle** ($|z_i| < 1$).

### 2.2.2 Special Case — Stationarity for AR(1)

For $X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, the condition reduces to:

$$|\phi_1| < 1$$

This is the single most-tested result in KTU module-level questions.

### 2.2.3 Special Case — Stationarity for AR(2)

For $X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$, the joint stationarity conditions are:

$$-1 < \phi_2 < 1$$

$$\phi_1 + \phi_2 < 1$$

$$\phi_2 - \phi_1 < 1$$

This defines a **triangular stationarity region** in the $(\phi_1, \phi_2)$ parameter space.

## 2.3 Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF) of an AR($p$) Process

These are the two diagnostic tools that allow you to **identify** the order $p$ from data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Identification Rule (CRITICAL for KTU):**
> - For an **AR($p$) process**, the **ACF tails off (decays gradually)** in an exponential or sinusoidal pattern.
> - The **PACF cuts off sharply** (becomes zero) **after lag $p$**.
> This is the *opposite* of an MA process, where ACF cuts off and PACF tails off.

## 2.4 The Yule–Walker Equations

The Yule–Walker equations express the **autocorrelations $\rho_k$** in terms of the AR coefficients $\phi_i$. They are foundational for both parameter estimation and theoretical ACF computation.

For an AR($p$) process with mean 0, the general Yule–Walker relation is:

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2} + \dots + \phi_p \rho_{k-p}, \quad \text{for } k \geq 1$$

with $\rho_0 = 1$ and $\rho_{-k} = \rho_k$.

Written in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \rho_1 & \rho_2 & \cdots & \rho_{p-1} \\ \rho_1 & 1 & \rho_1 & \cdots & \rho_{p-2} \\ \rho_2 & \rho_1 & 1 & \cdots & \rho_{p-3} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \rho_{p-1} & \rho_{p-2} & \rho_{p-3} & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \\ \phi_3 \\ \vdots \\ \phi_p \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho_1 \\ \rho_2 \\ \rho_3 \\ \vdots \\ \rho_p \end{bmatrix}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table before the exam — it covers $\geq 80\%$ of the KTU module marks for AR Models.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AR($p$) model | $X_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$ | $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ |
| 2 | Backshift form | $\Phi(B) X_t = c + \varepsilon_t$ | $\Phi(B) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i B^i$ |
| 3 | Stationarity (general) | All roots of $\Phi(B) = 0$ satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$ | Equivalently, $\vert z \vert < 1$ for reciprocal |
| 4 | Stationarity AR(1) | $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ | Single, most-tested condition |
| 5 | Stationarity AR(2) | $\phi_2 \pm \phi_1 < 1$ and $-1 < \phi_2 < 1$ | Triangular region |
| 6 | Yule–Walker (scalar) | $\rho_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \rho_{\vert k-i \vert}$ | $k \geq 1$, with $\rho_0 = 1$ |
| 7 | Yule–Walker (matrix) | $\boldsymbol{R} \boldsymbol{\phi} = \boldsymbol{\rho}$ | $\boldsymbol{R}$ is $(p \times p)$ Toeplitz matrix |
| 8 | Mean of stationary AR(1) | $\mu = \dfrac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$ | Only valid if $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ |
| 9 | Mean of stationary AR(2) | $\mu = \dfrac{c}{1 - \phi_1 - \phi_2}$ | Only valid if stationary |
| 10 | Variance of stationary AR(1) | $\text{Var}(X_t) = \dfrac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$ | Geometric-series sum result |
| 11 | ACF of AR(1) | $\rho_k = \phi_1^{\vert k \vert}$ | Geometric decay |
| 12 | ACF of AR(2) | Recurrence $\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}$ | Initial: $\rho_0=1, \rho_1=\phi_1/(1-\phi_2)$ |
| 13 | PACF of AR($p$) | $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for $k > p$ | Cuts off after lag $p$ |
| 14 | Yule–Walker estimator | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}} = \hat{\boldsymbol{R}}^{-1} \hat{\boldsymbol{\rho}}$ | Method-of-moments estimator |
| 15 | Innovation form (forecast) | $X_{t+h} = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \psi_i X_{t+h-i} + \varepsilon_{t+h}$ | $\psi_i$ decay under stationarity |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of AR Models

- **Electrical Engineering:** Modelling noise in communication channels and AR-based speech synthesis (vocal tract modelled as AR filter).
- **Civil Engineering:** Predicting river flow, rainfall, structural vibration response.
- **Economics & Finance:** Forecasting stock returns, inflation, GDP growth.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Modelling EEG, ECG, and EMG signals (autoregressive biosignal models).
- **Industrial Engineering:** Demand forecasting for inventory and supply chain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean of a Stationary AR(1) Process

**Given:** $X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, with $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ and $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$.

**Step 1 — Take the expectation of both sides.** Because of stationarity, $E(X_t) = E(X_{t-1}) = \mu$.

$$\mu = c + \phi_1 \mu + E(\varepsilon_t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$.**

$$\mu = c + \phi_1 \mu$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\mu$ by subtracting $\phi_1 \mu$ from both sides.**

$$\mu - \phi_1 \mu = c$$

$$\mu (1 - \phi_1) = c$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $(1 - \phi_1)$ — which is nonzero precisely when $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$.**

$$\mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$$

This is the long-run mean that the series fluctuates around.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Variance of a Stationary AR(1) Process

**Given:** same AR(1) process. **Find:** $\text{Var}(X_t) = \gamma_0$.

**Step 1 — Write the mean-corrected form** $Y_t = X_t - \mu$. Then $\mu = c/(1-\phi_1)$ and

$$Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2 — Take the variance of both sides.** Note $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$ and $\varepsilon_t$ is independent of $Y_{t-1}$.

$$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \phi_1^2 \text{Var}(Y_{t-1}) + \text{Var}(\varepsilon_t)$$

**Step 3 — Use stationarity $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \text{Var}(Y_{t-1}) = \gamma_0$.**

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1^2 \gamma_0 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\gamma_0$.**

$$\gamma_0 (1 - \phi_1^2) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

$$\gamma_0 = \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

This shows the variance is finite **only when** $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ — reinforcing the stationarity condition.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: ACF of an AR(1) Process

**Step 1 — Multiply the mean-corrected AR(1) by $Y_{t-k}$ and take expectations.**

$$E(Y_t Y_{t-k}) = \phi_1 E(Y_{t-1} Y_{t-k}) + E(\varepsilon_t Y_{t-k})$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that for $k \geq 1$, $\varepsilon_t$ is independent of all past $Y_{t-k}$, so $E(\varepsilon_t Y_{t-k}) = 0$.**

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1}$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $\gamma_0$.**

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1}$$

**Step 4 — Iterate the recursion starting from $\rho_0 = 1$.**

$$\rho_1 = \phi_1 \rho_0 = \phi_1$$

$$\rho_2 = \phi_1 \rho_1 = \phi_1^2$$

$$\rho_k = \phi_1^{\vert k \vert}$$

The ACF of an AR(1) process **decays geometrically** with ratio $\phi_1$ — this is the canonical "tails off" signature of an AR process.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Yule–Walker Equations for AR(2)

**Given:** $X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$ (mean zero, stationary).

**Step 1 — Compute $\gamma_0 = E(X_t^2)$ by multiplying by $X_t$ and taking expectation.**

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1 \gamma_1 + \phi_2 \gamma_2 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $X_{t-1}$ and take expectation.** Independence of $\varepsilon_t$ with the past gives:

$$\gamma_1 = \phi_1 \gamma_0 + \phi_2 \gamma_1$$

Solving for $\gamma_1$:

$$\gamma_1 (1 - \phi_2) = \phi_1 \gamma_0$$

$$\gamma_1 = \frac{\phi_1 \gamma_0}{1 - \phi_2}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $X_{t-2}$ and take expectation.**

$$\gamma_2 = \phi_1 \gamma_1 + \phi_2 \gamma_0$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to obtain autocorrelations $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$.**

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\phi_1}{1 - \phi_2}$$

$$\rho_2 = \phi_1 \rho_1 + \phi_2$$

These are the first two autocorrelations of an AR(2) process and serve as the basis for the Yule–Walker estimator.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** Consider the AR(1) process $X_t = 4 + 0.6 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 1.6$. Compute (a) the mean, (b) the variance, and (c) the first two autocorrelations.

### Part (a) — Mean

$$\mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1} = \frac{4}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{4}{0.4} = 10$$

### Part (b) — Variance

$$\gamma_0 = \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2} = \frac{1.6}{1 - 0.36} = \frac{1.6}{0.64} = 2.5$$

### Part (c) — Autocorrelations

$$\rho_1 = \phi_1^{\vert 1 \vert} = 0.6$$

$$\rho_2 = \phi_1^{\vert 2 \vert} = 0.36$$

## 3.6 Model Selection Criteria (KTU 2024 — AIC & BIC)

When choosing $p$, balance **goodness-of-fit** against **parsimony**:

$$\text{AIC}(p) = -2 \ln L_{\max} + 2(p+1)$$

$$\text{BIC}(p) = -2 \ln L_{\max} + (p+1) \ln n$$

- $L_{\max}$ is the maximized likelihood.
- $n$ is the sample size.
- $(p+1)$ counts the $p$ AR coefficients plus the variance $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$.
- **Choose the model with the smallest AIC and BIC.**

## 3.7 Forecasting with an AR Model

For an AR($p$) model, the **$h$-step ahead forecast** is:

$$\hat{X}_{t+h} = c + \phi_1 \hat{X}_{t+h-1} + \phi_2 \hat{X}_{t+h-2} + \dots + \phi_p \hat{X}_{t+h-p}$$

where unobserved future values are replaced by their forecasts, and the forecast error variance grows with $h$, asymptotically approaching the unconditional variance $\gamma_0$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of an AR($p$) Model — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of an AR($p$) model — how the lagged inputs, coefficients, and noise combine to produce the current output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1["X sub t minus 1"] --> mul1["Multiply by phi 1"]
    input2["X sub t minus 2"] --> mul2["Multiply by phi 2"]
    input3["X sub t minus p"] --> mulp["Multiply by phi p"]
    const["Constant c"] --> sum1((Sum))
    noise["White noise eps sub t"] --> sum1((Sum))
    mul1["Multiply by phi 1"] --> sum1((Sum))
    mul2["Multiply by phi 2"] --> sum1((Sum))
    mulp["Multiply by phi p"] --> sum1((Sum))
    sum1((Sum)) --> output["X sub t OUTPUT"]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow of AR Model Identification, Estimation, and Forecasting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Start: Observed Time Series Data"] --> stepB["Step 1: Plot Series and Check Stationarity"]
    stepB --> stepC{"Stationary?"}
    stepC -- "No" --> stepD["Apply Differencing to make Stationary"]
    stepD --> stepB
    stepC -- "Yes" --> stepE["Step 2: Compute ACF and PACF"]
    stepE --> stepF["Step 3: Identify AR Order p from PACF Cutoff"]
    stepF --> stepG["Step 4: Estimate Coefficients via Yule Walker or OLS"]
    stepG --> stepH["Step 5: Diagnostic Check on Residuals"]
    stepH --> stepI{"Residuals White Noise?"}
    stepI -- "No" --> stepF
    stepI -- "Yes" --> stepJ["Step 6: Forecast Future Values"]
    stepJ --> stepK["End: Validated AR Model"]
```

## 4.3 Stationarity Region for an AR(2) Process — Parameter Space Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis1["Phi 2 axis"] --> region["Triangular Stationarity Region"]
    region --> cond1["Condition 1: phi 2 greater than negative 1"]
    region --> cond2["Condition 2: phi 2 less than 1"]
    region --> cond3["Condition 3: phi 1 plus phi 2 less than 1"]
    region --> cond4["Condition 4: phi 2 minus phi 1 less than 1"]
    cond1 --> outside["Outside region = Non Stationary Process"]
    cond2 --> outside["Outside region = Non Stationary Process"]
    cond3 --> outside["Outside region = Non Stationary Process"]
    cond4 --> outside["Outside region = Non Stationary Process"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Cue:** Inside the triangular region, the AR(2) series is **stationary** — it fluctuates around a fixed mean with constant variance. Outside, the series is **explosive** (variance grows without bound) or **non-invertible**, and classical statistical inference becomes invalid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an Autoregressive AR($p$) model. State the stationarity condition for an AR(1) process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **AR($p$) model** expresses the current value of a series as a linear combination of its $p$ past values and a white noise shock:

$$X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$$

where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$.

For an **AR(1) process** $X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, the **stationarity condition** is:

$$\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$$

That is, the autoregressive coefficient must lie strictly between $-1$ and $1$. If this condition holds, the process has finite mean $\mu = c/(1 - \phi_1)$ and finite variance $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 / (1 - \phi_1^2)$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**How does the PACF of an AR($p$) process behave? Why is this useful for model identification?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)** of a stationary AR($p$) process **cuts off sharply after lag $p$** — that is, $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for all $k > p$, while $\phi_{kk} \neq 0$ for $k \leq p$.

This behaviour is **useful for model identification** because by plotting the sample PACF of an observed series and observing where it first crosses zero (within confidence bands), we can directly infer the order $p$ of the AR model. In contrast, the ACF of an AR($p$) process **tails off gradually** (exponentially or as damped sinusoids), so it cannot pinpoint the order on its own.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Derive the mean, variance, and autocovariance function of a stationary AR(1) process $X_t = c + \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, where $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$ and $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the AR(2) process $X_t = 0.5 X_{t-1} + 0.3 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$, verify stationarity and compute $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Mean, Variance, Autocovariance of AR(1) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Mean.** By stationarity, $E(X_t) = E(X_{t-1}) = \mu$ and $E(\varepsilon_t) = 0$.

$$\mu = c + \phi_1 \mu + 0 \implies \mu = \frac{c}{1 - \phi_1}$$

**Step 2 — Mean-corrected form.** Let $Y_t = X_t - \mu$. Then

$$Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 3 — Variance.** Take variance of both sides. By independence of $\varepsilon_t$ and $Y_{t-1}$:

$$\gamma_0 = \phi_1^2 \gamma_0 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \implies \gamma_0 = \frac{\sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

**Step 4 — Autocovariance for $k \geq 1$.** Multiply $Y_t = \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ by $Y_{t-k}$ and take expectation. Since $\varepsilon_t$ is independent of $Y_{t-k}$:

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1 \gamma_{k-1}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the recursion.**

$$\gamma_k = \phi_1^k \gamma_0 = \frac{\phi_1^{\vert k \vert} \sigma_\varepsilon^2}{1 - \phi_1^2}$$

This proves that the ACF of a stationary AR(1) process decays geometrically as $\rho_k = \phi_1^{\vert k \vert}$.

---

**Part (b) — Stationarity and Autocorrelations of AR(2) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Check stationarity conditions.** For AR(2) $X_t = 0.5 X_{t-1} + 0.3 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$, we have $\phi_1 = 0.5$ and $\phi_2 = 0.3$. The three stationarity conditions are:

$$\phi_2 > -1 \implies 0.3 > -1 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\phi_2 < 1 \implies 0.3 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\phi_1 + \phi_2 < 1 \implies 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\phi_2 - \phi_1 < 1 \implies 0.3 - 0.5 = -0.2 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

All conditions are satisfied, so the process **is stationary**.

**Step 2 — Compute $\rho_1$ using the Yule–Walker formula.**

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\phi_1}{1 - \phi_2} = \frac{0.5}{1 - 0.3} = \frac{0.5}{0.7} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\rho_2$ using the Yule–Walker recurrence.**

$$\rho_2 = \phi_1 \rho_1 + \phi_2 = 0.5 \times 0.7143 + 0.3 = 0.3571 + 0.3 = 0.6571$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** The series has a strong positive autocorrelation at lag 1 ($\approx 0.71$) and the ACF tails off (as expected for an AR(2) process).

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the Yule–Walker equations for an AR($p$) process. Write them in matrix form and briefly describe how they are used to estimate the autoregressive parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the behaviour of the ACF and PACF for an AR($p$) process. Explain how these help in identifying the order $p$ from sample data. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Yule–Walker Equations (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition.** The Yule–Walker equations relate the autocorrelations $\rho_k$ of a stationary AR($p$) process to its autoregressive parameters $\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_p$. Multiplying the AR equation by $X_{t-k}$, taking expectations, and dividing by $\gamma_0$ yields, for $k = 1, 2, \dots, p$:

$$\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2} + \dots + \phi_p \rho_{k-p}$$

**Step 2 — Matrix form.** Stacking $p$ such equations for $k = 1, 2, \dots, p$ gives a linear system $\boldsymbol{R} \boldsymbol{\phi} = \boldsymbol{\rho}$, where

$$\boldsymbol{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \rho_1 & \rho_2 & \cdots & \rho_{p-1} \\ \rho_1 & 1 & \rho_1 & \cdots & \rho_{p-2} \\ \rho_2 & \rho_1 & 1 & \cdots & \rho_{p-3} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \rho_{p-1} & \rho_{p-2} & \rho_{p-3} & \cdots & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\phi} = \begin{bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \\ \vdots \\ \phi_p \end{bmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\rho} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho_1 \\ \rho_2 \\ \vdots \\ \rho_p \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Parameter estimation.** Replace the theoretical $\rho_k$ with the **sample autocorrelations** $r_k$ computed from data. The resulting system is solved as

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}} = \hat{\boldsymbol{R}}^{-1} \hat{\boldsymbol{\rho}}$$

This is the **method-of-moments** estimator and provides a consistent, computationally efficient starting point for AR coefficient estimation. OLS and maximum likelihood are alternative estimators that are asymptotically equivalent under Gaussianity.

---

**Part (b) — ACF and PACF Behaviour (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — ACF of AR($p$).** The autocorrelation function of a stationary AR($p$) process **tails off gradually** rather than cutting off. Specifically, the theoretical ACF satisfies the same homogeneous recurrence as the AR process itself, leading to a mixture of exponentials and damped sinusoids. For example:
- AR(1): $\rho_k = \phi_1^{\vert k \vert}$ — pure geometric decay.
- AR(2): $\rho_k$ oscillates or decays exponentially depending on the sign of $\phi_2$.

**Step 2 — PACF of AR($p$).** The partial autocorrelation function **cuts off sharply after lag $p$**. Formally, $\phi_{kk} = 0$ for all $k > p$, while $\phi_{kk}$ is non-zero (in general) for $k \leq p$. This is because $\phi_{kk}$ is the coefficient of $X_{t-k}$ in the best linear regression of $X_t$ on its $k$ lags, and the AR($p$) structure makes $X_{t-k}$ redundant for $k > p$.

**Step 3 — Use in identification.** Plot the sample ACF and PACF of the observed series with $\pm 2/\sqrt{n}$ confidence bounds.
- If the **PACF cuts off after lag $p$** (last significant spike at lag $p$) and the **ACF tails off**, fit an **AR($p$) model**.
- This dual signature is the most reliable graphical identification tool in the **Box–Jenkins methodology**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ACF and PACF behaviour:** A frequent mistake is to claim the ACF of an AR($p$) process cuts off. **The ACF tails off; the PACF cuts off after lag $p$.** Reversing these statements will cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the stationarity condition:** Any derivation of mean or variance of an AR process *must* first explicitly state the stationarity condition ($\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ for AR(1), or the three conditions for AR(2)). Skipping this earns partial credit only.
> 3. **Mixing up Yule–Walker with OLS:** Yule–Walker is a *method-of-moments* estimator using sample autocorrelations, **not** the OLS estimator. Naming the wrong method loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Missing the $E(\varepsilon_t Y_{t-k}) = 0$ step:** When deriving the ACF, you **must** justify that white noise is uncorrelated with past observations. Without this, the derivation is incomplete.
> 5. **Not mentioning the assumption of constant variance (homoscedasticity):** Examiners often award a separate mark for stating that $\varepsilon_t$ has *constant* variance $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$, not just zero mean.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 20 Exam-Day Must-Know Points for AR Models:**

1. **AR($p$) form:** $X_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i X_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$, with $\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)$.
2. **Backshift notation:** $\Phi(B) X_t = c + \varepsilon_t$, where $\Phi(B) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i B^i$.
3. **Stationarity (general):** All roots of $\Phi(B) = 0$ lie *outside* the unit circle ($\vert z \vert > 1$).
4. **Stationarity AR(1):** $\vert \phi_1 \vert < 1$ — *single most tested condition*.
5. **Stationarity AR(2):** $\phi_2 \pm \phi_1 < 1$ and $-1 < \phi_2 < 1$ — triangular region.
6. **Mean of AR(1):** $\mu = c / (1 - \phi_1)$.
7. **Mean of AR(2):** $\mu = c / (1 - \phi_1 - \phi_2)$.
8. **Variance of AR(1):** $\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 / (1 - \phi_1^2)$.
9. **ACF of AR(1):** $\rho_k = \phi_1^{\vert k \vert}$ — geometric decay.
10. **ACF of AR(2):** Recurrence $\rho_k = \phi_1 \rho_{k-1} + \phi_2 \rho_{k-2}$, with $\rho_1 = \phi_1 / (1 - \phi_2)$.
11. **PACF of AR($p$):** Cuts off sharply after lag $p$; this is the **identification signature**.
12. **ACF of AR($p$):** Tails off gradually (exponential or damped sinusoidal).
13. **Yule–Walker (scalar):** $\rho_k = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_i \rho_{\vert k-i \vert}$ for $k \geq 1$.
14. **Yule–Walker (matrix):** $\boldsymbol{R} \boldsymbol{\phi} = \boldsymbol{\rho}$, solved as $\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}} = \hat{\boldsymbol{R}}^{-1} \hat{\boldsymbol{\rho}}$.
15. **Estimation methods:** Yule–Walker (moments), OLS, Maximum Likelihood — asymptotically equivalent under Gaussianity.
16. **Model selection:** Choose $p$ minimizing AIC = $-2 \ln L_{\max} + 2(p+1)$ or BIC = $-2 \ln L_{\max} + (p+1) \ln n$.
17. **Forecasting:** $h$-step ahead forecast is the recursive sum of past (observed or forecasted) values weighted by $\phi_i$.
18. **Diagnostic check:** Residuals must be white noise (Ljung–Box test).
19. **AR is a special case of ARMA** with only the autoregressive part present (no MA component).
20. **Box–Jenkins workflow:** Identification → Estimation → Diagnostic Checking → Forecasting — apply this exact order in long-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moving Average (MA) Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Moving Average (MA) Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MOVING AVERAGE (MA) MODELS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Moving Average (MA) model** of order $q$, denoted **MA$(q)$**, expresses the current value of a time series $Y_t$ as a finite linear combination of the current white-noise innovation and the $q$ most recent past white-noise shocks, superimposed on a constant mean term.

$$Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} - \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} - \cdots - \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}$$

where:
- $Y_t$ = observed value of the time series at time $t$
- $\mu$ = constant mean (intercept) of the process
- $\varepsilon_t$ = **white-noise** innovation at time $t$, with $E(\varepsilon_t)=0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t)=\sigma_\varepsilon^2$, and $E(\varepsilon_t \varepsilon_s)=0$ for $t \neq s$
- $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_q$ = **MA parameters** (real coefficients)
- $q$ = order of the moving average process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note (Dec 2023 Pattern):** The negative sign convention $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} - \cdots$ is the standard **Box–Jenkins** convention used in all KTU textbooks and is the only form accepted in university examinations. Some references use $+$ signs instead; **do not mix the two conventions in the exam hall**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stationarity Guarantee:** Unlike AR models, every MA$(q)$ process is **automatically (weakly) stationary** for *any finite real values* of $\theta_i$, because it is a finite linear combination of stationary white-noise shocks. This is a key advantage and a favourite KTU short-answer question.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Lake with Rainfall Memory"**
> Imagine a small lake whose water level today, $Y_t$, depends on:
> 1. Its long-term average level $\mu$ (the lake's natural equilibrium).
> 2. **Today's rainfall** $\varepsilon_t$ — a sudden, unpredictable downpour.
> 3. **Yesterday's rainfall shock** $\theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$ — water from yesterday's storm is still draining into the lake.
> 4. **The day-before-yesterday's rainfall shock** $\theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2}$ — older storm water still trickling in.
> 5. … and so on, up to $q$ days back.
>
> An MA$(q)$ model assumes the lake **forgets shocks older than $q$ days**. The series is "shock-driven" — its dynamics are explained entirely by recent random surprises, not by its own past values. A large random shock today propagates for $q$ periods, then vanishes forever.

This is the *opposite* of an **AR model**, where the lake's level is driven by its *own past levels* (a feedback loop). In MA models, there is **no feedback** — only memory of past *shocks*.

### 1.3 Special Cases Highlighted for KTU

| Case | Model | Practical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $q = 0$ | $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t$ | Pure white noise — no memory at all |
| $q = 1$ | $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$ | One-period shock memory |
| $q = 2$ | $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} - \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2}$ | Two-period shock memory |
| $q \to \infty$ | $Y_t = \mu + \sum_{j=0}^{\infty}(-\theta_j)\varepsilon_{t-j}$ | Convergent MA — equivalent to an AR$(\infty)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The MA model is **always stationary** but is **invertible only if** the roots of its characteristic polynomial lie **outside the unit circle**. This is the symmetry you must remember: Stationarity is free, but invertibility is conditional.

---

### 1.4 Why MA Models Matter in Engineering & Economics

- **Control Engineering:** MA filters are literally the *finite-impulse-response (FIR)* filters used in digital signal processing — $Y_t$ is the *filtered output* of white noise $\varepsilon_t$ by a filter of length $q+1$.
- **Economics:** Stock-price residuals and demand shocks follow short-memory MA patterns.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Random failure shocks with short persistence.
- **KTU Relevance:** MA models are the second pillar of the **ARIMA** family; without understanding MA, you cannot grasp ARIMA, ARMA, or forecasting.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics (KTU Board Expectation)

- **Mean:** $E(Y_t) = \mu$ (a constant, independent of $t$).
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \left(1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 + \cdots + \theta_q^2\right)$.
- **Autocovariance at lag $k$:** Finite and **cuts off after lag $q$** — the *signature* of an MA process.
- **Invertibility constant:** $C = \sum_{i=0}^{q} \theta_i^2$ (commonly used bound).

> All of the above will be **derived step by step** in Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the MA$(q)$ Process

The MA model can be decomposed into the following logical pillars. Each pillar answers a specific question an examiner loves to ask.

### Pillar 1 — Mean (Level)
- **Why?** To establish that the process fluctuates around a fixed equilibrium.
- **How?** Take expectation on both sides; since $E(\varepsilon_t)=0$ for all $t$, every shock term vanishes.
- **Result:** $E(Y_t) = \mu$, a constant for all $t$.

### Pillar 2 — Variance (Unconditional)
- **Why?** The total energy of the process = sum of squared coefficients times the noise variance.
- **How?** Use $\text{Var}(aX+b) = a^2\text{Var}(X)$ and independence of $\varepsilon_t$'s.
- **Result:** $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{i=0}^{q} \theta_i^2$ (where $\theta_0 := 1$).

### Pillar 3 — Autocovariance $\gamma_k$ and ACF $\rho_k$
- **Why?** The autocorrelation function (ACF) is the **diagnostic fingerprint** of an MA process.
- **How?** Compute $E[(Y_t-\mu)(Y_{t-k}-\mu)]$, expand, and apply the white-noise property $E(\varepsilon_s \varepsilon_j)=0$ for $s\neq j$.
- **Result:** $\gamma_k \neq 0$ only for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, q$; $\gamma_k = 0$ for $k > q$.
- **Consequence:** The ACF **cuts off after lag $q$** — a MA$(q)$ has *finite* memory.

### Pillar 4 — Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)
- **Why?** PACF tail behaviour is the *symmetric counterpart* used in model identification.
- **How?** Solve the Yule–Walker-style recursion for PACF; for MA it **tails off** (decays geometrically) rather than cutting off.
- **Result:** $\phi_{kk}$ is a damped exponential or sine-wave decay, never zero.

### Pillar 5 — Invertibility
- **Why?** A non-invertible MA is mathematically valid but cannot be rewritten as an AR$(\infty)$ for forecasting via past $Y$'s.
- **How?** Define the MA lag polynomial $\Theta(z) = 1 - \theta_1 z - \theta_2 z^2 - \cdots - \theta_q z^q$. The model is **invertible iff all roots of $\Theta(z)=0$ lie strictly outside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert > 1$.
- **Equivalent condition:** The companion matrix formed by $\theta_i$'s has all eigenvalues with magnitude $<1$.

### Pillar 6 — Back-Operator (Lag-Operator) Form
- **Why?** Compact representation; identical machinery to AR models.
- **How?** Use the back-shift operator $B Y_t = Y_{t-1}$.
- **Result:** $Y_t = \mu + \Theta(B)\,\varepsilon_t$ where $\Theta(B) = 1 - \theta_1 B - \theta_2 B^2 - \cdots - \theta_q B^q$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **canonical KTU reference** for MA$(q)$ models. Memorise it; every derivation in Section 3 is built from these building blocks. **For absolute value we use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (NOT the pipe character) to keep the markdown tables valid.**

| # | Property | Mathematical Expression | Valid For | Notes for KTU Board |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Model equation | $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t - \sum_{i=1}^{q}\theta_i \varepsilon_{t-i}$ | All $q$ | Box–Jenkins sign convention |
| 2 | Mean | $E(Y_t) = \mu$ | All $q$ | Constant, time-invariant |
| 3 | Variance | $\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \left(1 + \sum_{i=1}^{q}\theta_i^2\right)$ | All $q$ | Finite, $\theta_0 = 1$ |
| 4 | Autocovariance, lag $k$ | $\gamma_k = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \left(-\theta_{\vert k \vert} + \sum_{i=0}^{q-\vert k \vert}\theta_i \theta_{i+\vert k \vert}\right)$ for $1 \le \vert k \vert \le q$ | $1 \le \vert k \vert \le q$ | $\theta_0 := 1$ |
| 5 | Autocovariance, lag $>q$ | $\gamma_k = 0$ | $\vert k \vert > q$ | **Cuts off after lag $q$** |
| 6 | ACF, lag $k$ | $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ | All $k$ | Cuts off after lag $q$ |
| 7 | PACF | Tails off (damped exponentials/sine) | All lags | Never cuts off |
| 8 | Lag polynomial | $\Theta(z) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{q}\theta_i z^i$ | All $q$ | Roots outside $\vert z \vert = 1$ for invertibility |
| 9 | Invertibility condition | All roots of $\Theta(z)=0$ satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$ | All $q$ | Equivalent: $\sum \theta_i^2 < \infty$ constraints |
| 10 | Stationarity | **Always stationary** for any finite $\theta_i$ | All $q$ | Free of conditions |
| 11 | Equivalent AR$(\infty)$ | $\varepsilon_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \pi_j Y_{t-j}$ (if invertible) | Invertible case | Used for forecasting |
| 12 | MA(1) special: ACF | $\rho_1 = -\theta_1 / (1+\theta_1^2)$; $\rho_k=0$ for $k \ge 2$ | $q=1$ | Most-tested KTU case |
| 13 | MA(2) special: ACF | $\rho_1 = \dfrac{-(\theta_1 + \theta_1 \theta_2)}{1+\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2}$, $\rho_2 = \dfrac{-\theta_2}{1+\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2}$ | $q=2$ | Frequently asked |
| 14 | Invertibility MA(1) | $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$ | $q=1$ | Most-tested bound |
| 15 | Invertibility MA(2) | $\theta_1 + \theta_2 < 1$, $\theta_2 - \theta_1 < 1$, $\vert \theta_2 \vert < 1$ | $q=2$ | Triangle region |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of MA Models in Engineering

| Engineering Field | MA Application | Typical $q$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Digital Signal Processing** | FIR filtering, noise smoothing | $q = 5, 7, 15$ (window length) |
| **Quality Control** | Short-term process disturbances | $q = 1, 2$ |
| **Finance** | Idiosyncratic shock components in returns | $q = 1, 2$ |
| **Load Forecasting** | Random demand shocks over baseline | $q = 1, 2, 3$ |
| **Biomedical Signals** | EMG / ECG noise cancellation | $q = 3$–$7$ |
| **Econometrics** | Monetary policy shock effects | $q = 4, 8$ (quarterly lags) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **MA(1) model is the single most-tested case** in KTU exams. The KTU board expects you to derive the ACF formula $\rho_1 = -\theta_1/(1+\theta_1^2)$ and the invertibility condition $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$ *from memory* in any 14-mark question.

---

## 2.4 The Symmetric Diagnostic Table (Identification Cheat-Sheet)

This table is **the most-asked KTU diagram**. The board examiner will often describe a series and ask "Which model — AR or MA?"

| Property | AR$(p)$ | MA$(q)$ | ARMA$(p,q)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ACF | Tails off | **Cuts off after lag $q$** | Tails off |
| PACF | **Cuts off after lag $p$** | Tails off | Tails off |
| Stationarity | Conditional (roots outside unit circle) | **Always** | Conditional |
| Invertibility | **Always** | Conditional (roots outside unit circle) | Conditional |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** *Stationarity ↔ AR roots, Invertibility ↔ MA roots*. AR is always invertible; MA is always stationary. This is the **only** symmetry you need for identification questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains the **exhaustive algebraic derivations** required for KTU board examination answers. Every step is justified and no shortcut is taken.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean of MA$(q)$

**Claim:** $E(Y_t) = \mu$.

**Step 1.** Write the model with the convention that $\theta_0 := 1$:

$$Y_t = \mu + \sum_{i=0}^{q}(-\theta_i)\,\varepsilon_{t-i}, \quad \text{with } \theta_0 = 1$$

**Step 2.** Take expectation on both sides. White noise has $E(\varepsilon_s)=0$ for every $s$:

$$E(Y_t) = E(\mu) + \sum_{i=0}^{q}(-\theta_i)\,E(\varepsilon_{t-i})$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $E(\mu) = \mu$ and $E(\varepsilon_{t-i})=0$:

$$E(Y_t) = \mu + \sum_{i=0}^{q}(-\theta_i)\cdot 0$$

**Step 4.** The sum vanishes, leaving:

$$E(Y_t) = \mu \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Interpretation:** The process is **ergodic in the mean** — its long-run average equals the constant $\mu$, regardless of the $\theta_i$'s.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance of MA$(q)$

**Claim:** $\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \left(1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 + \cdots + \theta_q^2\right)$.

**Step 1.** Subtract the mean to centre the process:

$$Y_t - \mu = \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} - \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} - \cdots - \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}$$

**Step 2.** The variance of a sum of independent random variables equals the sum of their variances:

$$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) + \theta_1^2\,\text{Var}(\varepsilon_{t-1}) + \theta_2^2\,\text{Var}(\varepsilon_{t-2}) + \cdots + \theta_q^2\,\text{Var}(\varepsilon_{t-q})$$

**Step 3.** Each $\varepsilon_s$ has the same variance $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$ (white noise is identically distributed):

$$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \theta_1^2 \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \theta_2^2 \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \cdots + \theta_q^2 \sigma_\varepsilon^2$$

**Step 4.** Factor out $\sigma_\varepsilon^2$:

$$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2\left(1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2 + \cdots + \theta_q^2\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Autocovariance Function (ACF) of MA$(q)$

This is the **central derivation** for KTU. We derive $\gamma_k$ for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Setup.** Define the centred process $\tilde{Y}_t = Y_t - \mu$:

$$\tilde{Y}_t = \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} - \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2} - \cdots - \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q}$$

The autocovariance at lag $k$ is:

$$\gamma_k = E(\tilde{Y}_t \cdot \tilde{Y}_{t-k})$$

**Step 1.** Expand $\tilde{Y}_t$ and $\tilde{Y}_{t-k}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma_k &= E\left[\left(\sum_{i=0}^{q}(-\theta_i)\varepsilon_{t-i}\right)\left(\sum_{j=0}^{q}(-\theta_j)\varepsilon_{t-k-j}\right)\right] \\
&= \sum_{i=0}^{q}\sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_i \theta_j \cdot E(\varepsilon_{t-i}\,\varepsilon_{t-k-j})
\end{aligned}$$

> Here we used $E(aX \cdot bY) = ab\,E(XY)$ for constants $a,b$ (linearity of expectation). The minus signs cancel because $(-1)\cdot(-1)=1$.

**Step 2.** Apply the white-noise property:

$$E(\varepsilon_s\,\varepsilon_r) = \begin{cases}\sigma_\varepsilon^2 & \text{if } s = r \\ 0 & \text{if } s \neq r\end{cases}$$

**Step 3.** The expectation is non-zero **only when the indices match**: $t-i = t-k-j$, i.e. $j = i-k$.

**Step 4.** Substitute $j = i-k$ in the double sum. The cross-terms vanish, leaving only the diagonal $j = i-k$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma_k &= \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{i=0}^{q}\theta_i\,\theta_{i-k} \quad \text{(where } \theta_{i-k} := 0 \text{ if } i-k < 0 \text{ or } i-k > q)
\end{aligned}$$

> The constraint $0 \le i-k \le q$ implies $k \le i \le k+q$, intersected with $0 \le i \le q$. So $i$ ranges over $\max(0,k) \le i \le \min(q, k+q)$.

**Step 5.** Split into three cases.

**Case A: $k = 0$ (variance, already derived):**

$$\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{i=0}^{q}\theta_i^2 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2\left(1 + \theta_1^2 + \cdots + \theta_q^2\right)$$

**Case B: $1 \le k \le q$ (intermediate lag):**

The summation index $i$ runs from $k$ to $q$:

$$\gamma_k = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{i=k}^{q} \theta_i\,\theta_{i-k}$$

Using $\theta_0 = 1$, this can be rewritten (re-index $m = i-k$):

$$\gamma_k = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{m=0}^{q-k} \theta_{m+k}\,\theta_{m}$$

> The KTU textbook form (using $\theta_0=1$) is:
> $$\gamma_k = \sigma_\varepsilon^2\left(-\theta_k + \sum_{i=0}^{q-k}\theta_i\,\theta_{i+k}\right) \quad \text{for } 1 \le k \le q$$

**Case C: $k > q$ (long lag):**

The summation index $i$ must satisfy $k \le i \le q$, but $k > q$ makes this range **empty**. Hence:

$$\gamma_k = 0 \quad \text{for } k > q \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Punch-line:** *"An MA$(q)$ process has zero autocovariance at all lags greater than $q$."* This single sentence, with the derivation above, routinely carries **7 marks** in a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ACF of MA(1) [Most-Tested KTU Case]

For $q=1$: $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}$.

**Step 1.** Mean: $E(Y_t) = \mu$.

**Step 2.** Variance (using formula 3 with $q=1$):

$$\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2(1 + \theta_1^2)$$

**Step 3.** Autocovariance at lag 1 (using Case B with $k=1$, $q=1$):

$$\gamma_1 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{i=1}^{1}\theta_i\,\theta_{i-1} = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \cdot \theta_1 \cdot \theta_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \cdot \theta_1 \cdot 1 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \theta_1$$

> With the convention form: $\gamma_1 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 (-\theta_1 + \theta_0 \theta_1) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2(-\theta_1+\theta_1)=0$? — **No, careful!** The convention $\theta_0=1$ gives the variance in *positive* sign; the lag-1 form is $\gamma_1 = -\sigma_\varepsilon^2 \theta_1$ in standard Box–Jenkins convention. KTU board uses:
> $$\gamma_1 = -\sigma_\varepsilon^2 \theta_1$$

**Step 4.** Autocovariance at lag $k \ge 2$ (Case C with $k=2 > q=1$):

$$\gamma_k = 0 \quad \text{for } k \ge 2$$

**Step 5.** Divide by $\gamma_0$ to obtain the ACF:

$$\rho_1 = \frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_0} = \frac{-\sigma_\varepsilon^2\,\theta_1}{\sigma_\varepsilon^2(1+\theta_1^2)} = \frac{-\theta_1}{1+\theta_1^2}$$

$$\rho_k = 0 \quad \text{for } k \ge 2 \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary of $\rho_1$:** Setting $d\rho_1/d\theta_1 = 0$ gives $\theta_1 = \pm 1$, and the maximum magnitude is $\vert\rho_1\vert = 1/2$. KTU favourite: *"Show that for an MA(1) process, $\vert\rho_1\vert \le 1/2$."* — be ready to derive this.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — ACF of MA(2) [Second Most-Tested Case]

For $q=2$: $Y_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} - \theta_2 \varepsilon_{t-2}$.

**Step 1.** Variance:

$$\gamma_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2(1 + \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2)$$

**Step 2.** Autocovariance at lag 1 ($k=1$, Case B):

$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma_1 &= \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{i=1}^{2}\theta_i\,\theta_{i-1} \\
&= \sigma_\varepsilon^2(\theta_1\theta_0 + \theta_2\theta_1) \\
&= \sigma_\varepsilon^2(\theta_1 + \theta_1\theta_2) \\
&= \sigma_\varepsilon^2\,\theta_1(1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}$$

With the standard Box–Jenkins sign convention, this is:

$$\gamma_1 = -\sigma_\varepsilon^2(\theta_1 + \theta_1\theta_2) = -\sigma_\varepsilon^2\,\theta_1(1+\theta_2)$$

**Step 3.** Autocovariance at lag 2 ($k=2$, Case B with $i$ from 2 to 2):

$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma_2 &= \sigma_\varepsilon^2 \sum_{i=2}^{2}\theta_i\,\theta_{i-2} \\
&= \sigma_\varepsilon^2\cdot \theta_2\cdot\theta_0 = \sigma_\varepsilon^2\,\theta_2
\end{aligned}$$

With convention: $\gamma_2 = -\sigma_\varepsilon^2\,\theta_2$.

**Step 4.** Autocovariance for $k \ge 3$:

$$\gamma_k = 0 \quad (k \ge 3)$$

**Step 5.** ACF values:

$$\rho_1 = \frac{-\theta_1(1+\theta_2)}{1+\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2}, \qquad \rho_2 = \frac{-\theta_2}{1+\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2}, \qquad \rho_k = 0 \text{ for } k \ge 3 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Invertibility Condition for MA(1)

**Claim:** MA(1) is invertible iff $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$.

**Step 1.** Write the MA(1) model:

$$Y_t - \mu = \varepsilon_t - \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} = (1 - \theta_1 B)\,\varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2.** To invert, solve for $\varepsilon_t$ in terms of past $Y$'s:

$$\varepsilon_t = (1 - \theta_1 B)^{-1}(Y_t - \mu) = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \theta_1^j\,(Y_{t-j} - \mu)$$

> This geometric-series expansion converges iff $\vert \theta_1 B \vert < 1$ as an operator, i.e. $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$.

**Step 3.** Verify by substituting back. Truncating at $N$:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{N}\theta_1^j(Y_{t-j}-\mu) = (1 - \theta_1^{N+1}B^{N+1})\varepsilon_t + \theta_1\varepsilon_{t-N-1} \cdot (\text{lower order})$$

As $N \to \infty$ and $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$, the term $\theta_1^{N+1} \to 0$, recovering $\varepsilon_t$ in the limit. Hence invertibility holds.

**Step 4.** Conversely, if $\vert \theta_1 \vert \ge 1$, the geometric series diverges and the recursion for $\varepsilon_t$ in terms of past $Y$'s does not exist. Hence:

$$\boxed{\text{MA(1) is invertible} \iff \vert \theta_1 \vert < 1} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Invertible"?** When $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$, the MA(1) can be rewritten as an **infinite-order AR** (AR$(\infty)$): $\varepsilon_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\theta_1^j Y_{t-j}$. This is the "inversion" — hence the name. It is essential for **forecasting** because we need to express future innovations in terms of *observable* past $Y$'s.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Invertibility Region for MA(2)

**Claim:** MA(2) is invertible iff $\theta_1 + \theta_2 < 1$, $\theta_2 - \theta_1 < 1$, and $\vert \theta_2 \vert < 1$.

**Step 1.** Lag polynomial: $\Theta(z) = 1 - \theta_1 z - \theta_2 z^2$.

**Step 2.** Roots $z_1, z_2$ of $\Theta(z)=0$ are:

$$z = \frac{-\theta_1 \pm \sqrt{\theta_1^2 + 4\theta_2}}{2\theta_2}$$

> (Assume $\theta_2 \neq 0$ for the quadratic case; if $\theta_2 = 0$ it reduces to MA(1) in $\theta_1$.)

**Step 3.** Invertibility requires $\vert z_1 \vert > 1$ and $\vert z_2 \vert > 1$. By Vieta's formulas, $z_1 z_2 = -1/\theta_2$ and $z_1 + z_2 = -\theta_1/\theta_2$.

**Step 4.** Use the fact that for a quadratic with real coefficients, both roots lie outside the unit circle iff the three conditions hold simultaneously (derivable from $\vert z_1 \vert^2 \vert z_2 \vert^2 > 1$, $\vert z_1 \vert^2 + \vert z_2 \vert^2 > 2$, and $\vert z_1 + z_2 \vert^2 < (\vert z_1 \vert^2 + 1)(\vert z_2 \vert^2 + 1)$):

$$\begin{aligned}
z_1 z_2 = -1/\theta_2 &\;\Rightarrow\; \vert \theta_2 \vert < 1 \\
z_1 + z_2 = -\theta_1/\theta_2 &\;\Rightarrow\; \text{Re}(z_1 + z_2) \text{ constraints} \\
&\;\Rightarrow\; \theta_1 + \theta_2 < 1 \text{ and } \theta_2 - \theta_1 < 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5.** Hence the **invertibility region** in the $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$ plane is the triangle:

$$\theta_1 + \theta_2 < 1, \quad \theta_2 - \theta_1 < 1, \quad \vert \theta_2 \vert < 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric view:** This is a triangular region in the $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$ plane with vertices at $(-2, 1)$, $(2, 1)$, and $(0, -1)$. KTU board frequently asks: *"Sketch the invertibility region for an MA(2) model."* — reproduce this triangle.

---

## 3.8 Derivation 8 — PACF of MA$(q)$ (Tail-Off Behaviour)

**Claim:** The PACF $\phi_{kk}$ of an MA$(q)$ process *tails off* (decays geometrically) and is **never zero** beyond any finite lag.

**Step 1.** The PACF is defined via the recursive relation:

$$\phi_{kk} = \frac{\rho_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\phi_{k-1,j}\,\rho_{k-j}}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\phi_{k-1,j}\,\rho_j}$$

**Step 2.** For an MA$(q)$, $\rho_k = 0$ for $k > q$. Substituting this into the recursion shows that the PACF for $k > q$ satisfies a **homogeneous linear recurrence** whose characteristic roots are the reciprocals of the MA polynomial's roots.

**Step 3.** If all MA roots lie **outside the unit circle** (invertibility), then all reciprocals lie **inside the unit circle**, so the PACF decays as a **damped exponential** (or damped sinusoid if roots are complex).

**Step 4.** Therefore: $\phi_{kk} \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$, but $\phi_{kk} \neq 0$ for any finite $k$ — this is the **tail-off** pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **mirror image** of an AR model: for AR$(p)$, the ACF tails off and the PACF cuts off; for MA$(q)$, the ACF cuts off and the PACF tails off. This is the **Yule–Walker symmetry** that KTU tests in model-identification questions.

---

## 3.9 Worked Numerical Example — MA(1) Identification

**Problem (KTU 2022 Pattern):** Given an MA(1) model $Y_t = 10 + \varepsilon_t - 0.5\varepsilon_{t-1}$ with $\sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 4$, compute the mean, variance, lag-1 autocovariance, ACF, and check invertibility.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Mean: } E(Y_t) = 10$$

$$\text{Variance: } \gamma_0 = 4(1 + 0.5^2) = 4(1.25) = 5$$

$$\text{Lag-1 autocovariance: } \gamma_1 = -4 \cdot 0.5 = -2$$

$$\text{Lag-1 ACF: } \rho_1 = -0.5 / 1.25 = -0.4$$

$$\text{Lag-}k \text{ ACF: } \rho_k = 0 \text{ for } k \ge 2$$

$$\text{Invertibility: } \vert \theta_1 \vert = 0.5 < 1 \;\checkmark\; \text{(invertible)}$$

> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote the result $\rho_1 = -0.4$ and explicitly verify $\vert \theta_1 \vert < 1$. Partial credit is awarded even if numerical substitution is wrong, provided the formula structure is correct.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The MA$(q)$ Process as an FIR Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["epsilon_t (current shock)"] -->|coefficient theta_0 = 1| S1[Weighted Sum Node]
    E2["epsilon_{t-1} (lag-1 shock)"] -->|coefficient -theta_1| S1
    E3["epsilon_{t-2} (lag-2 shock)"] -->|coefficient -theta_2| S1
    E4["epsilon_{t-q} (lag-q shock)"] -->|coefficient -theta_q| S1
    MU["mu (mean constant)"] --> S1
    S1 --> YT["Y_t (observed series)"]
    style E1 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style E2 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style E3 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style E4 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style MU fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#333
    style S1 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#333
    style YT fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading guide:** Each $\varepsilon_{t-i}$ is an i.i.d. white-noise shock. The "Weighted Sum Node" multiplies each shock by $-\theta_i$ and adds them along with the constant $\mu$ to produce $Y_t$. The shading encodes the *type* of input (yellow = random shocks, green = deterministic constant, blue = output).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Algorithm for MA Model Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Observed time series Y_1...Y_n] --> B[Plot series and sample ACF]
    B --> C{ACF cuts off after lag q?}
    C -->|Yes, clean cut-off| D[Strong evidence of MA process]
    C -->|No, gradual decay| E[Try AR or ARMA models]
    D --> F[Compute sample PACF]
    F --> G{PACF tails off geometrically?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Confirm MA q model]
    G -->|No| I[Re-examine: may be over-differenced]
    H --> J[Estimate theta_i by ML or method of moments]
    J --> K{Check invertibility: roots of Theta z outside unit circle?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Acceptable MA q model]
    K -->|No| M[Restrict parameters to invertibility region]
    L --> N[Forecast and validate via residuals]
    M --> J
    style A fill:#fef9c3
    style D fill:#bbf7d0
    style H fill:#86efac
    style L fill:#4ade80,stroke:#065f46
    style N fill:#22c55e,stroke:#052e16,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Diagram — ACF / PACF Fingerprints

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ARP[AR p process]
        AR_ACF["ACF: Tails off"]
        AR_PACF["PACF: Cuts off after lag p"]
    end
    subgraph MAP[MA q process]
        MA_ACF["ACF: Cuts off after lag q"]
        MA_PACF["PACF: Tails off"]
    end
    subgraph ARMAP[ARMA p q process]
        ARMA_ACF["ACF: Tails off"]
        ARMA_PACF["PACF: Tails off"]
    end
    style AR_ACF fill:#fde68a
    style AR_PACF fill:#fde68a
    style MA_ACF fill:#fca5a5
    style MA_PACF fill:#fca5a5
    style ARMA_ACF fill:#bef264
    style ARMA_PACF fill:#bef264
```

> **Reading guide:** This is the **canonical Box–Jenkins identification triangle**. Note the *symmetric swap* between AR and MA: what is "cut off" in one is "tails off" in the other. ARMA shows tail-off in both — which is precisely why ARMA identification is *harder* and is the topic of Module 4.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Lag-Operator Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EPS["epsilon_t"] -->|input| THETA["Theta B = 1 - theta_1 B - theta_2 B squared - ... - theta_q B to power q"]
    THETA -->|output| YTILDE["Y_t minus mu"]
    YTILDE -->|add mu| YT["Y_t"]
    style EPS fill:#fde047
    style THETA fill:#fb923c,stroke:#7c2d12
    style YTILDE fill:#67e8f9
    style YT fill:#a78bfa
```

> **Reading guide:** The lag operator $B$ shifts time backwards: $B \varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_{t-1}$. The "Theta(B)" block is the **finite impulse response filter** — equivalent to a moving-average filter in DSP. This is the *cleanest* mathematical picture of an MA model.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid State Diagram — Invertibility of MA(1)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NonInvertible: Initial parameter guess
    NonInvertible: theta_1 absolute value is greater than or equal to 1
    Invertible: theta_1 absolute value is less than 1
    NonInvertible --> Invertible: Restrict theta_1 to range minus 1, 1
    Invertible --> Forecasting: Express epsilon_t as infinite AR
    Forecasting: epsilon_t equals sum theta_1 to power j times Y_{t-j} minus mu
    Invertible --> Residuals: Compute residuals for diagnostics
    Residuals --> Forecasting
    Forecasting --> [*]
    style NonInvertible fill:#fecaca
    style Invertible fill:#bbf7d0
    style Forecasting fill:#bae6fd
```

> **Reading guide:** A non-invertible MA model *cannot* generate forecasts in the usual way. The state diagram shows the only escape route — restricting parameters to the invertibility region so that $\varepsilon_t$ can be expressed as an AR$(\infty)$ in observable $Y$'s.
