# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define a multivariate time series. Explain the difference between auto-covariance and cross-covariance with reference to a $k$-variate process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A multivariate time series is an ordered sequence of random vectors $\{\mathbf{Y}_t = (Y_{1t}, Y_{2t}, \dots, Y_{kt})^\top : t = 1, 2, \dots, T\}$ where $k \ge 2$ series are observed simultaneously and the joint dynamic evolution of all components must be modelled together.

The **cross-covariance matrix** at lag $\tau$ is $\Gamma(\tau) = \mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t-\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top]$, a $k \times k$ matrix whose $(i,j)$-th element is $\gamma_{ij}(\tau) = \mathbb{E}[(Y_{it} - \mu_i)(Y_{j,t-\tau} - \mu_j)]$.

- **Auto-covariance** corresponds to the *diagonal* elements $\gamma_{ii}(\tau)$ — dependence of series $i$ on its *own* past.
- **Cross-covariance** corresponds to the *off-diagonal* elements $\gamma_{ij}(\tau), \; i \neq j$ — dependence of series $i$ on the *past of other* series $j$.

The full matrix description is essential because in MTS, ignoring cross-covariances leads to **inefficient forecasts and biased inference** (1 mark for definition + 1 mark for matrix form + 1 mark for distinction).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**State the conditions for weak stationarity of a multivariate time series $\{\mathbf{Y}_t\}$. Why is stationarity a prerequisite for fitting a VAR(p) model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A $k$-variate process $\{\mathbf{Y}_t\}$ is **weakly (second-order) stationary** if:

1. $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_t) = \boldsymbol{\mu}$, a **constant** $k \times 1$ vector (not depending on $t$).
2. Each component has finite variance: $\text{Var}(Y_{it}) = \gamma_{ii}(0) < \infty$.
3. The cross-covariance matrix $\Gamma(\tau) = \mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t-\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top]$ depends **only on the lag** $\tau$, not on $t$.

**Why stationarity is required for VAR(p):** A stationary VAR(p) admits a convergent *Wold / MA$(\infty)$* representation, so its parameters $\Phi_1, \dots, \Phi_p$ are *uniquely identified* from second moments. Without stationarity, sample covariances do not converge to population covariances (the law of large numbers fails), $h$-step forecasts blow up geometrically, and impulse-responses are no longer interpretable. Practically, we difference or cointegrate non-stationary series before fitting VAR(p) (1 mark each for the three conditions + 1 mark each for "why" — total 3 marks distributed as: 1.5 marks for conditions, 1.5 marks for justification).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU ESE Module-3 regulations, **answer ANY ONE** of the following two questions. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** Define the cross-covariance matrix $\Gamma(\tau)$ for a $k$-variate time series. Prove the symmetry identity $\Gamma(-\tau) = \Gamma(\tau)^\top$ under weak stationarity. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the structure of a $\text{VAR}(p)$ model with $k$ variables. Write down the stationarity condition. For a $\text{VAR}(1)$ model with $\Phi_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0}$, verify stationarity by computing eigenvalues and derive the unconditional mean vector. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — Cross-covariance matrix and symmetry (7 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* For a $k$-variate process $\{\mathbf{Y}_t\}$ with mean $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_t)$, the **cross-covariance matrix at lag $\tau$** is:

$$
\Gamma(\tau) = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t-\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top\bigr] \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}
$$

*Proof (5 marks):*

Starting from the definition:

$$
\Gamma(\tau) = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t-\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top\bigr]
$$

Transpose:

$$
\Gamma(\tau)^\top = \mathbb{E}\bigl[((\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t-\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top)^\top\bigr] = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{Y}_{t-\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top\bigr]
$$

By weak stationarity, $\Gamma(\tau)$ depends only on the lag, so replacing the time-pair $(t, t-\tau)$ by $(t+\tau, t)$ preserves the lag:

$$
\Gamma(\tau)^\top = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t+\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top\bigr]
$$

Substituting $\tau \mapsto -\tau$ in the original definition:

$$
\Gamma(-\tau) = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t+\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top\bigr]
$$

Comparing the last two equations yields:

$$
\boxed{\Gamma(-\tau) = \Gamma(\tau)^\top}
$$

**Part (b) — VAR(p) structure, stationarity, mean (7 marks):**

*Structure (2 marks):* A $\text{VAR}(p)$ model is:

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \Phi_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \Phi_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} + \dots + \Phi_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{W}_t
$$

with $\mathbf{Y}_t, \mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^k$, $\Phi_i \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}$, and $\mathbf{W}_t \sim (\mathbf{0}_k, \Sigma_{\mathbf{W}})$ white noise.

*Stationarity condition (2 marks):* All roots of $\det(\mathbf{I}_k - \Phi_1 z - \Phi_2 z^2 - \dots - \Phi_p z^p) = 0$ lie **outside** the unit circle. For $\text{VAR}(1)$, this simplifies to: all eigenvalues of $\Phi_1$ satisfy $\vert \lambda_i(\Phi_1) \vert < 1$.

*Eigenvalue computation (1.5 marks):*

Characteristic polynomial:

$$
\det(\Phi_1 - \lambda \mathbf{I}_2) = (0.5 - \lambda)(0.4 - \lambda) - (0.3)(0.1) = \lambda^2 - 0.9\lambda + 0.17
$$

Roots:

$$
\lambda = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.81 - 0.68}}{2} = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.13}}{2} \approx \frac{0.9 \pm 0.3606}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 \approx 0.6303, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 0.2697
$$

Both $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$ → **stationary.** ✓

*Unconditional mean (1.5 marks):*

With $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0}$, $(\mathbf{I}_k - \Phi_1)\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{0}$. Since $\Phi_1$ is non-singular (eigenvalues $\neq 0$), the only solution is:

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{0}_2}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** What is multivariate white noise? Define the cross-correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\tau)$. For a $k$-variate process, derive the condition $\vert\rho_{ij}(\tau)\vert \leq 1$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 2-variate $\text{VAR}(1)$ process has $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0}$, $\Phi_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$, $\Sigma_{\mathbf{W}} = \mathbf{I}_2$. Write the system in scalar form and compute the unconditional covariance matrix $\Gamma(0)$ using the Lyapunov equation $\Gamma(0) - \Phi_1 \Gamma(0) \Phi_1^\top = \Sigma_{\mathbf{W}}$. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — MV white noise and CCF bound (7 marks):**

*MV white noise (2 marks):* A process $\{\mathbf{W}_t\}$ is **$k$-variate white noise**, written $\mathbf{W}_t \sim WN(\mathbf{0}_k, \Sigma_{\mathbf{W}})$, if:
- $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{W}_t) = \mathbf{0}_k$ for all $t$;
- $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{W}_t \mathbf{W}_t^\top) = \Sigma_{\mathbf{W}}$ (a constant positive-definite matrix);
- $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{W}_t \mathbf{W}_s^\top) = \mathbf{0}_k$ for all $t \neq s$ (no serial correlation).

*Cross-correlation matrix (2 marks):* $\mathbf{R}(\tau) = \mathbf{D}^{-1}\Gamma(\tau)\mathbf{D}^{-1}$ where $\mathbf{D} = \text{diag}(\sqrt{\gamma_{11}(0)}, \dots, \sqrt{\gamma_{kk}(0)})$. Its $(i,j)$-th entry is $\rho_{ij}(\tau) = \gamma_{ij}(\tau)\big/\!\sqrt{\gamma_{ii}(0)\,\gamma_{jj}(0)}$.

*Bound derivation (3 marks):* Apply Cauchy–Schwarz to scalar random variables $X = Y_{it} - \mu_i$ and $Y = Y_{j,t-\tau} - \mu_j$:

$$
\bigl[\mathbb{E}(XY)\bigr]^2 \leq \mathbb{E}(X^2)\,\mathbb{E}(Y^2)
$$

Substitute:

$$
\bigl[\gamma_{ij}(\tau)\bigr]^2 \leq \gamma_{ii}(0)\,\gamma_{jj}(0)
$$

Divide and take the square root:

$$
\bigl\vert \rho_{ij}(\tau) \bigr\vert \leq 1
$$

**Part (b) — Scalar form and Lyapunov solution (7 marks):**

*Scalar form (1.5 marks):* Letting $\mathbf{Y}_t = (Y_{1t}, Y_{2t})^\top$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{1t} &= 0.6\,Y_{1,t-1} + 0.2\,Y_{2,t-1} + W_{1t} \\
Y_{2t} &= 0.1\,Y_{1,t-1} + 0.5\,Y_{2,t-1} + W_{2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Lyapunov equation set-up (1.5 marks):* Let $\Gamma(0) = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & d \end{bmatrix}$. The Lyapunov equation is $\Gamma(0) - \Phi_1 \Gamma(0) \Phi_1^\top = \Sigma_{\mathbf{W}} = \mathbf{I}_2$.

*Computation (4 marks):* Following the algebra derived in Section 3.4 step 6, the resulting scalar system is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.64 a - 0.24 b - 0.04 d &= 1 \\
-0.01 a - 0.15 b + 0.75 d &= 1 \\
0.06 a - 0.68 b + 0.10 d &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving (e.g. via Cramer's rule or matrix inversion):

$$
a \approx 1.917, \quad b \approx 0.529, \quad d \approx 1.625
$$

$$
\boxed{\Gamma(0) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1.917 & 0.529 \\ 0.529 & 1.625 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

This is **positive-definite** (eigenvalues $\approx 2.265, 1.277$, both $> 0$) — required for any covariance matrix.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in MTS questions (compiled from KTU valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\Gamma(\tau)$ with $\Gamma(\tau)^\top$.** The matrix is **NOT symmetric** in general. The correct identity is $\Gamma(-\tau) = \Gamma(\tau)^\top$, *not* $\Gamma(\tau) = \Gamma(\tau)^\top$. Writing "$\Gamma(\tau)$ is symmetric" loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the dimension $k$.** Students write $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_t \mathbf{Y}_{t-\tau}^\top)$ without subtracting $\boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}^\top$. The correct object is $\mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t-\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top]$. Off by $\boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}^\top$ → lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up auto-covariance and cross-covariance notation.** Always state explicitly that diagonal elements are auto-covariances and off-diagonal elements are cross-covariances.
> 4. **VAR stationarity condition error.** Students write "eigenvalues of $\Phi_1$ are $< 1$" without the **modulus** (absolute value). For complex eigenvalues this is critical. Always write $\vert\lambda_i(\Phi_1)\vert < 1$.
> 5. **Scalar VAR(1) miswritten.** In Question A(b), students forget that the system of $k$ equations must be written explicitly. KTU examiners expect to see all $k$ scalar equations on the page.
> 6. **Skipping the stationarity check before claiming forecasts/IRFs.** Always verify $\rho(\Phi_1) < 1$ *before* interpreting any output of a VAR.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Multivariate time series** = sequence of random vectors $\mathbf{Y}_t \in \mathbb{R}^k$, $k \ge 2$, observed over equally-spaced time indices.
- **Two pillars**: auto-dependence (own past) and cross-dependence (past of others).
- **Mean vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_t)$ — constant under stationarity.
- **Cross-covariance matrix** $\Gamma(\tau) = \mathbb{E}[(\mathbf{Y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{Y}_{t-\tau} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top] \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times k}$.
- **Cross-correlation** $\rho_{ij}(\tau) = \gamma_{ij}(\tau)\big/\!\sqrt{\gamma_{ii}(0)\gamma_{jj}(0)}$, bounded by $\pm 1$ (Cauchy–Schwarz).
- **Symmetry identity** (KTU favourite): $\Gamma(-\tau) = \Gamma(\tau)^\top$.
- **Weak stationarity**: mean constant + $\Gamma(\tau)$ depends only on $\tau$ + finite second moments.
- **VAR(p)**: $\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^p \Phi_i \mathbf{Y}_{t-i} + \mathbf{W}_t$ — coefficient matrices are $k \times k$.
- **VAR(p) stationarity**: all roots of $\det(\mathbf{I}_k - \sum_i \Phi_i z^i)$ lie *outside* the unit circle.
- **VAR(1) stationarity shortcut**: spectral radius $\rho(\Phi_1) < 1$, i.e. $\vert\lambda_i(\Phi_1)\vert < 1$ for all $i$.
- **Mean vector of stationary VAR(1)**: $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_k - \Phi_1)^{-1}\mathbf{c}$.
- **Covariance recursion (Lyapunov)**: $\Gamma(0) - \Phi_1 \Gamma(0) \Phi_1^\top = \Sigma_{\mathbf{W}}$.
- **Parameter count grows quadratically** with $k$: $\mathcal{O}(k^2)$ — curse of dimensionality.
- **MV white noise** has positive-definite $\Sigma_{\mathbf{W}}$ — its off-diagonal entries encode contemporaneous correlation across series.
- **VMA(q), VARMA(p,q), VARIMA(p,d,q), VECM** are generalisations treated in later modules.
- **Cointegration / Granger causality / IRF / FEVD** are introduced here but elaborated in Module 4–5.
- **Engineering applications**: macro-forecasting, portfolio risk, IoT sensor fusion, biomedical signals, multivariate SPC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line KTU-ready takeaway:** A multivariate time series is a sequence of $k$-dimensional random vectors whose second-order structure is fully described by the mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and the lag-dependent cross-covariance matrices $\Gamma(\tau)$, and the dominant parametric model family is the VAR(p) — stationary iff the spectral radius of its autoregressive matrices is strictly less than one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector Autoregressive (VAR) Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vector Autoregressive (VAR) Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vector Autoregressive (VAR)** model is a multivariate extension of the univariate autoregressive (AR) model, used to capture the linear interdependencies among multiple time series variables. In a VAR model, each variable is expressed as a linear function of its own past values and the past values of all other variables in the system.

For an $n$-dimensional stationary time series vector $\mathbf{Y}_t = (y_{1t}, y_{2t}, \dots, y_{nt})'$, a **VAR model of order $p$**, denoted **VAR($p$)**, is specified as:

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} + \dots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{u}_t
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{c}$ is an $(n \times 1)$ vector of intercepts (constants).
- $\mathbf{A}_i$ are $(n \times n)$ coefficient matrices for lag $i$, with $i = 1, 2, \dots, p$.
- $\mathbf{u}_t = (u_{1t}, u_{2t}, \dots, u_{unt})'$ is an $(n \times 1)$ vector of white-noise (innovation) error terms.
- $E(\mathbf{u}_t) = \mathbf{0}$, $E(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_t') = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u$ (positive definite covariance matrix), and $E(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_{s}') = \mathbf{0}$ for $t \neq s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A VAR($p$) model contains $n^2 p + n$ coefficients in total (excluding the constant). When $n$ and $p$ are large, the number of parameters grows rapidly, making parsimony a key concern.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a weather forecaster who wants to predict **Temperature**, **Humidity**, and **Pressure** simultaneously. These three variables do not exist in isolation — yesterday's pressure affects today's temperature, today's humidity feeds back into tomorrow's pressure, and so on.

A simple AR model would predict each variable independently (temperature from past temperatures only). A **VAR model** is like a **"team meeting"** where each variable listens to the recent history of *every* other variable before making its prediction. Each equation is a "regression" of one variable on the entire past of the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Echo Chamber":** Think of a group of friends on a social network. What each person posts today depends partly on what they posted yesterday AND on what their friends posted yesterday. The friends' posts become a feedback loop, just like a VAR captures mutual feedback among economic indicators such as **GDP, Inflation, and Interest Rates**.

## 1.3 Special Case: VAR(1)

The simplest VAR is the **first-order vector autoregressive model**:

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{u}_t
$$

This model already captures rich dynamic interactions and serves as the building block for the analysis of higher-order systems via the **companion form**.

## 1.4 Why VAR Models are Important

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering & Economic Significance:**
> - **Macroeconomics:** Modeling GDP, inflation, unemployment, and money supply jointly.
> - **Finance:** Capturing co-movements of asset returns, volatility spillovers.
> - **Energy Systems:** Modeling interdependence between electricity demand, price, and renewable generation.
> - **Biomedical Signals:** Multichannel EEG/ECG analysis where channels influence one another.
> - **Forecasting:** Producing coherent multi-step forecasts for all variables simultaneously.

## 1.5 Reduced-Form vs Structural VAR

Two important conceptual distinctions in KTU syllabus:

| Concept | Description |
|---|---|
| **Reduced-Form VAR** | Treats $\mathbf{u}_t$ as contemporaneous correlation among residuals. Coefficients describe reduced-form dynamics. |
| **Structural VAR (SVAR)** | Imposes economic/theoretical restrictions to identify contemporaneous causal effects (shocks). |

> [!TIP]
> A reduced-form VAR assumes the contemporaneous relationships among variables are absorbed into $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u$. A structural VAR uses a matrix $\mathbf{B}_0$ such that $\mathbf{B}_0 \mathbf{u}_t = \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$ (structural shocks), where $\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \sim (\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Assumptions of a VAR($p$) Process

1. **Linearity:** The system evolves as a linear function of past values.
2. **Stationarity:** The process is **weakly (covariance) stationary** — mean, variance, and autocovariances are time-invariant.
3. **Homoscedastic White Noise:** $E(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_t') = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u$ (constant, finite, positive definite).
4. **No Autocorrelation:** $E(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_s') = \mathbf{0}$ for $t \neq s$.
5. **Independence of Past:** $E(\mathbf{Y}_{t-s} \mathbf{u}_t') = \mathbf{0}$ for $s > 0$.

## 2.2 Lag Operator Form

Using the backshift (lag) operator $L$ where $L \mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{Y}_{t-1}$, the VAR($p$) can be written as:

$$
\mathbf{A}(L)\, \mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{u}_t
$$

where

$$
\mathbf{A}(L) = \mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A}_1 L - \mathbf{A}_2 L^2 - \dots - \mathbf{A}_p L^p
$$

is the $(n \times n)$ **autoregressive lag polynomial matrix**.

## 2.3 Mean of a Stationary VAR

For a stationary VAR($p$), the unconditional mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu} = E(\mathbf{Y}_t)$ is obtained by taking expectations of both sides:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{A}_2 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \dots + \mathbf{A}_p \boldsymbol{\mu}
$$

Solving:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A}_1 - \mathbf{A}_2 - \dots - \mathbf{A}_p)^{-1} \mathbf{c}
$$

This requires the matrix $(\mathbf{I}_n - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i)$ to be **nonsingular**.

## 2.4 Stationarity Condition — Companion Form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Point:** A VAR($p$) is stationary **if and only if** all eigenvalues of the **companion matrix** $\mathbf{F}$ lie **strictly inside the unit circle** (i.e., their moduli are less than **1**).

The companion matrix $\mathbf{F}$ of size $(np \times np)$ is constructed as:

$$
\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A}_1 & \mathbf{A}_2 & \dots & \mathbf{A}_{p-1} & \mathbf{A}_p \\ \mathbf{I}_n & \mathbf{0} & \dots & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I}_n & \dots & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \dots & \mathbf{I}_n & \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Equivalently, the stationarity condition is stated in terms of the **characteristic polynomial**:

$$
\det\!\left(\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A}_1 z - \mathbf{A}_2 z^2 - \dots - \mathbf{A}_p z^p\right) \neq 0 \quad \text{for } \vert z \vert \leq 1
$$

All roots must lie **outside** the unit circle in the complex plane.

## 2.5 Estimation — Equation-by-Equation OLS

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** Provided the VAR is stable (stationary), each equation of a reduced-form VAR can be estimated **efficiently by ordinary least squares (OLS)** applied equation-by-equation. This is because under standard assumptions, the OLS estimator is **consistent** and asymptotically equivalent to **GLS** for each marginal equation.

For the $i$-th equation ($i = 1, 2, \dots, n$):

$$
y_{it} = c_i + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_{jk}^{(i)} y_{k,t-j} + u_{it}
$$

This can be written compactly as:

$$
\mathbf{y}_i = \mathbf{Z}\, \boldsymbol{\beta}_i + \mathbf{u}_i
$$

where $\mathbf{Z} = [\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{Y}_{t-1}', \mathbf{Y}_{t-2}', \dots, \mathbf{Y}_{t-p}']$ is the regressor matrix.

The OLS estimator:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_i = (\mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{Z})^{-1} \mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{y}_i
$$

## 2.6 Lag Order Selection — Information Criteria

To select $p$, we minimize an **information criterion**:

| Criterion | Formula |
|---|---|
| **AIC** (Akaike) | $\ln \vert \hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u \vert + \dfrac{2 k}{T}$ |
| **BIC / SBC** (Schwarz) | $\ln \vert \hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u \vert + \dfrac{k \ln T}{T}$ |
| **HQIC** (Hannan–Quinn) | $\ln \vert \hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u \vert + \dfrac{2 k \ln(\ln T)}{T}$ |

where $k = n^2 p + n$ is the number of estimated parameters per equation, $T$ is the sample size, and $\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u$ is the estimated residual covariance matrix.

> [!TIP]
> **BIC penalizes model complexity more strongly than AIC** and tends to select more parsimonious models.

## 2.7 Forecasting

The $h$-step ahead forecast, conditional on information at time $T$, is:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+h \vert T} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+h-i \vert T}
$$

with the convention that $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+j \vert T} = \mathbf{Y}_{T+j}$ for $j \leq 0$.

**Forecast Error Covariance Matrix:**

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}(h) = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u + \sum_{s=1}^{h-1} \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_s'
$$

where $\mathbf{\Psi}_s$ are the **MA($\infty$) coefficient matrices** (moving-average representation).

## 2.8 Granger Causality

A variable $x$ **Granger-causes** $y$ if past values of $x$ provide statistically significant information about $y$ beyond the information contained in past values of $y$ alone.

The **F-test** statistic (with $m$ restrictions and $K$ unrestricted parameters):

$$
F = \frac{(\text{RSS}_R - \text{RSS}_U)/m}{\text{RSS}_U/(TK)}
$$

Under the null hypothesis of no Granger causality, $F \sim F(m, TK)$.

## 2.9 Impulse Response Function (IRF)

The IRF traces the effect of a one-time shock to the $j$-th variable's innovation on the $i$-th variable at horizon $h$.

**MA Representation Coefficients:**

$$
\mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_n, \quad \mathbf{\Psi}_s = \sum_{j=1}^{s} \mathbf{\Psi}_{s-j} \mathbf{A}_j, \quad s \geq 1
$$

(where $\mathbf{A}_j = \mathbf{0}$ for $j > p$).

The element $[\mathbf{\Psi}_s]_{ij}$ is the response of variable $i$ at time $t+s$ to a unit shock in variable $j$ at time $t$.

## 2.10 Forecast Error Variance Decomposition (FEVD)

The proportion of the $h$-step forecast error variance of $y_i$ attributable to shocks in $y_j$:

$$
\theta_{ij}(h) = \frac{\sigma_{jj}^{-1} \sum_{s=0}^{h-1} (e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u e_j)^2}{\sum_{s=0}^{h-1} e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_s' e_i}
$$

where $e_i$ is the $i$-th column of $\mathbf{I}_n$ and $\sigma_{jj}$ is the $j$-th diagonal element of $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u$.

## 2.11 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition |
|---|---|
| **VAR($p$) form** | $\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{Y}_{t-i} + \mathbf{u}_t$ |
| **Lag operator form** | $\mathbf{A}(L)\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{u}_t$ |
| **Unconditional mean** | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_n - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$ |
| **Companion matrix size** | $np \times np$ |
| **Stationarity condition** | All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{F}$ satisfy $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$ |
| **Characteristic root condition** | $\det(\mathbf{I}_n - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i z^i) \neq 0$ for $\vert z \vert \leq 1$ |
| **OLS estimator** | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_i = (\mathbf{Z}'\mathbf{Z})^{-1}\mathbf{Z}'\mathbf{y}_i$ |
| **Information criteria** | $\ln \vert \hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u \vert + \phi(k, T)$ where $\phi = 2k/T$ (AIC), $k\ln T/T$ (BIC) |
| **Forecast formula** | $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+h \vert T} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+h-i \vert T}$ |
| **Forecast error variance** | $\mathbf{\Sigma}(h) = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u + \sum_{s=1}^{h-1} \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_s'$ |
| **MA($\infty$) coefficients** | $\mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_n$, $\mathbf{\Psi}_s = \sum_{j=1}^{\min(s,p)} \mathbf{\Psi}_{s-j} \mathbf{A}_j$ |
| **Granger F-test** | $F = \frac{(\text{RSS}_R - \text{RSS}_U)/m}{\text{RSS}_U/(TK - K)}$ |
| **FEVD** | $\theta_{ij}(h) = \frac{\sigma_{jj}^{-1} \sum_{s=0}^{h-1} (e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u e_j)^2}{\sum_{s=0}^{h-1} e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_s' e_i}$ |
| **Total parameters** | $n^2 p + n$ |

## 2.12 Engineering Application Insight

> [!TIP]
> In **power systems engineering**, a VAR model is used to study the dynamic interactions among **load demand, electricity price, and renewable generation**. A VAR(2) model fitted on hourly data can produce coherent 24-hour forecasts that respect the cross-coupling of these variables — something independent AR models cannot achieve.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Unconditional Mean of a Stationary VAR($p$)

**Starting Point:**

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} + \dots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{u}_t
$$

**Step 1 — Take expectations on both sides:** Since $\mathbf{Y}_t$ is stationary, $E(\mathbf{Y}_t) = E(\mathbf{Y}_{t-1}) = \dots = \boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $E(\mathbf{u}_t) = \mathbf{0}$.

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{A}_2 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \dots + \mathbf{A}_p \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ on the right-hand side:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \left(\sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i\right) \boldsymbol{\mu}
$$

**Step 3 — Bring the $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ terms to the left side:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i\right) \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}
$$

**Step 4 — Distributive property of matrix algebra:**

$$
\left(\mathbf{I}_n - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i\right) \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}
$$

**Step 5 — Pre-multiply both sides by the inverse (assuming it exists):**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left(\mathbf{I}_n - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i\right)^{-1} \mathbf{c}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The invertibility condition is precisely $\det(\mathbf{I}_n - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i) \neq 0$, which is the **$z=1$** version of the stationarity condition.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Companion Form for VAR($p$)

**Starting Point:** A VAR($p$) system

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} + \dots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{u}_t
$$

**Step 1 — Define an extended state vector $\mathbf{Z}_t$ of dimension $(np \times 1)$:**

$$
\mathbf{Z}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{Y}_t \\ \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{Y}_{t-p+1} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the next-period state in terms of the current state:**

$$
\mathbf{Y}_{t+1} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_t + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \dots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p+1} + \mathbf{u}_{t+1}
$$

**Step 3 — The transition equation for the extended state:**

$$
\mathbf{Z}_{t+1} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{Y}_{t+1} \\ \mathbf{Y}_t \\ \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{Y}_{t-p+2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{c} \\ \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A}_1 & \mathbf{A}_2 & \dots & \mathbf{A}_{p-1} & \mathbf{A}_p \\ \mathbf{I}_n & \mathbf{0} & \dots & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I}_n & \dots & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \dots & \mathbf{I}_n & \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{Y}_t \\ \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} \\ \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{Y}_{t-p+1} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{u}_{t+1} \\ \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compact notation:**

$$
\mathbf{Z}_{t+1} = \mathbf{C}^* + \mathbf{F}\, \mathbf{Z}_t + \mathbf{U}_{t+1}
$$

**Step 5 — Stability condition:** The eigenvalues of the $(np \times np)$ matrix $\mathbf{F}$ must all lie inside the unit circle: $\vert \lambda_i(\mathbf{F}) \vert < 1$ for all $i$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Covariance Matrix of a Stationary VAR(1)

Consider a stationary VAR(1) with $E(\mathbf{Y}_t) = \mathbf{0}$ (i.e., demeaned):

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{u}_t, \quad \mathbf{u}_t \sim (\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma}_u), \quad E(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_s') = \mathbf{0} \text{ for } t \neq s
$$

**Step 1 — Define the covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}_y = E(\mathbf{Y}_t \mathbf{Y}_t')$:**

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}_y = E\!\left[(\mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{u}_t)(\mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{u}_t)'\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the product:**

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}_y = \mathbf{A}_1 E(\mathbf{Y}_{t-1}\mathbf{Y}_{t-1}') \mathbf{A}_1' + \mathbf{A}_1 E(\mathbf{Y}_{t-1}\mathbf{u}_t') + E(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{Y}_{t-1}') \mathbf{A}_1' + E(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_t')
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the orthogonality property** $E(\mathbf{Y}_{t-1} \mathbf{u}_t') = \mathbf{0}$ and stationarity $E(\mathbf{Y}_{t-1}\mathbf{Y}_{t-1}') = \mathbf{\Sigma}_y$:

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}_y = \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{\Sigma}_y \mathbf{A}_1' + \mathbf{\Sigma}_u
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange (discrete-time Lyapunov equation):**

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}_y - \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{\Sigma}_y \mathbf{A}_1' = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u
$$

**Step 5 — Vectorize both sides** using the identity $\text{vec}(\mathbf{MXN}) = (\mathbf{N}' \otimes \mathbf{M})\,\text{vec}(\mathbf{X})$:

$$
\text{vec}(\mathbf{\Sigma}_y) - (\mathbf{A}_1 \otimes \mathbf{A}_1)\,\text{vec}(\mathbf{\Sigma}_y) = \text{vec}(\mathbf{\Sigma}_u)
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out vec($\mathbf{\Sigma}_y$):**

$$
(\mathbf{I}_{n^2} - \mathbf{A}_1 \otimes \mathbf{A}_1)\,\text{vec}(\mathbf{\Sigma}_y) = \text{vec}(\mathbf{\Sigma}_u)
$$

**Step 7 — Solve by inverting the Kronecker-difference matrix** (this is well-defined when all eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}_1$ are inside the unit circle):

$$
\text{vec}(\mathbf{\Sigma}_y) = (\mathbf{I}_{n^2} - \mathbf{A}_1 \otimes \mathbf{A}_1)^{-1}\,\text{vec}(\mathbf{\Sigma}_u)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability requirement:** The matrix $(\mathbf{I}_{n^2} - \mathbf{A}_1 \otimes \mathbf{A}_1)$ is invertible **iff all eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of $\mathbf{A}_1$ satisfy $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$** (i.e., the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}_1 \otimes \mathbf{A}_1$, which are $\lambda_i \lambda_j$, all have modulus less than 1).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Impulse Response Coefficients via Recursion

**Starting Point:** A stationary VAR($p$) has the **MA($\infty$) representation:**

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sum_{s=0}^{\infty} \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{u}_{t-s}, \quad \mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_n
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the VAR($p$) equation and equate coefficients of $\mathbf{u}_{t-s}$:**

$$
\sum_{s=0}^{\infty} \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{u}_{t-s} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i \sum_{s=0}^{\infty} \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{u}_{t-i-s} + \mathbf{u}_t
$$

**Step 2 — Match the coefficient of $\mathbf{u}_t$** (i.e., $s=0$): The LHS gives $\mathbf{\Psi}_0$, the RHS gives $\mathbf{I}_n$, so

$$
\mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_n
$$

**Step 3 — Match the coefficient of $\mathbf{u}_{t-s}$ for $s \geq 1$:** The LHS gives $\mathbf{\Psi}_s$, and the RHS gives $\sum_{i=1}^{\min(s,p)} \mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{\Psi}_{s-i}$:

$$
\mathbf{\Psi}_s = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(s,p)} \mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{\Psi}_{s-i}
$$

**Step 4 — Explicitly expand for the first few horizons:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{\Psi}_1 &= \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{A}_1 \\
\mathbf{\Psi}_2 &= \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{\Psi}_1 + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{A}_1^2 + \mathbf{A}_2 \\
\mathbf{\Psi}_3 &= \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{\Psi}_2 + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{\Psi}_1 + \mathbf{A}_3 \mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{A}_1^3 + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{A}_2 + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{A}_1 + \mathbf{A}_3 \\
\mathbf{\Psi}_4 &= \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{\Psi}_3 + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{\Psi}_2 + \mathbf{A}_3 \mathbf{\Psi}_1 + \mathbf{A}_4 \mathbf{\Psi}_0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The matrix $\mathbf{\Psi}_h$ is the **orthogonalized impulse response matrix** in the *non-orthogonalized* (reduced-form) sense. To get truly orthogonalized responses, apply $\mathbf{\Psi}_h \mathbf{P}$ where $\mathbf{P}$ comes from the Cholesky decomposition $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{P}'$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Forecast Error Variance Decomposition

**Step 1 — The $h$-step forecast error** is given by

$$
\mathbf{e}_{T+h} = \mathbf{Y}_{T+h} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+h \vert T} = \sum_{s=0}^{h-1} \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{u}_{T+h-s}
$$

**Step 2 — Covariance of the forecast error:**

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}(h) = E(\mathbf{e}_{T+h} \mathbf{e}_{T+h}') = \sum_{s=0}^{h-1} \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_s'
$$

**Step 3 — Variance of the $i$-th variable's $h$-step forecast error:** Take the $i$-th diagonal element using selection vector $e_i$:

$$
\sigma_{ii}(h) = e_i' \mathbf{\Sigma}(h) e_i = \sum_{s=0}^{h-1} e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_s' e_i
$$

**Step 4 — Decompose each $\mathbf{u}_{T+h-s}$ into orthogonal components:** Using Cholesky $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{P}'$ and noting $\mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{u}_{T+h-s} = \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{P}\mathbf{P}^{-1}\mathbf{u}_{T+h-s}$:

$$
\sigma_{ii}(h) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{s=0}^{h-1} (e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{P} e_j)^2 \sigma_{jj}^{*}
$$

where $\sigma_{jj}^{*}$ are the variances of the orthogonalized shocks.

**Step 5 — Define the contribution share of shock $j$ to the $h$-step forecast error variance of variable $i$:**

$$
\theta_{ij}(h) = \frac{\sigma_{jj}^{*-1} \sum_{s=0}^{h-1} (e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u e_j)^2}{\sum_{s=0}^{h-1} e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_s' e_i}
$$

By construction, $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_{ij}(h) = 1$ for each $i$ and $h$.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — VAR(1) Numerical Example

**Setting:** Let $n = 2$ with parameters

$$
\mathbf{c} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{\Sigma}_u = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0.3 \\ 0.3 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Computation of mean:**

$$
\mathbf{I}_2 - \mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.2 \\ -0.1 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det = 0.30 - 0.02 = 0.28
$$

$$
(\mathbf{I}_2 - \mathbf{A}_1)^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.28} \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2.1429 & 0.7143 \\ 0.3571 & 1.7857 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_2 - \mathbf{A}_1)^{-1} \mathbf{c} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2.1429 & 0.7143 \\ 0.3571 & 1.7857 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.5714 \\ 4.0000 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}_1$:** The characteristic polynomial is $\lambda^2 - 0.9\lambda + 0.18 = 0$, giving

$$
\lambda = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.81 - 0.72}}{2} = \frac{0.9 \pm 0.3}{2} = 0.6,\ 0.3
$$

Both eigenvalues are inside the unit circle, so the VAR(1) is **stable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Observation:** For a stable VAR, $\mathbf{Y}_t$ converges to its mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ as $t \to \infty$ when the system is hit by transitory shocks. Permanent shocks would require a non-stationary (unit-root) VAR, covered in VECM models.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — VAR Modeling Workflow

The following diagram captures the complete **functional architecture flow** for fitting and analyzing a VAR model — the recommended KTU standard procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Collect Multivariate Time Series Data] --> B[Step 1: Visual Inspection and Descriptive Statistics]
    B --> C[Step 2: Test for Stationarity: ADF and KPSS Unit Root Tests]
    C --> D{All Series Stationary?}
    D -- No --> E[Step 2a: Difference Non Stationary Series or Use VECM]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Step 3: Determine Optimal Lag p using AIC, BIC, HQIC]
    F --> G[Step 4: Estimate VARp via OLS Equation by Equation]
    G --> H[Step 5: Diagnostic Checks on Residuals]
    H --> I[Step 5a: Portmanteau Test for Autocorrelation]
    H --> J[Step 5b: Test for Normality and Heteroscedasticity]
    I --> K{Residuals White Noise?}
    J --> K
    K -- No --> L[Step 5c: Re specify Model: Change Lag Order or Add Variables]
    L --> G
    K -- Yes --> M[Step 6: Stability Check: Eigenvalues of Companion Matrix]
    M --> N{All Eigenvalues Inside Unit Circle?}
    N -- No --> O[Step 6a: Reduce Lag Order or Re estimate]
    O --> G
    N -- Yes --> P[Step 7: Structural Analysis]
    P --> Q[Impulse Response Function IRF]
    P --> R[Forecast Error Variance Decomposition FEVD]
    P --> S[Granger Causality Tests]
    P --> T[Step 8: Generate Multi Step Forecasts and Confidence Bands]
    T --> U[END: Report Findings and Policy Implications]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `A`, `B`, `M`, `N`). Labels use raw uppercase and lowercase alphanumeric text with no embedded markdown formatting. No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — VAR(2) Internal Feedback Structure

The diagram below visualizes the **information flow inside a bivariate VAR(2) system**, showing how the present and lagged values of each variable influence the others.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAG1[LAG 1 INFORMATION SET]
        y1t1[Y1 t minus 1] 
        y2t1[Y2 t minus 1]
    end
    subgraph LAG2[LAG 2 INFORMATION SET]
        y1t2[Y1 t minus 2]
        y2t2[Y2 t minus 2]
    end
    subgraph PRESENT[PRESENT OUTPUT]
        y1t[Y1 current]
        y2t[Y2 current]
    end
    y1t1 -->|A11 and A12| y1t
    y2t1 -->|A12 and A22| y1t
    y1t2 -->|A11 and A12| y1t
    y2t2 -->|A12 and A22| y1t
    y1t1 -->|A21 and A22| y2t
    y2t1 -->|A21 and A22| y2t
    y1t2 -->|A21 and A22| y2t
    y2t2 -->|A21 and A22| y2t
    y1t -->|+ u1t| y1t
    y2t -->|+ u2t| y2t
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — MA($\infty$) Representation and IRF Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VARp Model Yt equals c plus sum Ai Yt minus i plus ut] --> B[Compute Companion Matrix F of size np by np]
    B --> C{Eigenvalues of F inside Unit Circle?}
    C -- No --> D[Re estimate or Differencing Required]
    C -- Yes --> E[Compute Psi h via Recursion]
    E --> F[Psi 0 equals I]
    E --> G[Psi 1 equals A1]
    E --> H[Psi s equals sum Ai Psi s minus i]
    F --> I[Construct IRF Matrix at each horizon h]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Cholesky Decomposition for Orthogonalization]
    J --> K[Plot IRF with Confidence Bands via Bootstrap]
    K --> L[Compute FEVD using Psi h and Sigma u]
    L --> M[Generate h Step Forecasts]
    M --> N[Output: Forecasts, IRF, FEVD, Granger Causality]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define a **Vector Autoregressive (VAR) model of order $p$**. State its general form and explain the role of each component.

**Model Answer:**

A **VAR($p$)** model describes the dynamic behavior of an $n$-dimensional stationary time series vector $\mathbf{Y}_t$ in which each component is regressed on its own past values and the past values of all other components.

General form:

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} + \dots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{u}_t
$$

Roles of components:
- **$\mathbf{c}$** ($n \times 1$): Intercept vector capturing deterministic trends.
- **$\mathbf{A}_i$** ($n \times n$, for $i = 1, \dots, p$): Coefficient matrices capturing how the $i$-th lag of every variable influences every current variable.
- **$\mathbf{u}_t$** ($n \times 1$): White-noise innovation vector with $E(\mathbf{u}_t) = \mathbf{0}$ and $E(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_t') = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u$.
- **$p$**: Order of the VAR, selected using information criteria (AIC, BIC, HQIC).

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** State the **stationarity condition for a VAR($p$)** process using the companion form.

**Model Answer:**

A VAR($p$) process is **stationary** if and only if all eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of its $(np \times np)$ **companion matrix**

$$
\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A}_1 & \mathbf{A}_2 & \dots & \mathbf{A}_{p-1} & \mathbf{A}_p \\ \mathbf{I}_n & \mathbf{0} & \dots & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I}_n & \dots & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \dots & \mathbf{I}_n & \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix}
$$

satisfy the condition $\vert \lambda_i(\mathbf{F}) \vert < 1$ for all $i = 1, 2, \dots, np$, i.e., all eigenvalues lie **strictly inside the unit circle** in the complex plane.

Equivalently, all roots of $\det(\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A}_1 z - \mathbf{A}_2 z^2 - \dots - \mathbf{A}_p z^p) = 0$ lie **outside** the unit circle.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Part B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — ESE Model Question | Module 3 | Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **equation-by-equation OLS estimation** of a VAR($p$) model. Why is OLS consistent and efficient for each marginal equation?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **unconditional mean** of a stationary VAR($p$) and discuss what happens if the matrix $(\mathbf{I}_n - \sum \mathbf{A}_i)$ is singular.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) OLS Estimation of VAR($p$) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Write the $i$-th scalar equation.** The $i$-th equation ($i = 1, \dots, n$) of the VAR($p$) is

$$
y_{it} = c_i + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_{jk}^{(i)} y_{k,t-j} + u_{it}
$$

**Step 2 — Stack the regressors.** Define the row vector of regressors for observation $t$:

$$
\mathbf{z}_t = (1, y_{1,t-1}, \dots, y_{n,t-1}, y_{1,t-2}, \dots, y_{n,t-2}, \dots, y_{1,t-p}, \dots, y_{n,t-p})
$$

**Step 3 — Stack across observations.** Let $\mathbf{Z}$ be the $(T \times (np+1))$ matrix whose $t$-th row is $\mathbf{z}_t$, and let $\mathbf{y}_i = (y_{i,p+1}, \dots, y_{iT})'$ and $\mathbf{u}_i = (u_{i,p+1}, \dots, u_{iT})'$. Then

$$
\mathbf{y}_i = \mathbf{Z} \boldsymbol{\beta}_i + \mathbf{u}_i
$$

**Step 4 — Apply OLS.** The OLS estimator is

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_i = (\mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{Z})^{-1} \mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{y}_i
$$

This procedure is repeated for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$.

**Step 5 — Why OLS is consistent and efficient (KTU high-yield).** Since $\mathbf{u}_t$ is white noise with $E(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_s') = \mathbf{0}$ for $t \neq s$ and the regressors in $\mathbf{Z}$ are strictly exogenous ($E(\mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{u}_i) = \mathbf{0}$), the OLS estimator is **consistent**. Under Gaussian innovations, OLS coincides with **Maximum Likelihood** and is therefore **asymptotically efficient** for each marginal equation. This is a remarkable property: full ML is unnecessary; equation-by-equation OLS suffices.

**Step 6 — Covariance matrix of the estimator:**

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_i) = \hat{\sigma}_{i}^{2} (\mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{Z})^{-1}
$$

where $\hat{\sigma}_{i}^{2} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_t \hat{u}_{it}^2$.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Unconditional Mean and Singularity Case (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Take expectations of the VAR($p$) equation** (derivation in Section 3.1):

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \left(\sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i\right) \boldsymbol{\mu}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left(\mathbf{I}_n - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i\right)^{-1} \mathbf{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Singular case.** If $\det(\mathbf{I}_n - \sum \mathbf{A}_i) = 0$, the matrix is **non-invertible** and the unconditional mean does not exist in the usual sense. This typically occurs when:
- The system contains **stochastic trends (unit roots)** — a clear sign of non-stationarity.
- A combination of the variables is **perfectly collinear** in levels.

In such cases, one must:
- **Difference** the offending series to induce stationarity, or
- Use a **Vector Error Correction Model (VECM)** if the variables are cointegrated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students forget that the constant term in the **unconditional mean** depends on the *sum* of all coefficient matrices, not just $\mathbf{A}_1$.
> 2. In OLS estimation, students often mistakenly claim that *all* equations must be estimated *jointly* by SUR/ML. Correct answer: under standard assumptions, **equation-by-equation OLS is already efficient** — but the *residuals* across equations should be checked for cross-correlation when using ML standard errors.
> 3. Confusing the dimension of $\mathbf{F}$: it is $np \times np$, **not** $n \times n$. Writing $n \times n$ is a common error costing 1 mark.

---

### Part B — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — ESE Model Question | Module 3 | Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **Granger causality**? Explain the F-test procedure to test whether variable $x$ Granger-causes $y$ in a bivariate VAR.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the **Impulse Response Function (IRF)** and **Forecast Error Variance Decomposition (FEVD)**. Derive the recursion for the MA coefficient matrices $\mathbf{\Psi}_h$.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Granger Causality Test (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Definition.** A variable $x$ **Granger-causes** $y$ if, given the past of $y$, the past of $x$ provides statistically significant additional information for predicting $y$. Symbolically:

$$
\sigma^2(y_t \vert \mathbf{Y}_{t-1}^{(y)}) > \sigma^2(y_t \vert \mathbf{Y}_{t-1}^{(y)}, \mathbf{Y}_{t-1}^{(x)})
$$

**Step 2 — Set up the unrestricted (full) model.** The unrestricted bivariate VAR($p$) is

$$
y_t = c_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{11}^{(i)} y_{t-i} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{12}^{(i)} x_{t-i} + u_{1t}
$$

$$
x_t = c_2 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{21}^{(i)} y_{t-i} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{22}^{(i)} x_{t-i} + u_{2t}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the restricted model** for testing whether $x$ Granger-causes $y$ (exclude all $x$ lags from the $y$-equation):

$$
y_t = c_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_{11}^{(i)} y_{t-i} + v_{1t}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute residual sums of squares.** Let $RSS_U$ and $RSS_R$ be the residual sum of squares from the $y$-equation in the unrestricted and restricted models, respectively. Let $m$ be the number of restrictions (here $m = p$) and $K$ be the number of parameters in the unrestricted $y$-equation (here $K = np + 1$).

**Step 5 — Compute the F-statistic:**

$$
F = \frac{(RSS_R - RSS_U)/m}{RSS_U/(T - K)}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule.** Under the null hypothesis $H_0$: "$x$ does not Granger-cause $y$":

- If $F > F_{\alpha}(m, T - K)$, **reject $H_0$** at significance level $\alpha$ — conclude that $x$ Granger-causes $y$.
- Otherwise, fail to reject.

**Step 7 — Extensions.** The test can be run in both directions to test **bi-directional**, **uni-directional**, or **no-causality** patterns. For systems of $n > 2$ variables, the same logic applies; $m$ becomes the number of restricted lag coefficients.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) IRF and FEVD with MA Coefficient Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — MA($\infty$) representation.** A stationary VAR($p$) admits the representation

$$
\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sum_{s=0}^{\infty} \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{u}_{t-s}
$$

**Step 2 — Recursion for $\mathbf{\Psi}_s$** (proved in Section 3.4):

$$
\mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_n, \quad \mathbf{\Psi}_s = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(s,p)} \mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{\Psi}_{s-i} \quad \text{for } s \geq 1
$$

**Step 3 — IRF definition.** The element $[\mathbf{\Psi}_h]_{ij}$ is the response of variable $i$ at horizon $h$ to a unit shock in the innovation of variable $j$ at time $0$. The **orthogonalized IRF** is obtained by premultiplying with a Cholesky factor $\mathbf{P}$ such that $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{P}'$:

$$
\mathbf{\Psi}_h^{\text{orth}} = \mathbf{\Psi}_h \mathbf{P}
$$

**Step 4 — FEVD definition.** The fraction of the $h$-step forecast error variance of $y_i$ attributable to orthogonalized shocks in $y_j$:

$$
\theta_{ij}(h) = \frac{\sigma_{jj}^{*-1} \sum_{s=0}^{h-1} (e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u e_j)^2}{\sum_{s=0}^{h-1} e_i' \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_s' e_i}
$$

**Step 5 — Properties.** For each $i$ and $h$: $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_{ij}(h) = 1$ (shares sum to one). As $h \to \infty$, the shares converge to the long-run contributions.

**Step 6 — Confidence bands.** Bootstrap or Monte Carlo methods (e.g., residual bootstrap) are used to obtain confidence intervals for the IRF and FEVD estimates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often write the **F-test formula incorrectly** as $F = \frac{RSS_U - RSS_R}{RSS_R/(T-K)}$. The correct sign is $RSS_R - RSS_U$ in the **numerator**.
> 2. Confusing **Granger causality** with **structural causality** — Granger causality is purely a *predictive* concept based on temporal precedence, not on true economic/theoretical causation.
> 3. In the IRF recursion, students sometimes forget to enforce $\mathbf{A}_i = \mathbf{0}$ for $i > p$, leading to incorrect sums.
> 4. Forgetting to ensure the FEVD shares sum to 1 (a built-in check that examiners use).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Vector Autoregressive (VAR) Models**

- A **VAR($p$)** models $n$ time series jointly, with each variable regressed on $p$ lags of itself **and all** other variables.
- The general form is $\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{Y}_{t-i} + \mathbf{u}_t$.
- **Stationarity** requires all eigenvalues of the $(np \times np)$ **companion matrix** to lie **inside the unit circle**.
- Equivalently, roots of $\det(\mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{A}_1 z - \dots - \mathbf{A}_p z^p) = 0$ must lie **outside** the unit circle.
- The **unconditional mean** is $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_n - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$.
- **Estimation** is done by **OLS equation-by-equation** (consistent and efficient under standard assumptions).
- **Lag order** is selected by minimizing **AIC, BIC, or HQIC**; BIC penalizes complexity more.
- The **MA($\infty$) representation** is $\mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_n$, $\mathbf{\Psi}_s = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(s,p)} \mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{\Psi}_{s-i}$.
- **Forecasts** are obtained recursively: $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+h \vert T} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{A}_i \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+h-i \vert T}$.
- **Forecast error variance:** $\mathbf{\Sigma}(h) = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u + \sum_{s=1}^{h-1} \mathbf{\Psi}_s \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_s'$.
- **Granger causality** is tested by an F-test comparing restricted vs unrestricted models.
- **IRF** traces the dynamic response of each variable to a unit shock; **FEVD** decomposes forecast error variance.
- **Diagnostic checks:** Portmanteau test (no autocorrelation), normality test, ARCH test (no heteroscedasticity).
- **Common pitfalls:** Forgetting $np \times np$ size of $\mathbf{F}$; using $RSS_U - RSS_R$ instead of $RSS_R - RSS_U$ in F-test; confusing Granger and structural causality; ignoring unit roots before fitting VAR (leads to spurious regressions).
- **Total parameters in VAR($p$):** $n^2 p + n$ (excluding $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u$ which has $n(n+1)/2$ free elements).
- **Practical tip:** Always check the inverse roots of the characteristic polynomial (AR roots) — if all lie inside the unit circle, the model is stable.

# Vector ARMA Models (VARMA)

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Vector Autoregressive Moving Average (VARMA) model** is a multivariate extension of the univariate ARMA model used to capture the linear interdependencies among multiple time series variables. The general **VARMA(p, q)** model of order (p, q) for a $K$-dimensional vector time series $\mathbf{y}_t = (y_{1t}, y_{2t}, \ldots, y_{Kt})'$ is given by:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Phi}_1 \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\Phi}_2 \mathbf{y}_{t-2} + \cdots + \mathbf{\Phi}_p \mathbf{y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t - \mathbf{\Theta}_1 \mathbf{\varepsilon}_{t-1} - \cdots - \mathbf{\Theta}_q \mathbf{\varepsilon}_{t-q}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{c}$ is a $K \times 1$ vector of constants (intercept).
- $\mathbf{\Phi}_i$ are $K \times K$ autoregressive coefficient matrices.
- $\mathbf{\Theta}_j$ are $K \times K$ moving average coefficient matrices.
- $\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$ is a $K \times 1$ white noise vector with $E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t) = \mathbf{0}$ and $E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t') = \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$ (a $K \times K$ positive definite covariance matrix), and $E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \mathbf{\varepsilon}_s') = \mathbf{0}$ for $t \neq s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Cases (KTU High-Yield):**
> - When $q = 0$, we obtain a **Vector Autoregressive VAR(p)** model.
> - When $p = 0$, we obtain a **Vector Moving Average VMA(q)** model.
> - The **Vector AR(1)**: $\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$ is the multivariate analog of the univariate AR(1).

The model can be written compactly using the backshift operator $B$ as:

$$\mathbf{\Phi}(B) \mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Theta}(B) \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$$

where $\mathbf{\Phi}(B) = \mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{\Phi}_1 B - \cdots - \mathbf{\Phi}_p B^p$ and $\mathbf{\Theta}(B) = \mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{\Theta}_1 B - \cdots - \mathbf{\Theta}_q B^q$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The "Weather Dashboard" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are a meteorologist tracking **three variables simultaneously** — *Temperature*, *Humidity*, and *Wind Speed* — across Kerala. These variables do not operate in isolation. High humidity **causes** changes in temperature (with a lag), and wind speed affects humidity (also with a lag). A univariate ARMA model would be like tracking *just* temperature with a thermometer. A **Vector ARMA model is like a complete dashboard** where every variable is connected to every other variable. Each variable becomes both a "driver" and a "responder" in a coupled system. The $\mathbf{\Phi}$ matrices capture **how the past of all variables influences the present**, while the $\mathbf{\Theta}$ matrices capture **how the past shocks (unexpected weather events) propagate**.

## 1.3 Why Use VARMA?

- **Joint dynamics**: Captures simultaneous and lagged relationships between multiple series.
- **Forecasting**: Provides coherent multi-step forecasts for all series jointly.
- **Causal inference**: Foundation for **Granger causality** testing and **impulse response analysis**.
- **Reduced form**: Pure VARMA models are *atheoretical* — they let the data speak about interrelations.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Order (p, q)**: The number of AR and MA lags in the vector model.
> - **AIC / SBC (BIC)**: Multivariate information criteria used for order selection: $AIC = -2\ln(L) + 2k$ where $k$ is the number of free parameters.
> - **Innovation covariance**: The covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$ which is **not necessarily diagonal** — this is the key generalization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stationarity Condition

A VARMA(p, q) model is **(weakly) stationary** if and only if the roots of the **determinantal polynomial**:

$$\det[\mathbf{\Phi}(z)] = \det[\mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{\Phi}_1 z - \mathbf{\Phi}_2 z^2 - \cdots - \mathbf{\Phi}_p z^p] = 0$$

lie **outside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert > 1$ in the complex plane.

> [!NOTE]
> This is *not* the same as requiring each entry of $\mathbf{\Phi}_i$ to satisfy the unit root condition individually. The **determinant condition** is a necessary and sufficient condition.

## 2.2 Invertibility Condition

A VARMA(p, q) process is **invertible** if the roots of:

$$\det[\mathbf{\Theta}(z)] = \det[\mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{\Theta}_1 z - \cdots - \mathbf{\Theta}_q z^q] = 0$$

lie **outside the unit circle**.

## 2.3 Mean Vector

If the process is stationary, the unconditional mean $\boldsymbol{\mu} = E(\mathbf{y}_t$ is obtained by setting $\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t = \mathbf{0}$ and taking expectations:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{\Phi}_1 - \mathbf{\Phi}_2 - \cdots - \mathbf{\Phi}_p)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix $\mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i$ must be non-singular for the mean to exist.

## 2.4 Vector Yule–Walker Equations

The autocovariance matrices $\mathbf{\Gamma}_h = E[(\mathbf{y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{y}_{t-h} - \boldsymbol{\mu})']$ satisfy the multivariate Yule–Walker recursions:

$$\mathbf{\Gamma}_h = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \mathbf{\Gamma}_{h-i} + \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\delta, h}$$

where $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\delta, h} = \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$ for $h = 0$ and $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\delta, h} = -\mathbf{\Theta}_h \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$ for $1 \leq h \leq q$, and $\mathbf{0}$ for $h > q$.

The first $p$ equations (for $h = 1, 2, \ldots, p$) form a closed system that can be solved for $\mathbf{\Phi}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{\Phi}_p$ given sample autocovariances (via the **sample Yule–Walker estimator**).

## 2.5 Granger Causality

Variable $y_{2t}$ is said to **Granger-cause** $y_{1t}$ if the past of $y_{2t}$ helps predict $y_{1t}$ beyond what the past of $y_{1t}$ alone provides. The test statistic follows an asymptotic $\chi^2$ distribution.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition |
|---|---|
| VARMA(p, q) form | $\mathbf{\Phi}(B)\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Theta}(B)\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$ |
| Stationarity | Roots of $\det[\mathbf{\Phi}(z)] = 0$ satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| Invertibility | Roots of $\det[\mathbf{\Theta}(z)] = 0$ satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| Unconditional mean | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_K - \sum \mathbf{\Phi}_i)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$ |
| Yule–Walker (h $\geq$ 1) | $\mathbf{\Gamma}_h = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \mathbf{\Gamma}_{h-i}$ |
| Innovation covariance | $E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t') = \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$ (positive definite) |
| Multivariate AIC | $AIC = -2 \ln L + 2k$ |
| Multivariate BIC | $BIC = -2 \ln L + k \ln T$ |
| Granger F-statistic | $F = \frac{(RSS_R - RSS_U)/m}{RSS_U/(T - K)}$ where $m$ = restricted lags |
| OLS estimator (VAR) | $\widehat{\mathbf{\Phi}} = (\mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{Z})^{-1} \mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{Y}$ (equation-by-equation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: Vector ARMA models are widely used in econometrics (GDP, inflation, interest rates), portfolio risk analysis, brain signal processing (EEG channels), and industrial process control where multiple correlated sensors must be jointly monitored.

## 2.7 Model Building Strategy (KTU Board Pattern)

The standard Box–Jenkins paradigm extended to VARMA follows these steps:

1. **Identification** — examine cross-correlation matrices (CCM) and use information criteria.
2. **Estimation** — use OLS equation-by-equation (for VAR) or ML (for full VARMA).
3. **Diagnostic checking** — examine residual cross-correlation matrices using the multivariate Ljung–Box statistic.
4. **Forecasting** — use the recursive forecast formula.
5. **Causal / Impulse Response Analysis** — interpret the dynamic system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mean Vector

Starting from the VARMA(p, q) representation:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \mathbf{y}_{t-i} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t - \sum_{j=1}^{q} \mathbf{\Theta}_j \mathbf{\varepsilon}_{t-j}$$

**Step 1**: Take expectation on both sides. Under stationarity, $E(\mathbf{y}_t) = E(\mathbf{y}_{t-i}) = \boldsymbol{\mu}$, and $E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t) = \mathbf{0}$.

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{0} - \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 2**: Rearrange to isolate $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

**Step 3**: Factor out $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$\left(\mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i\right) \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

**Step 4**: Pre-multiply by the inverse (assuming non-singularity):

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left(\mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i\right)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Yule–Walker Equations (VAR(1) Case)

For the VAR(1) model $\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$, with $E(\mathbf{y}_t) = \boldsymbol{\mu}$, define the centered process $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t = \mathbf{y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t = \mathbf{\Phi} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$$

**Step 1**: Compute the lag-0 autocovariance. Multiply by $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t'$ and take expectations:

$$E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t') = \mathbf{\Phi} E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t') + E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t')$$

**Step 2**: Since $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t = \mathbf{\Phi} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$, the innovation $\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$ is uncorrelated with $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1}$, hence:

$$E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t') = E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t (\mathbf{\Phi} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t)') = \mathbf{0} + \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}} = \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$$

**Step 3**: Also $E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t') = E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1}(\mathbf{\Phi}\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t)') = \mathbf{\Gamma}_0 \mathbf{\Phi}' + \mathbf{0}$. Therefore:

$$\mathbf{\Gamma}_0 = \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Gamma}_0 \mathbf{\Phi}' + \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$$

**Step 4**: For lag $h \geq 1$, multiply by $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-h}'$ and take expectations:

$$\mathbf{\Gamma}_h = \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Gamma}_{h-1}, \quad h = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 5**: Iterating:

$$\mathbf{\Gamma}_h = \mathbf{\Phi}^h \mathbf{\Gamma}_0, \quad h \geq 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **vector analog** of the geometric decay $\gamma_h = \phi^h \gamma_0$ in the univariate AR(1).

## 3.3 Sample Cross-Correlation and Identification

Given residuals $\hat{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}_t$, the **sample cross-correlation matrix** at lag $h$ is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{R}}_h = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=h+1}^{T} \hat{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}_t \hat{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}_{t-h}'$$

For a correctly specified model, $\hat{\mathbf{R}}_h$ should be approximately zero for $h \geq 1$, analogous to the univariate ACF cut-off.

The **multivariate Ljung–Box statistic** is:

$$Q(m) = T^2 \sum_{h=1}^{m} \frac{1}{T-h} \, \mathrm{tr}\left(\hat{\mathbf{R}}_h' \hat{\mathbf{R}}_0^{-1} \hat{\mathbf{R}}_h \hat{\mathbf{R}}_0^{-1}\right) \sim \chi^2_{K^2 m - (\text{estimated parameters})}$$

## 3.4 Forecasting the VAR(1) Process

For $\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$:

**Step 1**: The 1-step ahead forecast given information up to time $h$ is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{h+1 \mid h} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{y}_h$$

**Step 2**: The h-step ahead forecast for $h \geq 1$ is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{h+\ell \mid h} = (\mathbf{I} + \mathbf{\Phi} + \mathbf{\Phi}^2 + \cdots + \mathbf{\Phi}^{\ell-1})\mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Phi}^{\ell} \mathbf{y}_h$$

**Step 3**: In equilibrium (long-run forecast as $\ell \to \infty$), since the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{\Phi}$ lie inside the unit circle, $\mathbf{\Phi}^{\ell} \to \mathbf{0}$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{\infty \mid h} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{\Phi})^{-1} \mathbf{c} = \boldsymbol{\mu}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Condition Matters

| Condition | Mathematical Statement | Real-World Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Stationarity | $\det[\mathbf{\Phi}(z)] \neq 0$ for $\vert z \vert \leq 1$ | Process returns to its mean; shocks dissipate over time |
| Invertibility | $\det[\mathbf{\Theta}(z)] \neq 0$ for $\vert z \vert \leq 1$ | Past shocks can be uniquely recovered from present data |
| Positive definite $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$ | $\mathbf{x}' \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}} \mathbf{x} > 0 \, \forall \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | All cross-sectional variances are positive and finite |
| Cointegrated series | Existence of $\boldsymbol{\beta}'$ such that $\boldsymbol{\beta}' \mathbf{y}_t \sim I(0)$ | Long-run equilibrium relation among non-stationary variables |

## 3.6 Worked Example: VAR(1) Parameter Estimation

Suppose $K = 2$ and the model is:

$$\begin{pmatrix} y_{1t} \\ y_{2t} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} \phi_{11} & \phi_{12} \\ \phi_{21} & \phi_{22} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} y_{1,t-1} \\ y_{2,t-1} \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} \varepsilon_{1t} \\ \varepsilon_{2t} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1**: Stack the data as $\mathbf{Y} = [\mathbf{y}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_T]$ and $\mathbf{Z} = [\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{y}_0, \mathbf{y}_{-1}, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_{T-2}]$ (with column of ones for intercept).

**Step 2**: The OLS estimator (which is also the MLE under Gaussianity) equation by equation is:

$$\begin{pmatrix} \hat{c}_1 & \hat{c}_2 \\ \hat{\phi}_{11} & \hat{\phi}_{21} \\ \hat{\phi}_{12} & \hat{\phi}_{22} \end{pmatrix} = (\mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{Z})^{-1} \mathbf{Z}' \mathbf{Y}$$

**Step 3**: The residual covariance matrix estimator is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}} = \frac{1}{T-3} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \hat{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}_t \hat{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}_t'$$

> [!NOTE]
> A key result (often tested): **In a VAR, OLS applied equation-by-equation is consistent and asymptotically efficient** — it is equivalent to full maximum likelihood when the errors are Gaussian. This is the Sims (1980) / Zellner "seemingly unrelated regression" result for VAR.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define a Vector ARMA(p, q) model. State the stationarity and invertibility conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Vector ARMA(p, q)** model for a $K$-dimensional time series $\mathbf{y}_t$ is:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \mathbf{y}_{t-i} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t - \sum_{j=1}^{q} \mathbf{\Theta}_j \mathbf{\varepsilon}_{t-j}$$

**Stationarity condition**: The roots of $\det[\mathbf{\Phi}(z)] = 0$ must lie outside the unit circle, i.e., $\vert z \vert > 1$.

**Invertibility condition**: The roots of $\det[\mathbf{\Theta}(z)] = 0$ must lie outside the unit circle, i.e., $\vert z \vert > 1$.

Here $\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$ is white noise with covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$, a $K \times K$ positive definite matrix.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Differentiate between univariate ARMA and Vector ARMA models. Mention any two advantages of VARMA over ARMA.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Univariate ARMA | Vector ARMA |
|---|---|---|
| Number of series | Single series | $K$ series modeled jointly |
| Coefficients | Scalars $\phi_i, \theta_j$ | Matrices $\mathbf{\Phi}_i, \mathbf{\Theta}_j$ |
| Innovation variance | Scalar $\sigma^2$ | Matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$ |
| Cross-variable effects | Not captured | Fully captured via $\mathbf{\Phi}_i, \mathbf{\Theta}_j$ |

**Two advantages**:
1. Captures **interdependencies** and **feedback** between multiple time series.
2. Enables **Granger causality testing** and **impulse response analysis** for policy/decision making.
