## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the **unconditional mean vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ for a stationary VARMA(p, q) process. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Yule–Walker equations** for the stationary VAR(1) model $\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$ and show that $\mathbf{\Gamma}_h = \mathbf{\Phi}^h \mathbf{\Gamma}_0$ for $h \geq 1$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Starting from the VARMA(p, q) representation:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \mathbf{y}_{t-i} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t - \sum_{j=1}^{q} \mathbf{\Theta}_j \mathbf{\varepsilon}_{t-j}$$

**Step 1**: Take expectations of both sides. Under stationarity, $E(\mathbf{y}_t) = E(\mathbf{y}_{t-i}) = \boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{0} - \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 2**: Subtract the sum term from both sides:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

**Step 3**: Factor out $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$\left( \mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \right) \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

**Step 4**: Pre-multiply by the inverse matrix:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left( \mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \right)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$$

**Condition for existence**: The matrix $\left( \mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p} \mathbf{\Phi}_i \right)$ must be **non-singular** (its determinant non-zero), which is guaranteed by the **stationarity condition** evaluated at $z = 1$ being outside the unit circle roots.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Consider the centered process $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t = \mathbf{y}_t - \boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t = \mathbf{\Phi} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$$

**Step 1 — Lag-0 autocovariance**: Multiply both sides by $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t'$ and take expectations:

$$E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t') = \mathbf{\Phi} E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t') + E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t')$$

**Step 2 — Innovation orthogonality**: Substituting $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t = \mathbf{\Phi} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$:

$$E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t') = E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1}') \mathbf{\Phi}' + E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t') = \mathbf{0} + \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}} = \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$$

Also, $E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t') = \mathbf{\Gamma}_0 \mathbf{\Phi}'$. Therefore:

$$\mathbf{\Gamma}_0 = \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Gamma}_0 \mathbf{\Phi}' + \mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$$

**Step 3 — Lag-h autocovariance** ($h \geq 1$): Multiply by $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-h}'$ and take expectations:

$$E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_t \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-h}') = \mathbf{\Phi} E(\tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1} \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-h}') + E(\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t \tilde{\mathbf{y}}_{t-h}')$$

Since $\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$ is white noise (uncorrelated with past), the second term is $\mathbf{0}$, giving:

$$\mathbf{\Gamma}_h = \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Gamma}_{h-1}$$

**Step 4 — Iterating the recursion**:

$$\mathbf{\Gamma}_h = \mathbf{\Phi} \cdot \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{\Gamma}_{h-2} = \mathbf{\Phi}^2 \mathbf{\Gamma}_{h-2} = \cdots = \mathbf{\Phi}^h \mathbf{\Gamma}_0 \quad \text{for } h \geq 1$$

**Geometric decay**: Because the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{\Phi}$ lie inside the unit circle (stationarity), $\mathbf{\Phi}^h \to \mathbf{0}$ as $h \to \infty$, so $\mathbf{\Gamma}_h \to \mathbf{0}$, ensuring ergodicity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **stationarity and invertibility conditions** for a VARMA(p, q) model. Why is the *determinantal* polynomial condition used instead of checking individual matrix entries? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Consider the bivariate VAR(1) model:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$$

with $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.0 & 0.3 \\ 0.3 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the **unconditional mean vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and verify **stationarity** by checking the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{\Phi}$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The VARMA(p, q) model is:

$$\mathbf{\Phi}(B) \mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Theta}(B) \mathbf{\varepsilon}_t$$

where $\mathbf{\Phi}(B) = \mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{\Phi}_1 B - \cdots - \mathbf{\Phi}_p B^p$ and $\mathbf{\Theta}(B) = \mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{\Theta}_1 B - \cdots - \mathbf{\Theta}_q B^q$.

**Stationarity Condition**: The process is (weakly) stationary if and only if all roots of:

$$\det[\mathbf{\Phi}(z)] = 0$$

satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$ (lie outside the unit circle).

**Invertibility Condition**: The process is invertible if and only if all roots of:

$$\det[\mathbf{\Theta}(z)] = 0$$

satisfy $\vert z \vert > 1$.

**Why the determinantal condition?**

The determinantal condition is a **joint** condition on the entire coefficient matrix polynomial. Checking individual entries of $\mathbf{\Phi}_i$ for unit-root-like behavior is *insufficient* because:

1. **Cross-coupling**: Each $\mathbf{\Phi}_i$ is a $K \times K$ matrix; the dynamic behavior is governed by the **eigenvalues of the companion form**, not by individual entries.

2. **Eigenvalue equivalence**: The stationarity condition is equivalent to all eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of the companion matrix satisfying $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$. The determinantal condition on $\mathbf{\Phi}(z)$ is algebraically equivalent.

3. **Counter-example intuition**: A matrix $\mathbf{\Phi}$ may have all entries with absolute value greater than 1, yet still produce a stationary process (via cancellation), and vice versa.

> [!NOTE]
> The determinantal condition is **necessary and sufficient**, whereas entry-wise conditions are neither.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the mean vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:

We have $\mathbf{I}_2 - \mathbf{\Phi} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & -0.1 \\ -0.2 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$.

The determinant is $(0.4)(0.5) - (-0.1)(-0.2) = 0.20 - 0.02 = 0.18$.

The inverse is $\dfrac{1}{0.18} \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.778 & 0.556 \\ 1.111 & 2.222 \end{pmatrix}$.

Therefore:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.778 & 0.556 \\ 1.111 & 2.222 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.389 + 0.111 \\ 0.556 + 0.444 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.500 \\ 1.000 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Verify stationarity via eigenvalues of $\mathbf{\Phi}$**:

The characteristic equation of $\mathbf{\Phi} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$ is:

$$\det(\mathbf{\Phi} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (0.6 - \lambda)(0.5 - \lambda) - (0.1)(0.2) = 0$$

$$0.30 - 0.6\lambda - 0.5\lambda + \lambda^2 - 0.02 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - 1.1\lambda + 0.28 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.1 \pm \sqrt{1.21 - 1.12}}{2} = \frac{1.1 \pm \sqrt{0.09}}{2} = \frac{1.1 \pm 0.3}{2}$$

So $\lambda_1 = 0.7$ and $\lambda_2 = 0.4$.

Both eigenvalues have $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$, hence the VAR(1) process is **stationary**.

**Step 3 — Interpretation**:
- $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (1.5, 1.0)'$ is the long-run equilibrium mean of the bivariate process.
- Eigenvalues 0.7 and 0.4 confirm that shocks dissipate at a rate of roughly 30–60% per period.
- The off-diagonal entries of $\mathbf{\Phi}$ (0.1 and 0.2) confirm weak cross-variable influence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
>
> 1. **Confusing stationarity with invertibility** — Remember: stationarity is about $\det[\mathbf{\Phi}(z)] = 0$, invertibility is about $\det[\mathbf{\Theta}(z)] = 0$.
> 2. **Forgetting to take expectations of the constant** — Many students write $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$ incorrectly; the constant $\mathbf{c}$ must be multiplied by $(\mathbf{I} - \sum \mathbf{\Phi}_i)^{-1}$.
> 3. **Treating $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$ as diagonal** — This is a frequent error. The off-diagonal elements represent **contemporaneous cross-correlation** between innovations and are central to VARMA.
> 4. **Mixing up $\mathbf{\Phi}_i$ order** — Always use the backshift form $\mathbf{\Phi}(B)\mathbf{y}_t = \cdots$ to avoid sign errors.
> 5. **Forgetting the invertibility requirement for MA estimation** — A non-invertible model is valid as a representation but cannot be uniquely estimated from data.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VARMA(p, q)** is the natural multivariate extension of ARMA(p, q) using coefficient matrices instead of scalars.
- The **innovation covariance matrix** $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\mathbf{\varepsilon}}$ is **not diagonal** in general — it captures contemporaneous correlations between variables.
- **Stationarity** is determined by the roots of $\det[\mathbf{\Phi}(z)] = 0$ lying *outside* the unit circle.
- **Invertibility** is determined by the roots of $\det[\mathbf{\Theta}(z)] = 0$ lying *outside* the unit circle.
- The **unconditional mean** is $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_K - \sum \mathbf{\Phi}_i)^{-1} \mathbf{c}$.
- The **Yule–Walker recursion** for a VAR(1) reduces to $\mathbf{\Gamma}_h = \mathbf{\Phi}^h \mathbf{\Gamma}_0$ for $h \geq 1$.
- **OLS equation-by-equation** is consistent and efficient for VAR estimation (Sims/Zellner result).
- **AIC / BIC** are the standard tools for order selection in VARMA.
- The **multivariate Ljung–Box statistic** is the diagnostic tool for residual white noise checking.
- **Granger causality** tests are conducted by comparing restricted vs unrestricted VAR models via $F$-test.
- **Impulse response functions** trace out the dynamic response of one variable to a shock in another.
- **Forecasts** are generated recursively: $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{h+1 \mid h} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Phi} \mathbf{y}_h$ for VAR(1).
- For non-stationary but cointegrated series, use the **VECM** (Error Correction Model) instead of VARMA in levels.
- Always **center the data** before estimation by subtracting $\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fitting VAR and VARMA Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fitting VAR and VARMA Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Vector Autoregressive (VAR) Model

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Vector Autoregressive (VAR) model of order $p$**, denoted **VAR($p$)**, is a multivariate time series model in which a $K$-dimensional vector of endogenous variables $\mathbf{Y}_t = (Y_{1t}, Y_{2t}, \ldots, Y_{Kt})'$ is regressed on its own $p$ lagged values, possibly augmented by a vector of deterministic terms (constant, trend, dummies). The standard reduced-form representation is:

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} + \cdots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{u}_t$$

where $\mathbf{c}$ is a $K \times 1$ vector of intercepts, $\mathbf{A}_i$ are $K \times K$ coefficient matrices for lags $i = 1, 2, \ldots, p$, and $\mathbf{u}_t$ is a $K \times 1$ vector of white-noise (innovation) errors with $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{u}_t) = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_t') = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u$ (positive definite covariance matrix), and $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{u}_t \mathbf{u}_s') = \mathbf{0}$ for $t \neq s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The VAR model is the workhorse of modern multivariate time series analysis, introduced by **Christopher Sims (1980)** as a critique of large-scale simultaneous-equation macroeconomic models. It treats every variable as endogenous, avoiding the "incredible identification restrictions" of structural models.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **weather dashboard** tracking temperature, humidity, and pressure simultaneously. Each variable doesn't just depend on time — it depends on the **recent past of all three**. If pressure dropped yesterday, it affects today's temperature; if humidity was high two days ago, it influences pressure today. A VAR model is essentially a **smart interlinked prediction engine** where every indicator "listens" to the recent behavior of every other indicator. Unlike a single AR model that predicts one variable from itself, VAR lets all variables **talk to each other dynamically**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Identifier:** If a time series problem gives you **2 or more correlated variables** measured over time, the model of first resort is the VAR, not a set of separate univariate ARs.

---

## 1.2 The Vector Autoregressive Moving-Average (VARMA) Model

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Vector Autoregressive Moving-Average model of orders $(p, q)$**, denoted **VARMA($p, q$)**, generalizes the VAR by adding $q$ lagged white-noise vectors, exactly as ARMA extends AR in the univariate case. The reduced form is:

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \cdots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{u}_t - \mathbf{M}_1 \mathbf{u}_{t-1} - \cdots - \mathbf{M}_q \mathbf{u}_{t-q}$$

where $\mathbf{M}_j$ are $K \times K$ moving-average coefficient matrices, and the remaining terms are as defined for VAR. Equivalently, using the **backshift (lag) operator** $L$:

$$\mathbf{\Phi}(L)\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Theta}(L)\mathbf{u}_t$$

with matrix polynomials $\mathbf{\Phi}(L) = \mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{A}_1 L - \cdots - \mathbf{A}_p L^p$ and $\mathbf{\Theta}(L) = \mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{M}_1 L - \cdots - \mathbf{M}_q L^q$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
A VARMA model is to a VAR model what an **ARMA model is to an AR model** in the univariate world. If a pure VAR tries to capture all dynamics through lags of the variables themselves, adding a moving-average (MA) part allows the model to absorb **short-lived random shocks** that propagate across variables. Think of it as a **VAR model with a "memory" for recent unexpected disturbances** — like remembering that a sudden, brief cold front hit last week, which still affects today's readings, even though the temperature itself has since recovered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Lütkepohl):** Most empirical multivariate problems are well-served by a **pure VAR** with sufficiently high $p$. Pure VARMA is rarely identified in practice because of **over-parameterization** — hence the famous Tiao–Box (1981) **"VARMA in disguise"** doctrine. A VARMA($p,q$) with $q \ge 1$ is almost always a VAR($\infty$) in disguise, and a finite-order VAR approximation usually suffices.

---

## 1.3 Why Fit Multivariate Models? The Core Motivation

| **Reason** | **Description** | **Standard Metric** |
|---|---|---|
| **Cross-variable dynamics** | Captures how shocks in one variable propagate to others | Cross-correlation function (CCF) |
| **Improved forecasting** | Joint modeling reduces individual forecast error variance | **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)**, **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** |
| **Granger causality** | Tests whether one variable helps predict another | $F$-statistic / Wald test |
| **Impulse Response Analysis (IRA)** | Traces the time path of a unit shock | Orthogonalized IRF |
| **Variance Decomposition (VDC)** | Attributes forecast-error variance across variables | Forecast Error Variance Decomposition (FEVD) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants / Benchmarks:** No physical constants here, but the **asymptotic reference** is $\chi^2$ distribution for likelihood-ratio and portmanteau tests. The critical **Schwert (1989) rule** for maximum VAR lag is $p_{\max} = 12(T/100)^{1/4}$, where $T$ is the sample size.

---

## 1.4 Stationarity Condition for VAR($p$)

A VAR($p$) is **covariance-stationary** if and only if all roots of the **reverse characteristic polynomial** lie strictly outside the unit circle:

$$\det\bigl(\mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{A}_1 z - \mathbf{A}_2 z^2 - \cdots - \mathbf{A}_p z^p\bigr) \neq 0 \quad \text{for all } |z| \le 1$$

Equivalently, the eigenvalues of the **companion matrix** $\mathbf{F}$:

$$\mathbf{F} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{A}_1 & \mathbf{A}_2 & \cdots & \mathbf{A}_p \\ \mathbf{I}_K & \mathbf{0} & \cdots & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I}_K & \cdots & \mathbf{0} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \cdots & \mathbf{0} \end{pmatrix}$$

must all lie **inside the unit circle** ($\vert \lambda_i(\mathbf{F}) \vert < 1$ for every $i$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The VAR($p$) in Matrix Companion Form

Define the $Kp \times 1$ stacked vector $\mathbf{Z}_t = (\mathbf{Y}_t', \mathbf{Y}_{t-1}', \ldots, \mathbf{Y}_{t-p+1}')'$. Then the VAR($p$) admits the **first-order companion (state-space) representation**:

$$\mathbf{Z}_t = \mathbf{F} \mathbf{Z}_{t-1} + \mathbf{G}\mathbf{u}_t + \mathbf{d}$$

where the **companion matrix** $\mathbf{F}$ is the $Kp \times Kp$ block matrix shown above, $\mathbf{G} = (\mathbf{I}_K, \mathbf{0}, \ldots, \mathbf{0})'$, and $\mathbf{d} = (\mathbf{c}', \mathbf{0}, \ldots, \mathbf{0})'$.

This recasts a high-order VAR as a **first-order vector autoregression** — a vital reformulation for deriving the **MA($\infty$) representation**, the **spectral density**, and **forecasting recursions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** A stationary VAR($p$) can be inverted into a **VMA($\infty$)** (vector moving average of infinite order):

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \mathbf{\Psi}_i \mathbf{u}_{t-i}$$

where the **MA coefficient matrices** $\mathbf{\Psi}_i$ are computed recursively from the companion form as $\mathbf{\Psi}_i = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{F}^i\mathbf{G}$, with $\mathbf{J} = (\mathbf{I}_K, \mathbf{0}, \ldots, \mathbf{0})$. These are essential for **Impulse Response Functions**.

---

## 2.2 The VARMA($p,q$) Stationarity and Invertibility Conditions

- **Stationarity** requires all roots of $\det\mathbf{\Phi}(z) = 0$ to lie outside the unit circle.
- **Invertibility** requires all roots of $\det\mathbf{\Theta}(z) = 0$ to lie outside the unit circle.

Invertibility is required to give the model a **unique** reduced-form representation; otherwise, an infinite family of equivalent VARMA specifications can produce identical likelihoods.

## 2.3 Estimation Strategy: Three Layers

### Layer 1 — Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Equation-by-Equation
Because the regressors in a VAR are the **same set of lagged $\mathbf{Y}$'s for every equation**, and the OLS estimator is **numerically identical to GLS** when the regressor matrix is identical across equations, you can estimate each equation of the VAR($p$) by **simple OLS** — one regression per endogenous variable. This is the **Sims (1980)** insight that made VARs practical.

### Layer 2 — Multivariate Normal Maximum Likelihood (ML)
Assuming $\mathbf{u}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma}_u)$, the **concentrated log-likelihood** (after concentrating out the coefficients) is:

$$\ell_c(\mathbf{\Sigma}_u) = -\frac{TK}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{T}{2}\ln\det(\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u) - \frac{TK}{2}$$

with the **ML estimator** of the residual covariance being $\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T \hat{\mathbf{u}}_t\hat{\mathbf{u}}_t'$ (using the OLS residuals).

### Layer 3 — For VARMA — Nonlinear Iterative Estimation
VARMA requires **nonlinear estimation** because the MA lags are unobserved. Methods include:
- **Hannan–Rissanen (HR) method** (two-step linear initialization, then nonlinear refinement).
- **Maximum Likelihood via Kalman Filter** (state-space representation).
- **Conditional ML (CML)** given initial residuals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** KTU examiners routinely deduct marks when students confuse **OLS, GLS, and ML** estimates. In a VAR, OLS = GLS = ML **only asymptotically and only equation-by-equation** when the regressors are identical. Be precise in your answer!

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Statement** | **Conditions / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| **VAR($p$) reduced form** | $\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\mathbf{A}_i\mathbf{Y}_{t-i} + \mathbf{u}_t$ | $\mathbf{u}_t \sim WN(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma}_u)$ |
| **VARMA($p,q$) reduced form** | $\mathbf{\Phi}(L)\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Theta}(L)\mathbf{u}_t$ | $\mathbf{\Phi}(z), \mathbf{\Theta}(z)$ matrix polynomials |
| **Companion matrix eigenvalues** | $\vert \lambda_i(\mathbf{F}) \vert < 1$ for all $i$ | Equivalent to stationarity |
| **MA($\infty$) representation** | $\mathbf{Y}_t = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \sum_{i=0}^\infty \mathbf{\Psi}_i \mathbf{u}_{t-i}$ | $\mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_K$, recursion: $\mathbf{\Psi}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{\min(i,p)} \mathbf{A}_j \mathbf{\Psi}_{i-j}$ |
| **OLS / ML equivalence (VAR)** | Equation-by-equation OLS = efficient GLS = ML | Requires **identical regressor matrix** per equation |
| **Akaike IC (AIC)** | $AIC(p) = \ln\det\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u(p) + \frac{2K^2 p}{T}$ | $K^2 p$ parameters in coefficient matrices |
| **Schwarz BIC (SBIC)** | $SBIC(p) = \ln\det\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u(p) + \frac{K^2 p \ln T}{T}$ | Consistent, tends to choose lower $p$ |
| **Hannan–Quinn (HQ)** | $HQ(p) = \ln\det\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u(p) + \frac{2K^2 p \ln(\ln T)}{T}$ | Intermediate penalty |
| **Lütkepohl LR test** | $LR = T\bigl[\ln\det\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u(p_0) - \ln\det\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u(p_1)\bigr]$ | $\sim \chi^2(K^2(p_1 - p_0))$ under $H_0$ |
| **Portmanteau (Ljung–Box, multivariate)** | $Q_h = T^2 \sum_{i=1}^{h} \frac{1}{T-i}\,\mathrm{tr}\bigl(\hat{\mathbf{R}}_i'\hat{\mathbf{R}}_0^{-1}\hat{\mathbf{R}}_i\hat{\mathbf{R}}_0^{-1}\bigr)$ | $\sim \chi^2(K^2(h - p - q))$ asymptotically |
| **Granger causality $Y_{2} \to Y_{1}$** | $F = \frac{(RSS_r - RSS_u)/q_2}{RSS_u/(T - K)}$ | Reject $H_0$ if $Y_2$ Granger-causes $Y_1$ |
| **$h$-step forecast** | $\mathbf{Y}_{T+h} = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\mathbf{A}_i \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+h-i}$ | Replace unobserved $\mathbf{Y}_{T+j}$ ($j \ge 1$) with its forecast |
| **Forecast Error Covariance (FEC)** | $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\hat{u}}(h) = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u + \sum_{i=1}^{h-1}\mathbf{\Psi}_i\mathbf{\Sigma}_u\mathbf{\Psi}_i'$ | Used to construct forecast intervals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation safeguard:** Throughout these notes, $\vert \cdot \vert$ inside formulas is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table structurally intact.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application of VAR / VARMA** |
|---|---|
| **Macroeconomics** | GDP, inflation, interest rate joint modeling; monetary policy shocks |
| **Financial Econometrics** | Portfolio risk: VAR of asset returns, VaR (Value at Risk) computation |
| **Power Systems Engineering** | Multivariate load forecasting (temperature × humidity × demand) |
| **Signal Processing** | Multichannel EEG/ECG modeling, source separation |
| **Climate Science** | ENSO (El Niño) indices, sea-surface temperature, atmospheric pressure |
| **Industrial Process Control** | MIMO (multiple-input multiple-output) dynamic systems |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Mean of a Stationary VAR($p$)

We want the **unconditional mean** $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_t)$. Take expectations on both sides of the reduced form:

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \cdots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{t-p} + \mathbf{u}_t$$

Since $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{u}_t) = \mathbf{0}$ and stationarity implies $\mathbb{E}(\mathbf{Y}_{t-i}) = \boldsymbol{\mu}$ for all $i$:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{A}_2 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \cdots + \mathbf{A}_p \boldsymbol{\mu}$$

Rearranging by pulling all $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ terms to the left:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} - \sum_{i=1}^{p}\mathbf{A}_i \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

Factor the left-hand side:

$$\left(\mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p}\mathbf{A}_i\right) \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

Provided $\mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p}\mathbf{A}_i$ is **nonsingular** (which is implied by stationarity evaluated at $z=1$), we obtain the closed-form:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left(\mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p}\mathbf{A}_i\right)^{-1}\mathbf{c}$$

> **Engineering intuition:** This is the **steady-state (DC) gain** of the dynamic system. If the system is shocked by a permanent change in the intercept, the long-run mean shifts by exactly this matrix inverse times $\mathbf{c}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The MA($\infty$) Coefficient Recursion

Starting from the **companion form** $\mathbf{Z}_t = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{Z}_{t-1} + \mathbf{G}\mathbf{u}_t$, iterate forward:

$$\mathbf{Z}_t = \mathbf{F}^p \mathbf{Z}_{t-p} + \sum_{i=0}^{p-1}\mathbf{F}^i \mathbf{G}\mathbf{u}_{t-i}$$

By stationarity, $\mathbf{F}^p \to \mathbf{0}$ as $p \to \infty$, so in the limit:

$$\mathbf{Z}_t = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty}\mathbf{F}^i \mathbf{G}\mathbf{u}_{t-i}$$

Extract the top $K$ rows using the selection matrix $\mathbf{J} = (\mathbf{I}_K, \mathbf{0}, \ldots, \mathbf{0})$:

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{Z}_t = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty}\underbrace{\mathbf{J}\mathbf{F}^i\mathbf{G}}_{\mathbf{\Psi}_i}\mathbf{u}_{t-i}$$

This gives the matrix-recursion identity:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{\Psi}_i = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{F}^i\mathbf{G}, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots}$$

Equivalently, by direct substitution in the VAR reduced form:

$$\mathbf{\Psi}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{\min(i, p)} \mathbf{A}_j \mathbf{\Psi}_{i-j}, \quad \mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_K$$

> **Why this matters for KTU:** The $\mathbf{\Psi}_i$ matrices are precisely the **orthogonalized impulse response functions** at horizon $i$. They answer: *"If I shock variable $k$ by one standard deviation today, what is the cumulative effect on every variable $h$ steps later?"*

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Concentrated Log-Likelihood for VAR($p$)

Assume $\mathbf{u}_t \overset{iid}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma}_u)$. The joint density over $T$ observations is:

$$L(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{A}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{A}_p, \mathbf{\Sigma}_u) = \prod_{t=p+1}^{T} \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{K/2}\det(\mathbf{\Sigma}_u)^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{u}_t'\mathbf{\Sigma}_u^{-1}\mathbf{u}_t\right)$$

Take logarithms:

$$\ell = -\frac{(T-p)K}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{(T-p)}{2}\ln\det(\mathbf{\Sigma}_u) - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{t=p+1}^{T}\mathbf{u}_t'\mathbf{\Sigma}_u^{-1}\mathbf{u}_t$$

For **fixed** coefficient matrices, maximize with respect to $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u$. By the standard identity $\frac{\partial}{\partial\mathbf{\Sigma}_u}\ln\det(\mathbf{\Sigma}_u) = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u^{-1}$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial\mathbf{\Sigma}_u}\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{\Sigma}_u^{-1}) = -\mathbf{\Sigma}_u^{-1}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{\Sigma}_u^{-1}$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mathbf{\Sigma}_u} = -\frac{(T-p)}{2}\mathbf{\Sigma}_u^{-1} + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{\Sigma}_u^{-1}\left(\sum_{t=p+1}^{T}\mathbf{u}_t\mathbf{u}_t'\right)\mathbf{\Sigma}_u^{-1} = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving for $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u = \frac{1}{T-p}\sum_{t=p+1}^{T}\hat{\mathbf{u}}_t\hat{\mathbf{u}}_t'$$

Substitute this back into $\ell$ to obtain the **concentrated log-likelihood**:

$$\ell_c = -\frac{(T-p)K}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{(T-p)K}{2} - \frac{(T-p)}{2}\ln\det(\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u)$$

Dropping constants (which don't depend on $p$):

$$\ell_c \propto -\frac{T}{2}\ln\det(\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u)$$

This justifies the AIC, BIC, HQ information criteria — they are penalized versions of this concentrated likelihood.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: VAR(1) for Two Variables

Suppose $K = 2$ and we observe $T = 100$ observations of $\mathbf{Y}_t = (Y_{1t}, Y_{2t})'$. Suppose the OLS estimation yields:

$$\hat{\mathbf{A}}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \hat{\mathbf{c}} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.3 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u = \begin{pmatrix} 1.0 & 0.2 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step A — Compute the unconditional mean:**

$$\mathbf{I}_K - \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - 0.6 & -0.1 \\ -0.2 & 1 - 0.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & -0.1 \\ -0.2 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

Its determinant: $0.4 \times 0.5 - (-0.1)(-0.2) = 0.20 - 0.02 = 0.18$.

Inverse:

$$(\mathbf{I}_K - \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1)^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.18}\begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 2.778 & 0.556 \\ 1.111 & 2.222 \end{pmatrix}$$

Then:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_K - \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1)^{-1}\hat{\mathbf{c}} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.778 & 0.556 \\ 1.111 & 2.222 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.389 - 0.167 \\ 0.556 - 0.667 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.222 \\ -0.111 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step B — Companion matrix eigenvalues (stationarity check):**

For VAR(1), the companion matrix IS $\hat{\mathbf{A}}_1$. Compute its eigenvalues by solving $\det(\hat{\mathbf{A}}_1 - \lambda\mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$\det\begin{pmatrix} 0.6 - \lambda & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = (0.6 - \lambda)(0.5 - \lambda) - 0.02 = \lambda^2 - 1.1\lambda + 0.30 - 0.02$$

$$= \lambda^2 - 1.1\lambda + 0.28 = 0$$

By the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.1 \pm \sqrt{1.21 - 1.12}}{2} = \frac{1.1 \pm 0.3}{2} \implies \lambda_1 = 0.7, \; \lambda_2 = 0.4$$

Both eigenvalues are **inside** the unit circle ($\vert 0.7 \vert < 1$, $\vert 0.4 \vert < 1$) $\Rightarrow$ the VAR(1) is **covariance-stationary**. ✓

**Step C — Compute $\mathbf{\Psi}_1$ and $\mathbf{\Psi}_2$ (impulse response matrices):**

Using the recursion $\mathbf{\Psi}_i = \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1 \mathbf{\Psi}_{i-1}$ with $\mathbf{\Psi}_0 = \mathbf{I}_K$:

$$\mathbf{\Psi}_1 = \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{\Psi}_2 = \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.38 & 0.11 \\ 0.22 & 0.27 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Interpretation:** A unit shock to $Y_1$ at time $t$ has impact $0.6$ on $Y_1$ and $0.2$ on $Y_2$ at time $t+1$; by time $t+2$, the impacts decay to $0.38$ and $0.22$ respectively.

**Step D — Compute AIC for VAR(1):**

$$AIC(1) = \ln\det(\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u) + \frac{2K^2 \cdot 1}{T} = \ln(0.78) + \frac{2 \times 4 \times 1}{100} \approx -0.248 + 0.080 = -0.168$$

If VAR(2) gave $\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u^{(2)} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.95 & 0.18 \\ 0.18 & 0.75 \end{pmatrix}$ with $\det = 0.6803$:

$$AIC(2) = \ln(0.6803) + \frac{2 \times 4 \times 2}{100} \approx -0.385 + 0.160 = -0.225$$

**Decision rule:** Since $-0.225 < -0.168$, **AIC selects VAR(2)** as the preferred model (lower AIC = better).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Forecasting in VAR($p$)

The **point forecast** $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+1}$ is obtained by replacing all unknown future values with their conditional expectations:

$$\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+1} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_T + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{T-1} + \cdots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{T-p+1}$$

For $h = 2$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+2} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+1} + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_T + \cdots + \mathbf{A}_p \mathbf{Y}_{T-p+2}$$

The **forecast error** is $\mathbf{e}_{T+h} = \mathbf{Y}_{T+h} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+h}$, with mean-zero covariance:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}(h) = \mathbb{E}(\mathbf{e}_{T+h}\mathbf{e}_{T+h}') = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u + \sum_{i=1}^{h-1}\mathbf{\Psi}_i \mathbf{\Sigma}_u \mathbf{\Psi}_i'$$

The forecast interval for the $j$-th component is:

$$\hat{Y}_{j,T+h} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{[\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}(h)]_{jj}}$$

where $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the standard normal quantile (e.g., **1.96** for 95% intervals).

> **Key Result:** The forecast error covariance **grows monotonically** with horizon $h$, and as $h \to \infty$, $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}(h) \to \mathbf{\Sigma}_Y(0)$ — the unconditional covariance of the process.

---

## 3.6 Diagnostic Checking: Multivariate Portmanteau Test

The **Ljung–Box portmanteau test** for residual autocorrelation in a fitted VARMA($p,q$) is:

$$Q_h = T^2 \sum_{i=1}^{h} \frac{1}{T - i}\,\mathrm{tr}\!\left(\hat{\mathbf{R}}_i'\hat{\mathbf{R}}_0^{-1}\hat{\mathbf{R}}_i \hat{\mathbf{R}}_0^{-1}\right)$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{R}}_i = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=i+1}^{T}\hat{\mathbf{u}}_t\hat{\mathbf{u}}_{t-i}'$ is the residual cross-correlation matrix at lag $i$, and $\hat{\mathbf{R}}_0 = \hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u$.

**Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \mathbf{R}_1 = \mathbf{R}_2 = \cdots = \mathbf{R}_h = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(no residual autocorrelation)}$$

$$H_1: \exists\, i \le h : \mathbf{R}_i \neq \mathbf{0}$$

**Decision:** Reject $H_0$ at significance level $\alpha$ if $Q_h > \chi^2_{K^2(h - p - q),\, 1-\alpha}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modification (Dufour–Roy, 1986; Lütkepohl):** A small-sample correction is often applied: replace $T^2$ with $T(T+2)$ in the sum, and use $\chi^2$ degrees of freedom as $K^2(h - p - q)$. The choice $h \approx \sqrt{T}$ or $h = 10$ for monthly data is conventional.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — VAR($p$) Fitting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Multivariate Time Series Data Y sub t] --> B[Stationarity Testing<br/>ADF KPSS Johansen]
    B --> C{Stationary?}
    C -- No --> D[Differencing or Cointegration Analysis]
    D --> E[Recheck Stationarity]
    E --> C
    C -- Yes --> F[Select Maximum Lag p sub max]
    F --> G[Estimate Candidate VAR Models<br/>OLS Equation by Equation]
    G --> H[Compute Information Criteria<br/>AIC BIC HQ]
    H --> I{IC Selects Optimal p}
    I --> J[Fit Final VAR p Model]
    J --> K[Diagnostic Checks<br/>Residual Portmanteau Test]
    K --> L{Pass Diagnostics}
    L -- No --> M[Re-specify Model<br/>Add Variables or Lags]
    M --> G
    L -- Yes --> N[Generate Forecasts and<br/>Impulse Response Functions]
    N --> O[Forecast Error Variance<br/>Decomposition]
    O --> P[Final Model Output]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — VARMA($p,q$) Fitting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stationary Multivariate Series Y sub t] --> B[Identify Tentative p and q<br/>CCF and PACF]
    B --> C[Stage 1 Hannan Rissanen<br/>Linear OLS Initialization]
    C --> D[Compute Initial Residuals]
    D --> E[Stage 2 Nonlinear MLE<br/>via Kalman Filter]
    E --> F[Refined Coefficient Estimates]
    F --> G[Verify Invertibility det Theta z]
    G --> H{Invertible and<br/>Stationary?}
    H -- No --> I[Re-parameterize<br/>Constrain Eigenvalues]
    I --> E
    H -- Yes --> J[Diagnostic Portmanteau Test]
    J --> K{Residuals White Noise?}
    K -- No --> L[Re-identify Orders p and q]
    L --> B
    K -- Yes --> M[Final VARMA Model Approved]
    M --> N[Forecasting and IRF]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Equation-by-Equation OLS Estimation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lagged Y Matrix X] --> B[Equation 1<br/>Y1 sub t on Lags]
    A --> C[Equation 2<br/>Y2 sub t on Lags]
    A --> D[Equation 3<br/>Y3 sub t on Lags]
    A --> E[Equation K<br/>YK sub t on Lags]
    B --> F[Residuals u1]
    C --> G[Residuals u2]
    D --> H[Residuals u3]
    E --> I[Residuals uK]
    F --> J[Sigma sub u Matrix<br/>Residual Cross Products]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[AIC BIC HQ<br/>Model Selection]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Architecture — Information Flow in a VAR(2) with K = 2

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y1t1[Y1 sub t minus 1] --> Y1t[Y1 sub t]
    Y2t1[Y2 sub t minus 1] --> Y1t
    Y1t1 --> Y2t[Y2 sub t]
    Y2t1 --> Y2t
    Y1t2[Y1 sub t minus 2] --> Y1t
    Y2t2[Y2 sub t minus 2] --> Y1t
    Y1t2 --> Y2t
    Y2t2 --> Y2t
    u1t[u1 sub t] --> Y1t
    u2t[u2 sub t] --> Y2t
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each endogenous variable $Y_{1t}, Y_{2t}$ receives inputs from the previous two lags of **both** itself and its partner variable, plus contemporaneous innovations $u_{1t}, u_{2t}$. This fully captures the **intertemporal, cross-variable dependencies** that define the VAR.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a VAR(1) model. State the stationarity condition.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Vector Autoregressive model of order 1 (VAR(1))** for a $K$-dimensional time series $\mathbf{Y}_t$ is:

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{u}_t$$

where $\mathbf{A}_1$ is a $K \times K$ coefficient matrix, $\mathbf{c}$ is a $K \times 1$ intercept vector, and $\mathbf{u}_t \sim WN(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma}_u)$.

**Stationarity condition:** All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}_1$ must lie strictly inside the unit circle, i.e., $\vert \lambda_i(\mathbf{A}_1) \vert < 1$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, K$. This ensures the process is covariance-stationary with a finite, time-invariant mean and covariance structure.

---

### Question 2: Why are the OLS estimates of a VAR(p) efficient equation-by-equation?
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a VAR($p$), **every equation has the same set of regressors** — namely, a constant and the lagged vectors $\mathbf{Y}_{t-1}, \ldots, \mathbf{Y}_{t-p}$. When regressors are identical across equations, the **Gauss–Markov theorem** implies that the OLS estimator is **identical to the GLS estimator** that accounts for the cross-equation error covariance $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u$. Therefore, OLS is **efficient (BLUE)** in each equation without needing to estimate the cross-equation covariance first. This result, due to **Sims (1980)**, is what made large multivariate VARs computationally tractable — we can estimate $K$ separate OLS regressions instead of solving a large SUR system.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: VAR(2) Model Fitting and Forecasting

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** For a bivariate series $\mathbf{Y}_t = (Y_{1t}, Y_{2t})'$, state the general VAR(2) model in matrix form and derive the **unconditional mean vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ in terms of the coefficient matrices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following VAR(1) for two variables fitted on $T = 200$ observations:

$$\hat{\mathbf{A}}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \hat{\mathbf{c}} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{pmatrix}$$

and current observation $\mathbf{Y}_{T} = (1.5, 0.8)'$, compute the **one-step-ahead forecast** $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+1}$ and the **two-step-ahead forecast** $\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+2}$. Also verify **stationarity** by computing the eigenvalues. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — VAR(2) Model and Mean Vector (7 marks):**

The general VAR(2) model in matrix form is:

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \mathbf{Y}_{t-1} + \mathbf{A}_2 \mathbf{Y}_{t-2} + \mathbf{u}_t$$

where $\mathbf{Y}_t \in \mathbb{R}^K$, $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^K$, $\mathbf{A}_1, \mathbf{A}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times K}$, and $\mathbf{u}_t \sim WN(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma}_u)$.

**Derivation of $\boldsymbol{\mu}$:**

Take expectations on both sides, using stationarity ($\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Y}_{t-i}] = \boldsymbol{\mu}$) and $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{u}_t] = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{A}_1 \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{A}_2 \boldsymbol{\mu}$$

Rearrange:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} - \mathbf{A}_1 \boldsymbol{\mu} - \mathbf{A}_2 \boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

$$(\mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{A}_1 - \mathbf{A}_2)\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{c}$$

Provided the matrix $(\mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{A}_1 - \mathbf{A}_2)$ is nonsingular (a condition implied by stationarity at $z=1$), the **unconditional mean** is:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\mu} = (\mathbf{I}_K - \mathbf{A}_1 - \mathbf{A}_2)^{-1}\mathbf{c}}$$

This represents the **long-run equilibrium** of the dynamic system. **(7 marks awarded for derivation, identification of invertibility requirement, and final boxed formula.)**

---

**Solution (b) — Forecasts and Stationarity Check (7 marks):**

The fitted VAR(1) is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+1} = \hat{\mathbf{c}} + \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1 \mathbf{Y}_T$$

Substituting values:

$$\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1.5 \\ 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute the matrix product:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 0.4 \times 1.5 + 0.3 \times 0.8 \\ 0.1 \times 1.5 + 0.6 \times 0.8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.60 + 0.24 \\ 0.15 + 0.48 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.84 \\ 0.63 \end{pmatrix}$$

Add the intercept:

$$\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+1} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 + 0.84 \\ -0.1 + 0.63 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.04 \\ 0.53 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Two-step-ahead forecast:**

$$\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+2} = \hat{\mathbf{c}} + \hat{\mathbf{A}}_1 \hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+1}$$

$$= \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1.04 \\ 0.53 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 0.4 \times 1.04 + 0.3 \times 0.53 \\ 0.1 \times 1.04 + 0.6 \times 0.53 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.416 + 0.159 \\ 0.104 + 0.318 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.575 \\ 0.422 \end{pmatrix}$$

Add intercept:

$$\hat{\mathbf{Y}}_{T+2} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 + 0.575 \\ -0.1 + 0.422 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.775 \\ 0.322 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Stationarity check (eigenvalues of $\hat{\mathbf{A}}_1$):**

Solve $\det(\hat{\mathbf{A}}_1 - \lambda \mathbf{I}_2) = 0$:

$$\det\begin{pmatrix} 0.4 - \lambda & 0.3 \\ 0.1 & 0.6 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = (0.4 - \lambda)(0.6 - \lambda) - 0.03 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - \lambda + 0.24 - 0.03 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - \lambda + 0.21 = 0$$

Quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 0.84}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{0.16}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm 0.4}{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{1.4}{2} = 0.7, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{0.6}{2} = 0.3$$

Since $\vert 0.7 \vert < 1$ and $\vert 0.3 \vert < 1$, the VAR(1) is **covariance-stationary**. ✓ **(7 marks.)**

---

### Question B: VARMA Model Identification, Estimation, and Diagnostic Testing

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **two-stage Hannan–Rissanen procedure** for estimating a VARMA($p,q$) model. State its asymptotic properties and any limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A practitioner fits a VARMA(1,1) to a bivariate series and obtains residual cross-correlations. The multivariate Ljung–Box $Q_{10}$ statistic is computed as **47.3**. Using $K = 2$, $p = 1$, $q = 1$, and $\alpha = 0.05$, perform the **portmanteau diagnostic test**. State the hypotheses, the degrees of freedom, the critical value, and your conclusion. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — Hannan–Rissanen Procedure (7 marks):**

The **Hannan–Rissanen (HR) method (1982)** is a computationally efficient two-stage estimator for VARMA models. It exploits the fact that once initial consistent estimates of the innovations are available, the VARMA becomes a **linear regression problem**.

**Stage 1 — Linear OLS Approximation:**

Fit a **long VAR** (typically of order $p + q$ or higher) to the data using OLS equation-by-equation:

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p+q}\mathbf{A}_i^{OLS}\mathbf{Y}_{t-i} + \hat{\mathbf{u}}_t^{(1)}$$

The OLS residuals $\hat{\mathbf{u}}_t^{(1)}$ serve as **proxies** for the unobserved true innovations.

**Stage 2 — Generalized Least Squares on Full VARMA Form:**

Using the Stage-1 residuals as "data" for the MA lags, fit the full VARMA($p,q$) specification by **OLS/GLS**:

$$\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{Y}_{t-i} - \sum_{j=1}^{q}\mathbf{M}_j \hat{\mathbf{u}}_{t-j}^{(1)} + \mathbf{u}_t$$

This is linear in parameters because the MA regressors are now observed (proxied by Stage-1 residuals).

**Stage 3 — Optional Refinement:**

Optionally, iterate: re-estimate the residuals using the Stage-2 coefficients and re-fit, or perform full **ML** via the Kalman filter to refine the estimates.

**Asymptotic Properties:**

- The HR estimator is **consistent** and **asymptotically normal** under mild regularity conditions (stationarity, invertibility, no unit roots in $\mathbf{\Phi}$ or $\mathbf{\Theta}$).
- It is **first-order efficient** — its asymptotic covariance equals the Cramér–Rao bound of the exact ML estimator.
- Computational cost is **linear** in sample size, unlike the full ML which requires iterative nonlinear optimization.

**Limitations:**

- **Infeasible in finite samples** if the true MA order is high — the linear prewhitening VAR must be long enough to produce approximately white residuals.
- **Sensitive to over-differencing** or pre-test biases from incorrect identification of $(p, q)$.
- For small samples, **ML via Kalman filter** is generally preferred for bias reduction.
- The HR residuals from Stage 1 are not the true innovations, introducing a small-sample bias.

> **Conclusion:** HR is the **practitioner's workhorse** for initializing and approximating VARMA models, with ML used for the final refined estimate. **(7 marks.)**

---

**Solution (b) — Multivariate Portmanteau Test (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \mathbf{R}_1 = \mathbf{R}_2 = \cdots = \mathbf{R}_{10} = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(residuals are white noise; model adequate)}$$

$$H_1: \exists\, i \in \{1, \ldots, 10\} \text{ such that } \mathbf{R}_i \neq \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(residual autocorrelation remains)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute degrees of freedom:**

For a VARMA($p,q$) with $p = 1$, $q = 1$:

$$\text{df} = K^2(h - p - q) = 2^2 \times (10 - 1 - 1) = 4 \times 8 = 32$$

**Step 3 — Critical value:**

From the $\chi^2$ distribution table with $32$ degrees of freedom at $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$\chi^2_{32, \, 0.95} \approx 46.19$$

(One may also report the more precise value: **46.19**.)

**Step 4 — Decision rule:**

Reject $H_0$ if $Q_{10} > \chi^2_{32, \, 0.95}$.

Compare: $Q_{10} = 47.3 > 46.19 = \chi^2_{32, \, 0.95}$.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Since the test statistic **exceeds** the critical value, we **reject $H_0$ at the 5% significance level**. There is evidence of significant **residual autocorrelation** in the fitted VARMA(1,1). The model is **inadequate** and should be re-specified — typically by:

- Increasing the AR order $p$,
- Increasing the MA order $q$,
- Adding exogenous variables, or
- Re-examining stationarity / differencing structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student errors in this problem:**
> 1. Using $h = 10$ as the degrees of freedom directly (ignoring $p$ and $q$ correction).
> 2. Forgetting the $K^2$ factor in the df formula.
> 3. Concluding "fail to reject" when the statistic clearly exceeds the critical value.
> 4. Not stating the model-specific degrees-of-freedom correction, which is what differentiates a "VARMA Ljung–Box" from a "VAR Ljung–Box" test.

**(7 marks.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Top 5 Marks Losers for This Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating reduced-form and structural VAR:** Always specify whether your model is the *reduced form* $\mathbf{A}_i$ matrices or the *structural* $\mathbf{B}_i$ matrices. The default in KTU papers is reduced form.
> 2. **Forgetting the lag-penalty in information criteria:** The penalty term uses $K^2 p$ (the number of coefficient parameters), NOT $Kp$. Mixing these up gives the wrong IC and wrong model selection.
> 3. **Using $\det(\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u)$ as the scalar residual measure:** You must show that $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u$ is the $K \times K$ matrix, not a single variance.
> 4. **Confusing Granger causality with structural causality:** Granger causality is a *predictive* statement, not a *causal* one in the philosophical/structural sense.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify stationarity BEFORE interpretation:** KTU examiners explicitly require eigenvalues of the companion matrix to be checked and reported.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **VAR($p$) form:** $\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\mathbf{A}_i \mathbf{Y}_{t-i} + \mathbf{u}_t$, with $\mathbf{u}_t \sim WN(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{\Sigma}_u)$.
- [ ] **VARMA($p,q$) form:** $\mathbf{\Phi}(L)\mathbf{Y}_t = \mathbf{c} + \mathbf{\Theta}(L)\mathbf{u}_t$.
- [ ] **Stationarity:** All eigenvalues of the companion matrix inside the unit circle.
- [ ] **Invertibility:** All roots of $\det\mathbf{\Theta}(z) = 0$ outside the unit circle.
- [ ] **OLS = GLS in VAR:** Identical regressors across equations make equation-by-equation OLS efficient.
- [ ] **Estimation of VARMA:** Hannan–Rissanen (linear) → ML via Kalman filter (refined).
- [ ] **Model selection:** AIC, SBIC, HQ — based on $\ln\det(\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u)$ plus penalty.
- [ ] **LR test for lag order:** $T[\ln\det\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u(p_0) - \ln\det\hat{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_u(p_1)] \sim \chi^2(K^2 \Delta p)$.
- [ ] **Diagnostic test:** Multivariate Ljung–Box $Q_h \sim \chi^2\bigl(K^2(h - p - q)\bigr)$.
- [ ] **Forecasting recursion:** Substitute conditional expectations for unknown future values; compute $\mathbf{\Sigma}_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}(h) = \mathbf{\Sigma}_u + \sum_{i=1}^{h-1}\mathbf{\Psi}_i\mathbf{\Sigma}_u\mathbf{\Psi}_i'$.
- [ ] **MA($\infty$) representation:** $\mathbf{\Psi}_i = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{F}^i\mathbf{G}$; recursion $\mathbf{\Psi}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{\min(i,p)}\mathbf{A}_j\mathbf{\Psi}_{i-j}$.
- [ ] **Unconditional mean:** $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \left(\mathbf{I}_K - \sum_{i=1}^{p}\mathbf{A}_i\right)^{-1}\mathbf{c}$.
- [ ] **Granger causality:** Tested via $F$-test of lagged-coefficient restrictions across equations.
- [ ] **Impulse Response Functions (IRF):** Use the $\mathbf{\Psi}_i$ matrices; orthogonalize via Cholesky of $\mathbf{\Sigma}_u$ for structural interpretation.
- [ ] **Schwert rule:** $p_{\max} \approx 12(T/100)^{1/4}$ for the maximum lag to consider.
- [ ] **Practical reality:** In KTU problems and applied work, **pure VAR is preferred**; pure VARMA is rarely needed because of identification and over-parameterization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024):** When asked to *fit* a VAR or VARMA, your answer MUST include — (1) stationarity test on the data, (2) lag-order selection by an information criterion, (3) coefficient estimation with the chosen method, (4) diagnostic checking via the multivariate portmanteau test, and (5) interpretation (IRF, FEVD, or forecast) if asked. A complete, well-structured answer typically fetches full marks; an answer that jumps straight to coefficients without these steps loses 4–5 marks routinely.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Regression Models: Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT755 - TIME SERIES MODELLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Regression Models: Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Dynamic Regression Model (DRM)** is a regression equation in which the independent variables are allowed to be lagged, the dependent variable is allowed to be contemporaneously influenced by the past of the error process, and the residuals themselves are modeled as a stationary **ARMA(p, q)** stochastic process. In the general form used throughout this module, the model is written as:

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_{i,t} + n_t, \qquad n_t = \frac{\theta_q(B)}{\phi_p(B)} a_t$$

where:
- $y_t$ is the response / output series.
- $x_{i,t}$ are the exogenous predictor (input) series.
- $\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_k$ are the **regression coefficients**.
- $n_t$ is a *dynamic* error term (also called the *intervention noise* or *regression residual*).
- $\phi_p(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \dots - \phi_p B^p$ is the **autoregressive polynomial** of order $p$.
- $\theta_q(B) = 1 - \theta_1 B - \theta_2 B^2 - \dots - \theta_q B^q$ is the **moving average polynomial** of order $q$.
- $B$ is the **backshift operator** such that $B^j a_t = a_{t-j}$.
- $a_t$ is a sequence of independent, identically distributed **white-noise** shocks with $a_t \sim N(0, \sigma_a^2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the qualifier "Dynamic"?** The term *dynamic* emphasizes that the error term $n_t$ is itself a *correlated* time series generated by an ARMA filter applied to white noise. The relationship between $y_t$ and the predictors is therefore not a static snapshot, but a path-dependent mapping that propagates shocks across time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The course text treats the DRM as the building block of *intervention analysis*, *transfer-function modelling*, and *regression with ARMA errors*. Estimation in this module is restricted to the case where the predictor variables $x_t$ are *fixed* (not stochastic), which is the standard assumption for university-level derivations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are an agricultural economist trying to predict the quarterly yield of a crop, $y_t$, using the amount of fertilizer applied, $x_t$, as your predictor. A naive regression of the form $y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_t + \varepsilon_t$ assumes that the unmeasured "noise" in the soil, weather, and biology this quarter is **independent** of the noise in previous quarters. In reality, soil moisture, microbial activity, and pest populations exhibit strong **temporal persistence**: a poor week tends to be followed by another poor week.

The Dynamic Regression Model is the equivalent of saying: *"Predict yield from fertilizer, but then, whatever residual is left over, model it as a self-driven ARMA process that remembers its own past."* This is exactly what a seasoned farmer does intuitively — he knows that if his last three months were bad, the next one is more likely to be bad as well, even if the fertilizer amount is held constant.

## 1.3 When to Use a Dynamic Regression Model

A DRM is preferred over a static OLS regression when **any one** of the following is observed:

1. The **residuals of an OLS fit** show significant autocorrelation in the ACF or PACF.
2. The **Durbin–Watson statistic** is significantly different from 2.
3. The **Ljung–Box Q-statistic** rejects the null of white-noise residuals at a chosen significance level.
4. **Theoretical knowledge** dictates that the system has inertia, memory, or carry-over effects.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Decision Rule (KTU Lab Convention):** Fit OLS → check residual ACF for the first 5 lags. If two or more lags lie outside the $\pm 1.96/\sqrt{N}$ band, upgrade the model to a DRM with ARMA errors.

## 1.4 Standard Notation Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $y_t$ | Output (response) series |
| $x_t$ | Input (predictor) series |
| $n_t$ | Dynamic regression residual (ARMA process) |
| $a_t$ | White-noise innovation, $N(0, \sigma_a^2)$ |
| $\beta_i$ | Regression coefficient (mean effect) |
| $\phi_j$ | AR coefficient of the residual process |
| $\theta_k$ | MA coefficient of the residual process |
| $p$ | AR order |
| $q$ | MA order |
| $N$ | Sample size |
| $L$ | Likelihood function |
| $\ell$ | Log-likelihood |
| AIC, BIC | Information criteria for model selection |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Estimation Philosophy

Estimation of a DRM is a **multi-stage** problem because the parameters of interest live in two distinct worlds:

1. The **regression world** — parameters $\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_k$ that govern the *mean* of $y_t$ as a function of $x_t$.
2. The **ARMA world** — parameters $\phi_1, \dots, \phi_p, \theta_1, \dots, \theta_q, \sigma_a^2$ that govern the *temporal correlation* of the residuals.

The estimation strategy is to **interleave** information from these two worlds. We begin with a simple OLS fit to get a "first cut" at the residuals, then identify and estimate the ARMA model on those residuals, then *re-estimate everything jointly* to remove the small bias introduced by the two-step procedure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Pedagogical Point:** OLS is *consistent* but *inefficient* when residuals are autocorrelated. The joint Maximum Likelihood (ML) estimator is both consistent and *asymptotically efficient* (achieves the Cramér–Rao lower bound).

## 2.2 Stage 1: OLS Pre-Whitening

The first stage of any DRM estimation procedure is to ignore the dynamics and fit the regression by ordinary least squares:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

The OLS residuals are then

$$\hat{n}_t^{(1)} = y_t - \hat{\beta}_0 - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_{i,t}, \quad t = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

These residuals are used in **Stage 2** to identify the ARMA order.

## 2.3 Stage 2: Identification of ARMA Order

The ACF and PACF of $\hat{n}_t^{(1)}$ are examined. The rules of thumb are:

| Pattern in ACF | Pattern in PACF | Tentative Model |
|---|---|---|
| Tails off | Cuts off after lag $p$ | AR(p) |
| Cuts off after lag $q$ | Tails off | MA(q) |
| Tails off | Tails off | ARMA(p, q) |

## 2.4 Stage 3: Joint Estimation Methods

Once the orders $(p, q)$ are fixed, the parameters are estimated jointly using one of the following methods:

### 2.4.1 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (ML)

The joint density of the $N$ observations is the conditional Gaussian likelihood:

$$L(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \boldsymbol{\phi}, \boldsymbol{\theta}, \sigma_a^2 \mid \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{N} f(a_t \mid \boldsymbol{\psi})$$

Under the assumption $a_t \sim N(0, \sigma_a^2)$,

$$\ell = -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2} \log \sigma_a^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma_a^2} \sum_{t=1}^{N} a_t^2$$

The estimators are obtained by numerical maximization of $\ell$ with respect to the parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\psi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}, \boldsymbol{\phi}, \boldsymbol{\theta}, \sigma_a^2)$.

### 2.4.2 Nonlinear Least Squares (NLS)

The NLS estimator minimizes the one-step-ahead prediction error variance:

$$S(\boldsymbol{\psi}) = \sum_{t=1}^{N} a_t^2(\boldsymbol{\psi})$$

For a Gaussian model, NLS and ML are equivalent up to a constant, so their estimates coincide. NLS is preferred in software because it avoids the explicit computation of the Jacobian of the likelihood.

### 2.4.3 Hannan–Rissanen Procedure

This is a computationally cheap *three-step* estimator that works very well in practice and is often used as a *starting value* for the ML optimizer:

1. Fit a high-order AR($K$) model to $y_t$ (after removing the regression mean) to get residuals $\tilde{a}_t$.
2. Regress $\tilde{a}_t$ on the lagged residuals and the MA regressors to obtain initial $\hat{\phi}_j, \hat{\theta}_k$.
3. Use these as starting values for a final NLS or ML refinement.

## 2.5 Stage 4: Initialization (Backcasting)

The first few values of $a_t$ are unobserved because they depend on $n_0, n_{-1}, \dots$ and on $a_0, a_{-1}, \dots$. There are two common fixes:

- **Forward initialization:** set the unknown starting values equal to the unconditional mean, i.e., $a_0 = a_{-1} = \dots = 0$.
- **Backcasting (preferred):** run the ARMA recursion *backwards* from the end of the sample to obtain starting values. This reduces bias, especially in short samples.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|
| $y_t = \mathbf{x}_t^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + n_t$ | Regression mean structure |
| $\phi_p(B) n_t = \theta_q(B) a_t$ | ARMA noise structure |
| $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$ | OLS estimator for mean coefficients |
| $a_t = \frac{\phi_p(B)}{\theta_q(B)} n_t$ | Innovations in terms of $n_t$ |
| $\ell = -\tfrac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma_a^2) - \tfrac{1}{2\sigma_a^2}\sum a_t^2$ | Gaussian log-likelihood |
| $\hat{\sigma}_a^2 = \tfrac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} a_t^2(\hat{\boldsymbol{\psi}})$ | Innovation variance estimator |
| $AIC = -2\ell + 2m$ | Akaike Information Criterion, $m$ = number of parameters |
| $BIC = -2\ell + m \log N$ | Bayesian Information Criterion |
| $Q(m) = N(N+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\tfrac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{N-k}$ | Ljung–Box portmanteau statistic |
| $DW = \tfrac{\sum_{t=2}^{N}(\hat{n}_t - \hat{n}_{t-1})^2}{\sum_{t=1}^{N}\hat{n}_t^2}$ | Durbin–Watson statistic |
| $\sqrt{\text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})} = \hat{\sigma}_a \sqrt{[\mathbf{X}^\top \hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}^{-1}\mathbf{X}]^{-1}}$ | Standard error of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ under ARMA errors |
| $\hat{n}_t = y_t - \mathbf{x}_t^\top \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ | Regression residuals used in identification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Dynamic regression is the workhorse of demand forecasting, climate attribution studies, macroeconomic policy evaluation, and signal-processing systems with auto-correlated noise (e.g., radar tracking, biomedical sensor calibration).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the OLS Pre-Estimator

**Step 1 — Write the model in matrix form.**

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1,t} + \beta_2 x_{2,t} + \dots + \beta_k x_{k,t} + n_t$$

In matrix form, stacking the $N$ observations:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \mathbf{n}$$

where $\mathbf{y}$ is $(N \times 1)$, $\mathbf{X}$ is $(N \times (k+1))$ with first column of ones, and $\mathbf{n}$ is the vector of ARMA errors.

**Step 2 — Define the OLS objective function.**

$$S(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{t=1}^{N} n_t^2 = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

**Step 3 — Take the gradient and set it to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2 \mathbf{X}^\top (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the normal equations.**

$$\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \, \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS} = \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

This gives the first-stage estimates used to obtain $\hat{n}_t^{(1)}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the OLS Variance Under ARMA Errors

When the OLS estimator is applied but the errors are ARMA-correlated, the standard textbook variance $\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}$ is **incorrect**. The correct variance is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS}) = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n \mathbf{X} (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n$ is the $(N \times N)$ covariance matrix of $\mathbf{n}$, with entries $[\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n]_{ij} = \gamma_n(\vert i - j \vert)$.

**Step 1 — Compute the autocovariances of an ARMA(p, q) process.**

For an ARMA(p, q) with white noise variance $\sigma_a^2$, the Yule–Walker system gives:

$$\gamma_n(k) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \phi_j \gamma_n(k-j) + \sigma_a^2 \sum_{j=0}^{q} \theta_j \psi_j, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

**Step 2 — Plug $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n$ into the sandwich formula** above. The resulting standard errors are valid under ML estimation as well, when $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n$ is replaced by its ML estimate $\hat{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}}_n$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gaussian Log-Likelihood

**Step 1 — Write the joint density as a product of conditional densities.**

$$f(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\psi}) = \prod_{t=1}^{N} f(y_t \mid y_{t-1}, \dots, y_1, x_t, x_{t-1}, \dots, \boldsymbol{\psi})$$

**Step 2 — Express each conditional as a Gaussian.**

$$y_t \mid y_{t-1}, \dots \sim N(\hat{y}_{t \mid t-1}, \; \sigma_a^2)$$

where $\hat{y}_{t \mid t-1}$ is the one-step-ahead forecast produced by the Kalman filter or by direct ARMA recursion.

**Step 3 — Form the log-likelihood.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell(\boldsymbol{\psi}) &= \log f(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\psi}) \\
&= \sum_{t=1}^{N} \log f(y_t \mid y_{t-1}, \dots) \\
&= -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2} \log \sigma_a^2 - \frac{1}{2 \sigma_a^2} \sum_{t=1}^{N} a_t^2(\boldsymbol{\psi})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Concentrate the likelihood with respect to $\sigma_a^2$.**

Differentiating $\ell$ with respect to $\sigma_a^2$ and setting to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma_a^2} &= -\frac{N}{2 \sigma_a^2} + \frac{1}{2 \sigma_a^4} \sum_{t=1}^{N} a_t^2 = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \hat{\sigma}_a^2 &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} a_t^2(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}, \hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}, \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}})
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back gives the *concentrated log-likelihood*, which is a function of $(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \boldsymbol{\phi}, \boldsymbol{\theta})$ only:

$$\ell_c = -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2} \log \hat{\sigma}_a^2 - \frac{N}{2}$$

Maximizing $\ell_c$ is equivalent to minimizing $\hat{\sigma}_a^2$.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Hannan–Rissanen Estimator

**Step 1 — Fit a long AR($K$) to the OLS residuals.**

Use OLS to estimate the auxiliary model:

$$\hat{n}_t^{(1)} = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \tilde{\phi}_j \hat{n}_{t-j}^{(1)} + \tilde{a}_t$$

with $K$ chosen as $K \approx \max(p, q) + \text{buffer (often 5–10)}$.

**Step 2 — Construct the regressor matrix for the ARMA model.**

The Hannan–Rissanen idea is to treat $\tilde{a}_t$ as a noisy *proxy* for the true $a_t$, and then perform a *linear* regression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{n}_t^{(1)} &= \phi_1 \hat{n}_{t-1}^{(1)} + \dots + \phi_p \hat{n}_{t-p}^{(1)} \\
&\quad - \theta_1 \tilde{a}_{t-1} - \dots - \theta_q \tilde{a}_{t-q} + a_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear regression** to obtain $\hat{\phi}_j^{(HR)}$ and $\hat{\theta}_k^{(HR)}$.

**Step 4 — Refine** these initial estimates using ML or NLS to remove the small-sample bias caused by using $\tilde{a}_t$ in place of $a_t$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Properties of the DRM Estimator

| Property | OLS Stage 1 | Joint ML |
|---|---|---|
| Consistency | Yes (under weak assumptions) | Yes |
| Asymptotic efficiency | No (inefficient) | Yes (attains CRLB) |
| Bias in small samples | O(1/N) for AR coefficients | O(1/N) but smaller |
| Standard errors | Use sandwich formula $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n$ | Direct Hessian inverse |
| Computational cost | O(Nk²) — single linear solve | O(N·iter) — iterative Newton |
| Robust to mis-specified ARMA order | No | No (must re-estimate) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to compare OLS and ML estimators, always mention *consistency, asymptotic efficiency,* and *sensitivity to ARMA mis-specification* in a single sentence. This is the canonical three-point answer expected by KTU examiners.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DRM Estimation

The following Mermaid flowchart describes the *algorithmic flow* of the canonical two-stage + refinement estimation procedure used for Dynamic Regression Models.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Series y_t, x_t] --> B[Stage 1: Fit OLS regression]
    B --> C[Compute OLS residuals n_hat_1t]
    C --> D[Stage 2: Identify ARMA order p, q from ACF and PACF]
    D --> E[Stage 3a: Hannan-Rissanen to obtain initial phi and theta]
    E --> F[Stage 3b: Joint ML or NLS refinement]
    F --> G[Compute innovation series a_hat_t]
    G --> H[Compute log-likelihood and information criteria]
    H --> I{Model adequate?}
    I -- No --> J[Re-identify order or add predictors]
    J --> D
    I -- Yes --> K[Compute standard errors using sandwich formula]
    K --> L[Output: final beta, phi, theta, sigma_a^2]
    L --> M[End: Estimated DRM]
```

## 4.2 Information-Theoretic Architecture: Likelihood Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Log-Likelihood Function psi] --> L1[Gradient w.r.t. beta]
    L --> L2[Gradient w.r.t. phi]
    L --> L3[Gradient w.r.t. theta]
    L --> L4[Gradient w.r.t. sigma_a^2]
    L1 --> H[Hessian Matrix H of size m by m]
    L2 --> H
    L3 --> H
    L4 --> H
    H --> V[Variance-Covariance Matrix = H inverse]
    V --> SE[Standard Errors of all estimates]
```

## 4.3 Diagnostic and Backcasting Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Estimated DRM] --> D1[Residual Diagnostics: ACF of a_hat_t]
    D1 --> D2{Ljung-Box Q p-value less than 0.05?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D3[Increase p or q and re-estimate]
    D3 --> M
    D2 -- No --> D4[Normality Test on a_hat_t]
    D4 --> D5{Box-Jenkins p-value less than 0.05?}
    D5 -- Yes --> D6[Consider variance stabilization transform]
    D6 --> M
    D5 -- No --> D7[Accept Model: Ready for Forecasting and Intervention]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Map: OLS vs. ML for DRM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OW[OLS World]
        O1[Linear in beta] --> O2[One-step solution]
        O2 --> O3[Sandwich standard errors]
    end
    subgraph MW[ML World]
        M1[Nonlinear in phi, theta] --> M2[Iterative Newton or BFGS]
        M2 --> M3[Hessian-based standard errors]
    end
    OW -- Pre-estimate beta, n_hat_t --> MW
    MW -- Refined beta, phi, theta, sigma --> OW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a computational step; each arrow shows data flow. The double-headed arrow between OLS World and ML World depicts the *interleaving* that defines the joint estimation philosophy.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**What is a Dynamic Regression Model? How does it differ from a static OLS regression model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Dynamic Regression Model is a regression equation in which the residual term is modeled as a stationary ARMA(p, q) stochastic process rather than as independent white noise. Mathematically, it is specified as $y_t = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_{i,t} + n_t$ with $\phi_p(B) n_t = \theta_q(B) a_t$.

The key differences from a static OLS regression are:
1. **OLS** assumes $n_t$ are uncorrelated, while **DRM** allows $n_t$ to be autocorrelated.
2. **DRM** is therefore more efficient (smaller standard errors) when autocorrelation is present.
3. **DRM** can capture persistence, inertia, and carry-over effects that static regression misses.

*(Allocate 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the difference in assumption, 1 mark for the implication on efficiency and dynamics.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]**
**State the two stages of the Hannan–Rissanen estimation procedure for a Dynamic Regression Model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hannan–Rissanen procedure proceeds in two main stages:

1. **Stage A — Long AR approximation:** A high-order AR(K) model is fitted to the regression residuals $\hat{n}_t$ obtained from a preliminary OLS fit, producing proxy innovations $\tilde{a}_t$.

2. **Stage B — Linear regression and refinement:** A linear regression is performed in which $\hat{n}_t$ is regressed on its own lags and on the lagged proxy innovations $\tilde{a}_{t-1}, \dots, \tilde{a}_{t-q}$. The estimated coefficients are used as initial values for a final non-linear least-squares (or ML) refinement to remove the small-sample bias.

*(1.5 marks per stage, with the last 0 mark reserved for the refinement mention.)*
