<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Bootstrapping and Bagging

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bootstrapping** is a **non-parametric resampling technique** introduced by **Bradley Efron (1979)**. It treats the observed sample as the *best available approximation* of the population, and generates a large number of **"pseudo-populations"** by drawing samples **with replacement** from the original data. From these replicate samples, the **sampling distribution** of virtually any statistic (mean, median, regression coefficient, ARMA parameter, forecast error variance, etc.) can be empirically approximated without relying on restrictive parametric assumptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bagging** stands for **Bootstrap AGGregatING**. It is an **ensemble learning algorithm** (Breiman, 1996) that combines the predictions of **$B$ independently trained models**, each fitted on a distinct bootstrap sample, by **averaging** (for regression) or **majority voting** (for classification). The central goal of bagging is **variance reduction** in unstable predictors.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Many Doctors" Intuition

Imagine you visit a single doctor and are given a diagnosis. You are not fully confident because one opinion can be biased. So you go to **100 different doctors**, but you don't give each doctor the *same* textbook — you give each one a **slightly different pile of case notes** (some case sheets are repeated, some are missing). Each doctor forms an independent opinion from their pile. You then **average** all 100 opinions.

- The **"case sheets"** are your data.
- The **"different piles"** are the **bootstrap samples**.
- The **"doctors' opinions"** are the model estimates.
- The **"average"** is the **aggregated prediction**.

This is precisely the spirit of **Bootstrap + Aggregation = Bagging**.

### Why Are These Methods Critical in Time Series?

Time series data violate the fundamental i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) assumption that underlies classical bootstrapping. Observations in a time series are **autocorrelated** — yesterday's stock price tells you something about today's. Therefore, KTU Module 4 emphasizes specialized variants:

- **Block Bootstrap (BB)** — Kunsch (1989), to preserve local dependence structure.
- **Moving Block Bootstrap (MBB)** — Liu & Singh (1992).
- **Stationary Bootstrap (SB)** — Politis & Romano (1994).
- **Bagged Forecast Combinations** — applied to unstable ARIMA / ML forecasters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric for bagging effectiveness:** the **Out-Of-Bag (OOB) error**, used as a free cross-validation estimate. Approximately **63.2%** of the original observations appear in any single bootstrap sample; the remaining **~36.8%** form the OOB test set.

| Bootstrap Coefficient | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Expected unique obs. per sample | $\approx 0.6321 \cdot n$ | Each bootstrap sample contains this many distinct original points |
| Expected duplicates | $\approx 0.3679 \cdot n$ | Observations NOT in the sample (OOB) |
| OOB fraction | $\mathbf{1 - (1 - 1/n)^n \to 1/e \approx 0.3679}$ | Theoretical limit as $n \to \infty$ |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Standard (Naïve) Bootstrap

**Step 1 — Data:** You observe a sample of size $n$:
$$\{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$$

**Step 2 — Resample:** For $b = 1, 2, \dots, B$, draw a **bootstrap sample** of size $n$ **with replacement** from the original sample:
$$\{X_1^{*b}, X_2^{*b}, \dots, X_n^{*b}\}$$

> Each draw is independent and uniform over $\{X_1, \dots, X_n\}$. The probability that a specific observation $X_i$ is selected in any given draw is exactly $1/n$.

**Step 3 — Compute the statistic of interest** on each replicate:
$$\hat{\theta}^{*b} = s(X_1^{*b}, X_2^{*b}, \dots, X_n^{*b})$$

**Step 4 — Approximate the sampling distribution** of $\hat{\theta}$ using the empirical distribution of the $\hat{\theta}^{*b}$ values.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The naïve bootstrap is **invalid for time series** because resampling individual observations **destroys the temporal dependence structure**. Always use a **block-based** variant in time-series contexts.

### 2.2 Block Bootstrap Variants for Time Series

**Method 1 — Non-Overlapping Block Bootstrap (NBB):**
- Partition the series into $k = n/l$ non-overlapping blocks of fixed length $l$.
- Sample $k$ blocks uniformly **with replacement**.
- Concatenate them to form a new pseudo-series of length $n$.

**Method 2 — Moving Block Bootstrap (MBB):**
- Form $n - l + 1$ overlapping blocks $B_i = (X_i, X_{i+1}, \dots, X_{i+l-1})$ for $i = 1, \dots, n-l+1$.
- Randomly draw $k = \lceil n/l \rceil$ blocks with replacement.
- Concatenate them. **Overlap preserved.**

**Method 3 — Stationary Bootstrap (SB):**
- Block lengths are **random**, drawn from a Geometric distribution with mean $l$.
- Eliminates discontinuities at block boundaries.
- Preserves **stationarity** of the resampled series (hence the name).

### 2.3 The Bagging Algorithm (Breiman 1996)

**Inputs:**
- Training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$.
- Base learning algorithm $\mathcal{A}$.
- Number of bootstrap iterations $B$.

**Procedure:**
For $b = 1$ to $B$:
1. Draw a bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}^{*b}$ of size $n$ from $\mathcal{D}$ (with replacement).
2. Train a base model: $\hat{f}^{*b}(x) = \mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D}^{*b})$.
3. Store the trained model.

**Output (Regression):**
$$\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}^{*b}(x)$$

**Output (Classification):** Majority vote over the $B$ class predictions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single draw inclusion prob. | $P(X_i \in \mathcal{D}^{*b}) = 1/n$ | Independent draws |
| 2 | Not-selected prob. (one draw) | $(1 - 1/n)$ | Per individual draw |
| 3 | OOB fraction (limit) | $\lim_{n \to \infty} (1 - 1/n)^n = 1/e \approx \mathbf{0.3679}$ | Used in OOB error |
| 4 | In-bag fraction (limit) | $1 - 1/e \approx \mathbf{0.6321}$ | Unique obs. coverage |
| 5 | Bootstrap variance estimate | $\widehat{\text{Var}}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \dfrac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \left( \hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\hat{\theta}}^{*} \right)^{2}$ | $B \geq 200$ recommended |
| 6 | Bootstrap mean of replicates | $\bar{\hat{\theta}}^{*} = \dfrac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*b}$ | Centering constant |
| 7 | Bias estimate | $\widehat{\text{Bias}}_B = \bar{\hat{\theta}}^{*} - \hat{\theta}$ | Subtraction form |
| 8 | Normal-approx CI ($1-\alpha$) | $\hat{\theta} \pm z_{1-\alpha/2}\cdot \widehat{\text{se}}_B$ | Symmetric interval |
| 9 | Percentile CI | $\left[ \hat{\theta}^{*(\alpha/2)}, \hat{\theta}^{*(1-\alpha/2)} \right]$ | Empirical quantiles |
| 10 | BCa (bias-corrected) CI | Uses $z_0$ & acceleration $a$ | Most accurate, advanced |
| 11 | Bagged regression predictor | $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \dfrac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{f}^{*b}(x)$ | Averaging rule |
| 12 | Bagging variance reduction | $\text{Var}\left(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{B}\text{Var}\left(\hat{f}^{*b}\right)$ | If independent fits |
| 13 | NBB block count | $k = \lfloor n / l \rfloor$ | Number of blocks drawn |
| 14 | MBB block count | $k = \lceil n / l \rceil$ | Overlapping allowed |
| 15 | SB block-length dist. | $P(L = \ell) = (1 - p)^{\ell-1} p$ | Geometric with mean $1/p$ |
| 16 | Optimal block length (MBB) | $l \propto n^{1/3}$ | Politis & White (2004) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In forecasting competitions (e.g., **M-competition, M3, M4**), bagging is used to combine ARIMA, exponential smoothing, and ML models. Bootstrapped prediction intervals (PIs) replace the unreliable Gaussian PI assumption for non-linear / non-Gaussian residuals.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 3.1 — The OOB Fraction $\approx 0.3679$

We need the probability that a specific observation $X_i$ is **NOT** selected in any of the $n$ draws of a single bootstrap sample.

**Step 1:** Probability that $X_i$ is *not* chosen in a single draw:
$$P(\text{not chosen in one draw}) = 1 - \frac{1}{n} = \frac{n-1}{n}$$

**Step 2:** Because draws are independent and with replacement, the probability of *not* being chosen in *any* of the $n$ draws is:
$$P(X_i \notin \mathcal{D}^{*b}) = \left( \frac{n-1}{n} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 3:** Take the limit as $n \to \infty$:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \left( \frac{n-1}{n} \right)^{n} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{n} \right)^{n} = e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical value:
$$e^{-1} \approx 0.367879 \approx 36.79\%$$

This is precisely the **Out-Of-Bag fraction**, and the corresponding **in-bag fraction** is $1 - 1/e \approx 63.21\%$.

---

### Derivation 3.2 — Bootstrap Estimate of Standard Error

We want to estimate the **sampling variability** of an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ (which could be a sample mean, a regression slope, an AR(1) coefficient $\hat{\phi}_1$, etc.) using $B$ bootstrap replicates.

**Step 1:** For each bootstrap sample $b$, compute the replicate statistic:
$$\hat{\theta}^{*b} = s(X_1^{*b}, X_2^{*b}, \dots, X_n^{*b}), \quad b = 1, 2, \dots, B$$

**Step 2:** Compute the mean of the bootstrap replicates:
$$\bar{\hat{\theta}}^{*} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*b}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the sample variance of the replicates. Using the unbiased divisor $(B-1)$:
$$\widehat{\text{Var}}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{1}{B - 1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \left( \hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\hat{\theta}}^{*} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 4:** The bootstrap standard error is the square root:
$$\widehat{\text{se}}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\widehat{\text{Var}}_B(\hat{\theta})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $(B-1)$ and not $B$?** It is Bessel's correction: with $B$ replicates whose mean is already estimated from the data, we have only $B-1$ degrees of freedom remaining. This makes the estimator unbiased for the true sampling variance.

---

### Derivation 3.3 — Variance Reduction by Bagging

Suppose each base model $\hat{f}^{*b}(x)$ has:
- **Bias:** $\text{Bias}(\hat{f}^{*b}) = \mu_{\text{bias}}$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^{2}$
- **Pairwise correlation:** $\rho$ between any two fits.

**Step 1:** Variance of the average of $B$ identically distributed (but not necessarily independent) random variables:
$$\text{Var}\left( \hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) \right) = \text{Var}\left( \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{f}^{*b}(x) \right)$$

**Step 2:** Expand using the variance-of-sum identity:
$$\text{Var}\left( \hat{f}_{\text{bag}} \right) = \frac{1}{B^{2}} \left[ \sum_{b=1}^{B}\text{Var}(\hat{f}^{*b}) + 2 \sum_{b < c}\text{Cov}(\hat{f}^{*b}, \hat{f}^{*c}) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $B \cdot \sigma^{2}$ for the sum of variances and $B(B-1)\rho\sigma^{2}$ for the covariance sum:
$$\text{Var}\left( \hat{f}_{\text{bag}} \right) = \frac{1}{B^{2}} \left[ B\sigma^{2} + B(B-1)\rho\sigma^{2} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Factor and simplify:
$$\text{Var}\left( \hat{f}_{\text{bag}} \right) = \rho\sigma^{2} + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^{2}}{B}$$

**Step 5:** As $B \to \infty$, the second term vanishes:
$$\text{Var}\left( \hat{f}_{\text{bag}} \right) \longrightarrow \rho \cdot \sigma^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial insight:** Bagging **does not eliminate** variance; it reduces it to a floor of $\rho \sigma^{2}$, where $\rho$ is the residual correlation between bagged models. This is why bagging is **most effective for unstable, high-variance learners** (e.g., deep regression trees) and **less useful for stable linear models**.

---

### Derivation 3.4 — Optimal Block Length (Moving Block Bootstrap)

For MBB applied to a stationary time series with $n$ observations, Politis & White (2004) showed the asymptotically optimal block length is:

$$l_{\text{opt}} \;\propto\; n^{1/3}$$

The exact form involves the **autocovariance function** $\gamma(h) = \text{Cov}(X_t, X_{t+h})$ and is given by:
$$l_{\text{opt}} = \left[ \frac{2\,\hat{G}^{2}(0)}{n\,\hat{D}} \right]^{1/3}$$

where:
$$\hat{G}^{2}(0) = \sum_{h=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \gamma(h) \vert^{2} \quad \text{and} \quad \hat{D} = 4 \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} h^{2}\,\gamma^{2}(h)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $n^{1/3}$ rate is **slower** than the $n^{1/2}$ rate in classical i.i.d. bootstrap — a direct consequence of the dependence structure that has to be preserved.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Bootstrap Resampling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Time Series X1 to Xn] --> B[Choose Resampling Method]
    B --> C[Naive Bootstrap]
    B --> D[Non Overlapping Block BB]
    B --> E[Moving Block Bootstrap]
    B --> F[Stationary Bootstrap]
    C --> G[Draw n samples with replacement]
    D --> H[Partition into k blocks of length l]
    E --> I[Form overlapping blocks]
    F --> J[Sample random geometric blocks]
    G --> K[Compute statistic theta star b]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Collect B replicates]
    L --> M[Estimate Standard Error]
    L --> N[Construct Confidence Interval]
    L --> O[Estimate Bias]
```

### 4.2 Bagging Ensemble Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DS[Original Dataset D of size n] --> R{Random Resample with Replacement}
    R --> S1[Bootstrap Sample D star 1]
    R --> S2[Bootstrap Sample D star 2]
    R --> S3[Bootstrap Sample D star B]
    S1 --> M1[Base Model f star 1]
    S2 --> M2[Base Model f star 2]
    S3 --> MB[Base Model f star B]
    M1 --> AGG[Aggregator]
    M2 --> AGG
    MB --> AGG
    AGG --> OUT1[Bagged Prediction average]
    AGG --> OUT2[OOB Error Estimate]
    AGG --> OUT3[Variance Reduced Forecast]
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic: Which Bootstrap Variant to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the data i.i.d.?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> NB[Use Naive Bootstrap]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Are observations serially dependent?}
    Q2 -- No --> NB
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is stationarity required?}
    Q3 -- No --> NBB[Use Non Overlapping Block BB]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Need smooth boundary behaviour?}
    Q4 -- No --> MBB[Use Moving Block Bootstrap]
    Q4 -- Yes --> SB[Use Stationary Bootstrap]
    NBB --> AP[Apply to Time Series Model]
    MBB --> AP
    SB --> AP
    NB --> AP
    AP --> PI[Generate Forecast Intervals]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Parametric Approach]
        T1[Assume Gaussian residuals] --> T2[Use closed form CI]
        T2 --> T3[Output Parametric Interval]
    end
    subgraph BOOT[Bootstrap Approach]
        B1[Resample data B times] --> B2[Refit model each time]
        B2 --> B3[Empirical distribution of estimates]
        B3 --> T3B[Output Empirical Interval]
    end
    T3 -- Comparison --> OUT[Coverage and Width Analysis]
    T3B -- Comparison --> OUT
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "bootstrap sample" in the context of time series analysis. Why is the naïve bootstrap unsuitable for serially correlated data?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A bootstrap sample is a sample of size $n$ drawn **with replacement** from the original observed dataset of size $n$, allowing each original observation to appear multiple times in the replicate.
- **(1 mark)** The naïve (i.i.d.) bootstrap treats observations as independent; it draws individual time points in isolation.
- **(1 mark)** In a time series, $X_t$ and $X_{t+1}$ are autocorrelated. Resampling individual points **shuffles** them and **destroys the temporal dependence structure**, leading to invalid inference (poor CI coverage, biased variance estimates). Block-based methods (NBB, MBB, SB) are required.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State the asymptotic value of the "out-of-bag" (OOB) fraction and briefly explain its role in bagging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The OOB fraction converges to $1/e \approx 0.3679$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **(1 mark)** It comes from $\lim_{n \to \infty}(1 - 1/n)^n = e^{-1}$.
- **(1 mark)** **Role:** The $\sim$36.8% observations not in a bootstrap sample act as a free **internal validation set**, allowing OOB error estimation without holding out data, which is critical in small-sample time series problems.

### Model Solutions

#### Q3(a) — Moving Block Bootstrap

**(a) [7 marks] Detailed Explanation**

**(i) Block formation (1.5 marks):**
For a series $\{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$ and chosen block length $l$, construct $n - l + 1$ overlapping blocks:
$$B_i = (X_i, X_{i+1}, \dots, X_{i+l-1}), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n - l + 1$$

**(ii) Block sampling (1.5 marks):**
Draw $k = \lceil n / l \rceil$ block indices $i_1, i_2, \dots, i_k$ independently and uniformly from $\{1, 2, \dots, n - l + 1\}$.

**(iii) Reconstruction (1.5 marks):**
Concatenate the $k$ selected blocks in order:
$$X^{*}_1, \dots, X^{*}_l, X^{*}_{l+1}, \dots, X^{*}_{2l}, \dots$$
This produces a pseudo-series of length $\approx n$ that **preserves the within-block dependence structure**.

**(iv) Optimal block length (2.5 marks):**
From Politis & White (2004), the asymptotically MSE-optimal block length is:
$$l_{\text{opt}} = \left[ \frac{2\,\hat{G}^{2}(0)}{n\,\hat{D}} \right]^{1/3} \;\propto\; n^{1/3}$$
where $\hat{G}^{2}(0) = \sum_{h} \vert \gamma(h) \vert^{2}$ captures overall dependence and $\hat{D} = 4 \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} h^{2}\gamma^{2}(h)$ captures the long-range-decay structure. The $n^{1/3}$ rate is **slower** than the classical $n^{1/2}$ because of dependence.

---

**(b) [7 marks] Numerical Computation**

**Step 1 — Bootstrap standard error (2.5 marks):**
$$\widehat{\text{se}}_B(\hat{\phi}) = \sqrt{\widehat{\text{Var}}_B} = \sqrt{0.0042}$$

$$\sqrt{0.0042} = \sqrt{42 \times 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{42} \times 10^{-2} \approx 6.4807 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{\widehat{\text{se}}_B(\hat{\phi}) \approx 0.0648}$$

**Step 2 — Bootstrap bias estimate (2 marks):**
$$\widehat{\text{Bias}}_B = \bar{\hat{\phi}}^{*} - \hat{\phi} = 0.661 - 0.652 = \boxed{+0.009}$$

Interpretation: the OLS estimator has a small upward bias of about 0.009 in finite samples for this AR(1).

**Step 3 — 95% Normal-approximation CI (2.5 marks):**
For $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical value is $z_{0.975} = 1.96$.

Margin of error:
$$\text{ME} = z_{0.975} \cdot \widehat{\text{se}}_B = 1.96 \times 0.0648 \approx 0.1270$$

Bootstrap-corrected centre (bias-corrected point estimate):
$$\hat{\phi}_{\text{bc}} = \hat{\phi} - \widehat{\text{Bias}}_B = 0.652 - 0.009 = 0.643$$

CI with bias correction:
$$0.643 \pm 0.1270 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{[0.516, \; 0.770]}$$

(If plain normal-approx CI without bias correction is required: $0.652 \pm 0.127 = [0.525, 0.779]$.)

The true parameter $\phi = 0.7$ is contained in both intervals ✓.

---

#### Q3(b) — Bagging Algorithm and Variance Reduction

**(a) [7 marks] Algorithm + Diagram**

**Algorithm steps (3 marks):**
1. For $b = 1, \dots, B$, draw bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}^{*b}$ of size $n$ from $\mathcal{D}$ (with replacement).
2. Fit an ARIMA model on $\mathcal{D}^{*b}$ to obtain forecast $\hat{y}^{*b}_{t+h}$.
3. Aggregate: $\hat{y}_{\text{bag}} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{y}^{*b}_{t+h}$.

**Architecture (already shown in mermaid diagram above in Section 4.2).** (1 mark)

**Variance reduction (1 mark):** As shown in Derivation 3.3, $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho\sigma^{2} + (1-\rho)\sigma^{2}/B$. As $B$ grows, the second term vanishes, leaving a residual floor of $\rho\sigma^{2}$.

**OOB role (1 mark):** Each observation is OOB for $\approx 36.8\%$ of trees; the average prediction error on OOB samples gives an **unbiased estimate of generalization error** without a separate validation set.

**Stability argument (1 mark):** Unstable learners (deep trees, neural nets) have high $\sigma^2$, so bagging yields **large absolute reduction**. Stable linear models have low $\sigma^2$ to begin with — bagging helps little.

---

**(b) [7 marks] Numerical Computation**

**Given:** $B = 500$, $\sigma^{2} = 4.0$, $\rho = 0.30$.

**Step 1 — Variance of bagged predictor (3 marks):**
Using $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = \rho\sigma^{2} + (1-\rho)\sigma^{2}/B$:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = 0.30 \times 4.0 + \frac{(1 - 0.30) \times 4.0}{500}$$

$$= 1.20 + \frac{0.70 \times 4.0}{500} = 1.20 + \frac{2.80}{500} = 1.20 + 0.0056$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) = 1.2056}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison & percentage reduction (2.5 marks):**
- Single tree variance: $\sigma^2 = 4.0$.
- Bagged variance: $1.2056$.
- Absolute reduction: $4.0 - 1.2056 = 2.7944$.
- Percentage reduction:
$$\frac{2.7944}{4.0} \times 100\% = 69.86\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Percentage reduction} \approx 69.86\%}$$

**Step 3 — Limiting behaviour (1.5 marks):**
As $B \to \infty$, the second term $(1-\rho)\sigma^{2}/B \to 0$, so:
$$\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}) \longrightarrow \rho\sigma^{2} = 0.30 \times 4.0 = 1.20$$

This is the **irreducible floor** imposed by inter-tree correlation $\rho$. To reduce variance further, one must **de-correlate** the base learners — e.g., by using **Random Forests** (which adds feature randomness on top of bagging) or by training on fundamentally different model classes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost**
> 1. **Confusing in-bag and OOB fractions** — students often swap 0.6321 and 0.3679. Memorize: **OOB = 0.3679**.
> 2. **Using $B$ instead of $(B-1)$ in the bootstrap variance formula** — costs 1 mark; remember **Bessel's correction**.
> 3. **Applying the naïve bootstrap to time series** — the **#1 conceptual error** in KTU answers. Always specify a block-based variant.
> 4. **Forgetting bias correction** in CI construction — examiners explicitly test whether the candidate subtracts $\widehat{\text{Bias}}_B$ from $\hat{\phi}$.
> 5. **Saying bagging "eliminates" variance** — it only **reduces** it to the floor $\rho\sigma^{2}$.
> 6. **Forgetting the $n^{1/3}$ scaling** in block-length derivations — KTU specifically tests this rule.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bootstrapping** ≈ resampling with replacement; **Bagging** = Bootstrap + Aggregation.
- **OOB fraction** = $\lim(1 - 1/n)^n = 1/e \approx 0.3679$; in-bag $\approx 0.6321$.
- **Naïve bootstrap is INVALID for time series** — use NBB, MBB, or SB instead.
- **Moving Block Bootstrap (MBB)** uses overlapping blocks of length $l$; preserves local dependence.
- **Stationary Bootstrap (SB)** uses Geometric block lengths → smoother boundary, true stationarity.
- **Optimal block length** scales as $l_{\text{opt}} \propto n^{1/3}$ (Politis & White, 2004).
- **Bootstrap SE** uses **$(B-1)$** in the denominator (Bessel's correction).
- **Bootstrap bias** = $\bar{\hat{\theta}}^{*} - \hat{\theta}$ — subtract it to get a bias-corrected estimator.
- **Bagging variance** = $\rho\sigma^{2} + (1-\rho)\sigma^{2}/B$ → floor is $\rho\sigma^{2}$ as $B \to \infty$.
- **Bagging works best for unstable, high-variance learners** (deep trees, neural nets).
- **OOB error** = free internal cross-validation; no need for a held-out validation set.
- **Pairwise correlation $\rho$** between base learners determines the *residual* variance that bagging cannot remove.
- **KTU weightage cue:** Bootstrapping & Bagging routinely appear as **7-mark sub-questions** in Part B and **3-mark definitional questions** in Part A; the numerical variance-reduction computation is a **guaranteed examiner favourite**.
